name: Math4Stone

fields:
Derive the geometric dot product (step 1 of 2): get an expression for \(\lVert a-b\rVert^2\) by expanding it algebraically.
Formula: \(\lVert a-b\rVert^2 = (a-b)\cdot(a-b)\) and the law of cosines \(\lVert a-b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2 + \lVert b\rVert^2 - 2\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\)
Hint: Expand \((a-b)\cdot(a-b)\) using distributivity, then set it equal to the law-of-cosines form.

Algebraic expansion: \(\lVert a-b\rVert^2 = (a-b)\cdot(a-b) = \lVert a\rVert^2 - 2(a\cdot b) + \lVert b\rVert^2\)
Law of cosines on the triangle with sides \(a\), \(b\), \(a-b\): \(\lVert a-b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2 + \lVert b\rVert^2 - 2\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\)
Set the two equal
\(\boxed{\lVert a\rVert^2 - 2(a\cdot b) + \lVert b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2 + \lVert b\rVert^2 - 2\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta}\)

Derive the geometric dot product (step 2 of 2): solve the matched expression for \(a\cdot b\).
Formula: \(\lVert a\rVert^2 - 2(a\cdot b) + \lVert b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2 + \lVert b\rVert^2 - 2\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\); target \(a\cdot b = \lVert a\rVert\,\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\)
Hint: Cancel the \(\lVert a\rVert^2+\lVert b\rVert^2\) that appears on both sides, then divide by \(-2\).

Cancel \(\lVert a\rVert^2 + \lVert b\rVert^2\) from both sides: \(-2(a\cdot b) = -2\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\)
Divide by \(-2\)
\(\boxed{a\cdot b = \lVert a\rVert\,\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta}\)

Derive the robust angle formula and say why it beats \(\arccos\).
Formula: \(a\cdot b=\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\), \(\lVert a\times b\rVert=\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta\); target \(\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(\lVert a\times b\rVert,\, a\cdot b)\)
Hint: Divide the cross magnitude by the dot product so the \(\lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\) factors cancel into \(\tan\theta\).

Divide the two: \(\dfrac{\lVert a\times b\rVert}{a\cdot b} = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta\)
Invert with the two-argument arctangent so the quadrant and sign are kept
\(\boxed{\theta = \operatorname{atan2}\big(\lVert a\times b\rVert,\; a\cdot b\big)}\)
Stable near \(0\) and \(\pi\), where \(\arccos\) of a near-\(\pm1\) argument loses precision or returns NaN.

Derive the vector projection (step 1 of 2): the projection of \(a\) onto \(b\) is parallel to \(b\), so write it as \(C\,b\) and solve for the scalar \(C\).
Formula: residual orthogonality \((a - Cb)\cdot b = 0\); target \(C = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\)
Hint: Expand the dot product \((a-Cb)\cdot b=0\) and isolate \(C\), using \(b\cdot b=\lVert b\rVert^2\).

The residual \(a - Cb\) must be orthogonal to \(b\), so \((a - Cb)\cdot b = 0\)
Expand: \(a\cdot b - C\,(b\cdot b) = 0\)
\(\boxed{C = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{b\cdot b} = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}}\)

Derive the vector projection (step 2 of 2): write the final projection and its unit-vector form.
Formula: \(C=\dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\), \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = C\,b\); target \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b = (a\cdot\hat b)\,\hat b\)
Hint: Substitute \(C\) into \(C\,b\), then split one \(\lVert b\rVert\) off each side to form \(\hat b = b/\lVert b\rVert\).

Substitute \(C\) back into \(C\,b\): \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b\)
Group \(b/\lVert b\rVert = \hat b\) to get the safe form \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \left(a\cdot\dfrac{b}{\lVert b\rVert}\right)\dfrac{b}{\lVert b\rVert}\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b = (a\cdot\hat b)\,\hat b}\)

Derive the scalar projection (signed shadow length) of \(a\) onto \(b\).
Formula: start from \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b\); target \(\operatorname{comp}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert}\)
Hint: Take the signed length by multiplying the scalar coefficient by \(\lVert b\rVert\), then cancel one factor of \(\lVert b\rVert\).

Take the signed length: \(\operatorname{comp}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,\lVert b\rVert\)
Cancel one factor of \(\lVert b\rVert\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{comp}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert}}\)

Derive the cross-product magnitude (step 1 of 2): express \(\lVert a\times b\rVert\) via \(\theta\).
Formula: Lagrange identity \(\lVert a\times b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2 - (a\cdot b)^2\) and \(a\cdot b = \lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\); target \(\lVert a\times b\rVert = \lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta\)
Hint: Substitute the geometric dot product into the Lagrange identity, then use \(1-\cos^2\theta=\sin^2\theta\).

Substitute \(a\cdot b = \lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta\): \(\lVert a\times b\rVert^2 = \lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2 - \lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2\cos^2\theta\)
Factor and use \(1-\cos^2\theta=\sin^2\theta\): \(= \lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2\sin^2\theta\)
Take the root (\(\sin\theta\ge0\) for \(\theta\in[0,\pi]\))
\(\boxed{\lVert a\times b\rVert = \lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta}\)

Derive the parallelogram area (step 2 of 2): show \(\lVert a\times b\rVert\) equals the area of the parallelogram on \(a\) and \(b\), and give the triangle area.
Formula: \(\lVert a\times b\rVert = \lVert a\rVert\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta\); area \(=\) base \(\times\) height; target \(A_{\triangle} = \tfrac12\lVert a\times b\rVert\)
Hint: Take base \(=\lVert a\rVert\) and height \(=\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta\) (the part of \(b\) perpendicular to \(a\)); the triangle is half.

The parallelogram has base \(\lVert a\rVert\) and height \(\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta\) (the component of \(b\) perpendicular to \(a\))
Area \(=\) base \(\times\) height \(= \lVert a\rVert\,(\lVert b\rVert\sin\theta) = \lVert a\times b\rVert\)
The triangle is half the parallelogram
\(\boxed{A_{\triangle} = \tfrac12\lVert a\times b\rVert}\)

Derive a unit surface normal from three points (step 1 of 2): given non-collinear \(p_0,p_1,p_2\), build a vector orthogonal to both edges and make it unit length.
Formula: \(\hat n = \dfrac{(p_1-p_0)\times(p_2-p_0)}{\lVert(p_1-p_0)\times(p_2-p_0)\rVert}\)
Hint: Form the two edge vectors from \(p_0\) first, cross them, then divide by the magnitude.

Edge vectors from \(p_0\): \(e_1 = p_1-p_0\), \(e_2 = p_2-p_0\)
Their cross product is orthogonal to both: \(n = e_1\times e_2\)
Normalize
\(\boxed{\hat n = \dfrac{(p_1-p_0)\times(p_2-p_0)}{\lVert(p_1-p_0)\times(p_2-p_0)\rVert}}\)

Orient a normal toward a viewpoint (step 2 of 2): derive the sign test that flips a raw unit normal \(\hat n\) to face viewpoint \(v\) (the IFC/scan orientation step).
Formula: view direction \(d = v - p_0\); flip rule \(\hat n \leftarrow \operatorname{sign}(\hat n\cdot d)\,\hat n\)
Hint: Compute the dot product of \(\hat n\) with the direction to the viewer; positive means it already faces the viewer.

Form the view direction from the surface point \(p_0\): \(d = v - p_0\)
\(\hat n\) faces the viewer iff their dot product is positive: \(\hat n\cdot d > 0\)
Flip when it is not
\(\boxed{\hat n \leftarrow \operatorname{sign}(\hat n\cdot d)\,\hat n}\)

Derive how a single coordinate is recovered in an orthonormal basis.
Formula: \(a=\sum_j c_j e_j\) with \(e_i\cdot e_j=\delta_{ij}\); target \(c_i = a\cdot e_i\)
Hint: Dot both sides with \(e_i\); the Kronecker delta kills every term except \(j=i\).

Dot both sides with \(e_i\): \(a\cdot e_i = \sum_j c_j (e_j\cdot e_i)\)
Only the \(j=i\) term survives because \(e_j\cdot e_i=\delta_{ij}\)
\(\boxed{c_i = a\cdot e_i,\quad\text{so } a=\sum_i (a\cdot e_i)\,e_i}\)

(worked example, mm scale) In the shop, \(a=(6,8,0)\,\text{mm}\) and \(b=(2,0,0)\,\text{mm}\). Compute \(\lVert a\rVert\) and \(a\cdot b\).
Formula: \(\lVert a\rVert=\sqrt{a_1^2+a_2^2+a_3^2}\), \(a\cdot b=\sum_i a_ib_i\)
Hint: Square and sum the components of \(a\) for the norm; multiply matched components and add for the dot product.

(worked example, mm scale) For \(a=(6,8,0)\,\text{mm}\), \(b=(2,0,0)\,\text{mm}\), compute \(\operatorname{proj}_b a\) and the orthogonal remainder \(\operatorname{perp}_b a\).
Formula: \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b\), \(\operatorname{perp}_b a = a - \operatorname{proj}_b a\)
Hint: Compute \(\lVert b\rVert^2\) and \(a\cdot b\) first, get the scalar \(C=(a\cdot b)/\lVert b\rVert^2\), then scale \(b\) and subtract.

\(\lVert b\rVert^2 = 4\), \(a\cdot b=12\), so \(C=12/4=3\)
\(\operatorname{proj}_b a = 3\,(2,0,0)=(6,0,0)\,\text{mm}\)
\(\operatorname{perp}_b a = (6,8,0)-(6,0,0)=(0,8,0)\,\text{mm}\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{proj}_b a=(6,0,0),\ \operatorname{perp}_b a=(0,8,0)}\)

(worked example, site/quarry scale, m) A scan displacement \(a=(300,400,0)\,\text{m}\) projected onto the easting axis \(b=(1000,0,0)\,\text{m}\). Compute \(\operatorname{proj}_b a\).
Formula: \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b\)
Hint: Compute \(a\cdot b\) and \(\lVert b\rVert^2\) first; notice the answer should pick off the \(x\)-component just like the mm case.

\(a\cdot b = 300\cdot1000 = 3\times10^5\), \(\lVert b\rVert^2 = 10^6\)
\(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{3\times10^5}{10^6}(1000,0,0) = (300,0,0)\,\text{m}\)
Same shape as the mm case (projection picks off the \(x\)-component) confirming the formula is scale-free
\(\boxed{\operatorname{proj}_b a=(300,0,0)\,\text{m}}\)

(worked example, 3D normal) Three scan points \(p_0=(0,0,0)\), \(p_1=(2,0,0)\), \(p_2=(0,3,0)\) (m). Compute the unit normal \(\hat n\).
Formula: \(n=(p_1-p_0)\times(p_2-p_0)\), \(\hat n = n/\lVert n\rVert\), with \(a\times b=(a_2b_3-a_3b_2,\ a_3b_1-a_1b_3,\ a_1b_2-a_2b_1)\)
Hint: Compute the two edge vectors first, then plug into the cross-product component formula.

(closing test, close the loop) For \(a=(6,8,0)\), \(b=(2,0,0)\) you found \(\operatorname{perp}_b a=(0,8,0)\). Verify it is orthogonal to \(b\) and that \(\operatorname{proj}+\operatorname{perp}\) rebuilds \(a\).
Formula: orthogonality test \(\operatorname{perp}_b a\cdot b = 0\); reconstruction \(\operatorname{proj}_b a + \operatorname{perp}_b a = a\)
Hint: A pass means the dot product is exactly \(0\) and the two parts sum back to \(a\) componentwise.

Orthogonality: \((0,8,0)\cdot(2,0,0)=0+0+0=0\), so \(\operatorname{perp}_b a\perp b\)
Reconstruction: \((6,0,0)+(0,8,0)=(6,8,0)=a\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{perp}_b a\cdot b=0\ \text{and}\ \operatorname{proj}+\operatorname{perp}=a\ \checkmark}\)

(closing test, close the loop) For \(a=(1,2,2)\), \(b=(0,0,3)\), verify the Lagrange identity numerically.
Formula: \(\lVert a\times b\rVert^2=\lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2-(a\cdot b)^2\), with \(a\times b=(a_2b_3-a_3b_2,\ a_3b_1-a_1b_3,\ a_1b_2-a_2b_1)\)
Hint: Compute \(a\times b\) and its squared norm (left side) and the right side separately; a pass means both equal the same number.

\(a\times b=(2\cdot3-2\cdot0,\ 2\cdot0-1\cdot3,\ 1\cdot0-2\cdot0)=(6,-3,0)\), so \(\lVert a\times b\rVert^2=36+9+0=45\)
RHS: \(\lVert a\rVert^2\lVert b\rVert^2-(a\cdot b)^2 = 9\cdot9 - 6^2 = 81-36=45\)
Both sides equal \(45\)
\(\boxed{45=45\ \checkmark}\)

(closing test, limiting case) Check the angle of perpendicular vectors \(a=(1,0,0)\), \(b=(0,1,0)\) two ways.
Formula: \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(\lVert a\times b\rVert,\,a\cdot b)\); cross-check via \(a\cdot b=0\Rightarrow\theta=90^\circ\)
Hint: Compute \(a\times b\) and \(a\cdot b\) first; a pass means both methods give \(90^\circ\).

\(a\times b=(0,0,1)\) so \(\lVert a\times b\rVert=1\); \(a\cdot b=0\)
\(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(1,0)=\pi/2=90^\circ\)
Cross-check: \(a\cdot b=0\Rightarrow\cos\theta=0\Rightarrow\theta=90^\circ\)
\(\boxed{\theta=90^\circ\ \checkmark}\)

Definition: when is \(\hat a=a/\lVert a\rVert\) well defined, and what is the pitfall otherwise?
Formula: \(\hat a = a/\lVert a\rVert\) with tolerance \(\varepsilon=\max(\varepsilon_{\text{floor}},10^{-3}L)\)
Hint: The danger is dividing by a length that is below \(\varepsilon\).

Defined only when \(\lVert a\rVert>0\); guard with a relative tolerance \(\varepsilon=\max(\varepsilon_{\text{floor}},10^{-3}L)\)
\(\boxed{\text{Normalizing a near-zero vector amplifies noise into a garbage direction}}\)

When (and why) do you prefer `squaredNorm()` over `norm()` in projection and comparison code?
Formula: \(\lVert b\rVert^2 = b\cdot b\); projection scalar \(C=(a\cdot b)/(b\cdot b)\)
Hint: Think about what computing a square root costs and whether the projection even needs it.
Inside projections and length comparisons it avoids a square root and its rounding error; \(\lVert b\rVert^2=b\cdot b\) is exactly what the projection scalar \(C=(a\cdot b)/(b\cdot b)\) needs.

Pitfall: what is the single most common projection bug, and which form avoids it?
Formula: \(\operatorname{proj}_b a = \dfrac{a\cdot b}{\lVert b\rVert^2}\,b = (a\cdot\hat b)\,\hat b\)
Hint: Compare the \(1/\lVert b\rVert^2\) factor against using \(\hat b\), and which form makes the unit vectors explicit.
Forgetting the \(1/\lVert b\rVert^2\) factor (or using \(\hat b\) where you needed \(b\)). The form \((a\cdot\hat b)\,\hat b\) avoids it because both unit factors are explicit.

When should you prefer `stableNorm()` over `norm()` in Eigen?
Hint: Think about inputs scaled by something like \(10^{200}\) or \(10^{-200}\).
When vector components may be extremely large or small, to avoid overflow/underflow.
The cross product is {{c1::anticommutative}}, so \(a\times b = -(b\times a)\).

Foundations · Vectors
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Vectors · EoLA Ch.1
In an orthonormal basis the coordinates of \(a\) are recovered as {{c1::dot products}}: \(a=\sum_i (a\cdot e_i)e_i\).

Foundations · Vectors
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Vectors · EoLA Ch.1
The cross product is defined only in {{c1::three}} dimensions.

Foundations · Vectors
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Vectors · EoLA Ch.1
Reversing a triangle's {{c1::winding order}} flips the sign of its computed normal, which can invert IFC face orientation.

Foundations · Vectors
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Vectors · EoLA Ch.1
A length counts as "zero" when it falls below the relative tolerance {{c1::\(\varepsilon=\max(\varepsilon_{\text{floor}},10^{-3}L)\)}}, never a bare hard-coded constant.

Foundations · Vectors
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Vectors · EoLA Ch.1

Derive what column \(j\) of a matrix \(A\) must equal, the image of a basis vector.
Formula: start from \(Ax=\sum_j x_j (Ae_j)\), target \(a_j = Ae_j\).
Hint: substitute \(x=e_j\) so every weight is \(0\) except \(x_j=1\).

Set \(x=e_j\), so all weights are \(0\) except \(x_j=1\)
Then \(Ax=Ae_j\) picks out a single column
By the definition of the matrix-vector product, \(Ae_j\) is the \(j\)-th column \(a_j\)
\(\boxed{a_j = A e_j}\) (each column is the image of a basis vector).

Derive the expansion of \(Ax\) over the columns of \(A\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: start from \(x=\sum_j x_j e_j\), use linearity \(A(\alpha u+\beta v)=\alpha Au+\beta Av\).
Hint: apply \(A\) to both sides, then move \(A\) inside the sum and past each scalar.

Apply \(A\) to both sides: \(Ax = A\big(\sum_j x_j e_j\big)\)
Linearity moves \(A\) inside the sum and past the scalars: \(Ax=\sum_j x_j (A e_j)\)
\(\boxed{Ax = \sum_{j} x_j\,(A e_j)}\).

Continue: derive \(Ax\) as a combination of columns (step 2 of 2).
Formula: start from \(Ax=\sum_j x_j (Ae_j)\) and the column identity \(Ae_j=a_j\).
Hint: substitute \(Ae_j=a_j\) into the sum.

Substitute the column identity \(Ae_j=a_j\)
\(Ax=\sum_j x_j a_j\)
So the output is a weighted sum of the columns, weights \(=\) entries of \(x\)
\(\boxed{Ax = \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j\,a_j}\) (the column space is the span of the \(a_j\)).

Derive the row-dot-column formula for the \(i\)-th entry \((Ax)_i\).
Formula: start from \(Ax=\sum_j x_j a_j\), target \((Ax)_i=\sum_j A_{ij}x_j\).
Hint: take component \(i\) of each column, using \((a_j)_i=A_{ij}\).

The \(i\)-th entry of column \(a_j\) is \(A_{ij}\)
Take component \(i\) of both sides: \((Ax)_i=\sum_j x_j (a_j)_i=\sum_j x_j A_{ij}\)
\(\boxed{(Ax)_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\,x_j}\).

Derive the product entry \(C_{ij}\) for \(C=AB\).
Formula: column \(j\) of \(C\) is \(Ab_j\); entry \(i\) is \((Ab_j)_i=\sum_l A_{il}(b_j)_l\).
Hint: write \(b_j\) as column \(j\) of \(B\), then use \((b_j)_l=B_{lj}\).

Column \(j\) of \(C\) is \(A b_j\) where \(b_j\) is column \(j\) of \(B\)
Its \(i\)-th entry is \((Ab_j)_i=\sum_l A_{il}(b_j)_l=\sum_l A_{il}B_{lj}\)
\(\boxed{C_{ij}=\sum_{l=1}^{k} A_{il}B_{lj}}\).

Derive why composition \(C=AB\) reads right-to-left (do \(B\) first).
Formula: \((AB)x = A(Bx)\) by associativity.
Hint: read the right side from the inside out, like the function composition \(A(B(x))\).

For any \(x\), \((AB)x = A(Bx)\) by associativity
So \(B\) acts first, then \(A\) acts on the result
This mirrors function composition \(A(B(x))\)
\(\boxed{(AB)x=A(Bx)\ \Rightarrow\ \text{do }B\text{ first, then }A}\) (hence \(AB\neq BA\) in general).

Derive the transpose-of-a-product rule \((AB)^\top\).
Formula: entry definition \((M^\top)_{ij}=M_{ji}\) and product \((AB)_{ji}=\sum_l A_{jl}B_{li}\).
Hint: expand \(((AB)^\top)_{ij}=(AB)_{ji}\), then rewrite each factor as a transpose entry.

\(((AB)^\top)_{ij}=(AB)_{ji}=\sum_l A_{jl}B_{li}\)
Rewrite each factor as a transpose entry: \(A_{jl}=(A^\top)_{lj}\), \(B_{li}=(B^\top)_{il}\)
\(=\sum_l (B^\top)_{il}(A^\top)_{lj}=(B^\top A^\top)_{ij}\)
\(\boxed{(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top}\).

Derive the inverse-of-a-product rule \((AB)^{-1}\).
Formula: the inverse satisfies \((AB)(AB)^{-1}=I\); target \((AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}\).
Hint: guess the undo-order \(B^{-1}A^{-1}\) and check it cancels from the inside out.

Guess the undo-order: apply \(B^{-1}\) then \(A^{-1}\)
Check: \((AB)(B^{-1}A^{-1})=A(BB^{-1})A^{-1}=A I A^{-1}=I\)
Uniqueness of the inverse then forces the guess
\(\boxed{(AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}}\) (order reverses).

Derive \(\det A\) of \(A=\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}\) as the signed area of the column parallelogram (step 1 of 2).
Formula: signed area \(=\) cross-product \(z\)-component of columns \((a,c)\) and \((b,d)\).
Hint: the 2D cross-product of \((a,c)\) and \((b,d)\) is \(a\cdot d - c\cdot b\).

Columns \((a,c)\) and \((b,d)\) span a parallelogram
Signed area \(=\) cross-product \(z\)-component \(=ad-cb\)
\(\boxed{\det A = ad-bc}\).

Derive the \(2\times2\) inverse \(A^{-1}\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: use \(\det A=ad-bc\) and the requirement \(AA^{-1}=I\); target \(A^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}\).
Hint: try the adjugate (swap the diagonal, negate the off-diagonal), multiply by \(A\), then divide by \(\det A\).

Try \(A^{-1}=\frac{1}{\det A}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}\) (swap diagonal, negate off-diagonal)
Check: \(\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}ad-bc&0\\0&ad-bc\end{bmatrix}=(\det A) I\)
Divide by \(\det A\) to get \(I\)
\(\boxed{A^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}}\) (needs \(\det A\neq0\)).

Derive what \(|\det A|\) and \(\operatorname{sign}(\det A)\) mean geometrically, from the unit square.
Formula: \(A\) maps the unit square (area \(1\)) to the parallelogram on columns \(a_1,a_2\) with signed area \(\det A\).
Hint: compare output area to input area \(1\); a negative sign means the square was flipped over.

\(A\) sends the unit square (area \(1\)) to the parallelogram on columns \(a_1,a_2\)
Its signed area is \(\det A\), so area scales by \(|\det A|\)
A negative sign means the square was flipped over
\(\boxed{|\det A|=\text{area/volume scale},\ \ \det A<0\Rightarrow\text{orientation reversed}}\).

Derive why \(\det A=0\) forces \(A\) to be singular.
Formula: \(|\det A|\) is the area/volume scale of the column parallelogram.
Hint: set that area to zero and ask what it says about the columns' independence.

\(\det A=0\) means the output parallelogram has zero area
So the columns are linearly dependent and span \( The map collapses space onto a lower dimension, so no inverse can recover it
\(\boxed{\det A=0\iff \text{rank-deficient}\iff \text{singular}}\).

Compute \(\det A\) for the \(2\times2\) matrix \(A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\4&3\end{bmatrix}\) and state the area/orientation effect.
Formula: \(\det\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}=ad-bc\).
Hint: compute the main-diagonal product \(2\cdot3\) first, then subtract the off-diagonal product \(1\cdot4\).

\(\det A=(2)(3)-(1)(4)=6-4=2\)
Nonzero, so invertible
\(\boxed{\det A=2}\): area scaled \(\times 2\), orientation preserved.

Build the \(2\times2\) rotation by \(30^\circ\) and compute its determinant.
Formula: \(R=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{bmatrix}\), \(\det R=\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta\).
Hint: plug in \(\cos30^\circ=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2},\ \sin30^\circ=\tfrac12\), then add the squares.

\(R=\begin{bmatrix}\cos30^\circ&-\sin30^\circ\\ \sin30^\circ&\cos30^\circ\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}&-\tfrac12\\[2pt]\tfrac12&\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\end{bmatrix}\)
\(\det R=\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=\tfrac34+\tfrac14\)
\(\boxed{\det R=1}\): rotations preserve area and orientation.

For the reflection \(A=\operatorname{diag}(1,-1)\) compute \(\det A\) and say what it does to a stone scan.
Formula: for a diagonal matrix, \(\det\operatorname{diag}(d_1,d_2)=d_1 d_2\).
Hint: multiply the two diagonal entries; check the sign for the orientation flip.

\(\det A=(1)(-1)=-1\)
\(|\det A|=1\) so area is preserved
Sign \(<0\) so winding/handedness flips
\(\boxed{\det A=-1}\): it mirrors the block (must not be used as a rotation).

A uniform scale \(A=\operatorname{diag}(s,s,s)\) maps a \(2\,\text{m}\) stone block; find the volume factor for \(s=1.5\).
Formula: \(\det\operatorname{diag}(s,s,s)=s^3\).
Hint: cube \(s=1.5\); the block's size in metres does not enter.

\(\det A=s\cdot s\cdot s=s^3\)
For \(s=1.5\): \(\det A=1.5^3=3.375\)
\(\boxed{\det A=3.375}\): volume scales \(\times3.375\) regardless of the block's metres.

Factor \(A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\4&3\end{bmatrix}\) by LU with partial pivoting (step 1 of 2): pivot and eliminate.
Formula: \(PA=LU\); multiplier \(\ell_{21}=A_{21}/A_{11}\) after pivoting; Row2 \(\leftarrow\) Row2 \(-\ell_{21}\cdot\)Row1.
Hint: compare the column-1 magnitudes \(2\) and \(4\) first, swap so \(4\) is the pivot, then compute the multiplier.

Column-1 candidates \(2,4\); larger is \(4\), so swap rows (\(P\) swaps rows 1,2): \(PA=\begin{bmatrix}4&3\\2&1\end{bmatrix}\)
Multiplier \(\ell_{21}=2/4=0.5\); Row2 \(\leftarrow\) Row2 \(-0.5\cdot\)Row1
\(\boxed{U=\begin{bmatrix}4&3\\0&-0.5\end{bmatrix},\ L=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0.5&1\end{bmatrix}}\).

Solve \(Ax=b\) with \(b=(1,7)\) using the \(PA=LU\) factors of the previous card (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(Ly=Pb\) (forward substitution), then \(Ux=y\) (back substitution).
Hint: apply \(P\) to \(b\) first to get \(Pb=(7,1)\), then solve \(L\) top-down before \(U\) bottom-up.

Closing test: verify the solution \(x=(-2,5)\) for \(A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\4&3\end{bmatrix},\,b=(1,7)\).
Formula: substitute \(x\) back and check the residual \(\|Ax-b\|=0\).
Hint: a pass means each component of \(Ax\) matches \(b\) exactly; compute \(Ax\) row by row.

Closing test: re-solve \(A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\4&3\end{bmatrix},\,b=(1,7)\) via \(A^{-1}b\) and confirm it matches the LU answer.
Formula: \(A^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}\), then \(x=A^{-1}b\).
Hint: here \(\det A=2\), so build \(A^{-1}=\tfrac12\begin{bmatrix}3&-1\\-4&2\end{bmatrix}\) first, then multiply by \(b\).

Show the near-singular matrix \(A=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&1+\epsilon\end{bmatrix}\) is ill-conditioned as \(\epsilon\to0\).
Formula: \(\det A=ad-bc\) and \(\kappa(A)=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\).
Hint: compute \(\det A\) first; as it \(\to0\) the columns turn parallel so \(\sigma_{\min}\to0\) while \(\sigma_{\max}\approx2\).

\(\det A=(1)(1+\epsilon)-(1)(1)=\epsilon\to0\)
Columns become nearly parallel, so \(\sigma_{\min}\to0\) while \(\sigma_{\max}\approx2\)
\(\kappa=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\to\infty\)
\(\boxed{\epsilon\to0\Rightarrow\kappa(A)\to\infty}\): the plane/line fit through near-collinear scan points is ill-posed.

Closing test: check that \(\kappa(A)\ge1\) always, with equality for a pure rotation.
Formula: \(\kappa(A)=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\), and an orthogonal \(R\) has all \(\sigma_i=1\).
Hint: a pass means the ratio bottoms out at \(1\); use \(\sigma_{\max}\ge\sigma_{\min}\), then set every \(\sigma_i=1\).

Singular values obey \(\sigma_{\max}\ge\sigma_{\min}>0\), so the ratio is \(\ge1\)
A rotation is orthogonal: all \(\sigma_i=1\), so \(\sigma_{\max}=\sigma_{\min}=1\)
\(\boxed{\kappa(R)=1/1=1}\): rotations are perfectly conditioned (lower bound hit).

When-to-use: why factor once with `partialPivLu()` and reuse it for many right-hand sides \(b\)?
Formula: factor cost is \(O(n^3)\); each triangular solve is \(O(n^2)\) per \(b\).
Hint: compare the one-time cubic cost against the per-\(b\) quadratic cost.

Factoring costs \(O(n^3)\) but each back/forward solve is only \(O(n^2)\)
Reuse amortizes the cubic cost across all the \(b\)
\(\boxed{\text{factor once }O(n^3)\text{, then }O(n^2)\text{ per }b}\).

Pitfall: a rigid-fit rotation comes out with \(\det=-1\). What is wrong and the fix?
Formula: a valid rotation in \(SO(3)\) has \(\det=+1\); after SVD check \(\det(VU^\top)\).
Hint: \(\det=-1\) flags a reflection; flip the last singular vector so the product determinant becomes \(+1\).

It is a reflection, not a rotation
Flip the last singular vector so \(\det(VU^\top)=+1\), else the stone is mirrored
\(\boxed{\det=-1\Rightarrow\text{reflection; flip last column to force }\det=+1}\).

Pitfall: why is `det(A) == 0` a bad singularity test in floating point, and what to use instead?
Formula: use a scale-relative threshold \(|v|<\tau=\varepsilon\cdot\text{scale}\), or a rank-revealing decomposition.
Hint: roundoff never lands exactly on \(0\); reach for `colPivHouseholderQr().rank()`.

Roundoff never lands exactly on zero, so an exact compare misfires
Use a scale-relative threshold \(|v|<\tau=\varepsilon\cdot\text{scale}\) or a rank-revealing decomposition (`colPivHouseholderQr().rank()`)
\(\boxed{\text{test }|v|<\varepsilon\cdot\text{scale, not }==0}\).
Each column \(j\) of \(A\) is the image of the basis vector: \(a_j = {{c1::A e_j}}\).

Foundations · Matrices
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Linear transformations · EoLA Ch.3
\(Ax\) expands as \(\sum_j x_j a_j\), a {{c1::linear combination of the columns of \(A\)}} weighted by the entries of \(x\).

Foundations · Matrices
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Linear transformations · EoLA Ch.3
A square matrix is singular exactly when \(\det A = {{c1::0}}\), equivalently when it is rank-deficient.

Foundations · Matrices
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Linear transformations · EoLA Ch.3

To solve a general square system in Eigen, call {{c1::A.partialPivLu().solve(b)}} rather than forming the inverse.
hint: LU with partial pivoting, the fast default.

Foundations · Matrices
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Linear transformations · EoLA Ch.3
For a symmetric positive definite system such as the normal matrix \(A^\top A\), the fastest Eigen solver is {{c1::llt()}} (Cholesky).

Foundations · Matrices
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Linear transformations · EoLA Ch.3

From first principles, derive \(f'(x)\) for \(f(x)=x^2\).
Formula: \(f'(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}\), target \(\boxed{2x}\)
Hint: substitute \(f(x+h)=(x+h)^2\), expand the square, then cancel the \(x^2\) terms before dividing by \(h\).

Derive the central finite-difference identity from two Taylor expansions (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(f(x\pm h)=f(x)\pm f'(x)h+\tfrac12 f''(x)h^2\pm\tfrac16 f'''(x)h^3+\dots\)
Hint: write both expansions, then SUBTRACT \(f(x-h)\) from \(f(x+h)\) so the even-power terms cancel.

\(f(x+h)=f(x)+f'(x)h+\tfrac12 f''(x)h^2+\tfrac16 f'''(x)h^3+\dots\)
\(f(x-h)=f(x)-f'(x)h+\tfrac12 f''(x)h^2-\tfrac16 f'''(x)h^3+\dots\)
subtract: \(f(x+h)-f(x-h)=2f'(x)h+\tfrac13 f'''(x)h^3+\dots\)
\(\boxed{f(x+h)-f(x-h)=2f'(x)h+O(h^3)}\).

From \(f(x+h)-f(x-h)=2f'(x)h+O(h^3)\), derive the central-difference estimator and its error order (step 2 of 2).
Formula: start from \(f(x+h)-f(x-h)=2f'(x)h+O(h^3)\), target \(\boxed{f'(x)\approx\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}}\)
Hint: divide both sides by \(2h\); note \(O(h^3)/h=O(h^2)\).

divide by \(2h\): \(\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}=f'(x)+O(h^2)\)
the leading error term is \(\tfrac16 f'''(x)h^2\)
\(\boxed{f'(x)\approx\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h},\ \text{error }O(h^2)}\).

Derive why a FORWARD difference is only \(O(h)\) accurate.
Formula: \(f(x+h)=f(x)+f'(x)h+\tfrac12 f''(x)h^2+\dots\), target \(\boxed{f'(x)+O(h)}\)
Hint: rearrange the single expansion to isolate \(\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}\); the \(\tfrac12 f''(x)h\) term has no symmetric partner to cancel it.

\(f(x+h)=f(x)+f'(x)h+\tfrac12 f''(x)h^2+\dots\)
\(\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}=f'(x)+\tfrac12 f''(x)h+\dots\)
leading error \(\propto h\) does not cancel (no symmetric term)
\(\boxed{\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}=f'(x)+O(h)}\).

Differentiate \(\sin(x^2)\) using the chain rule.
Formula: \(\frac{d}{dx}f(g(x))=f'(g(x))\,g'(x)\), with \(\frac{d}{dx}\sin u=\cos u\) and \(\frac{d}{dx}x^2=2x\)
Hint: take the inner function \(g(x)=x^2\) first, differentiate the outer \(\sin\) at \(g(x)\), then multiply by \(g'(x)\).

Show the directional derivative \(D_u f\) is largest when \(u\) points along \( abla f\).
Formula: \(D_u f= abla f\cdot u=\lVert abla f Vert\,\lVert u Vert\cos heta\), \(u\) a unit vector
Hint: set \(\lVert u Vert=1\), then ask which value of \(\cos heta\) maximizes the expression.

\(D_u f= abla f\cdot u=\lVert abla f Vert\,\lVert u Vert\cos heta\)
with unit \(u\), \(\lVert u Vert=1\), so \(D_u f=\lVert abla f Vert\cos heta\)
maximized when \(\cos heta=1\), i.e. \(u\parallel abla f\)
\( oxed{\max_u D_u f=\lVert abla f Vert\ ext{at}\ u= abla f/\lVert abla f Vert}\).

Find the gradient of \(f(x,y)=x^2+xy\) at \((1,2)\) (marble-on-a-sheet, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(\nabla f=\left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right]^\top\)
Hint: compute \(\partial f/\partial x\) (treat \(y\) as constant) and \(\partial f/\partial y\) (treat \(x\) as constant), then plug in \(x=1,\ y=2\).

\(\partial f/\partial x=2x+y\), \(\partial f/\partial y=x\)
\( abla f=[2x+y,\ x]^ op\)
at \((1,2)\): \( abla f=[2(1)+2,\ 1]^ op\)
\( oxed{ abla f(1,2)=[4,\ 1]^ op}\).

From \( abla f(1,2)=[4,1]^ op\), find the unit steepest-DESCENT direction the marble rolls (step 2 of 2).
Formula: descent direction \(=- abla f\); unit vector \(=\dfrac{- abla f}{\lVert abla f Vert}\), \(\lVert v Vert=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}\)
Hint: negate the gradient first, then divide by its length \(\sqrt{4^2+1^2}\).

descent is \(-\nabla f=[-4,-1]^\top\)
\(\lVert\nabla f\rVert=\sqrt{4^2+1^2}=\sqrt{17}\)
normalize: \(\boxed{\hat d=\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}}[-4,\ -1]^\top}\).

Derive the Newton step by minimizing the second-order Taylor model.
Formula: \(m(\delta)=f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top\delta+\tfrac12\delta^\top H\delta\), target \(\boxed{\delta=-H^{-1}\nabla f(x)}\)
Hint: differentiate \(m\) with respect to \(\delta\), set the result to zero, then solve the linear system for \(\delta\).

\(m(\delta)=f(x)+ abla f(x)^ op\delta+ frac12\delta^ op H\delta\)
\( abla_\delta m= abla f(x)+H\delta\)
set \(=0\): \(H\delta=- abla f(x)\)
\( oxed{\delta=-H^{-1} abla f(x)}\) (the Newton step).

Evaluate \(\int_0^2 x^2\,dx\) using the Fundamental Theorem.
Formula: \(\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx=F(b)-F(a)\) with antiderivative \(F(x)=x^3/3\) (since \(F'(x)=x^2\))
Hint: find the antiderivative first, then evaluate at \(2\) minus at \(0\).

antiderivative of \(x^2\) is \(F(x)=x^3/3\) since \(F'(x)=x^2\)
\(\int_0^2 x^2\,dx=F(2)-F(0)\)
\(=8/3-0\)
\(\boxed{\int_0^2 x^2\,dx=8/3\approx2.667}\).

Worked example (small scale): estimate \(f'(3)\) for \(f(x)=x^2\) with a central difference, \(h=10^{-3}\).
Formula: \(f'(x)\approx\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}\)
Hint: compute \((3.001)^2\) and \((2.999)^2\) first, then divide their difference by \(2h=0.002\).

Worked example (large scale, m units): a quarry block has cross-section \(A(z)=4-z^2\) (m\(^2\)); find its volume over \(z\in[0,2]\) m.
Formula: \(V=\int_a^b A(z)\,dz\), antiderivative of \(4-z^2\) is \(4z-z^3/3\)
Hint: integrate term by term, then evaluate \([4z-z^3/3]\) at \(z=2\) minus \(z=0\).

Worked example (mm shop scale): a fillet profile \(y(x)=x^2\) (mm); find the area under it over \(x\in[0,5]\) mm.
Formula: \(A=\int_a^b y(x)\,dx\), antiderivative of \(x^2\) is \(x^3/3\)
Hint: evaluate \([x^3/3]\) at \(x=5\) minus at \(x=0\).

Worked example (3D gradient, registration error): for \(E(x,y,z)=x^2+y^2+z^2\), find \( abla E\) and the unit steepest-descent direction at \((1,-2,2)\).
Formula: \( abla E=[2x,2y,2z]^ op\); unit descent \(=\dfrac{- abla E}{\lVert abla E Vert}\)
Hint: plug the point into \( abla E\) first, then compute \(\lVert abla E Vert=\sqrt{2^2+4^2+4^2}\) before normalizing.

\( abla E=[2x,2y,2z]^ op\)
at \((1,-2,2)\): \( abla E=[2,-4,4]^ op\)
descent \(- abla E=[-2,4,-4]^ op\), \(\lVert abla E Vert=\sqrt{4+16+16}=6\)
\( oxed{\hat d= frac16[-2,4,-4]^ op=[- frac13, frac23,- frac23]^ op}\).

Closing test: gradient-check the analytic \(\nabla f=[2x+y,x]^\top\) for \(f=x^2+xy\) at \((1,2)\) against central differences (\(h=10^{-3}\)).
Formula: \(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\approx\frac{f(x+he_i)-f(x-he_i)}{2h}\); pass if the two agree within \(O(h^2)\)
Hint: compute the analytic vector \([4,1]\) first, then perturb \(x\) and \(y\) one at a time; a pass means the FD values round to \(4\) and \(1\).

Closing test (units + limiting case): for energy \(f\) in joules and position \(x\) in metres, give the units of \(\nabla f\) and check the FTC zero-width case \(\int_a^a f'\,dx\).
Formula: \([\nabla f]=[f]/[x]\); FTC \(\int_a^b f'\,dx=f(b)-f(a)\)
Hint: divide the unit of \(f\) by the unit of \(x\); for the integral, set both limits equal so \(b=a\). A pass gives \(0\).

\(\nabla f\) has units J/m (force-like, joules per metre)
FTC: \(\int_a^a f'(x)\,dx=f(a)-f(a)=0\), consistent with zero-width interval
\(\boxed{[\nabla f]=\text{J/m},\quad \int_a^a f'\,dx=0}\).

When do you use a gradient versus a Jacobian?
Hint: think about the SHAPE of the function's output: one number, or a vector?
Gradient for a scalar-valued \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\) (a vector); Jacobian (a matrix) for a vector-valued map \(F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m\).
Define the derivative \(f'(x)\) as a limit (recall).
\(\boxed{f'(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}}\), the slope of the tangent line.

Pitfall: why can a finite-difference step \(h\) be too SMALL?
Hint: think about what happens when you subtract two nearly equal floating-point numbers.
Catastrophic cancellation: subtracting nearly equal floating-point numbers loses significant digits; default \(h\approx\sqrt{\varepsilon}\,|x|\) balances truncation vs round-off.

When-to-use: which Taylor term drives gradient descent, which drives Newton's method?
Formula: \(f(x+\delta)\approx f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top\delta+\tfrac12\delta^\top H\delta\)
Hint: match the first-order term to one method, the second-order (Hessian) term to the other.
The first-order (gradient) term drives gradient descent; the second-order (Hessian) term drives Newton's method.
The {{c1::gradient}} points in the direction of steepest {{c2::ascent}}, and its norm equals the rate of climb in that direction.

Foundations · Calculus
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus Ch.1
The Laplacian \(\Delta f=\nabla\cdot\nabla f\) measures how much a value differs from the {{c1::average of its neighbours}}.

Foundations · Calculus
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus Ch.1

A central finite difference is {{c1::\(O(h^2)\)}} accurate, while a forward difference is only {{c2::\(O(h)\)}} accurate.
hint: the symmetric scheme cancels one more error term.

Foundations · Calculus
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus Ch.1
Divergence of a field \(F\) is {{c1::\(\nabla\cdot F=\sum_i \partial F_i/\partial x_i\)}}; the Laplacian is the divergence of the {{c2::gradient}}.

Foundations · Calculus
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus Ch.1
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus states \(\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx={{c1::f(b)-f(a)}}\), so differentiation and integration are {{c2::inverses}}.

Foundations · Calculus
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus Ch.1

Derive the equation whose roots are the eigenvalues of a square matrix \(A\).
Formula: start from \(Av=\lambda v\) (\(v\neq 0\)); target \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\)
Hint: move everything to one side and factor out \(v\), then ask when a nonzero \(v\) can exist.

rewrite as \(Av-\lambda v=0\)
factor: \((A-\lambda I)v=0\)
a nonzero \(v\) exists only if \(A-\lambda I\) is singular (not invertible)
\(\boxed{\det(A-\lambda I)=0}\)

Prove eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues of a symmetric \(A=A^T\) are orthogonal (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(Av_1=\lambda_1 v_1\), \(Av_2=\lambda_2 v_2\), with \(A=A^T\)
Hint: compute the scalar \(v_2^TAv_1\) two different ways and set them equal.

way 1: \(v_2^T(Av_1)=\lambda_1(v_2^Tv_1)\)
way 2: \((Av_2)^Tv_1=\lambda_2(v_2^Tv_1)\), using \(A=A^T\) so \(v_2^TA=(Av_2)^T\)
the two expressions are the same scalar, so equate them
\(\boxed{(\lambda_1-\lambda_2)(v_1^Tv_2)=0}\)

Finish the orthogonality proof (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \((\lambda_1-\lambda_2)(v_1^Tv_2)=0\) with \(\lambda_1\neq\lambda_2\)
Hint: divide by the factor you know is nonzero.

distinct eigenvalues give \(\lambda_1-\lambda_2\neq 0\)
divide both sides by that nonzero scalar
\(\boxed{v_1^Tv_2=0}\) so the eigenvectors are orthogonal

From the SVD, derive the eigendecomposition of \(A^TA\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(A=U\Sigma V^T\) with \(U^TU=I\); target \(A^TA=V(\Sigma^T\Sigma)V^T\)
Hint: write out \(A^TA=(U\Sigma V^T)^T(U\Sigma V^T)\), then cancel \(U^TU\).

\(A^TA=(U\Sigma V^T)^T(U\Sigma V^T)\)
\(=V\Sigma^T U^TU\Sigma V^T\)
use \(U^TU=I\) to drop the middle pair
\(\boxed{A^TA=V(\Sigma^T\Sigma)V^T}\)

Relate the singular values \(\sigma_i\) to the eigenvalues of \(A^TA\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(A^TA=V(\Sigma^T\Sigma)V^T\); target \(\sigma_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i(A^TA)}\)
Hint: recognize this as an eigendecomposition and read off the \(i\)-th diagonal entry.

\(V\) holds eigenvectors and \(\Sigma^T\Sigma\) is diagonal with the eigenvalues
the \(i\)-th diagonal entry is \(\sigma_i^2=\lambda_i(A^TA)\)
take the nonnegative square root
\(\boxed{\sigma_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i(A^TA)}}\)

Derive the covariance matrix of a centered point neighborhood.
Formula: centroid \(\bar p=\frac1N\sum_i p_i\); target \(C=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_i (p_i-\bar p)(p_i-\bar p)^T\)
Hint: first center each point, then average the outer products \(\tilde p_i\tilde p_i^T\) using the unbiased divisor \(N-1\).

center each point: \(\tilde p_i=p_i-\bar p\)
each contributes the outer product \(\tilde p_i\tilde p_i^T\)
average with the unbiased divisor \(N-1\)
\(\boxed{C=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_i (p_i-\bar p)(p_i-\bar p)^T}\)

From the covariance eigendecomposition, derive which eigenvector is the surface normal.
Formula: \(C=Q\Lambda Q^T\), \(\lambda_0\le\lambda_1\le\lambda_2\); each \(\lambda\) = variance along its eigenvector axis
Hint: the surface is locally flat, so ask along which axis the spread is smallest.

each \(\lambda\) is the variance (spread) along its eigenvector axis
a locally flat patch spreads least perpendicular to itself
least spread = smallest eigenvalue \(\lambda_0\)
\(\boxed{n=\text{eigenvector of }\lambda_0}\)

Build a right-handed orthonormal local frame from the max-spread axis \(v_2\) and the normal \(n\).
Formula: \(v_1=n\times v_2\); frame \([\,v_2\;\;v_1\;\;n\,]\)
Hint: the third tangent must be orthogonal to both \(n\) and \(v_2\) — take their cross product.

the missing tangent must be orthogonal to both \(n\) and \(v_2\)
cross product gives it: \(v_1=n\times v_2\)
\(v_2\), \(v_1=n\times v_2\), \(n\) are mutually orthogonal and unit
\(\boxed{[\,v_2\;\;n\times v_2\;\;n\,]\ \text{is right-handed orthonormal}}\)

Derive the PCL surface-curvature estimate as a normalized least-spread ratio.
Formula: target \(\sigma_{\text{curv}}=\dfrac{\lambda_0}{\lambda_0+\lambda_1+\lambda_2}\)
Hint: total spread is the sum of the three eigenvalues; put the smallest over the total so a flat patch gives \(0\).

total spread is the sum of variances \(\lambda_0+\lambda_1+\lambda_2\)
flatness is captured by the smallest share \(\lambda_0\)
normalize so a flat patch (\(\lambda_0=0\)) gives \(0\)
\(\boxed{\sigma_{\text{curv}}=\dfrac{\lambda_0}{\lambda_0+\lambda_1+\lambda_2}}\)

Derive the rule to flip a PCA normal toward the sensor viewpoint \(v_p\).
Formula: the normal should satisfy \(n^T(v_p-\bar p)>0\)
Hint: the sign of the dot product tells you if \(n\) points toward or away from the sensor; flip when it is negative.

PCA fixes the normal axis but not its sign
the normal should point toward the sensor: \(n^T(v_p-\bar p)>0\)
a negative dot product means \(n\) points away
\(\boxed{\text{flip }n\to -n\text{ when }n^T(v_p-\bar p)<0}\)

From the rigid-fit objective, derive the cross-covariance \(H\) used to solve for \(R\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(\min_{R,t}\sum_i\lVert Rp_i+t-q_i\rVert^2\); target \(H=\sum_i(p_i-\bar p)(q_i-\bar q)^T\)
Hint: the optimal \(t\) centers both clouds; then expand the squared norm and collect the \(R\)-dependent term as \(\mathrm{tr}(RH)\).

optimal \(t\) centers both clouds, leaving \(\tilde p_i,\tilde q_i\)
the rotation part maximizes \(\sum_i \tilde q_i^T R\tilde p_i=\mathrm{tr}(R\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T)\)
collect the \(3\times3\) matrix inside the trace
\(\boxed{H=\sum_i(p_i-\bar p)(q_i-\bar q)^T}\)

From \(H=U\Sigma V^T\), derive the optimal proper rotation (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\mathrm{tr}(RH)\) is maximized by \(R=VU^T\); correction \(d=\det(VU^T)\)
Hint: the bare maximizer can be a reflection (\(\det=-1\)) — insert \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\) to force \(\det R=+1\).

\(\mathrm{tr}(RH)\) is maximized by \(R=VU^T\)
but this can have \(\det=-1\) (a reflection) on degenerate data
insert \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\) with \(d=\det(VU^T)\) to force \(\det R=+1\)
\(\boxed{R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T}\)

Derive the Kabsch translation \(t\) once \(R\) is known.
Formula: centroids must map exactly: \(R\bar p+t=\bar q\)
Hint: isolate \(t\) by subtracting \(R\bar p\) from both sides.

\(R\) already maps the centered clouds, so the centroids must map onto each other
\(R\bar p+t=\bar q\)
solve for \(t\)
\(\boxed{t=\bar q-R\bar p}\)

Derive why the 2-norm condition number is the ratio of extreme singular values.
Formula: \(A=U\Sigma V^T\) maps the unit sphere to an ellipsoid with axes \(\sigma_i\); target \(\kappa_2(A)=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\)
Hint: the largest stretch is \(\sigma_{\max}\), the smallest is \(\sigma_{\min}\); the ratio bounds relative error growth.

\(A=U\Sigma V^T\) stretches the unit sphere into an ellipsoid with axes \(\sigma_i\)
worst-case amplification is \(\sigma_{\max}\), worst-case shrink is \(\sigma_{\min}\)
their ratio bounds relative error growth
\(\boxed{\kappa_2(A)=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}}\)

Worked example (2D flat patch, m scale). Five points \((0,0),(2,0),(0,2),(2,2),(1,1)\) in the \(z=0\) plane (meters). Find \(C\), its eigenvalues, and the normal.
Formula: \(\bar p=\frac1N\sum p_i\), \(C=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(p_i-\bar p)(p_i-\bar p)^T\), \(N=5\)
Hint: compute the centroid first, then the sums \(\sum\tilde x^2\), \(\sum\tilde y^2\), \(\sum\tilde x\tilde y\) to fill \(C\).

centroid \(\bar p=(1,1,0)\)
centered: \((-1,-1,0),(1,-1,0),(-1,1,0),(1,1,0),(0,0,0)\)
\(\sum\tilde x^2=\sum\tilde y^2=4\), \(\sum\tilde x\tilde y=0\), all \(z=0\)
\(C=\frac14\,\mathrm{diag}(4,4,0)=\mathrm{diag}(1,1,0)\)
eigenvalues \(1,1,0\)
\(\boxed{n=(0,0,1),\ \lambda_0=0}\)

Worked example (mm scale, scale check). The same five-point square is now in millimeters (side \(2\text{ mm}\)). What is \(C\) and the curvature \(\sigma_{\text{curv}}\)?
Formula: \(C=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum\tilde p_i\tilde p_i^T\); \(\sigma_{\text{curv}}=\lambda_0/(\lambda_0+\lambda_1+\lambda_2)\)
Hint: the coordinate numbers are identical, only the unit changes; note \(C\) carries units of length\(^2\) but curvature is a ratio.

same numbers, so \(C=\mathrm{diag}(1,1,0)\ \text{mm}^2\) (entries unchanged, units now mm\(^2\))
eigenvalues \(1,1,0\) in mm\(^2\)
curvature is a ratio, so the mm\(^2\) units cancel
\(\boxed{\sigma_{\text{curv}}=0/(0+1+1)=0\ \text{(unitless, scale-free)}}\)

Worked example (tilted patch, 3D). Covariance eigenvalues are \(\lambda_0=0.01,\ \lambda_1=0.9,\ \lambda_2=1.0\). Compute the curvature and judge flatness.
Formula: \(\sigma_{\text{curv}}=\dfrac{\lambda_0}{\lambda_0+\lambda_1+\lambda_2}\)
Hint: sum the three eigenvalues for the denominator first, then divide.

Worked example (rigid solve, \(90°\) about \(z\)). Source \((0,0,0),(1,0,0)\); target \((0,0,0),(0,1,0)\). Build \(H\) and read off \(R\).
Formula: \(H=\sum_i(p_i-\bar p)(q_i-\bar q)^T\), then \(H=U\Sigma V^T\Rightarrow R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\)
Hint: compute both centroids first, center the points, then form the two outer products and add.

Worked example (condition number, near-singular). Singular values are \((1,\,10^{-6},\,1)\). Compute \(\kappa_2\) and the digits lost.
Formula: \(\kappa_2=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\); digits lost \(\approx\log_{10}\kappa_2\)
Hint: pick out the largest and smallest singular values first, then take the ratio.

Closing test (rigid solve). From the \(90°\) example you found \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\). Verify the source lands on the target.
Formula: \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\), then check \(Rp_2+t=q_2\)
Hint: a pass means \(t=0\) here and \(Rp_2+t\) equals \(q_2=(0,1,0)\) exactly.

Closing test (degenerate neighborhood). A perfectly collinear set of points: find the covariance rank and confirm the normal is undefined.
Formula: trust guard \(\lambda_1\gg\lambda_0\); rank \(=\) number of nonzero eigenvalues of \(C\)
Hint: a pass is the guard FIRING (refusing the normal) because two eigenvalues are zero.

all points lie on one line, so spread exists in one direction only
rank \(C=1\): eigenvalues \(\lambda_2>0,\ \lambda_1=\lambda_0=0\)
two zero eigenvalues = a whole plane of tied "least-spread" eigenvectors
guard check: \(\lambda_1\not\gg\lambda_0\) fires
\(\boxed{\text{normal undefined; reject or enlarge the neighborhood}}\)

Closing test (reflection check). You skipped the \(\det\) correction on mirrored data and got \(\det R=-1\). Verify it is a reflection and fix it.
Formula: proper rotation needs \(\det R=+1\); fix via \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\), \(d=\det(VU^T)\)
Hint: a pass is \(\det R=+1\) after inserting \(d=-1\) on the last diagonal entry.

\(\det R=-1\neq +1\), so \(R\notin SO(3)\): it mirrors geometry
recompute \(d=\det(VU^T)=-1\)
apply \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,-1)\,U^T\)
now \(\det R=\det V\cdot(1\cdot1\cdot d)\cdot\det U^T=+1\)
\(\boxed{\det R=+1\text{ restores a proper rotation}}\)

Recall: when do the SVD and the eigendecomposition give the same factorization?
Hint: think which matrices have \(U=V\).

for a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix the two coincide
\(\boxed{U=V=Q,\ \sigma_i=\lambda_i}\)

Pitfall: when is a PCA-estimated normal untrustworthy?
Formula: trust requires \(\lambda_1\gg\lambda_0\)
Hint: think about collinear or coplanar neighborhoods where two eigenvalues nearly tie.

when the neighborhood is degenerate (collinear/coplanar) and \(\lambda_1\) is not \(\gg\lambda_0\)
the smallest two eigenvalues nearly tie, leaving the least-spread direction ambiguous
\(\boxed{\text{require }\lambda_1\gg\lambda_0\text{ before trusting }n}\)
For a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix the SVD and eigendecomposition coincide: \(U=V=Q\) and \(\sigma_i={{c1::\lambda_i}}\).

Foundations · Eigen / SVD / PCA
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Eigenvectors & eigenvalues · EoLA Ch.14

`Eigen::SelfAdjointEigenSolver` returns eigenvalues in {{c1::ascending}} order, so the normal is column \(0\).
hint: smallest first.

Foundations · Eigen / SVD / PCA
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Eigenvectors & eigenvalues · EoLA Ch.14

Eigenvalue "zero" thresholds must be {{c1::relative}} (\(\varepsilon\,\lambda_{\max}\)), because covariance entries scale with the square of the length unit.
hint: a fixed absolute cutoff breaks when you switch m to mm.

Foundations · Eigen / SVD / PCA
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Eigenvectors & eigenvalues · EoLA Ch.14
Use {{c1::SelfAdjointEigenSolver}} for symmetric covariance matrices and {{c2::JacobiSVD}} for the small \(3\times3\) Kabsch SVD.

Foundations · Eigen / SVD / PCA
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Eigenvectors & eigenvalues · EoLA Ch.14

Derive the 2D rotation matrix \(R(\theta)\) by tracking where the basis vectors land.
Formula: a point at angle \(\theta\) on the unit circle is \((\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\); target \(R(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: first write down the image of \(\hat e_1\), then note \(\hat e_2\) leads it by \(90^\circ\).

A point at angle \(\theta\) on the unit circle is \((\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\), so \(\hat e_1\mapsto(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\)
\(\hat e_2\) leads \(\hat e_1\) by \(90^\circ\), so \(\hat e_2\mapsto(-\sin\theta,\cos\theta)\)
Stack the images as columns: \(\boxed{R(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}}\).

Prove that composing two 2D rotations adds their angles: \(R(\alpha)R(\beta)=R(\alpha+\beta)\).
Formula: \(\cos(\alpha+\beta)=\cos\alpha\cos\beta-\sin\alpha\sin\beta\) and \(\sin(\alpha+\beta)=\sin\alpha\cos\beta+\cos\alpha\sin\beta\)
Hint: multiply the matrices; compute the top-left entry first and match the cosine identity, then the bottom-left and match the sine identity.

Top-left of the product \(=\cos\alpha\cos\beta-\sin\alpha\sin\beta=\cos(\alpha+\beta)\)
Bottom-left \(=\sin\alpha\cos\beta+\cos\alpha\sin\beta=\sin(\alpha+\beta)\)
The remaining entries follow by the same identities, giving the matrix \(R(\alpha+\beta)\)
\(\boxed{R(\alpha)R(\beta)=R(\alpha+\beta)}\), so composing 2D rotations adds angles.

From the \(SO(3)\) orthonormality condition, derive that inverting a rotation is just transposing it.
Formula: \(R^{\mathsf T}R=I\); target \(R^{-1}=R^{\mathsf T}\)
Hint: read \(R^{\mathsf T}R=I\) as "\(R^{\mathsf T}\) is a left inverse" and use that for square matrices a left inverse is the inverse.

\(R^{\mathsf T}R=I\) says \(R^{\mathsf T}\) is a left inverse of \(R\)
For square matrices a left inverse is the inverse
\(\boxed{R^{-1}=R^{\mathsf T}}\), so inverting a rotation is just transposing it.

Prove the skew-matrix identity for a unit axis, \([\hat\omega]^2=\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I\).
Formula: \([\hat\omega]v=\hat\omega\times v\) and BAC-CAB: \(a\times(b\times c)=b(a\cdot c)-c(a\cdot b)\)
Hint: apply \([\hat\omega]^2\) to a test vector \(v\) so it becomes \(\hat\omega\times(\hat\omega\times v)\), then expand with BAC-CAB and use \(\hat\omega\cdot\hat\omega=1\).

\([\hat\omega]^2 v=\hat\omega\times(\hat\omega\times v)\)
BAC-CAB: \(\hat\omega\times(\hat\omega\times v)=\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)-v(\hat\omega\cdot\hat\omega)\)
With \(\hat\omega\cdot\hat\omega=1\) this is \((\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I)v\) for all \(v\)
\(\boxed{[\hat\omega]^2=\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I}\).

Derive Rodrigues' vector form by splitting \(v\) into parallel and perpendicular parts.
Formula: \(v_\parallel=\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)\), \(v_\perp=v-v_\parallel\); target \(v_{\text{rot}}=v\cos\theta+(\hat\omega\times v)\sin\theta+\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)(1-\cos\theta)\)
Hint: the parallel part is unchanged; rotate \(v_\perp\) in its plane using \(\cos\theta\) along itself and \(\sin\theta\) along \(\hat\omega\times v\), then substitute \(v_\perp=v-\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)\).

\(v_\parallel\) is on the axis and is unchanged
\(v_\perp\) rotates in its plane: \(\cos\theta\,v_\perp\) along itself plus \(\sin\theta\,(\hat\omega\times v)\) perpendicular
Add and use \(v_\perp=v-\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)\): \(\boxed{v_{\text{rot}}=v\cos\theta+(\hat\omega\times v)\sin\theta+\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)(1-\cos\theta)}\).

Convert Rodrigues' vector form into the matrix form \(R=I+\sin\theta[\hat\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\hat\omega]^2\).
Formula: \(v_{\text{rot}}=v\cos\theta+(\hat\omega\times v)\sin\theta+\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)(1-\cos\theta)\) with \(\hat\omega\times v=[\hat\omega]v\) and \(\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}=[\hat\omega]^2+I\)
Hint: rewrite each term as (matrix)\(\,v\), then collect the \(I\) terms since \(\cos\theta+(1-\cos\theta)=1\).

Write each term as a matrix times \(v\): \(v\cos\theta=\cos\theta\,I\,v\); \((\hat\omega\times v)=[\hat\omega]v\); \(\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)=\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}v=([\hat\omega]^2+I)v\)
So \(R=\cos\theta\,I+\sin\theta[\hat\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)([\hat\omega]^2+I)\)
Collect the \(I\) terms (\(\cos\theta+1-\cos\theta=1\)): \(\boxed{R=I+\sin\theta[\hat\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\hat\omega]^2}\).

Derive the angle-recovery formula \(\theta=\arccos((\operatorname{tr}R-1)/2)\) from Rodrigues' \(R\).
Formula: \(R=I+\sin\theta[\hat\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\hat\omega]^2\) with \(\operatorname{tr}I=3\), \(\operatorname{tr}[\hat\omega]=0\), \(\operatorname{tr}[\hat\omega]^2=-2\)
Hint: take the trace term by term, simplify to \(1+2\cos\theta\), then solve for \(\theta\).

Derive the skew (axis) part of the matrix log, \([\hat\omega]=\frac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R-R^{\mathsf T})\).
Formula: \(R=I+\sin\theta[\hat\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\hat\omega]^2\) with \([\hat\omega]^{\mathsf T}=-[\hat\omega]\) and \([\hat\omega]^2\) symmetric
Hint: form \(R-R^{\mathsf T}\); the \(I\) and symmetric \([\hat\omega]^2\) terms cancel, leaving only the antisymmetric term.

\(R-R^{\mathsf T}=\sin\theta([\hat\omega]-[\hat\omega]^{\mathsf T})+(1-\cos\theta)([\hat\omega]^2-([\hat\omega]^2)^{\mathsf T})\)
Symmetric parts cancel; \([\hat\omega]^{\mathsf T}=-[\hat\omega]\) gives \(R-R^{\mathsf T}=2\sin\theta[\hat\omega]\)
\(\boxed{[\hat\omega]=\dfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R-R^{\mathsf T})}\).

At \(\theta=\pi\) the skew log divides by zero; derive the diagonal axis-recovery formula \(\hat\omega_i^2=(R_{ii}+1)/2\).
Formula: at \(\theta=\pi\), \(R=I+2[\hat\omega]^2\), and \([\hat\omega]^2=\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I\)
Hint: substitute the second into the first to get \(R=2\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I\), then read off a diagonal entry \(R_{ii}\).

At \(\theta=\pi\): \(\sin\theta=0\), \(1-\cos\theta=2\), so \(R=I+2[\hat\omega]^2\)
Substitute \([\hat\omega]^2=\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I\): \(R=2\hat\omega\hat\omega^{\mathsf T}-I\)
Diagonal entry \(R_{ii}=2\hat\omega_i^2-1\), so \(\boxed{\hat\omega_i^2=(R_{ii}+1)/2}\).

Derive the half-angle in the quaternion: for axis \(\hat z\) and angle \(\theta\), find \(\phi\) in \(q=(\cos\phi,0,0,\sin\phi)\) so \(R(q)=R_z(\theta)\).
Formula: \(R(q)_{11}=1-2z^2\) with \(z=\sin\phi\), double-angle \(1-2\sin^2\phi=\cos2\phi\), and \(R_z(\theta)_{11}=\cos\theta\)
Hint: set the two top-left entries equal so \(\cos2\phi=\cos\theta\), then solve for \(\phi\).

From the half-angle form, derive why \(q\) and \(-q\) represent the same rotation (the double cover).
Formula: \(q=(\cos\tfrac{\theta}{2},\hat\omega\sin\tfrac{\theta}{2})\), and \(\cos\tfrac{\theta+2\pi}{2}=-\cos\tfrac{\theta}{2}\), \(\sin\tfrac{\theta+2\pi}{2}=-\sin\tfrac{\theta}{2}\)
Hint: add \(2\pi\) to \(\theta\) (same physical rotation) and watch what happens to the sign of both components.

Replacing \(\theta\to\theta+2\pi\) leaves the physical rotation identical
But \(\cos\tfrac{\theta+2\pi}{2}=-\cos\tfrac{\theta}{2}\) and \(\sin\tfrac{\theta+2\pi}{2}=-\sin\tfrac{\theta}{2}\)
So the quaternion flips sign while the rotation is unchanged: \(\boxed{q\text{ and }-q\text{ map to the same }R\text{ (double cover)}}\).

A scanned 3 m slab is mis-registered by \(\theta=0.01\) rad about \(\hat z\). By how much does its far corner at \((3,0,0)\) m move sideways?
Formula: \(R_z(\theta)(x,0,0)=(x\cos\theta,\,x\sin\theta,\,0)\)
Hint: the sideways shift is the \(y\)-component \(3\sin\theta\); compute \(\sin 0.01\) first (radians, so \(\approx0.01\)).

\(R_z(0.01)(3,0,0)=(3\cos 0.01,\,3\sin 0.01,\,0)\)
\(3\sin 0.01\approx3(0.0099998)\approx0.0300\) m lateral; \(x\) drops by \(3(1-\cos0.01)\approx1.5\times10^{-4}\) m
\(\boxed{\approx30\text{ mm sideways}}\), showing tiny angle errors ruin a 3 m placement.

Rotate \(v=(1,0,0)\) by \(\theta=120^\circ\) about \(\hat\omega=(1,1,1)/\sqrt3\) using Rodrigues' vector form.
Formula: \(v_{\text{rot}}=v\cos\theta+(\hat\omega\times v)\sin\theta+\hat\omega(\hat\omega\cdot v)(1-\cos\theta)\)
Hint: compute the three scalars first (\(\cos120^\circ=-\tfrac12\), \(\sin120^\circ=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\), \(\hat\omega\cdot v=1/\sqrt3\)) and the vector \(\hat\omega\times v\), then plug in.

Build the unit quaternion for a \(60^\circ\) rotation about \(\hat\omega=(0,0,1)\) and state its scalar part \(w\).
Formula: \(q=(\cos\tfrac{\theta}{2},\,\hat\omega\sin\tfrac{\theta}{2})\)
Hint: use the half-angle \(\theta/2=30^\circ\) first, then read off \(w=\cos30^\circ\) (not \(\cos60^\circ\)).

Closing test: you got \(\theta=\arccos((\operatorname{tr}R-1)/2)\) from an \(R\) with \(\operatorname{tr}R=2\). Find \(\theta\) and confirm it against a \(60^\circ\) rotation's trace.
Formula: forward check \(\operatorname{tr}R=1+2\cos\theta\)
Hint: a pass means the recovered \(\theta\) plugged back into \(1+2\cos\theta\) returns the original trace \(2\).

\(\theta=\arccos((2-1)/2)=\arccos(0.5)=60^\circ\)
Check forward: \(\operatorname{tr}R=1+2\cos60^\circ=1+2(0.5)=2\)
\(\boxed{\theta=60^\circ,\ \text{trace round-trips to }2}\), loop closed.

Closing test: slerp from identity to a \(90^\circ\) \(z\)-rotation; predict the rotation angle at \(t=1/3\).
Formula: \(\cos\Omega=q_0\cdot q_1\), and constant angular speed means the angle scales linearly with \(t\)
Hint: a pass means the angle is exactly \(t\) times the total; the quaternions sit \(\Omega=45^\circ\) apart (half-angle of \(90^\circ\)).

Quaternions \(90^\circ\) apart means \(\Omega=\arccos(q_0\cdot q_1)=45^\circ\) (half-angle)
Constant speed: at \(t=1/3\) the rotation angle is \(\tfrac13(90^\circ)=30^\circ\)
Check: \(\cos(\theta/2)=\cos15^\circ\) matches the slerp scalar part, so \(\boxed{\theta=30^\circ}\).

State the two conditions that define a matrix as a member of \(SO(3)\).
Formula: orthonormality and a determinant condition
Hint: one condition makes the columns orthonormal, the other rules out reflections.
\(\boxed{R^{\mathsf T}R = I\ \text{and}\ \det R = +1}\) (orthonormal columns, no reflection).

Which rotation representation should be a solver's internal state, and why?
Hint: name the one with no gimbal lock that is cheap to compose and renormalize.
\(\boxed{\text{Unit quaternions}}\): no gimbal lock, cheap to compose and renormalize; convert to Euler only at I/O.

When is the matrix-log skew formula \(\frac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R-R^{\mathsf T})\) invalid, and what is the fix at \(\theta=\pi\)?
Formula: diagonal fallback \(\hat\omega_i^2=(R_{ii}+1)/2\)
Hint: spot the two angles where \(\sin\theta=0\); at \(\theta=\pi\) recover the axis from the diagonal instead.

At \(\theta=0\) (axis undefined, pick any) and at \(\theta=\pi\) (\(\sin\theta=0\), divide-by-zero)
\(\boxed{\theta=\pi:\ \text{recover axis from the diagonal },\hat\omega_i^2=(R_{ii}+1)/2}\).

Why must you check the sign of \(q_0\cdot q_1\) before slerp, and what do you do if it is negative?
Formula: the 4D dot \(q_0\cdot q_1=\cos\Omega\); double cover means \(q_1\equiv-q_1\)
Hint: a negative dot means \(\Omega>90^\circ\), i.e. the long arc; negating one quaternion fixes it.

The double cover means \(q_1\) and \(-q_1\) are the same orientation
If \(q_0\cdot q_1<0\) the formula traces the long way around
\(\boxed{\text{Negate one quaternion when }q_0\cdot q_1<0\text{ to take the short arc}}\).

How do you re-orthonormalize a drifted rotation matrix via SVD, and how do you build/slerp rotations in Eigen?
Formula: SVD \(R=U\Sigma V^{\mathsf T}\); nearest rotation is \(R\leftarrow UV^{\mathsf T}\)
Hint: drop the singular-value matrix \(\Sigma\) and keep \(UV^{\mathsf T}\); in Eigen build with `AngleAxisd(angle, axis)` and interpolate with `q1.slerp(t, q2)`.

SVD: compute \(R=U\Sigma V^{\mathsf T}\) and drop \(\Sigma\), giving \(\boxed{R\leftarrow UV^{\mathsf T}}\) (nearest \(SO(3)\))
Eigen: build with `Eigen::AngleAxisd(angle, axis)` (axis normalized), interpolate with `q1.slerp(t, q2)`.
Rotation composition is {{c1::non-commutative}}, so \(R_1 R_2 \neq R_2 R_1\) in general.
