Optimizing a full \(3\times3\) rotation matrix directly is wrong because it has nine entries but only {{c1::three}} degrees of freedom; parameterize with an {{c2::axis-angle / Lie-algebra}} increment instead.

Optimization · Calculus & Jacobians
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)

Derive the first-order optimality (stationarity) condition for \(\min_x f(x)\), starting from the first-order Taylor expansion.
Formula: start from \(f(x^\star+\delta)\approx f(x^\star)+ abla f(x^\star)^ op\delta\); target \( abla f(x^\star)=0\).
Hint: at a minimum no direction \(\delta\) may lower \(f\), so the linear term \( abla f(x^\star)^ op\delta\) must vanish for every \(\delta\).

Near \(x^\star\), \(f(x^\star+\delta)\approx f(x^\star)+ abla f(x^\star)^ op\delta\)
For \(x^\star\) to be a min, no direction \(\delta\) may lower \(f\), so the linear term must vanish for all \(\delta\)
\( abla f(x^\star)^ op\delta=0\ orall\delta\) forces the vector itself to be zero
\( oxed{ abla f(x^\star)=0}\)

Derive the gradient-descent direction (step 1 of 2): which direction \(d\) gives the steepest local decrease of \(f\) at \(x_k\)?
Formula: \(f(x_k+d)\approx f(x_k)+ abla f(x_k)^ op d\); target \(d=- abla f(x_k)\).
Hint: minimize \( abla f(x_k)^ op d\) over unit-length \(d\); by Cauchy-Schwarz it is most negative when \(d\) points opposite the gradient.

\(f(x_k+d)\approx f(x_k)+ abla f(x_k)^ op d\) for small \(d\)
minimize the change \( abla f(x_k)^ op d\) over unit \(d\); by Cauchy-Schwarz it is most negative when \(d\parallel- abla f(x_k)\)
\( oxed{d=- abla f(x_k)}\) (steepest-descent direction)

Continue from the steepest-descent direction to the gradient-descent update (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(x_{k+1}=x_k+\alpha d\) with \(d=- abla f(x_k)\); target \(x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha abla f(x_k)\).
Hint: just substitute \(d=- abla f(x_k)\) into the step \(x_{k+1}=x_k+\alpha d\).

take a step of size \(\alpha>0\) along \(d\): \(x_{k+1}=x_k+\alpha d\)
substitute \(d=-\nabla f(x_k)\)
\(\boxed{x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha\,\nabla f(x_k)}\)

Derive Newton's update from the second-order Taylor model of \(f\) at \(x_k\) (step 1 of 2): write the model and set its gradient to zero.
Formula: \(m(p)=f(x_k)+ abla f(x_k)^ op p+ frac12 p^ op H(x_k)p\); target \(H(x_k)p=- abla f(x_k)\).
Hint: take \( abla_p m(p)= abla f(x_k)+H(x_k)p\) and set it to \(0\).

\(m(p)=f(x_k)+ abla f(x_k)^ op p+ frac12 p^ op H(x_k)\,p\)
\( abla_p m(p)= abla f(x_k)+H(x_k)\,p\)
set \(=0\): \( oxed{H(x_k)\,p=- abla f(x_k)}\) (the Newton system)

From the Newton system finish the Newton update (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(H(x_k)p=-\nabla f(x_k)\) and \(x_{k+1}=x_k+p\); target \(x_{k+1}=x_k-H(x_k)^{-1}\nabla f(x_k)\).
Hint: solve the system for \(p\) by left-multiplying by \(H^{-1}\), then add \(p\) to \(x_k\).

solve for the step \(p=-H(x_k)^{-1}\nabla f(x_k)\)
apply it: \(x_{k+1}=x_k+p\)
\(\boxed{x_{k+1}=x_k-H(x_k)^{-1}\nabla f(x_k)}\)

Derive the Lagrange tangency condition for \(\min f(x)\) s.t. \(g(x)=0\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: feasible steps satisfy \( abla g^ op\delta=0\) and at the optimum \( abla f^ op\delta=0\); target \( abla f\parallel abla g\).
Hint: a feasible move stays on \(g=0\) (so it is tangent, \( abla g\perp\delta\)); at the optimum no feasible move lowers \(f\), so \( abla f\perp\delta\) too — both are orthogonal to the same tangent.

A feasible step \(\delta\) must keep \(g=0\), so \( abla g^ op\delta=0\) (tangent to the curve)
at the optimum no feasible step lowers \(f\), so \( abla f^ op\delta=0\) for every such \(\delta\)
both \( abla f\) and \( abla g\) are orthogonal to the same tangent direction
\( oxed{ abla f \parallel abla g}\) (collinear gradients at \(x^\star\))

From the collinearity \( abla f\parallel abla g\) derive the Lagrangian stationarity condition (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\mathcal{L}(x,\lambda)=f(x)+\lambda^ op g(x)\); target \( abla_x\mathcal{L}=0\).
Hint: collinear means \( abla f=-\lambda abla g\) for some scalar \(\lambda\); move everything to one side.

collinear means \( abla f=-\lambda\, abla g\) for some scalar \(\lambda\)
rearrange: \( abla f+\lambda\, abla g=0\)
this is exactly \( abla_x\mathcal{L}=0\) for \(\mathcal{L}(x,\lambda)=f(x)+\lambda^ op g(x)\)
\( oxed{ abla_x ig(f+\lambda^ op g ig)=0}\)

Solve \(\min x^2+y^2\) s.t. \(x+y=1\) in closed form via the Lagrangian.
Formula: \(\mathcal{L}=x^2+y^2+\lambda(x+y-1)\); stationarity \(\partial_x\mathcal{L}=\partial_y\mathcal{L}=0\) plus feasibility \(x+y=1\).
Hint: set \(\partial_x\mathcal{L}=2x+\lambda\) and \(\partial_y\mathcal{L}=2y+\lambda\) to zero first; they force \(x=y\), then use \(x+y=1\).

Show why one Newton step solves a quadratic \(f(x)= frac12 x^ op A x - b^ op x\) exactly, and state the step.
Formula: \( abla f=Ax-b\), \(H=A\), Newton step \(x_1=x_0-H^{-1} abla f(x_0)\); target \(x_1=A^{-1}b\).
Hint: substitute \( abla f(x_0)=Ax_0-b\) and \(H=A\) into the Newton step; the \(x_0\) terms cancel.

\(\nabla f=Ax-b\), \(H=A\) (constant), so the Taylor model equals \(f\)
Newton step from any \(x_0\): \(x_1=x_0-A^{-1}(Ax_0-b)\)
\(=x_0-x_0+A^{-1}b\)
\(\boxed{x_1=A^{-1}b=x^\star}\) (exact in one step)

Worked example (1D, small scale): minimize \(f(x)=(x-3)^2\) by gradient descent from \(x_0=0\) with \(\alpha=0.1\); compute \(x_1\) and \(x_2\).
Formula: \(f'(x)=2(x-3)\) and \(x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha f'(x_k)\).
Hint: compute \(f'(0)\) first, take one step to get \(x_1\), then plug \(x_1\) back into \(f'\) for \(x_2\).

Worked example (same problem, curvature): minimize \(f(x)=(x-3)^2\) with one Newton step from \(x_0=0\).
Formula: \(f'(x)=2(x-3)\), \(f''=2\), and \(x_1=x_0-f'(x_0)/f''\).
Hint: evaluate \(f'(0)\) and divide by \(f''=2\); for a quadratic this lands exactly on the minimum.

Worked example (large step, divergence regime): for \(f(x)=(x-3)^2\) a fixed step \(\alpha\) gives the error recursion below. For which \(\alpha\) does it diverge, and check \(\alpha=1.1\).
Formula: \(x_{k+1}-3=(1-2\alpha)(x_k-3)\); it converges iff \(|1-2\alpha|<1\).
Hint: solve \(|1-2\alpha|<1\) for the convergent range, then evaluate the factor \(1-2\alpha\) at \(\alpha=1.1\) and compare its magnitude to \(1\).

Worked example (2D, robotics scale, units in metres): minimize \(f(x,y)=x^2+4y^2\) from \((1,1)\) with \(\alpha=0.1\); compute one GD step.
Formula: \(\nabla f=(2x,\,8y)\) and \((x,y)\leftarrow(x,y)-\alpha\nabla f\).
Hint: evaluate the gradient \((2x,8y)\) at \((1,1)\) first, then subtract \(\alpha\) times each component.

Test yourself + verify (limiting case): plug the candidate \(( frac12, frac12)\) back into \(\min x^2+y^2\) s.t. \(x+y=1\) and confirm it is optimal.
Formula: check feasibility \(x+y=1\) and collinearity \( abla f=-\lambda abla g\) with \( abla f=(2x,2y)\), \( abla g=(1,1)\); cross-check with point-to-line distance \( frac{|x+y-1|}{\sqrt2}\) evaluated at the origin.
Hint: a pass means feasibility holds AND the two gradients are parallel; the distance \( frac1{\sqrt2}\) should match the known closest-point distance.

feasibility: \(\tfrac12+\tfrac12=1\) holds
cost \(=\tfrac14+\tfrac14=\tfrac12\); the multiplier \(\lambda=-2x=-1\) so \(\nabla f=(1,1)=-\lambda\nabla g=(1,1)\), gradients collinear
distance to origin \(=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\) equals the known point-to-line distance \(\tfrac{|0+0-1|}{\sqrt2}\)
\(\boxed{(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)\ \text{is the global optimum}}\)

Test yourself + verify (units / limit check): for \(x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha abla f\) with \(x\) in m and \(f\) in m\(^2\), check the units of \(\alpha abla f\) and the \(\alpha o0\) limit.
Formula: \([ abla f]=[f]/[x]= ext{m}^2/ ext{m}= ext{m}\), and \(\alpha abla f\) must have units of m.
Hint: a pass means \(\alpha\) comes out dimensionless and \(\alpha o0\) leaves \(x_{k+1}=x_k\) (no move).

\(\nabla f\) has units m\(^2\)/m \(=\) m, so \(\alpha\) must be dimensionless for \(\alpha\nabla f\) to be in m
as \(\alpha\to0\), \(x_{k+1}\to x_k\) (no move, no divergence)
\(\boxed{[\alpha]=1\ \text{(dimensionless)},\ \alpha\to0\Rightarrow\text{stationary}}\) — confirms the update is consistent

Define a convex function two equivalent ways (segment form and Hessian form).
Formula: \(f(\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b)\le\lambda f(a)+(1-\lambda)f(b)\); equivalently \(H=\nabla^2 f\succeq 0\) everywhere.
Hint: the first says the chord lies above the graph; the second says curvature is non-negative.

function form: \(f(\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b)\le\lambda f(a)+(1-\lambda)f(b)\)
curvature form: \(\boxed{H=\nabla^2 f\succeq0\ \text{everywhere}}\)

Name the four KKT conditions for \(\min f(x)\) s.t. \(g(x)=0,\ h(x)\le0\).
Formula: stationarity \( abla f+\lambda^ op abla g+\mu^ op abla h=0\); primal feasibility; dual feasibility \(\mu\ge0\); complementary slackness \(\mu_i h_i(x)=0\).
Hint: two are feasibility conditions, one signs the inequality multipliers, one ties each \(\mu_i\) to its constraint being active.

Stationarity \(
abla f+\lambda^	op
abla g+\mu^	op
abla h=0\); primal feasibility; dual feasibility \(\mu\ge0\); complementary slackness \(\mu_i h_i=0\).

When-to-use: pick the solver by problem structure.
Formula: match structure \(\to\) method (convex+linear, smooth non-convex, least-squares residuals, discrete/combinatorial).
Hint: closed form when the optimum is unique and linear; gradient/Newton when smooth; Gauss-Newton/LM when residuals are squared; search+refine when discrete.

convex+linear \(\to\) closed form; smooth non-convex \(\to\) gradient/Newton from a good start; least-squares residuals \(\to\) Gauss-Newton/LM; discrete/combinatorial \(\to\) search + continuous refine.

Pitfall: why do ICP and IK fail from a bad initial guess, and what is the fix?
Formula: gradient methods satisfy only the local condition \(\nabla f(x^\star)=0\).
Hint: think about what "local minimum" means on a non-convex landscape.
Their objectives are non-convex; gradient methods only find a local minimum near the start, so warm-start them.

Pitfall: name the symptom and fix for an ill-conditioned (zig-zagging) descent.
Formula: conditioning is governed by the Hessian condition number \(\kappa(H)\); large \(\kappa\) means a long narrow valley.
Hint: the cure either averages past steps or rescales by curvature.
A long narrow valley (large Hessian condition number); use momentum, preconditioning, or Newton's method.

Pitfall: what is the right stopping criterion for an iterative optimizer?
Formula: stop when \(\|\nabla f(x_k)\|<\text{tol}\) and/or \(\|x_{k+1}-x_k\|<\text{tol}\).
Hint: tie the test to the optimality condition \(\nabla f=0\), not to a fixed iteration budget.
Small gradient norm and/or small step size, not iteration count alone.

Code idiom: which Eigen solver applies the Newton step \(p=-H^{-1}g\) for an SPD Hessian?
Formula: solve \(Hp=-g\) via an \(LDL^\top\) factorization.
Hint: it is the Cholesky-style decomposition Eigen exposes for symmetric positive-definite matrices.
For a {{c1::convex}} function every local minimum is also a {{c2::global}} minimum, so gradient descent reaches the true optimum.

Optimization · Optimization Foundations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
At a constrained optimum the cost gradient is {{c1::parallel}} to the constraint gradient: \(\nabla f=-\lambda^\top\nabla g\).

Optimization · Optimization Foundations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

Gauss-Newton approximates the Hessian as {{c1::\(J^\top J\)}}, and Levenberg-Marquardt {{c2::damps}} it.
hint: the approximation drops the second-derivative term of the residuals.

Optimization · Optimization Foundations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

Derive the gradient \(\nabla F\) of the cost (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: start from \(F(x)=\tfrac12 r(x)^\top r(x)\), target \(\nabla F = J^\top r\) with \(J=\partial r/\partial x\).
Hint: use the chain rule on the quadratic in \(r\); the \(\tfrac12\) cancels the factor 2.

\(\nabla F = \big(\tfrac{\partial r}{\partial x}\big)^\top r\)
name the Jacobian \(J=\partial r/\partial x\)
\(\boxed{\nabla F = J^\top r \equiv g}\)

From the gradient, derive the exact Hessian \( abla^2 F\) (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: start from \(g_j=\sum_i J_{ij} r_i\), target \( abla^2 F = J^ op J + \sum_i r_i\, abla^2 r_i\).
Hint: differentiate \(g_j\) w.r.t. \(x_k\) with the product rule; one factor gives \(J_{ik}\), the other gives \(\partial^2 r_i\).

\(\partial g_j/\partial x_k=\sum_i J_{ij}J_{ik} + \sum_i r_i\,\partial^2 r_i/\partial x_j\partial x_k\)
the first sum is \((J^\top J)_{jk}\)
\(\boxed{\nabla^2 F = J^\top J + \sum_i r_i\,\nabla^2 r_i}\)

From the exact Hessian, derive the Gauss-Newton approximation and say why it is cheap.
Formula: exact \( abla^2 F = J^ op J + \sum_i r_i\, abla^2 r_i\), target \( abla^2 F \approx J^ op J\).
Hint: decide which term needs second derivatives and drop it; note when it is small.

Drop the second-derivative term \(\sum_i r_i\,\nabla^2 r_i\) (small when residuals are small or the model is nearly linear)
no per-residual second derivatives are needed, and what remains is symmetric PSD
\(\boxed{\nabla^2 F \approx J^\top J}\)

Derive the Gauss-Newton normal equations from the linearized residual (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: tangent model \(r(x_k+\Delta x)\approx r+J\,\Delta x\); minimize \(F=\tfrac12\|r+J\Delta x\|^2\); target \((J^\top J)\Delta x=-J^\top r\).
Hint: expand the squared norm in \(\Delta x\), then set \(\nabla_{\Delta x}F=0\).

\(F=\tfrac12(r^\top r + 2\,\Delta x^\top J^\top r + \Delta x^\top J^\top J\,\Delta x)\)
\(\nabla_{\Delta x}F = J^\top r + J^\top J\,\Delta x = 0\)
\(\boxed{(J^\top J)\,\Delta x = -J^\top r}\)

From the normal equations, derive the Gauss-Newton update \(x_{k+1}\) (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \((J^\top J)\Delta x=-J^\top r\), target \(x_{k+1}=x_k-(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r\).
Hint: solve for \(\Delta x\), then add it to \(x_k\).

\(\Delta x = -(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r\)
\(x_{k+1}=x_k+\Delta x\)
\(\boxed{x_{k+1}=x_k-(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r}\)

Construct the Levenberg-damped form that is always invertible.
Formula: from \((J^\top J)\Delta x=-J^\top r\), target \((J^\top J + \lambda I)\,\Delta x = -J^\top r\).
Hint: \(J^\top J\) can be singular; add a positive multiple of \(I\) to make it positive definite.

\(J^\top J\) may be singular/rank-deficient
add \(\lambda I\) (\(\lambda>0\)) so the matrix is symmetric positive definite
\(\boxed{(J^\top J + \lambda I)\,\Delta x = -J^\top r}\)

From the Levenberg form, write the Marquardt scale-invariant variant and say what changes.
Formula: from \((J^\top J+\lambda I)\Delta x=-J^\top r\), target \((J^\top J + \lambda\,\operatorname{diag}(J^\top J))\Delta x = -J^\top r\).
Hint: replace the unit \(I\) by something carrying each parameter's own curvature scale.

Replace \(I\) by \(\operatorname{diag}(J^\top J)\) so each parameter is damped in its own units (unit-invariant)
\(\boxed{(J^\top J + \lambda\,\operatorname{diag}(J^\top J))\,\Delta x = -J^\top r}\)

Take the limit \(\lambda\to\infty\) of the Levenberg system and identify the method.
Formula: \((J^\top J+\lambda I)\Delta x=-g\) with \(g=J^\top r\); target \(\Delta x\to-\tfrac1\lambda J^\top r\).
Hint: for huge \(\lambda\), drop \(J^\top J\) next to \(\lambda I\), then solve.

\(\lambda I\,\Delta x \approx -g\)
\(\Delta x \approx -\tfrac1\lambda g = -\tfrac1\lambda J^\top r\)
\(\boxed{\Delta x \to -\tfrac1\lambda\,J^\top r}\), a short steepest-descent step

Construct the LM gain ratio \(\rho\) used to accept/reject a trial step.
Formula: \(\rho=\dfrac{F(x_k)-F(x_k+\Delta x)}{\tfrac12\Delta x^\top(\lambda\Delta x - g)}\), with \(g=J^\top r\).
Hint: numerator = actual cost drop; denominator = the drop the linear model predicted (always positive).

actual drop \(=F(x_k)-F(x_k+\Delta x)\)
predicted drop \(=\tfrac12\Delta x^\top(\lambda\Delta x - g)>0\)
\(\boxed{\rho=\dfrac{F(x_k)-F(x_k+\Delta x)}{\tfrac12\Delta x^\top(\lambda\Delta x - g)}}\)

Construct the damped-least-squares IK step (the LM idea applied to inverse kinematics).
Formula: kinematic error \(e\), Jacobian \(J\); target \(\Delta\theta = J^\top(JJ^\top + \lambda^2 I)^{-1}e\).
Hint: start from \(\Delta\theta=J^\dagger e\), then damp the Gram matrix \(JJ^\top\) by \(\lambda^2 I\) before inverting.

bare \(\Delta\theta=J^\dagger e\) blows up near singularities
damp the right-inverse Gram matrix by \(\lambda^2 I\)
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = J^\top(JJ^\top + \lambda^2 I)^{-1}e}\)

Worked example, GN step (small/near regime). Fit \(y=e^{at}\) to \((0,2),(1,3)\) at \(a_0=1\).
Formula: \(\Delta a = -(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r\), given \(J^\top J=7.3891\), \(J^\top r=-0.7658\); then \(a_1=a_0+\Delta a\).
Hint: compute \(\Delta a\) first (watch the double negative), then add to \(a_0=1\).

Worked example, LM step (mild damping). Same \(e^{at}\) setup.
Formula: \((J^\top J+\lambda)\Delta a = -J^\top r\) with \(J^\top J=7.3891\), \(-J^\top r=0.7658\), \(\lambda=1\).
Hint: add \(\lambda\) to the \(7.3891\) in the denominator, then divide \(0.7658\) by it.

\((7.3891+1)\Delta a = 0.7658\)
\(\Delta a = 0.7658/8.3891\)
\(\boxed{\Delta a = 0.0913}\), shorter than the GN \(0.1036\), same sign

Worked example, LM step (heavy damping / steepest-descent regime). Same \(e^{at}\) setup.
Formula: \((J^\top J+\lambda)\Delta a = -J^\top r\) with \(J^\top J=7.3891\), \(-J^\top r=0.7658\), \(\lambda=100\).
Hint: \(\lambda\) now dwarfs \(7.3891\), so the denominator is \(\approx 107.39\); name the regime after dividing.

\((7.3891+100)\Delta a = 0.7658\)
\(\Delta a = 0.7658/107.39\)
\(\boxed{\Delta a = 0.00713}\), almost pure gradient descent

Worked example, large-residual 2-point linear fit (scalar parameter). Residuals \(r=(2,-4)\), constant Jacobian \(J=(1,1)^\top\).
Formula: \(\Delta x = -(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r\).
Hint: compute the scalars \(J^\top J\) and \(J^\top r\) first by summing the two components, then divide.

\(J^\top J = 1^2+1^2 = 2\), \(J^\top r = 2+(-4)=-2\)
\(\Delta x = -(-2)/2\)
\(\boxed{\Delta x = +1}\) (exact in one step: residuals are affine)

Test yourself (close the loop). For the \(e^{at}\) example, \(a_1=1.1036\) gives \(e^{1.1036}=3.0150\).
Formula: \(F(a)=\tfrac12\sum_i r_i^2\) with \(r=(e^{a\cdot0}-2,\,e^{a}-3)\); compare to \(F(a_0)=0.5397\).
Hint: a pass is \(F(a_1)

Test yourself (verify the minimizer). Confirm the linear-fit GN step \(\Delta x=+1\) from \(r=(2,-4)\), \(J=(1,1)^\top\) actually minimizes \(F\).
Formula: updated residual \(r_{new}=r+J\Delta x\); minimum iff gradient \(J^\top r_{new}=0\).
Hint: a pass is a zero gradient; form \(r_{new}\) first, then dot with \(J\).

\(r_{new}=(2+1,\,-4+1)=(3,-3)\)
\(J^\top r_{new}=3+(-3)=0\)
\(\boxed{\nabla F=0\Rightarrow \text{exact minimizer in one step}}\)

Test yourself (verify damping shrinks the step). For \(-J^\top r=0.7658\), \(J^\top J=7.3891\), compare the \(\lambda=1\) and \(\lambda=100\) LM steps.
Formula: \(|\Delta a|=0.7658/(7.3891+\lambda)\) for each \(\lambda\).
Hint: a pass is ratio \(<1\); compute both magnitudes, then divide the larger-\(\lambda\) one by the smaller-\(\lambda\) one.

\(|\Delta a|_{\lambda=1}=0.7658/8.3891=0.0913\); \(|\Delta a|_{\lambda=100}=0.7658/107.39=0.00713\)
ratio \(0.00713/0.0913\approx 0.078<1\)
\(\boxed{\text{larger }\lambda\Rightarrow\text{shorter step, same direction}}\)

When is the Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation \( abla^2 F\approx J^ op J\) exact?
Formula: exact Hessian \( abla^2 F = J^ op J + \sum_i r_i\, abla^2 r_i\).
Hint: ask when the dropped sum is exactly zero.

When the residuals are affine (linear) in the parameters, so every \(\nabla^2 r_i=0\) and the dropped term vanishes
\(\boxed{r \text{ affine} \Rightarrow \nabla^2 F = J^\top J}\)

Definition: what does nonlinear least squares minimize, and what is a residual?
Formula: \(F(x)=\tfrac12\sum_i r_i(x)^2\) with \(r_i=\text{model}-\text{measurement}\).
Hint: name what the cost sums and what a single residual measures.

It minimizes \(F(x)=\tfrac12\sum_i r_i(x)^2\)
each residual \(r_i=\text{model}-\text{measurement}\) is an error number you want near zero
\(\boxed{\min_x \tfrac12\|r(x)\|^2}\)

When to use LM over plain Gauss-Newton?
Hint: think about the three cases where the undamped step \(-(J^\top J)^{-1}J^\top r\) overshoots or fails.

When started far from the solution, with large residuals/outliers, or a rank-deficient \(J^\top J\)
there the undamped GN step overshoots or diverges, and \(\lambda\) leashes it
\(\boxed{\text{far start / big residuals / singular } J^\top J}\)

Pitfall: why solve \(\min_{\Delta x}\|J\Delta x+r\|\) via QR/SVD of \(J\) rather than forming \(J^\top J\)?
Formula: \(\operatorname{cond}(J^\top J)=\operatorname{cond}(J)^2\).
Hint: compare conditioning of working with \(J\) directly vs its product with itself.

Forming \(J^\top J\) squares the condition number
on ill-conditioned problems that doubles the lost digits, so factor \(J\) directly via QR/SVD
\(\boxed{\operatorname{cond}(J^\top J)=\operatorname{cond}(J)^2}\)

Pitfall: how must a rigid rotation be parameterized inside NLLS, and why?
Hint: count the free parameters of a rotation vs entries of a \(3\times3\) matrix; the gap creates null directions in \(J\).

Use a 3-parameter \(\mathfrak{so}(3)\) tangent increment composed onto the current rotation
a full 9-entry matrix or unnormalized quaternion is over-parameterized and makes \(J^\top J\) singular
\(\boxed{\text{optimize on the }\mathfrak{so}(3)\text{ tangent, } J \text{ is } m\times6}\)

In Eigen, how do you solve the SPD system \((J^\top J+\lambda I)\Delta x=-g\) without inverting?
Formula: the system is symmetric positive definite, so use an \(LDL^\top\) (Cholesky-type) solve.
Hint: form \(H=J^\top J+\lambda I\), then call the factorization that takes \(-g\) as the right-hand side.

build \(H = J^\top J + \lambda I\)
solve via the SPD factorization, no explicit inverse
\(\boxed{\texttt{(JtJ + lambda*I).ldlt().solve(-g)}}\)
As \(\lambda \to 0\) Levenberg-Marquardt reduces to {{c1::Gauss-Newton}}, and as \(\lambda \to \infty\) it reduces to {{c1::gradient descent}}.

Optimization · Nonlinear Least Squares
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
The LM gain ratio is {{c1::actual cost reduction divided by predicted reduction}}; a value above zero means {{c2::accept the step and decrease \(\lambda\)}}.

Optimization · Nonlinear Least Squares
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

When a trial step is rejected (\(\rho\le 0\)) LM keeps \(x_k\) and {{c1::increases \(\lambda\)}}, e.g. \(\lambda\gets\lambda\nu\) with \(\nu\) doubling.
hint: a bad step means distrust the linear model, so damp harder.

Optimization · Nonlinear Least Squares
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
Large LM damping both shrinks the step length and bends its direction toward {{c1::the negative gradient}}, the trust-region connection.

Optimization · Nonlinear Least Squares
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

The single most useful debugging check for a new residual is comparing `J.transpose()*r` against a {{c1::finite-difference gradient}} of the cost.
hint: \(\tilde g_j=(F(x+he_j)-F(x-he_j))/(2h)\) with \(h\approx10^{-6}\).

Optimization · Nonlinear Least Squares
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

Derive the RANSAC iteration count \(N\) (step 1 of 3): the probability one minimal sample of \(s\) points is all-inlier, given inlier ratio \(w\).
Formula: \(P(\text{one draw is inlier})=w\), and independent events multiply.
Hint: the \(s\) draws are independent, so multiply \(w\) by itself \(s\) times.

Sampling is independent (with replacement, or \(n\gg s\)), so each draw is an inlier with probability \(w\)
the \(s\) draws are independent, so probabilities multiply: \(w\cdot w\cdots w\) (\(s\) times)
\(\boxed{P(\text{sample all-inlier}) = w^s}\)

Derive the RANSAC iteration count \(N\) (step 2 of 3): the probability that across \(N\) independent iterations you NEVER draw a clean sample.
Formula: start from \(P(\text{clean})=w^s\); complement and independence give \((1-w^s)^N\).
Hint: first write the chance ONE iteration is not clean, then raise it to the \(N\)th power.

One iteration fails to be clean with probability \(1-w^s\)
the \(N\) iterations are independent, so failures multiply
\(\boxed{P(\text{no clean sample in }N) = (1-w^s)^N}\)

Derive the RANSAC iteration count \(N\) (step 3 of 3): solve for \(N\) given target success probability \(p\).
Formula: start from \(1-(1-w^s)^N = p\); target \(N = \dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}\).
Hint: isolate \((1-w^s)^N\), then take \(\log\) of both sides to pull \(N\) down.

Success means at least one clean sample: \(1-(1-w^s)^N = p\)
rearrange: \((1-w^s)^N = 1-p\)
take logs: \(N\log(1-w^s) = \log(1-p)\)
\(\boxed{N = \dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}}\)

From the OLS cost, derive the influence function \(\psi\) and state the breakdown point.
Formula: \(\rho_{\text{LS}}(r)=\tfrac12 r^2\), influence \(\psi(r)=\rho'(r)\).
Hint: differentiate \(\tfrac12 r^2\) with respect to \(r\), then ask if the result is bounded as \(r\to\infty\).

Influence is the derivative \(\psi(r)=\rho'(r)\)
\(\psi(r)=\frac{d}{dr}\big(\tfrac12 r^2\big)=r\)
\(\psi\) is unbounded, so as \(r\to\infty\) the pull on \(\theta\) grows without limit
\(\boxed{\psi_{\text{LS}}(r)=r,\ \text{breakdown point}=0\%}\)

Derive the Huber tail influence (step 1 of 2): find \(\psi_k(u)\) for \(|u|>k\).
Formula: Huber tail loss \(\rho_k(u)=k|u|-\tfrac12 k^2\), influence \(\psi_k=\rho_k'\).
Hint: differentiate term by term and use \(\frac{d}{du}|u|=\operatorname{sgn}(u)\).

Derive the Huber IRLS weight in the tail (step 2 of 2): find \(w_k(u)\) for \(|u|>k\).
Formula: \(\psi_k(u)=k\,\operatorname{sgn}(u)\), weight \(w(u)=\psi(u)/u\).
Hint: divide by \(u\) and simplify \(\operatorname{sgn}(u)/u=1/|u|\).

From the Tukey loss, derive the IRLS weight for \(|u|\le c\).
Formula: \(\rho_c(u)=\tfrac{c^2}{6}\big[1-(1-(u/c)^2)^3\big]\), then \(\psi_c=\rho_c'\) and \(w_c=\psi_c/u\); target \(w_c=(1-(u/c)^2)^2\).
Hint: differentiate the cube via the chain rule (outer \(3(\cdot)^2\), inner \(-2u/c^2\)), then divide by \(u\).

Show the MSAC score caps an outlier's contribution at \(t^2\).
Formula: \(C=\sum_i\min(r_i^2,t^2)\).
Hint: split into the inlier case \(|r_i|\le t\) and outlier case \(|r_i|>t\), and ask which argument the \(\min\) selects.

Top-hat RANSAC counts inliers but gives outliers influence \(0\) and inliers influence \(1\), ignoring fit quality
truncate the squared cost at the threshold: contribution \(=\min(r_i^2,t^2)\)
if \(|r_i|\le t\) it pays \(r_i^2\); if \(|r_i|>t\) then \(r_i^2>t^2\) so the min selects \(t^2\)
\(\boxed{\text{outlier contributes the constant }t^2}\)

Derive the inlier threshold \(t\) for a scalar (point-to-plane) residual with Gaussian noise \(\sigma\) at 95%.
Formula: \((r/\sigma)^2\sim\chi^2_1\); the \(95\%\) one-DOF cutoff is \(1.96^2\).
Hint: set \((r/\sigma)^2\le 1.96^2\) and take the square root to bound \(|r|\).

\((r/\sigma)^2\sim\chi^2\) with \(1\) DOF (residual codimension \(1\))
95% one-DOF cutoff: \((r/\sigma)^2\le 1.96^2\)
so \(|r|\le 1.96\,\sigma\)
\(\boxed{t=1.96\,\sigma\approx 2\sigma}\)

Worked example (2D, small). Fit \(y=ax+b\) by RANSAC to \(P_1(0,0),P_2(1,1),P_3(2,2),P_4(3,3),P_5(2,8)\) with \(t=0.5\); sample \(\{P_2,P_4\}\) and report the model and consensus count.
Formula: slope \(a=\dfrac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\), residual \(|y_i-(ax_i+b)|\), inlier if \(\le t\).
Hint: compute the slope from the two sampled points first, then score all five residuals.

Line through \((1,1),(3,3)\) has slope \(1\), intercept \(0\), so \(y=x\)
residuals \(|y_i-x_i|\): \(P_1{=}0,P_2{=}0,P_3{=}0,P_4{=}0,P_5{=}|8-2|=6\)
inliers (\(\le0.5\)): \(P_1,P_2,P_3,P_4\)
\(\boxed{\text{consensus}=4,\ \text{model }y=x}\)

Worked example (the outlier sample). Same 5 points and \(t=0.5\); instead sample \(\{P_1,P_5\}=\{(0,0),(2,8)\}\) and report the model and consensus.
Formula: slope \(a=\dfrac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\), residual \(|y_i-ax_i|\), inlier if \(\le t\).
Hint: get the slope through the two points first; note one is the outlier, so expect a tiny consensus.

Slope \(=(8-0)/(2-0)=4\), through origin, so \(y=4x\)
residuals \(|y_i-4x_i|\): \(P_1{=}0,P_2{=}3,P_3{=}6,P_4{=}9,P_5{=}0\)
inliers (\(\le0.5\)): only \(P_1,P_5\)
\(\boxed{\text{consensus}=2}\), far worse than the clean sample

Worked example (OLS contrast). For the same 5 points compute the plain least-squares slope. Givens: \(n=5,\ \sum x_i=8,\ \sum y_i=14,\ \sum x_iy_i=30,\ \sum x_i^2=18\).
Formula: \(a=\dfrac{n\sum x_iy_i-\sum x_i\sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2-(\sum x_i)^2}\).
Hint: plug the five given sums straight in; compute the numerator and denominator separately.

\(a=\dfrac{n\sum x_iy_i-\sum x_i\sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2-(\sum x_i)^2}\)
\(=\dfrac{5(30)-8(14)}{5(18)-64}=\dfrac{150-112}{90-64}=\dfrac{38}{26}\)
\(\boxed{a\approx1.46}\) vs the true \(1.0\): a \(46\%\) error from one outlier

Worked example (iteration budget, easy regime). With \(w=0.8\), \(s=2\), \(p=0.99\), compute \(N\).
Formula: \(N=\dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}\).
Hint: compute \(w^s=0.8^2\) first, then \(1-w^s\), then the ratio of logs; round up.

Worked example (iteration budget, hard regime). Same \(p=0.99\), \(s=2\), but the inlier ratio drops to \(w=0.3\); compute \(N\).
Formula: \(N=\dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}\).
Hint: recompute \(w^s=0.3^2\) and \(1-w^s\); the denominator log is now tiny, so \(N\) balloons.

Worked example (3D, larger \(s\), low \(w\)). Sphere fit with \(w=0.1\), \(s=4\), \(p=0.99\); estimate \(N\).
Formula: \(N=\dfrac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}\), with \(\log(1-x)\approx -x\) for small \(x\).
Hint: \(w^s=0.1^4\) is tiny, so approximate \(\log(1-w^s)\approx -w^s\) before dividing.

\(w^s=0.1^4=10^{-4}\), so \(1-w^s\approx0.9999\)
\(N=\dfrac{\log 0.01}{\log 0.9999}\approx\dfrac{-2}{-4.34\times10^{-5}}\)
\(\boxed{N\approx46{,}000}\) — minimal \(s\) and pre-filtering matter when \(w\) is small

Closing test (verify \(N\)). You computed \(N=5\) for \(w=0.8,s=2\); check what success probability \(5\) iterations actually deliver.
Formula: \(P(\text{success})=1-(1-w^s)^N\).
Hint: a pass is \(P_{\text{actual}}\ge p=0.99\); plug \(1-w^s=0.36\) and \(N=5\) in.

Closing test (units check on \(t\)). A scan is in millimetres with sensor noise \(\sigma=2\,\text{mm}\); a colleague sets \(t=0.01\). Find the correct \(t\) and show why \(0.01\) fails.
Formula: \(t\approx 2\sigma\), in the SAME units as the data.
Hint: a pass keeps the true surface points; compute \(2\sigma\) in mm and compare it to \(0.01\) mm.

Correct \(t\approx2\sigma=2(2\,\text{mm})=4\,\text{mm}\)
\(t=0.01\) has no stated unit; if read as mm it admits only \(|r|\le0.01\,\text{mm}\ll\sigma\)
almost every true point becomes an outlier
\(\boxed{t=4\,\text{mm},\ \text{never a unitless }0.01}\)

Closing test (Huber continuity). Verify the Huber weight \(w_k(u)\) is continuous at the knee \(|u|=k\).
Formula: \(w_k=1\) for \(|u|\le k\) and \(w_k=k/|u|\) for \(|u|>k\).
Hint: a pass is the two pieces agreeing; evaluate the tail branch at \(|u|=k\).

Quadratic side: \(w_k(u)=1\) for \(|u|\le k\)
tail side: \(w_k(u)=k/|u|\), evaluate at \(|u|=k\)
\(k/k=1\)
\(\boxed{w_k(k^-)=w_k(k^+)=1}\) — the weight is continuous, as a valid loss requires

When to use a robust loss (Huber/Tukey) versus RANSAC?
Hint: think contamination level and whether you already have a good initial estimate.

Robust loss/IRLS handles moderate contamination from a good seed and gives a smooth downweighted estimate; RANSAC searches for the inlier set when outliers are gross or numerous; they compose (RANSAC seeds IRLS).

Minimal sample size \(s\) for a 2D line, a plane, and a sphere?
Hint: \(s\) is the smallest count of points that uniquely determines the model.
\(2\) (line), \(3\) (plane), \(4\) (sphere) — the smallest count that determines the model.

Key difference between Huber and Tukey influence in the tails?
Hint: ask whether the tail influence stays nonzero or returns to exactly zero.
Huber influence saturates to a nonzero constant \(\pm k\) (downweight, never reject); Tukey redescends to exactly zero past \(|u|=c\) (full rejection).

Pitfall: why must you always refit the best RANSAC model on all its inliers?
Hint: count how many points the kept model was actually fit to.
The kept model was fit to only \(s\) points and is high variance; refitting on the full inlier set lowers variance and removes minimal-sample bias.

Pitfall: why can RANSAC return the saw-kerf wall instead of the slab face?
Hint: RANSAC optimises one quantity across hypotheses — which one?
RANSAC keeps the LARGEST consensus set; if the wall has more points it wins. Segment iteratively (fit, remove inliers, refit) and check primitive plausibility.

Pitfall: why does Tukey IRLS need a RANSAC or Huber seed?
Hint: recall the shape of the redescending cost surface.
Its redescending cost is non-convex, so a cold OLS start can land in a bad local minimum; a good seed places it in the correct basin.

How do you set a robust scale \(\sigma\) from residuals for IRLS standardisation?
Formula: \(\sigma=1.4826\cdot\text{MAD}\), \(\text{MAD}=\operatorname{median}_i|r_i-\operatorname{median}_j r_j|\).
Hint: compute the median of residuals, then the median absolute deviation, then scale by \(1.4826\).
\(\sigma=1.4826\cdot\text{MAD}\); the constant \(1.4826\) makes the median absolute deviation a consistent estimate of the Gaussian standard deviation.
The probability a single minimal sample is all-inlier is {{c1::\(w^s\)}}, so the chance of no clean sample in \(N\) tries is {{c2::\((1-w^s)^N\)}}.
Optimization · Robust / RANSAC
In RANSAC, \(p\) is the desired success probability, \(w\) is the {{c1::inlier ratio}}, and \(s\) is the {{c2::minimal sample size}}.
Optimization · Robust / RANSAC

M-estimators are solved by {{c1::iteratively reweighted least squares}}, where the weight is \(w(u)={{c2::\psi(u)/u}}\).
hint: influence over residual.
Optimization · Robust / RANSAC
Adaptive RANSAC recomputes \(N\) each iteration after updating \(w\) to {{c1::inliers found so far / total points}}.
Optimization · Robust / RANSAC
A least-squares plane normal is the {{c1::smallest-eigenvalue}} eigenvector of the centred scatter matrix, and the plane passes through the {{c2::centroid}}.
Optimization · Robust / RANSAC

Derive the forward Euler update from the slope rule \(\dot y=f(t_n,y_n)\).
Formula: \(\dfrac{y_{n+1}-y_n}{h}\approx f(t_n,y_n)\) (target: \(y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_n,y_n)\))
Hint: replace the derivative with the forward finite difference, then multiply both sides by \(h\).

The rule is \(\dot y=f(t_n,y_n)\)
approximate it over step \(h\): \(\dfrac{y_{n+1}-y_n}{h}\approx f(t_n,y_n)\)
multiply through by \(h\) and solve for the next value
\(\boxed{y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_n,y_n)}\).

Derive forward Euler as a truncated Taylor series (alternative route).
Formula: \(y(t_n+h)=y(t_n)+h\,\dot y(t_n)+\tfrac{h^2}{2}\ddot y(\xi)\), with \(\dot y=f(t,y)\)
Hint: substitute the rule for \(\dot y(t_n)\), then drop the \(\tfrac{h^2}{2}\ddot y\) remainder.

Expand \(y(t_n+h)=y(t_n)+h\,\dot y(t_n)+\tfrac{h^2}{2}\ddot y(\xi)\)
substitute the rule \(\dot y(t_n)=f(t_n,y_n)\)
drop the \(O(h^2)\) remainder term
\(\boxed{y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_n,y_n)},\ \text{local error }O(h^2)\).

Show forward Euler's GLOBAL error is \(O(h)\), i.e. it is first-order.
Formula: local error \(=\tfrac{h^2}{2}\ddot y=O(h^2)\); steps to time \(T\) are \(N=T/h\)
Hint: multiply the per-step error by the number of steps \(N=T/h\) and simplify.

The local (one-step) error is the dropped term \(\tfrac{h^2}{2}\ddot y=O(h^2)\)
reaching a fixed time \(T\) needs \(N=T/h\) steps
errors accumulate: global \(\sim N\cdot O(h^2)=\tfrac{T}{h}O(h^2)\)
\(\boxed{\text{global error}=O(h)\ \Rightarrow\ \text{first-order method}}\).

Derive the BACKWARD (implicit) Euler update by evaluating the slope at the END of the step.
Formula: \(\dfrac{y_{n+1}-y_n}{h}\approx f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1})\) (target: \(y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1})\))
Hint: take the slope at \(t_{n+1}=t_n+h\), then multiply by \(h\); note \(y_{n+1}\) appears on both sides.

Use a backward finite difference: \(\dfrac{y_{n+1}-y_n}{h}\approx f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1})\)
the slope is taken at the new point \(t_{n+1}=t_n+h\)
rearrange for the unknown \(y_{n+1}\) (it appears on both sides)
\(\boxed{y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1})}\) (solve an equation each step).

Derive the RK4 final combination from the four slope samples \(k_1,k_2,k_3,k_4\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(y_{n+1}=y_n+\int_{t_n}^{t_n+h} f\,dt\); Simpson weights \((1,4,1)/6\)
Hint: apply Simpson's rule with the two midpoint samples \(k_2,k_3\) sharing the weight-4 term, giving weights \((1,2,2,1)/6\).

Approximate \(y_{n+1}=y_n+\int_{t_n}^{t_n+h} f\,dt\)
Simpson's rule weights endpoints by 1 and midpoint by 4, and RK4 uses two midpoint samples \(k_2,k_3\)
weights become \((1,2,2,1)/6\) over the four slopes
\(\boxed{y_{n+1}=y_n+\tfrac{h}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)}\).

Derive the RK4 stage samples for \(\dot y=f(t,y)\) with step \(h\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(k_1=f(t_n,y_n)\); \(k_{i+1}=f(t_n+ch,\,y_n+ch\,k_i)\)
Hint: compute \(k_1\) at the start, then half-step along \(k_1\) for \(k_2\), along \(k_2\) for \(k_3\), and full-step along \(k_3\) for \(k_4\).

Sample the slope at the start: \(k_1=f(t_n,y_n)\)
step a half-\(h\) along \(k_1\) and resample: \(k_2=f(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_1)\)
refine the midpoint slope: \(k_3=f(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_2)\)
step a full \(h\) along \(k_3\): \(\boxed{k_4=f(t_n+h,\,y_n+h\,k_3)}\).

Derive the steady-state PDE (Poisson equation) from the heat equation (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\partial u/\partial t=\alpha\,\Delta u + f\) (target: \(\Delta u=f\))
Hint: set \(\partial u/\partial t=0\) for steady state, then divide through by \(\alpha\neq0\) and rearrange.

Steady state means the field stops changing in time: \(\partial u/\partial t=0\)
so \(0=\alpha\,\Delta u + \text{(source }f)\) with \(\alpha\neq 0\)
moving the source to the right-hand side
\(\boxed{\Delta u=f}\) (the central Poisson solve).

Derive the heat equation from conservation plus Fick's law (step 1 of 2).
Formula: conservation \(\partial u/\partial t=- abla\!\cdot\mathbf{q}\); Fick/Fourier \(\mathbf{q}=-\alpha abla u\)
Hint: substitute the flux law into conservation and use \( abla\!\cdot abla=\Delta\).

Conservation: \(\partial u/\partial t=- abla\!\cdot\mathbf{q}\)
Fick/Fourier law: flux down the gradient, \(\mathbf{q}=-\alpha abla u\)
substitute: \(\partial u/\partial t= abla\!\cdot(\alpha abla u)\)
\( oxed{\partial u/\partial t=\alpha\,\Delta u}\) since \( abla\!\cdot abla=\Delta\).

Derive the first-order state-space form of the spring-mass ODE \(m\ddot x=-kx\).
Formula: state \(\mathbf y=[x,v]^\top\); rows \(\dot x=v\) and \(\dot v=-\tfrac{k}{m}x\)
Hint: let \(v=\dot x\) be the new variable, then solve Newton's law for \(\dot v=\ddot x\).

Define the state \(\mathbf y=[x,\dot x]^\top=[x,v]^\top\)
the first row is the definition \(\dot x=v\)
the second row is the law solved for acceleration: \(\dot v=-\tfrac{k}{m}x\)
\(\boxed{\dot{\mathbf y}=\begin{bmatrix}v\\ -\tfrac{k}{m}x\end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf y(0)=[x_0,v_0]^\top}\).

Derive the closed form of \(\dot y=-ky\) by separation of variables.
Formula: \(\dfrac{dy}{y}=-k\,dt\) (target: \(y(t)=y_0 e^{-kt}\))
Hint: integrate both sides, then exponentiate and fix the constant with \(y(0)=y_0\).

Separate: \(\dfrac{dy}{y}=-k\,dt\)
integrate both sides: \(\ln y=-kt+C\)
exponentiate and fix \(C\) with \(y(0)=y_0\)
\(\boxed{y(t)=y_0 e^{-kt}}\).

Worked example (small step). For \(\dot y=-2y,\ y(0)=1\), take ONE forward-Euler step with \(h=0.1\) and compare to exact \(e^{-0.2}\approx0.8187\).
Formula: \(y_1=y_0+h\,f(t_0,y_0)\)
Hint: compute the slope \(f(0,1)=-2y_0\) first, then plug into the update.

Worked example (ten steps). For \(\dot y=-2y,\ y(0)=1\), integrate to \(t=1\) with forward Euler at \(h=0.1\).
Formula: \(y_{n+1}=(1+h\lambda)\,y_n\) with \(\lambda=-2\)
Hint: compute the per-step factor \(1+h\lambda\) first, then raise it to the power of 10 steps.

Each step multiplies by \((1+h\cdot(-2))=(1-0.2)=0.8\)
ten steps: \(y_{10}=0.8^{10}\)
\(0.8^{10}\approx0.107\)
\(\boxed{y_{10}\approx0.107\ \text{vs exact }e^{-2}\approx0.135}\).

Worked example (large step, instability). For \(\dot y=-2y,\ y(0)=1\), take forward-Euler steps with \(h=0.5\) to \(t=1\).
Formula: \(y_{n+1}=(1+h\lambda)\,y_n\) with \(\lambda=-2\); stable iff \(|1+h\lambda|<1\)
Hint: compute \(1+h\lambda\) first; if it lands at \(0\) or beyond \(\pm1\) the step is too large.

Step factor \((1+0.5\cdot(-2))=(1-1)=0\)
\(y_1=1\cdot 0=0\), then \(y_2=0\)
the step is too large: \(|1+h\lambda|=1\not<1\) at the stability edge
\(\boxed{y_2=0\ \text{(collapsed; useless, exact is }0.135)}\).

Worked example (3D robot regime). A joint state \(\mathbf y=[q,\dot q]\) with \(q=0.20\) rad, \(\dot q=0\), under \(\ddot q=-9\,q\), takes ONE forward-Euler step at \(h=0.01\) s.
Formula: \(q_{new}=q+h\,\dot q\), \(\quad\dot q_{new}=\dot q+h\,\ddot q\)
Hint: update the position row with the current \(\dot q\) first, then the velocity row with \(\ddot q=-9q\).

Test yourself (limiting case). Show forward Euler reduces to the exact solution as \(h\to0\) for \(\dot y=-2y\).
Formula: Euler \(y(h)=y_0(1-2h)\); exact \(y_0 e^{-2h}=y_0(1-2h+2h^2-\dots)\)
Hint: Taylor-expand the exact \(e^{-2h}\) and compare term by term; a pass means they agree to first order.

One step gives \(y(h)=y_0(1-2h)\)
compare to exact \(y_0 e^{-2h}=y_0(1-2h+2h^2-\dots)\)
the two agree to first order; difference is \(O(h^2)\)
\(\boxed{\lim_{h\to0}\text{Euler}=\text{exact}}\) (consistency check passes).

Test yourself (stability). Backward Euler on \(\dot y=-2y\) gives \(y_{n+1}=y_n/(1+2h)\). Verify it cannot blow up for any \(h>0\).
Formula: \(y_{n+1}=y_n+h(-2y_{n+1})\); amplification factor \(r=1/(1+2h)\)
Hint: solve for \(y_{n+1}\), then check \(|r|<1\); a pass means the factor stays in \((0,1)\).

Solve \(y_{n+1}=y_n+h(-2y_{n+1})\Rightarrow y_{n+1}(1+2h)=y_n\)
amplification factor \(|1/(1+2h)|<1\) for all \(h>0\)
so iterates decay monotonically like the true solution
\(\boxed{\text{unconditionally stable: }0<\tfrac{1}{1+2h}<1}\).

Test yourself (period check). For the spring-mass state \(\dot{\mathbf y}=[v,-\tfrac{k}{m}x]\) with \(m=2,k=8\), predict the period and check units.
Formula: \(\omega=\sqrt{k/m}\), \(\quad T=2\pi/\omega\)
Hint: compute \(\omega\) first, then \(T\); a pass means \(\sqrt{(\text{N/m})/\text{kg}}\) has units \(1/\text{s}\) so \(T\) is in seconds.

Natural frequency \(\omega=\sqrt{k/m}=\sqrt{8/2}=2\) rad/s
period \(T=2\pi/\omega=2\pi/2=\pi\approx3.14\) s
units: \(\sqrt{(\text{N/m})/\text{kg}}=\sqrt{1/\text{s}^2}=1/\text{s}\), so \(T\) is in seconds
\(\boxed{T=\pi\ \text{s, units consistent}}\).

When do you reach for an implicit method (backward Euler / BDF) over RK4?
Hint: think about which systems force explicit methods into tiny steps.
For stiff systems that mix very fast and slow dynamics, where explicit methods need impractically tiny steps to stay stable.

Why does RK4 with a coarse step often beat Euler with a fine one (cost vs order)?
Formula: RK4 error \(O(h^4)\) at 4 evals/step; Euler error \(O(h)\)
Hint: compare how much each method's error drops when \(h\) is halved.
RK4 is \(O(h^4)\), so halving \(h\) cuts error 16x for only 4 evals/step; Euler is \(O(h)\) and needs many more steps for the same accuracy.

Pitfall: a PDE solve gives a non-unique answer. What is the likely cause?
Hint: a PDE needs data on the domain boundary to be well-posed.
Missing or wrong boundary conditions; a PDE needs Dirichlet (fixed value) or Neumann (fixed normal derivative) data to be well-posed.

Pitfall: a long rigid-body simulation slowly gains energy. What integrator fixes it?
Hint: name the integrator class that conserves phase-space volume.
A symplectic integrator, which conserves energy/phase-space volume and avoids drift over many steps.
Forward Euler is {{c1::\(O(h)\)}} (first-order) accurate, while RK4 is {{c2::\(O(h^4)\)}} (fourth-order) accurate.

Optimization · Differential Equations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations · DE Ch.1
A method is stable when errors {{c1::do not blow up}}; explicit methods are only {{c2::conditionally}} stable, while backward Euler is {{c3::unconditionally}} stable.

Optimization · Differential Equations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations · DE Ch.1
On a mesh the Laplacian \(\Delta\) becomes the {{c1::cotangent Laplacian}} matrix and the heat/Poisson PDE becomes a {{c2::sparse linear system}}.

Optimization · Differential Equations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations · DE Ch.1
Short-time heat diffusion \(\partial u/\partial t=\alpha\Delta u\) is the first step of the {{c1::Signed Heat}} method and of the heat method for {{c2::geodesic distance}}.

Optimization · Differential Equations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations · DE Ch.1
A higher-order ODE is reduced to first order by stacking the variable and its derivatives into a {{c1::state vector}}, e.g. \([x,\dot x]\).

Optimization · Differential Equations
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations · DE Ch.1

Derive the normalized image coordinate \(x'\) from similar triangles (the small triangle from pinhole to the \(z=1\) plane is similar to the big triangle from pinhole to the camera point \((x,y,z)\)).
Formula: \(\dfrac{x'}{1}=\dfrac{x}{z}\ \Rightarrow\ x'=\dfrac{x}{z}\)
Hint: equate (height across axis)/(depth along axis) for the two triangles, then solve for \(x'\).

Small triangle: height \(x'\) at depth \(1\); big triangle: height \(x\) at depth \(z\)
equate the ratios: \(\dfrac{x'}{1}=\dfrac{x}{z}\)
\(\boxed{x'=\dfrac{x}{z}}\) (and identically \(y'=y/z\)).

Starting from \(x'=x/z\), derive the pixel column \(u\) for focal length \(f_x\) (px) and principal point \(c_x\).
Formula: \(u=f_x\,x'+c_x\), with \(x'=x/z\)
Hint: first scale \(x'\) by \(f_x\) (pixels-per-unit), then add \(c_x\) to shift the origin to top-left; finally substitute \(x'=x/z\).

Derive why the homogeneous scale factor equals depth, starting from the pixel maps \(u=f_x x/z+c_x\), \(v=f_y y/z+c_y\).
Formula: \(K[x\ y\ z]^T=[f_x x+c_x z,\ f_y y+c_y z,\ z]^T\), target \(s=z\)
Hint: multiply \(K\) by \([x\ y\ z]^T\) without dividing, then divide all three entries by the third and read off what cancels.

\(K[x\ y\ z]^T=[f_x x+c_x z,\ f_y y+c_y z,\ z]^T\)
divide all three by the third entry: \(u=(f_x x+c_x z)/z=f_x x/z+c_x\), same for \(v\)
the divisor is \(z\), so \(\boxed{s=z}\).

From the extrinsic map \(x=RX_w+t\), write the world-to-camera step as one matrix (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \([x\ y\ z]^T=[R\mid t]\,[X_w\ Y_w\ Z_w\ 1]^T\)
Hint: stack \(R\) (3x3) and \(t\) (3x1) side by side into a 3x4 block, then apply it to the homogeneous point \(P_w\).

Concatenate so one matrix rotates-then-translates: \([R\mid t]\) is \(3\times4\)
apply to \(P_w=[X_w\ Y_w\ Z_w\ 1]^T\)
\(\boxed{[x\ y\ z]^T=[R\mid t]\,P_w}\).

From \([x\ y\ z]^T=[R\mid t]P_w\) and \(s=z\), derive the OpenCV projection equation (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(s[u\ v\ 1]^T=K[x\ y\ z]^T\), target \(s[u\ v\ 1]^T=K[R\mid t]P_w\)
Hint: apply \(K\) to the camera point, then substitute the extrinsic block in for \([x\ y\ z]^T\).

Apply intrinsics with the scale on the left: \([su\ sv\ s]^T=K[x\ y\ z]^T\), \(s=z\)
substitute \([x\ y\ z]^T=[R\mid t]P_w\)
\(\boxed{s[u\ v\ 1]^T=K[R\mid t]P_w}\); recover \((u,v)\) by dividing the first two entries by the third.

Derive the camera center \(C\) in world coordinates from \(x=RX_w+t\).
Formula: \(0=RC+t\), target \(C=-R^T t\)
Hint: set the camera-frame point to \(x=0\), solve for \(C\), and use \(R^{-1}=R^T\) since \(R\in SO(3)\).

The camera center maps to the camera origin, so set \(0=R\,C+t\)
\(R\,C=-t\)
left-multiply by \(R^T\): \(\boxed{C=-R^T t}\) (so \(t\ne C\) in general).

Derive the back-projection ray direction \(d\) for a pixel \([u\ v\ 1]^T\) (camera frame, \(R=I,t=0\)).
Formula: \(s[u\ v\ 1]^T=K[x\ y\ z]^T\), target \(d=K^{-1}[u\ v\ 1]^T\)
Hint: left-multiply both sides by \(K^{-1}\); the unknown \(s=z\) scales the result, so you get a direction, not a point.

\(K^{-1}[u\ v\ 1]^T=\tfrac{1}{s}[x\ y\ z]^T\)
the right side is the camera point up to the unknown scale \(s=z\), i.e. a direction
\(\boxed{d=K^{-1}[u\ v\ 1]^T}\) (then normalize); depth stays unknown.

Derive the image-plane offset \(\Delta v\) of an object of true height \(Y\) at depth \(Z\), then take \(Z\to\infty\).
Formula: \(y'=Y/Z\), \(\Delta v=f_y\,y'\)
Hint: get \(y'\) from similar triangles first, multiply by \(f_y\), then let \(Z\to\infty\).

Similar triangles: \(y'=Y/Z\) on the \(z=1\) plane
pixel offset from principal point: \(\Delta v=f_y\,Y/Z\)
\(\boxed{\Delta v=f_y\dfrac{Y}{Z}}\); as \(Z\to\infty\), \(\Delta v\to0\) — distant objects collapse toward the principal point.

Worked example (near, 3D, meters). \(f_x=f_y=800\), \(c_x=320\), \(c_y=240\), \(R=I\), \(t=0\), \(P_w=(0.1,-0.2,2.0)\) m. Find the pixel.
Formula: \(u=f_x\dfrac{x}{z}+c_x\), \(v=f_y\dfrac{y}{z}+c_y\)
Hint: with \(R=I,t=0\) the camera point equals \(P_w\); compute \(x'=x/z\) and \(y'=y/z\) first, then scale and shift.

Worked example (camera retreat, regime change). Same \(f=800\) camera, moved back \(1\) m so \(C=(0,0,-1)\), giving \(t=(0,0,1)\). Re-project \(P_w=(0.1,-0.2,2.0)\).
Formula: \(z=Z_w+t_3\), \(u=f_x\dfrac{x}{z}+c_x\)
Hint: recompute the depth \(z=2.0+1\) first, then redo \(x'=x/z\) and \(u\); compare \(u\) to \(c_x=320\).

New depth \(z=2.0+1=3.0\)
\(x'=0.1/3.0=0.0333\), so \(u=800(0.0333)+320=346.7\)
\(\boxed{u\approx346.7}\) — the point slides toward \(c_x=320\) as the camera retreats, just as a farther object shrinks toward center.

Worked example (far / site-scale, large numbers). \(f_x=f_y=1200\) px, \(c_x=960\), \(c_y=540\), \(R=I\), \(t=0\). Stone edge at \(P_w=(2.0,-1.0,40.0)\) m. Find the pixel.
Formula: \(u=f_x\dfrac{x}{z}+c_x\), \(v=f_y\dfrac{y}{z}+c_y\)
Hint: divide by the large depth \(z=40\) first to get small \(x',y'\), then scale and shift.

Worked example (mm shop-scale, non-square pixels). \(f_x=900\), \(f_y=850\) px, \(c_x=640\), \(c_y=512\), \(R=I\), \(t=0\). Drill-hole edge at \(P_w=(15,-8,300)\) mm. Find the pixel.
Formula: \(u=f_x\dfrac{x}{z}+c_x\), \(v=f_y\dfrac{y}{z}+c_y\)
Hint: compute \(x'=x/z\) and \(y'=y/z\) once, then use the two different focal lengths \(f_x\ne f_y\) on each axis.

Closing test. Project \(P_w=(0.1,-0.2,2.0)\) with the \(f=800\) camera by the staged formula, then by the full matrix, and confirm they match.
Formula: \(K[R\mid t]P_w=[su,\,sv,\,s]^T\), then divide by \(s\)
Hint: a pass means the matrix's \([su,sv,s]^T\) divided by its third entry equals the staged \((360,160)\).

Closing test (round trip via back-projection). Back-project pixel \((360,160)\) with the \(f=800\) camera and confirm the ray passes through \(P_w=(0.1,-0.2,2.0)\) up to scale.
Formula: \(d=K^{-1}[u\ v\ 1]^T\); compare to \(P_w/z\)
Hint: a pass means \(d\) equals \(P_w\) divided by its own \(z\) (both reduced to \(z=1\)).

Closing test (units + limiting case) for \(\Delta v=f_y\,Y/Z\). Check dimensions and the \(Y=0\) case.
Formula: \(\Delta v=f_y\dfrac{Y}{Z}\)
Hint: a pass means units reduce to pixels and \(Y=0\) gives \(\Delta v=0\) (on-axis lands on the principal point).

Units: \([\text{px}]\cdot[\text{m}]/[\text{m}]=[\text{px}]\), a pure pixel offset \(\checkmark\)
\(Y=0\): \(\Delta v=0\), on-axis point images at the principal point \(\checkmark\)
\(\boxed{\text{dimensionally consistent; on-axis case exact}}\).

Define the projection matrix \(P\) and give its shape.
Formula: \(P=K[R\mid t]\)
Hint: multiply the \(3\times3\) intrinsics by the \(3\times4\) extrinsics and read off the shape.

When should you use back-projection instead of forward projection?
Hint: think about what is known (pixel vs 3D point) and what the perspective divide destroyed.

Use it when you have a pixel and want the 3D ray it came from
because depth \(s=z\) was destroyed by the divide, you recover a ray, not a single point.

Pitfall: why is \(t\) not the camera position in the world?
Formula: \(C=-R^T t\)
Hint: recall what frame \(t\) lives in versus where the camera center \(C\) lives.

\(t\) is the world origin expressed in the camera frame
the camera center is \(C=-R^T t\); plotting \(t\) as the camera location flips and offsets everything.

Pitfall: what must you check before the perspective divide, and why?
Formula: require \(z>0\)
Hint: consider what the divide \(x/z\) does when \(z\le0\) for a point behind the lens.

Test \(z>0\) (point in front of the camera)
for \(z\le0\) the divide yields a finite but meaningless pixel, placing a behind-camera point as if it were in front.
The pinhole model is one similar-triangles fact: a point at depth \(Z\), height \(Y\) images to height {{c1::\(f\,Y/Z\)}}, dressed in matrices to compose with rigid motion.
Vision · Pinhole Camera
The extrinsic transform maps a world point to the camera frame by {{c1::\(x = R X_w + t\)}}, and its inverse-derived camera center is {{c1::\(C=-R^T t\)}}.
Vision · Pinhole Camera
In OpenCV's camera convention, \(+Z\) points {{c1::forward out of the lens}} and \(+Y\) points {{c1::down}}; points in front satisfy {{c1::\(z>0\)}}.
Vision · Pinhole Camera
The {{c1::skew}} term in \(K\), often written \(\gamma\), is zero for real rectangular-pixel sensors and must not be confused with the scale factor \(s=z\).
Vision · Pinhole Camera
Lens distortion is applied to the {{c1::normalized coordinates \((x',y')\)}} before multiplying by \(K\), never to the final pixels.
Vision · Pinhole Camera
The homogeneous product \(K[R\mid t]P_w\) returns \([su,\,sv,\,s]\); you recover pixels by {{c1::dividing the first two entries by the third}}.
Vision · Pinhole Camera

Derive the normalized camera coordinates (step 1 of 3) by projecting the camera-frame point \(X_c=(X',Y',Z')\) onto the \(z=1\) plane.
Formula: a ray scales every coordinate by \(1/Z'\), giving target \(x'=\dfrac{X'}{Z'},\ y'=\dfrac{Y'}{Z'}\)
Hint: divide each of \(X'\) and \(Y'\) by the depth \(Z'\).

A ray from the center through \((X',Y',Z')\) meets \(z=1\) where each coordinate is scaled by \(1/Z'\)
divide out depth: \(x'=X'\cdot\frac{1}{Z'}\), \(y'=Y'\cdot\frac{1}{Z'}\)
\(\boxed{x'=\dfrac{X'}{Z'},\quad y'=\dfrac{Y'}{Z'}}\) (dimensionless; distortion acts here).

Derive the distorted radial coords (step 2 of 3) from \((x',y')\) using only radial coefficients \(k_1,k_2,k_3\) (denominator \(=1\)).
Formula: \(r^2=x'^2+y'^2\), factor \(L(r)=1+k_1r^2+k_2r^4+k_3r^6\), target \(x''=x'\,L(r)\)
Hint: build \(L(r)\) first, then multiply each normalized coord by it.

Compute \(r^2=x'^2+y'^2\)
build the even-power radial factor \(L(r)=1+k_1 r^2+k_2 r^4+k_3 r^6\)
scale both coords: \(x''=x'\,L(r)\), \(y''=y'\,L(r)\)
\(\boxed{x''=x'(1+k_1 r^2+k_2 r^4+k_3 r^6)}\) (tangential terms add separately).

Derive the pixel mapping (step 3 of 3) from the distorted coords \((x'',y'')\) by applying the intrinsic matrix \(K\).
Formula: target \(u=f_x x''+c_x,\ v=f_y y''+c_y\)
Hint: multiply each normalized coord by its focal length, then add the principal-point offset.

\(K\) scales normalized units to pixels by focal length and shifts by the principal point
\(u=f_x x''+c_x\), \(v=f_y y''+c_y\)
\(\boxed{u=f_x x''+c_x,\quad v=f_y y''+c_y}\) — this composed map is \(\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R,t;X)\).

Derive the full projective equation (step 1 of 2) from world\(\to\)camera \(X_c=[R\mid t]\tilde X\) then pixels \(=KX_c\), in homogeneous form.
Formula: target \(\tilde p = K\,[R\mid t]\,[X\ Y\ Z\ 1]^\top\)
Hint: substitute \(X_c=[R\mid t]\tilde X\) into \(\tilde p=KX_c\).

Stack the extrinsic: \(X_c=[R\mid t]\,[X\ Y\ Z\ 1]^\top\)
the intrinsic maps that camera ray to homogeneous pixels: \(\tilde p = K X_c\)
\(\boxed{\tilde p = K\,[R\mid t]\,[X\ Y\ Z\ 1]^\top}\).

From \(\tilde p = K[R\mid t]\tilde X\) derive why a free scale \(s\) appears (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\tilde p=(su,sv,s)\) with \(s=Z'\); target \(s\,[u\ v\ 1]^\top = K[R\mid t]\tilde X\)
Hint: a homogeneous pixel is defined only up to scale; pull that scale out as \(s\).

Pixels live in a projective plane; \(\tilde p=(su,sv,s)\) is defined only up to the scale \(s=Z'\)
write the equality with the scale explicit
\(\boxed{s\,[u\ v\ 1]^\top = K\,[R\mid t]\,[X\ Y\ Z\ 1]^\top}\); recover \(u,v\) by dividing the third row.

Derive the calibration objective (step 1 of 2): write the single residual for detected pixel \(\hat u_{ij}\) minus its projection.
Formula: target \(r_{ij}=\hat u_{ij}-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R_i,t_i;X_j)\)
Hint: subtract the projected point \(\pi(\cdots)\) from the detected pixel for corner \(j\) in view \(i\).

For corner \(j\) in view \(i\) the error is the detected pixel minus the projection
\(r_{ij}=\hat u_{ij}-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R_i,t_i;X_j)\) — the reprojection error in pixels
\(\boxed{r_{ij}=\hat u_{ij}-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R_i,t_i;X_j)}\).

Derive the full calibration least-squares problem (step 2 of 2) from the per-corner residual \(r_{ij}\).
Formula: target \(\min_{K,\mathbf{d},\{R_i,t_i\}}\ \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\lVert r_{ij}\rVert^2\)
Hint: square each residual and double-sum over \(N\) corners and \(M\) views, minimizing over all unknowns.

Square each residual and sum over all \(N\) corners and \(M\) views
minimize jointly over intrinsics, distortion, and one pose per view
\(\boxed{\min_{K,\mathbf{d},\{R_i,t_i\}}\ \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\lVert \hat u_{ij}-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R_i,t_i;X_j)\rVert^2}\).

Derive the PnP objective from the calibration objective when \(K,\mathbf{d}\) are already known.
Formula: target \(\min_{R,t}\ \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lVert \hat u_j-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R,t;X_j)\rVert^2\)
Hint: treat \(K,\mathbf{d}\) as fixed constants, use one view, and drop the view index.

Fix \(K,\mathbf{d}\) as constants and use a single view, so only one pose remains
drop the view index and minimize over \((R,t)\) alone
\(\boxed{\min_{R,t}\ \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lVert \hat u_j-\pi(K,\mathbf{d},R,t;X_j)\rVert^2}\) — 6 DoF instead of the full intrinsic set.

Derive the minimum point count for PnP from a degrees-of-freedom count.
Formula: pose DoF \(=6\), equations per correspondence \(=2\); solve \(2n\ge 6\)
Hint: set the equation count \(2n\) at least equal to the 6 pose unknowns.

A pose has 6 unknowns; each 2D-3D correspondence supplies 2 scalar equations
require \(2n\ge 6\Rightarrow n\ge 3\) (P3P, up to 4 discrete solutions)
\(\boxed{n\ge 3\ \text{minimal; a 4th point disambiguates}}\).

Derive the camera position in world coordinates from the world-to-camera relation \(X_c=RX_w+t\).
Formula: set \(X_c=0\) and use \(R^{-1}=R^\top\); target \(C=-R^\top t\)
Hint: the camera center is the world point that maps to \(X_c=0\) — set it to zero and solve for \(X_w\).

The camera center is the world point that maps to \(X_c=0\)
\(0=R X_w+t\Rightarrow R X_w=-t\Rightarrow X_w=-R^{-1}t\)
since \(R\in SO(3)\), \(R^{-1}=R^\top\)
\(\boxed{C = -R^\top t}\) (not \(t\)).

Derive the Rodrigues rotation matrix from the exponential map \(R=\exp(\theta K_\times)\), with \(K_\times\) the skew matrix of the unit axis \(\hat{\mathbf r}\).
Formula: use \(K_\times^3=-K_\times\); target \(R=I+\sin\theta\,K_\times+(1-\cos\theta)K_\times^2\)
Hint: expand the series and collapse odd/even powers with the identity \(K_\times^3=-K_\times\).

Expand \(\exp(\theta K_\times)\) and use \(K_\times^3=-K_\times\) to collapse the series
odd powers sum to \(\sin\theta\,K_\times\), even powers to \((1-\cos\theta)K_\times^2\)
\(\boxed{R=I+\sin\theta\,K_\times+(1-\cos\theta)K_\times^2}\), with \(\theta=\lVert\mathbf r\rVert\).

Worked example (near, no distortion). With \(f_x=f_y=800\), \(c_x=320\), \(c_y=240\) and identity pose, project \(X=(0.1,-0.05,2.0)\) m.
Formula: \(x'=X/Z\), \(y'=Y/Z\); \(u=f_x x'+c_x\), \(v=f_y y'+c_y\)
Hint: divide by \(Z=2.0\) first, then plug into the pixel equations.

Worked example (far / large depth, same \(K\), identity pose). Project \(X=(-0.2,0.1,4.0)\) m, no distortion.
Formula: \(x'=X/Z\), \(y'=Y/Z\); \(u=800x'+320\), \(v=800y'+240\)
Hint: divide by \(Z=4.0\) first; expect a smaller off-center offset than at \(Z=2\).

Worked example (mm shop fixture, different scale). Same \(K\), identity pose, project \(X=(10,-5,200)\) mm.
Formula: \(x'=X/Z\), \(y'=Y/Z\); \(u=800x'+320\), \(v=800y'+240\)
Hint: compute the ratios \(X/Z\) and \(Y/Z\) first — normalization is unit-free.

Normalization is scale-free: \(x'=10/200=0.05\), \(y'=-5/200=-0.025\)
identical normalized coords to the 2.0 m case
\(u=800(0.05)+320=360\), \(v=800(-0.025)+240=220\)
\(\boxed{(u,v)=(360,220)}\) — pixels depend on the ratio, not the unit; but \(t\) returns in mm.

Worked example (distortion regime). With \(k_1=-0.2\) (barrel), \(x'=0.05\), \(y'=-0.025\), recompute \(x''\).
Formula: \(r^2=x'^2+y'^2\), \(L=1+k_1r^2\), \(x''=x'\,L\)
Hint: compute \(r^2\) first, then the scalar factor \(L\), then multiply.

Test yourself (close the loop). You projected \(X\) to \((360,220)\); the detector found \((361.5,219.0)\). Compute the reprojection residual and its norm.
Formula: \(r=\hat u-u_{\text{proj}}\), \(\lVert r\rVert=\sqrt{r_x^2+r_y^2}\)
Hint: subtract componentwise first, then take the norm; a healthy single-point error is sub-pixel-ish.

\(r=(361.5-360,\ 219.0-220)=(1.5,-1.0)\) px
\(\lVert r\rVert=\sqrt{1.5^2+1.0^2}=\sqrt{3.25}\approx1.80\)
both terms are pixel differences, so the norm is in px
\(\boxed{\lVert r\rVert\approx1.80\ \text{px}}\) — exactly the per-point term the sum-of-squares minimizes.

Test yourself (limiting-case check). Confirm the radial factor \(L(r)=1+k_1r^2+k_2r^4+k_3r^6\) is identity at the principal point, then apply \(k_1=0.1\) at \(r^2=0.003125\).
Formula: evaluate \(L\) at \(r=0\), then at \(r^2=0.003125\); \(u=800x''+320\)
Hint: a pass means \(L=1\) exactly when \(r=0\) (no on-axis distortion).

At the center \(r=0\Rightarrow L=1\), so \(x''=x'\) (no distortion on-axis) — pass; this is why corner coverage matters
with \(k_1=0.1\), \(r^2=0.003125\): \(L=1.0003125\), \(x''=0.05\cdot1.0003125=0.05001563\), \(u=800(0.05001563)+320=360.0125\)
\(\boxed{\Delta u\approx+0.0125\ \text{px outward}}\) — small near center, grows with \(r^2\).

Test yourself (sanity-check a calibration run). After `calibrateCamera` you get RMS \(=0.4\) px on 15 varied views. Decide pass/fail and state the criterion.
Formula: RMS \(=\sqrt{\frac{1}{NM}\sum\lVert r_{ij}\rVert^2}\); healthy when well under \(1.0\) px
Hint: a pass is sub-pixel RMS on diverse views; suspect overfitting if a tiny RMS comes from too few or too-coplanar views.

Criterion: sub-pixel RMS (well under \(1.0\) px) on depth/tilt/corner-varied views indicates healthy calibration
\(0.4<1.0\) and the views are varied, so this is a pass
\(\boxed{\text{PASS: }0.4\ \text{px}<1.0\ \text{px}}\) — but a low RMS on a non-diverse set would be overfitting, not accuracy.

When do you reach for `SOLVEPNP_IPPE` instead of the iterative default, and why?
Formula: coplanar PnP has a near-mirror ambiguity (two poses with similar reprojection error)
Hint: think about planar targets / square markers.

Use it for coplanar point sets / planar targets (`SOLVEPNP_IPPE_SQUARE` for square markers)
plain coplanar PnP has a near-mirror ambiguity, so check both returned poses
\(\boxed{\text{coplanar / planar targets} \Rightarrow \texttt{IPPE}}\).

Give a robust PnP recipe for outlier-contaminated correspondences, and say why plain LM fails.
Formula: the residual is squared, so one bad match dominates the sum
Hint: reject first, refine second.

A single mismatch dominates because residuals are squared
reject outliers with `solvePnPRansac`, then refine on the inliers with `solvePnPRefineLM`
\(\boxed{\texttt{solvePnPRansac} \to \texttt{solvePnPRefineLM}}\).

Why does the same PnP code give a 1000x-wrong translation across stone fixtures, and how do you fix it?
Formula: \(t\) returns in the same units as the 3D object points \(X_j\)
Hint: look for a meters/millimeters mixup (\(1\,\text{m}=1000\,\text{mm}\)).

\(t\) comes out in the units of the 3D object points; mixing meters and millimeters gives a 1000x scale error
keep object points and reported \(t\) in one consistent unit system
\(\boxed{\text{units}(t)=\text{units}(X_j)}\).

Which `solvePnP` flag is the general-purpose default, and what does it require to run?

Derive the linear part \(v\) of a pure-rotation screw axis (axis through point \(q\), no pitch).
Formula: a body-fixed point at \(r\) relative to axis point \(q\) has velocity \(\dot r = \omega \times r\); target result \(v = -\omega \times q\).
Hint: apply the velocity rule to the point currently at the origin \(O\), so \(r = O - q\), then set \(O = 0\).

offset of origin from axis point: \(r = O - q\)
its velocity: \(v = \omega \times (O - q)\)
set \(O = 0\): \(v = \omega \times (-q) = -\omega \times q\)
\(\boxed{v = -\omega \times q}\) (pure rotation, \(h=0\)).

Extend the screw linear part to include pitch (slide along the axis).
Formula: start from \(v = -\omega \times q\); a pitch \(h\) adds translation \(h\,\omega\) per radian; target \(v = -\omega \times q + h\,\omega\).
Hint: the pitch term is parallel to \(\omega\), so just superpose it onto the rotation term.

rotation contributes \(-\omega \times q\)
pitch slide contributes \(+h\,\omega\) (parallel to the axis)
superpose: \(v = -\omega \times q + h\,\omega\)
\(\boxed{v = -\omega \times q + h\,\omega}\).

Derive the power reduction identity that collapses Rodrigues' series (step 1 of 2).
Formula: for a unit axis, \([\omega]^2 = \omega\omega^\top - I\); target \([\omega]^3 = -[\omega]\).
Hint: multiply \([\omega]\cdot[\omega]^2\) and use \([\omega]\,\omega = \omega\times\omega = 0\).

for a unit axis, \([\omega]^2 = \omega\omega^\top - I\)
\([\omega]^3 = [\omega](\omega\omega^\top - I) = ([\omega]\omega)\omega^\top - [\omega] = 0 - [\omega]\)
so higher powers cycle: \([\omega]^4 = -[\omega]^2\), \([\omega]^5 = [\omega]\), ...
\(\boxed{[\omega]^3 = -[\omega]}\).

Collapse the matrix-exponential series into Rodrigues' formula (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(e^{[\omega]\theta} = I + \theta[\omega] + \tfrac{\theta^2}{2}[\omega]^2 + \tfrac{\theta^3}{6}[\omega]^3 + \cdots\) with \([\omega]^3 = -[\omega]\); target \(R = I + \sin\theta\,[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\).
Hint: group the odd powers (all multiples of \([\omega]\)) and the even powers past \(I\) (all multiples of \([\omega]^2\)) and recognize the two Taylor series.

odd powers reduce to \(\pm[\omega]\): coefficient \(\theta - \tfrac{\theta^3}{6} + \cdots = \sin\theta\)
even powers past \(I\) reduce to \(\pm[\omega]^2\): coefficient \(\tfrac{\theta^2}{2} - \tfrac{\theta^4}{24} + \cdots = 1 - \cos\theta\)
regroup: \(I + \sin\theta\,[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\)
\(\boxed{R = e^{[\omega]\theta} = I + \sin\theta\,[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2}\).
▶ 3Blue1Brown — How to raise e to a matrix

Derive the translation factor \(G(\theta)\) in the SE(3) exponential.
Formula: \(p = \big(\int_0^\theta e^{[\omega]s}\,ds\big)v\) with \(e^{[\omega]s} = I + \sin s\,[\omega] + (1-\cos s)[\omega]^2\); target \(G(\theta) = I\theta + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega] + (\theta-\sin\theta)[\omega]^2\).
Hint: integrate the Rodrigues form term by term over \(s\) from \(0\) to \(\theta\).

\(\int_0^\theta I\,ds = I\theta\)
\(\int_0^\theta \sin s\,[\omega]\,ds = (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]\)
\(\int_0^\theta (1-\cos s)[\omega]^2\,ds = (\theta - \sin\theta)[\omega]^2\)
sum: \(G(\theta) = I\theta + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega] + (\theta-\sin\theta)[\omega]^2\)
\(\boxed{e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{[\omega]\theta} & G(\theta)v \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}\).
▶ 3Blue1Brown — How to raise e to a matrix

Derive how to recover \(\theta\) from \(R\) in the log map.
Formula: \(R = I + \sin\theta[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\); traces \(\mathrm{tr}\,I=3\), \(\mathrm{tr}\,[\omega]=0\), \(\mathrm{tr}\,[\omega]^2=-2\); target \(\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\big(\tfrac{\mathrm{tr}\,R-1}{2}\big)\).
Hint: take the trace of both sides of Rodrigues, then solve for \(\cos\theta\).
▶ 3Blue1Brown — How to raise e to a matrix

Derive how to recover the axis \([\omega]\) from \(R\) in the log map.
Formula: \(R = I + \sin\theta[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\) with \([\omega]^\top = -[\omega]\) and \(([\omega]^2)^\top = [\omega]^2\); target \([\omega] = \tfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R - R^\top)\).
Hint: write \(R^\top\) from Rodrigues, then subtract \(R - R^\top\) so the symmetric parts cancel.

\(R^\top = I - \sin\theta[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\)
subtract: \(R - R^\top = 2\sin\theta\,[\omega]\) (symmetric parts cancel)
solve: \([\omega] = \tfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R - R^\top)\)
\(\boxed{[\omega] = \tfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R - R^\top)}\) (valid for \(\sin\theta \neq 0\)).

Build the \(6\times6\) adjoint \(\mathrm{Ad}_T\) from the conjugation identity.
Formula: \([\mathcal{V}_s] = T[\mathcal{V}_b]T^{-1}\) with \(T=(R,p)\); target \(\mathrm{Ad}_T = \begin{bmatrix} R & 0 \\ [p]R & R \end{bmatrix}\).
Hint: expand the \(4\times4\) block product and read off the \(\omega\)-row and \(v\)-row separately; recall \(p\times(R\omega_b) = [p]R\,\omega_b\).

expanding gives \(\omega_s = R\,\omega_b\)
and \(v_s = R\,v_b + p \times (R\,\omega_b) = [p]R\,\omega_b + R\,v_b\)
stack as a \(6\times6\) acting on \([\omega_b;v_b]\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{Ad}_T = \begin{bmatrix} R & 0 \\ [p]R & R \end{bmatrix}}\).

Build the screw axis for a \(\pi/2\) rotation about \(z\) through \(q=(2,0,0)\), pitch \(h=0\).
Formula: \(\omega=(0,0,1)\), \(v = -\omega \times q\), \(\mathcal{S} = [\omega;\,v]\).
Hint: compute the cross product \(\omega \times q\) first, then negate it.

Find where the origin lands under that \(\pi/2\)-about-\(z\) screw.
Formula: \(p = G(\theta)v\), \(G(\theta) = I\theta + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega] + (\theta-\sin\theta)[\omega]^2\), with \(\mathcal{S}=(0,0,1,0,-2,0)\), \(\theta=\pi/2\).
Hint: evaluate the scalars first: \((1-\cos\theta)=1\), \((\theta-\sin\theta)\approx0.5708\), and \([\omega]^2=\mathrm{diag}(-1,-1,0)\).

Worked example (large scale, \(\theta=\pi\)). A quarry block rotates \(\theta=\pi\) about \(z\) through \(q=(5,0,0)\) m, \(h=0\). Build \(\mathcal{S}\) and find where the origin lands.
Formula: \(v = -\omega\times q\); for \(\theta=\pi\) the rotation flips the offset-from-center sign.

Worked example (pure translation, shop/mm scale). A slide of \(\theta = 30\) mm along \(x\): \(\omega=0\), \(v=(1,0,0)\). Build \(T = e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}\).
Formula: pure-translation branch \(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} I & v\theta \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\).
Hint: \(\omega=0\) means no rotation block; just compute \(v\theta\) for the translation column.

\(\omega = 0\) triggers the pure-translation branch
\(v\theta = (1,0,0)\cdot 30 = (30,0,0)\) mm
\(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} I & v\theta \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\)
\(\boxed{T = \begin{bmatrix} I & (30,0,0)^\top \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}\).

Worked example (small-angle, mm-scale ICP). A fine correction: \(\theta = 0.01\) rad about \(z\) through \(q=(0,0,0)\), \(h=0\). Approximate the resulting \(R\) to first order.
Formula: \(R = I + \sin\theta[\omega] + (1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\) with \(\sin\theta\approx\theta\), \(1-\cos\theta\approx\theta^2/2\).
Hint: first note \(v=-\omega\times q=0\) since the axis passes through the origin; then drop the \(\theta^2\) term and keep \(R \approx I + \theta[\omega]\).

\(v = -\omega \times q = 0\) (axis through origin)
small-angle: \(\sin\theta\approx\theta\), \(1-\cos\theta\approx\theta^2/2\approx 0\)
\(R \approx I + \theta[\omega] = I + 0.01[\omega]\)
\(\boxed{R \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.01 & 0 \\ 0.01 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}\).

Closing test (close the loop). For \(\theta=\pi/2\) about \(z\) through \((2,0,0)\), verify the origin image \((2,-2,0)\) by geometry, then round-trip through the log map.
Formula: geometry — rotate offset-from-center by \(90^\circ\); log map — \(\theta=\cos^{-1}(\tfrac{\mathrm{tr}\,R-1}{2})\), \([\omega]=\tfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R-R^\top)\), \(v=G^{-1}(\theta)p\).
Hint: a pass means both the geometric image and the recovered \((\mathcal{S},\theta)\) match the originals exactly.

geometry: offset of \(O\) from center \((2,0)\) is \((-2,0)\); rotate \(+90^\circ\Rightarrow(0,-2)\); add center \(\Rightarrow(2,-2)\) ✓
log map: \(\mathrm{tr}\,R=1\Rightarrow\theta=\cos^{-1}(0)=\pi/2\) ✓
\(\tfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}(R-R^\top)\Rightarrow\omega=(0,0,1)\), then \(v=G^{-1}(\theta)p=(0,-2,0)\) ✓
\(\boxed{\text{forward and inverse agree; screw recovered exactly}}\).

Closing test (units + limit check). For the quarry block (\(\theta=\pi\) about \(z\) through \(q=(5,0,0)\) m), confirm \(O\mapsto(10,0,0)\) m has consistent units and survives the \(\theta=0\) limit.

\(v = -\omega\times q\): \([1]\times[\text{m}] = [\text{m}]\), so \(v\) and \(p\) are in meters ✓
limit \(\theta\to 0\): \(G(\theta)\to 0\) so \(p\to 0\), origin stays put ✓
at \(\theta=\pi\) the result \((10,0,0)\) is the reflection of \(O\) through the axis at \(x=5\) ✓

Pitfall — why can't you compose two screw motions by adding their twists?

The matrix exponential is non-commutative, so adding the exponents is wrong; compose instead by multiplying the resulting \(T\) matrices.

Each joint \(i\) contributes a transform \(A_i(\theta_i)\) acting on everything outboard of it. Ride the chain from the base to write the end-effector pose \(T(\theta)\).
Formula: \(T(\theta)=A_1(\theta_1)\,A_2(\theta_2)\cdots A_n(\theta_n)\)
Hint: start at the base with \(A_1\), then right-multiply each next joint's motion as you move outward.

stand at the base and apply joint 1's motion \(A_1\)
from that new vantage apply joint 2's motion, composing on the right: \(A_1 A_2\)
continue outward to joint \(n\), each new motion right-multiplied
\(\boxed{T(\theta) = A_1(\theta_1)\,A_2(\theta_2)\cdots A_n(\theta_n)}\) exact, not an approximation.

Build the standard DH link matrix, step 1 of 2: compose the first three screw moves and give the translation column so far.
Formula: \(A_i'=\operatorname{Rot}_z(\theta_i)\operatorname{Trans}_z(d_i)\operatorname{Trans}_x(a_i)\)
Hint: \(\operatorname{Rot}_z\) fills the upper-left \(2\times2\) with \(\cos\theta_i,\sin\theta_i\); then read off where each translation lands \(p\).

\(\operatorname{Rot}_z(\theta_i)\) puts \(\cos\theta_i,\sin\theta_i\) in the upper-left \(2\times2\)
\(\operatorname{Trans}_z(d_i)\) adds \(d_i\) in the \(z\) row of \(p\) (commutes with \(\operatorname{Rot}_z\))
\(\operatorname{Trans}_x(a_i)\) adds an \(x\)-offset rotated by \(\theta_i\): \(p_x=a_i\cos\theta_i,\;p_y=a_i\sin\theta_i\)
\(\boxed{A_i' = \operatorname{Rot}_z(\theta_i)\operatorname{Trans}_z(d_i)\operatorname{Trans}_x(a_i),\quad p=(a_i\cos\theta_i,\,a_i\sin\theta_i,\,d_i)}\).

Finish the DH link matrix, step 2 of 2: right-multiply the step-1 result by \(\operatorname{Rot}_x(\alpha_i)\) and write the full \(A_i\).
Formula: \(A_i=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i\\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i\\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i\\ 0&0&0&1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: \(\operatorname{Rot}_x(\alpha_i)\) only mixes columns 2 and 3 by \(\cos\alpha_i,\sin\alpha_i\); column 1 and the translation \(p\) are untouched.

\(\operatorname{Rot}_x(\alpha_i)\) mixes the \(y,z\) columns by \(\cos\alpha_i,\sin\alpha_i\), leaving \(p\) unchanged
column 1 stays \((\cos\theta_i,\sin\theta_i,0)\); columns 2,3 pick up the \(\alpha_i\) factors
\(\boxed{A_i=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i\\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i\\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i\\ 0&0&0&1\end{bmatrix}}\).

A point coincident with the space origin moves under unit angular velocity \(\omega\) about an axis through point \(q\). Derive the screw's linear part \(v\).
Formula: \(\dot r=\omega\times(r-q)\), target \(v=-\omega\times q\)
Hint: substitute \(r=0\) (the space origin) into the velocity formula.

velocity of a point \(r\) on a body rotating about an axis through \(q\) is \(\dot r = \omega\times(r-q)\)
evaluate at the space-frame origin \(r=0\): \(\dot r = \omega\times(-q) = -\omega\times q\)
the screw's linear part is exactly this origin velocity
\(\boxed{v = -\omega\times q}\).

Formula: \(\omega=(0,0,1)\), \(v=-\omega\times q_2\)
Hint: compute the cross product \((0,0,1)\times(2,0,0)\) first, then negate it.

Formula: \(T=e^{[\mathcal{S}_n]\theta_n}\,M\)

at home the tool sits at \(M\)
moving only joint \(n\) screws the outboard chain about \(\mathcal{S}_n\) by \(\theta_n\), applied on the base side of \(M\)
a space-frame screw left-multiplies what it acts on
\(\boxed{T = e^{[\mathcal{S}_n]\theta_n}\,M}\).

Finish the PoE formula, step 2 of 2: add joints \(n-1,\dots,1\), each a space-frame screw on the base side. Write the full \(T(\theta)\).

Hint: each earlier joint prepends its exponential (left-multiplies), so chain from \(i=n\) down to \(i=1\).

each earlier joint \(i\) screws everything outboard (which already includes \(M\) and later exponentials) about \(\mathcal{S}_i\)
a space-frame screw left-multiplies, so joint \(i\) prepends \(e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}\)
chaining from \(i=n\) down to \(1\)
\(\boxed{T(\theta)=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\theta_1}e^{[\mathcal{S}_2]\theta_2}\cdots e^{[\mathcal{S}_n]\theta_n}\,M}\).

Derive Rodrigues' rotation formula by summing the matrix-exponential series for unit \(\omega\).
Formula: \(e^{[\omega]\theta}=\sum_k \tfrac{(\theta[\omega])^k}{k!}\), identity \([\omega]^3=-[\omega]\), target \(e^{[\omega]\theta}=I+\sin\theta\,[\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2\)
Hint: use \([\omega]^3=-[\omega]\) to collapse every power onto \(I\), \([\omega]\), or \([\omega]^2\), then recognize the two trig series.

\([\omega]^3=-[\omega]\Rightarrow\) even powers collapse to \(\pm[\omega]^2\), odd to \(\pm[\omega]\)
group the series: \(\big(\theta-\tfrac{\theta^3}{3!}+\cdots\big)[\omega]+\big(\tfrac{\theta^2}{2!}-\tfrac{\theta^4}{4!}+\cdots\big)[\omega]^2\), plus \(I\)
the brackets are \(\sin\theta\) and \((1-\cos\theta)\)
\(\boxed{e^{[\omega]\theta}=I+\sin\theta\,[\omega]+(1-\cos\theta)[\omega]^2}\).

Derive the prismatic twist exponential by exponentiating the twist with \(\omega=0\).
Formula: \([\mathcal{S}]=\begin{bmatrix}[\omega]&v\\0&0\end{bmatrix}\) with \(\omega=0\), target \(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}=\begin{bmatrix}I&v\theta\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: set \(\omega=0\), show \([\mathcal{S}]^2=0\), so the series truncates after the linear term.

with \(\omega=0\), \([\mathcal{S}]=\begin{bmatrix}0&v\\0&0\end{bmatrix}\) is nilpotent: \([\mathcal{S}]^2=0\)
series truncates: \(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}=I+\theta[\mathcal{S}]\)
\(\boxed{e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}=\begin{bmatrix}I & v\,\theta\\0&1\end{bmatrix}}\) a pure translation.

Worked example (2D, near reach). Planar 2R arm \(L_1=2,L_2=1\) at \(\theta_1=0,\theta_2=90^\circ\). Find the tip.
Formula: \(x=L_1\cos\theta_1+L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), \(y=L_1\sin\theta_1+L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\)
Hint: compute the combined angle \(\theta_1+\theta_2=90^\circ\) first, then plug each term in.

Worked example (cell scale, metres). Planar 2R arm \(L_1=0.3\,\text{m},L_2=0.2\,\text{m}\) at \(\theta_1=\theta_2=90^\circ\). Find the tip.
Formula: \(x=L_1\cos\theta_1+L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), \(y=L_1\sin\theta_1+L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\)
Hint: the combined angle is \(180^\circ\), so \(\cos180^\circ=-1\) and \(\sin180^\circ=0\) in the second term.

combined angle \(\theta_1+\theta_2=180^\circ\)
\(x=0.3\cos90^\circ+0.2\cos180^\circ=0-0.2=-0.2\)
\(y=0.3\sin90^\circ+0.2\sin180^\circ=0.3+0=0.3\)
\(\boxed{p=(-0.2,\,0.3,\,0)\ \text{m}}\) (folded back over the base).

Worked example (revolute + prismatic). Base revolute \(\mathcal{S}_1=(0,0,1,\,0,0,0)\) then prismatic \(\mathcal{S}_2=(0,0,0,\,1,0,0)\), \(M=I\), at \(\theta_1=90^\circ\), \(d=0.5\). Find the tip.
Formula: \(T=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\theta_1}e^{[\mathcal{S}_2]d}M\), prismatic block \(e^{[\mathcal{S}_2]d}=\begin{bmatrix}I & v\,d\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\) with \(v=(1,0,0)\)
