Hint: the slide contributes \(v\,d=(0.5,0,0)\), then the \(90^\circ\) rotation about \(+z\) sends \(+x\to+y\).

prismatic block contributes the slide \(v\,d=(0.5,0,0)\)
\(e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]90^\circ}\) rotates \(+x\to+y\), sending the slide to \((0,0.5,0)\)
\(T=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\theta_1}e^{[\mathcal{S}_2]d}M\) has this tip column
\(\boxed{p=(0,\,0.5,\,0)}\) slide runs along the rotated \(x\) axis.

Worked example (DH path, mid reach). DH planar 2R \(a_1=2,a_2=1,d=\alpha=0\) at \(\theta_1=30^\circ,\theta_2=40^\circ\). Find the tip from \(A_1A_2\).
Formula: \(x=a_1\cos\theta_1+a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), \(y=a_1\sin\theta_1+a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\)
Hint: the second term uses \(\theta_1+\theta_2=70^\circ\); evaluate \(\cos70^\circ\approx0.3420\), \(\sin70^\circ\approx0.9397\).

Test + verify (straight arm closes the loop). 2R arm \(L_1=2,L_2=1\) at \(\theta_1=45^\circ,\theta_2=0\). Find the tip, then check the reach.
Formula: \(x=L_1\cos\theta_1+L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), \(y=L_1\sin\theta_1+L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), check \(r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\)
Hint: with \(\theta_2=0\) the arm is straight, so a pass means \(r=L_1+L_2=3\).

\(\theta_2=0\) so both terms share angle \(45^\circ\)
\(x=2\cos45^\circ+1\cos45^\circ=3\cos45^\circ=2.1213\), \(y=3\sin45^\circ=2.1213\)
verify radius: \(\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=\sqrt{2\cdot2.1213^2}=3.000=L_1+L_2\)
\(\boxed{p=(2.1213,\,2.1213,\,0),\ r=3=L_1+L_2}\).

Test + verify (PoE and DH must agree). Worked 2R arm at \(\theta_1=90^\circ,\theta_2=0\). Show the PoE tip, then cross-check with the DH planar formula.
Formula: PoE \(T=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\pi/2}\,I\,M\) with \(M\) tip at \((3,0,0)\); DH \(x=a_1\cos\theta_1+a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), \(y=a_1\sin\theta_1+a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\)
Hint: a pass means both tip columns match exactly; compute each independently then compare.

PoE: \(T=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\pi/2}\,I\,M\) gives tip column \((0,3,0)\)
DH: \(x=2\cos90^\circ+1\cos90^\circ=0\), \(y=2\sin90^\circ+1\sin90^\circ=3\)
the two independent paths land identically
\(\boxed{p_{\text{PoE}}=(0,3,0)=p_{\text{DH}}}\).

Test + verify (zero-angle limit). Show that the revolute twist exponential \(\to I\) as \(\theta\to0\), confirming "no motion at home".
Formula: \(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}=\begin{bmatrix}e^{[\omega]\theta} & G(\theta)v\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\), \(G(\theta)=I\theta+(1-\cos\theta)[\omega]+(\theta-\sin\theta)[\omega]^2\)
Hint: a pass means both the rotation block \(\to I\) and \(G(\theta)v\to0\); substitute \(\theta=0\) into each.

\(e^{[\omega]\theta}\to I\) since \(\sin0=0,\,1-\cos0=0\)
\(G(0)=I\cdot0+0\cdot[\omega]+0\cdot[\omega]^2=0\), so translation \(G(0)v=0\)
\(\boxed{e^{[\mathcal{S}]0}=I}\) no motion at zero angle, as required.

Explain why screw axis 1 of the 2R arm has zero linear part, \(\mathcal{S}_1=(0,0,1,0,0,0)\).
Formula: \(v_1=-\omega\times q_1\) with axis \(+z\) through \(q_1=(0,0,0)\)
Hint: substitute \(q_1=0\) into the cross product.

axis 1 is \(+z\) through the origin \(q_1=(0,0,0)\)
\(v_1=-\omega\times q_1=-\omega\times 0=0\)
\(\boxed{\mathcal{S}_1=(0,0,1,\;0,0,0)}\) the space origin lies on the axis, so it has no velocity.

Define the forward kinematics function of a serial chain.
Hint: it is a map from one set of numbers to a pose in \(SE(3)\).

the map from joint variables to end-effector pose
\(\boxed{T(\theta)\in SE(3)}\).

In the PoE formula, what is \(M\) and where does it sit?
Formula: \(T(\theta)=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\theta_1}\cdots e^{[\mathcal{S}_n]\theta_n}\,M\)
Hint: set every \(\theta_i=0\) and see which factor survives.

\(M=T(0)\), the pose of \(\{b\}\) relative to \(\{s\}\) at the zero configuration
it sits on the far right, and must be the measured machine home, not a guess
\(\boxed{M=T(0)}\).

State when classical DH is ill-posed but PoE is not.
Hint: think about what the DH common normal needs in order to be defined.

DH is ill-posed when consecutive joint axes are parallel (the common normal is undefined)
PoE has no parameterization singularity, only kinematic Jacobian singularities
\(\boxed{\text{DH fails on parallel consecutive axes; PoE does not}}\).

How do you branch the twist exponential for a prismatic joint in code?
Formula: prismatic \(e^{[\mathcal{S}]\theta}=\begin{bmatrix}I&v\theta\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: test the magnitude of \(\omega\) against a small \(\varepsilon\) before calling Rodrigues.

test \(\|\omega\|<\varepsilon\)
if true, set rotation to identity and translation to \(v\theta\), skipping Rodrigues (which would divide by a zero angle)
\(\boxed{\text{if }\|\omega\|<\varepsilon:\ R=I,\ p=v\theta}\).

After many product compositions, \(T\)'s rotation block drifts. What invariant breaks, and how do you restore it before IFC export?
Formula: orthonormality \(R^\top R=I\)
Hint: a valid rotation has all singular values equal to 1; use the SVD to force that.

the invariant that breaks is orthonormality: \(R\) must stay in \(SO(3)\) with \(R^\top R=I\)
re-project via SVD, setting all singular values to 1 (\(R\leftarrow UV^\top\))
else IFC rejects the non-orthonormal axis triple
\(\boxed{\text{re-orthonormalize }R\text{ via SVD before export}}\).
Forward kinematics is the ordered product {{c1::\(T(\theta)=A_1A_2\cdots A_n\)}}, exact and not an approximation.
Robotics · Forward Kinematics

In the space-frame PoE product each exponential {{c1::left-multiplies}} and the home pose {{c2::\(M\)}} sits on the far right.
hint: set all \(\theta_i=0\) to see which factor remains.
Robotics · Forward Kinematics
The revolute closed-form exponential requires {{c1::\(\|\omega\|=1\)}} so that \(\theta\) equals the physical rotation angle.
Robotics · Forward Kinematics
For a revolute joint with unit axis \(\omega\) through point \(q\), the screw linear part is {{c1::\(v=-\omega\times q\)}}, the velocity of the space origin under unit rotation.
Robotics · Forward Kinematics
A space-frame screw {{c1::left}}-multiplies and a body-frame screw {{c2::right}}-multiplies; never interleave the two conventions.
Robotics · Forward Kinematics

A planar 2R arm has tip position \(x=L_1c_1+L_2c_{12}\), \(y=L_1s_1+L_2s_{12}\). Find the position Jacobian \(J=\partial(x,y)/\partial(\theta_1,\theta_2)\).
Formula: \(J_{rc}=\partial(\text{row }r)/\partial(\text{col }\theta_c)\), with \(\tfrac{d}{d\theta}\cos\theta=-\sin\theta\), and \(c_{12}=\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\) depends on BOTH \(\theta_1,\theta_2\).
Hint: differentiate \(x\) first; remember \(c_{12}\) contributes to both columns since \(\theta_1+\theta_2\) contains each angle.

\(\partial x/\partial\theta_1=-L_1s_1-L_2s_{12}\), \(\partial x/\partial\theta_2=-L_2s_{12}\)
\(\partial y/\partial\theta_1=L_1c_1+L_2c_{12}\), \(\partial y/\partial\theta_2=L_2c_{12}\)
\(\boxed{J=\begin{bmatrix}-L_1s_1-L_2s_{12} & -L_2s_{12}\\ L_1c_1+L_2c_{12} & L_2c_{12}\end{bmatrix}}\).

Evaluate the general 2R Jacobian numerically at \(L_1=L_2=1,\ \theta_1=0,\ \theta_2=\pi/2\).
Formula: \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-L_1s_1-L_2s_{12} & -L_2s_{12}\\ L_1c_1+L_2c_{12} & L_2c_{12}\end{bmatrix}\).
Hint: first compute \(s_1,c_1\) at \(\theta_1=0\) and \(s_{12},c_{12}\) at \(\theta_1+\theta_2=\pi/2\), then substitute.

Differentiate the PoE map \(T=P_{i-1}e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}\cdots M\) w.r.t. \(\theta_i\) to get the space-frame twist of column \(i\).
Formula: \(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta_i}e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}=[\mathcal{S}_i]e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}\); with \(P_{i-1}=e^{[\mathcal{S}_1]\theta_1}\cdots e^{[\mathcal{S}_{i-1}]\theta_{i-1}}\); target twist \(=P_{i-1}[\mathcal{S}_i]P_{i-1}^{-1}\).
Hint: only the \(i\)-th factor depends on \(\theta_i\); differentiate it, then form \((\partial T/\partial\theta_i)\,T^{-1}\) so the trailing factors cancel.

\(\partial T/\partial\theta_i=P_{i-1}[\mathcal{S}_i]e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}\cdots M\)
right-multiply by \(T^{-1}\); everything from \(e^{[\mathcal{S}_i]\theta_i}\) rightward cancels, leaving \(P_{i-1}[\mathcal{S}_i]P_{i-1}^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{(\partial T/\partial\theta_i)T^{-1}=P_{i-1}[\mathcal{S}_i]P_{i-1}^{-1}}\).

From the conjugation \(P_{i-1}[\mathcal{S}_i]P_{i-1}^{-1}\), derive the space-Jacobian column formula.
Formula: adjoint identity \((T[\mathcal{V}]T^{-1})^\vee=[\mathrm{Ad}_T]\mathcal{V}\); target \(J_{s,i}=[\mathrm{Ad}_{P_{i-1}}]\mathcal{S}_i\).
Hint: take the vee (\(^\vee\)) of the conjugation and read off the adjoint of \(P_{i-1}\).

conjugating a twist by a rigid motion is the adjoint action, \((T[\mathcal{V}]T^{-1})^\vee=[\mathrm{Ad}_T]\mathcal{V}\)
apply with \(T=P_{i-1}\), \(\mathcal{V}=\mathcal{S}_i\)
\(\boxed{J_{s,i}=[\mathrm{Ad}_{P_{i-1}}]\,\mathcal{S}_i}\), with \(J_{s,1}=\mathcal{S}_1\).

Map a unit ball of joint rates \(\|\dot\theta\|=1\) through \(J\) and find the matrix whose eigenvalues set the velocity-ellipsoid axes.
Formula: \(\mathcal{V}=J\dot\theta\); on the row space \(\dot\theta=J^+\mathcal{V}\); target quadric \(\mathcal{V}^\top(JJ^\top)^{-1}\mathcal{V}=1\).
Hint: substitute \(\dot\theta=J^+\mathcal{V}\) into \(\dot\theta^\top\dot\theta=1\) and simplify \( (J^+)^\top J^+\).

\(\dot\theta^\top\dot\theta=1\) becomes \(\mathcal{V}^\top(J^+)^\top J^+\mathcal{V}=\mathcal{V}^\top(JJ^\top)^{-1}\mathcal{V}=1\), a quadric
the shape matrix is the inverse of \(JJ^\top\)
\(\boxed{A=JJ^\top}\) governs the velocity ellipsoid.

From \(A=JJ^\top\), express the ellipsoid axis half-lengths using the SVD \(J=U\Sigma V^\top\).
Formula: \(A=JJ^\top=U\Sigma^2U^\top\); eigenvalues \(\lambda_i(A)=\sigma_i(J)^2\); half-length\(_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i}\).
Hint: substitute \(J=U\Sigma V^\top\) into \(JJ^\top\) and use \(V^\top V=I\) to collapse the middle.

\(JJ^\top=U\Sigma V^\top V\Sigma U^\top=U\Sigma^2U^\top\), so \(\lambda_i=\sigma_i^2\)
axis half-length is \(\sqrt{\lambda_i}\)
\(\boxed{\text{half-length}_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i(JJ^\top)}=\sigma_i(J)}\).

Derive the joint torques needed to hold an end-effector wrench \(\mathcal{F}\), starting from virtual work.
Formula: virtual-work balance \(\tau^\top\delta\theta=\mathcal{F}^\top\delta\mathcal{X}\) for all \(\delta\theta\); kinematics \(\delta\mathcal{X}=J\,\delta\theta\); target \(\tau=J^\top\mathcal{F}\).
Hint: substitute \(\delta\mathcal{X}=J\,\delta\theta\), factor out \(\delta\theta\), then equate the coefficients since it holds for every \(\delta\theta\).

\(\tau^\top\delta\theta=\mathcal{F}^\top J\,\delta\theta\) for all \(\delta\theta\)
so \(\tau^\top=\mathcal{F}^\top J\); transpose both sides
\(\boxed{\tau=J^\top(\theta)\,\mathcal{F}}\).

For the worked 2R \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-1&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}\), compute \(A=JJ^\top\) and its eigenvalues.
Formula: \(A=JJ^\top\); eigenvalues solve \(\lambda^2-(\mathrm{tr}\,A)\lambda+\det A=0\).
Hint: form \(JJ^\top\) first (multiply \(J\) by its transpose), then read off \(\mathrm{tr}\,A\) and \(\det A\) for the quadratic.

From eigenvalues \(\lambda\approx 2.618,\ 0.382\), compute the singular values and the condition number \(\mu_1\).
Formula: \(\sigma_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i}\); \(\mu_1=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\).
Hint: take square roots of the eigenvalues first, then divide the larger by the smaller.

From the general 2R \(J\), derive its determinant and the condition on \(\theta_2\) that makes it singular.
Formula: \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-L_1s_1-L_2s_{12} & -L_2s_{12}\\ L_1c_1+L_2c_{12} & L_2c_{12}\end{bmatrix}\); \(\det\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}=ad-bc\); identity \(\sin(B-A)=s_Bc_A-c_Bs_A\).
Hint: after \(ad-bc\) the \(L_2^2\) terms cancel; group the rest as \(L_1L_2(s_{12}c_1-c_{12}s_1)\) and apply the sine-difference identity with \(B=\theta_1+\theta_2,\ A=\theta_1\).

\(\det J=(-L_1s_1-L_2s_{12})(L_2c_{12})-(-L_2s_{12})(L_1c_1+L_2c_{12})\)
\(L_2^2\) terms cancel: \(=L_1L_2(s_{12}c_1-c_{12}s_1)=L_1L_2\sin\theta_2\)
\(\boxed{\det J=L_1L_2\sin\theta_2=0\iff\theta_2=0\text{ or }\pi}\) (arm straight or folded).

At the fully stretched config \(\theta_2=0,\ \theta_1=0,\ L_1=L_2=1\), build \(J\) and show it is singular.
Formula: \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-s_1-s_{12} & -s_{12}\\ c_1+c_{12} & c_{12}\end{bmatrix}\) (with \(L_1=L_2=1\)); singular \(\iff\det J=0\).
Hint: evaluate \(s_1,c_1\) at \(\theta_1=0\) and \(s_{12},c_{12}\) at \(\theta_1+\theta_2=0\), then check the determinant.

\(s_1=0,c_1=1\); \(\theta_1+\theta_2=0\) so \(s_{12}=0,c_{12}=1\)
\(J=\begin{bmatrix}0 & 0\\ 2 & 1\end{bmatrix}\), \(\det J=0\cdot1-0\cdot2=0\)
\(\boxed{\text{rank }1:\text{ tip moves only along }y,\text{ no }x\text{-velocity}}\).

Worked example (2D, near-folded). For the 2R arm at \(\theta_2=0.1\) rad, \(L_1=L_2=1\), compute \(\mu_3=|\det J|\).
Formula: \(\mu_3=|\det J|=L_1L_2|\sin\theta_2|\).
Hint: plug the lengths and angle straight in; use \(\sin(0.1)\approx0.0998\).

Worked example (2D, dexterous). For the 2R arm at \(\theta_2=\pi/2\), \(L_1=L_2=1\), compute \(\mu_3=|\det J|\) and compare to the near-folded \(0.0998\) case.
Formula: \(\mu_3=L_1L_2|\sin\theta_2|\).
Hint: \(|\sin\theta_2|\) peaks at \(\theta_2=\pi/2\); evaluate, then divide by \(0.0998\) for the ratio.

\(\mu_3=1\cdot1\cdot|\sin(\pi/2)|=1\)
the right-angle elbow maximizes \(|\sin\theta_2|\); ratio \(1/0.0998\approx10\)
\(\boxed{\mu_3=1,\ \approx10\times\text{ the near-folded value}}\).

Worked example (3D, mm scale). A revolute joint has unit axis \(\hat s=(0,0,1)\) through point \(q=(50,0,0)\) mm. Compute its screw axis.
Formula: \(\mathcal{S}=(\hat s,\ -\hat s\times q)\), with \(\hat s\times q=(s_yq_z-s_zq_y,\ s_zq_x-s_xq_z,\ s_xq_y-s_yq_x)\).
Hint: compute the cross product \(\hat s\times q\) first, then negate it for the linear part.

\(\hat s\times q=(0\cdot0-1\cdot0,\ 1\cdot50-0\cdot0,\ 0\cdot0-0\cdot0)=(0,50,0)\)
\(-\hat s\times q=(0,-50,0)\)
\(\boxed{\mathcal{S}=(0,0,1,\ 0,-50,0)}\) (linear part in mm).

Worked example (statics, m scale). At a config with \(J^\top=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\) (m/rad), an end-effector wrench \(\mathcal{F}=(10,5)\) N is applied. Compute the joint torques.
Formula: \(\tau=J^\top\mathcal{F}\).
Hint: it is a \(2\times2\) times \(2\times1\) matrix-vector product; multiply each row of \(J^\top\) by \(\mathcal{F}\).

Closing test (square-case identity). For the 2R arm at \(\theta_2=\pi/2\) you found \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-1&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}\) and eigenvalues \(2.618,0.382\) of \(A=JJ^\top\). Verify \(\sqrt{\det A}=|\det J|\).
Formula: \(\det A=\lambda_{\max}\lambda_{\min}\); \(\mu_3=\sqrt{\det A}\); for square \(J\) this must equal \(|\det J|\).
Hint: a pass means both numbers land on \(1\); compute \(\det A\) from the eigenvalue product and \(\det J\) from \(ad-bc\).

Closing test (force-velocity duality). The velocity-ellipsoid axes are \(\sigma_{\max}\approx1.618,\ \sigma_{\min}\approx0.618\). Verify the force-ellipsoid axes are their reciprocals.
Formula: velocity ellipsoid \(\sim A=JJ^\top\); force ellipsoid \(\sim A^{-1}\); force axis half-lengths \(=1/\sigma_i\).
Hint: a pass means the fast-motion axis matches the weak-force axis; take \(1/\sigma_{\max}\) and \(1/\sigma_{\min}\) and see they swap.

force ellipsoid governed by \(A^{-1}\), eigenvalues \(1/\lambda_i\), axes \(1/\sigma_i\)
\(1/1.618\approx0.618\) and \(1/0.618\approx1.618\)
\(\boxed{\text{fast-motion axis}=\text{weak-force axis},\ 1.618\leftrightarrow0.618\ \checkmark}\).

Closing test (singularity limit). Take \(\det J=L_1L_2\sin\theta_2\) and let \(\theta_2\to0\). Verify \(\mu_3\), \(\mu_1\), and rank all flag a singularity.
Formula: \(\mu_3=\sqrt{\det A}=|\det J|\); \(\mu_1=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\); singular \(\iff\sigma_{\min}\to0\).
Hint: a pass means \(\mu_3\to0\), \(\mu_1\to\infty\), rank drops; track what \(\det J\to0\) does to \(\sigma_{\min}\).

\(\det J\to0\Rightarrow\sigma_{\min}\to0\)
\(\mu_3=|\det J|\to0\), \(\mu_1=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}\to\infty\), rank drops \(2\to1\)
\(\boxed{\theta_2=0\text{ is a boundary singularity; all three measures confirm it}}\).

What does the manipulator Jacobian map, and in which direction?
Hint: think of it as the derivative of forward kinematics.
it maps joint velocities to the end-effector twist, \(\mathcal{V}=J(\theta)\dot\theta\) (a velocity / derivative map).

When do you use the space Jacobian vs the body Jacobian?
Formula: \(J_s=[\mathrm{Ad}_{T_{sb}}]\,J_b\).
Hint: match the frame to where the task is described — fixed world vs the tool itself.

space \(J_s\) for fixed/world-frame reasoning (scan-rig coords); body \(J_b\) for tool-relative tasks (feed rate along the tool axis)
they convert via the adjoint of the current pose, \(J_s=[\mathrm{Ad}_{T_{sb}}]J_b\).

Pitfall: why should you never test \(\det J=0\) exactly to detect a singularity?
Hint: think about floating-point arithmetic and what to test instead.

floating point never lands exactly on zero
instead test \(\sigma_{\min}\) against a tolerance or cap the condition number \(\mu_1\), and switch IK to damped least squares near singularities.

Pitfall: why is the manipulability-ellipsoid volume not frame-invariant?
Hint: look at the units of the angular vs linear rows of \(J\).

\(J\)'s angular rows are rad-based and its linear rows are meter-based, so \(JJ^\top\) mixes units
compare manipulability only at a fixed length scale, or weight the rows by a characteristic length before \(\det\).
In the space Jacobian, column \(i\) is the screw axis \(\mathcal{S}_i\) transported by the {{c1::adjoint}} of the product of the preceding exponentials.

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)

The space and body Jacobians satisfy \(J_s=[\mathrm{Ad}_{{{c1::T_{sb}}}}]\,J_b\), the adjoint of the current pose.
hint: it is the pose carrying body-frame into space-frame.

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)
A configuration is singular when the Jacobian {{c1::loses rank}}; for a square non-redundant arm this is \(\det J=0\).

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)
The Modern Robotics twist convention stacks {{c1::angular velocity}} first, \(\mathcal{V}=[\omega;v]\), while ROS uses \([v;\omega]\).

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)
For a redundant arm (\(n>6\)) the manipulability measure is {{c1::\(\sqrt{\det(JJ^\top)}\)}} because \(\det J\) is undefined for a non-square \(J\).

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)
Velocity and force ellipsoids are {{c1::dual}}: directions of fast motion are directions of weak force, and vice versa.

Robotics · Manipulator Jacobian
▶ 3Blue1Brown — The Jacobian matrix (Grant Sanderson)

Derive the linearized IK step: starting from forward kinematics \(T(\theta)\) and a desired pose, get the equation one Gauss-Newton step must solve for the joint update \(\Delta\theta\).
Formula: Taylor expand \(e(\theta_{i+1}) \approx e(\theta_i) + \frac{\partial e}{\partial\theta}\Delta\theta\) with \(\frac{\partial e}{\partial\theta}=J(\theta_i)\), target \(e \approx J(\theta_i)\,\Delta\theta\).
Hint: set the error after the step to zero, so \(0 \approx e(\theta_i) - J\,\Delta\theta\); identify \(e \equiv e(\theta_i)\).

Define the task error \(e\) between current and goal frame
Linearize FK about the current guess: \(e(\theta_i+\Delta\theta)\approx e(\theta_i)-J(\theta_i)\Delta\theta\)
Demand the linear prediction hit the goal, set it to \(0\)
\(\boxed{e = J(\theta_i)\,\Delta\theta},\quad \Delta\theta=\theta_{i+1}-\theta_i\).

Derive step 1 of the DLS step: write the regularized objective in \(\Delta\theta\) whose minimizer stays bounded near a singularity.
Formula: residual \(\|J\Delta\theta-e\|^2\) plus a step penalty \(\lambda^2\|\Delta\theta\|^2\).
Hint: just add the two terms; the penalty is the only new piece beyond plain least squares.

Plain solve \(e=J\Delta\theta\) explodes near singularities, so penalize step size
Add a Tikhonov term \(\lambda^2\|\Delta\theta\|^2\) to the residual
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = \arg\min_{\Delta\theta}\ \|J\Delta\theta - e\|^2 + \lambda^2\|\Delta\theta\|^2}\).

Derive step 2 of the DLS step: from \(\min_{\Delta\theta}\|J\Delta\theta-e\|^2+\lambda^2\|\Delta\theta\|^2\) obtain the closed form.
Formula: normal equations \((J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)\,\Delta\theta = J^T e\); target \(\Delta\theta = J^T(JJ^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} e\).
Hint: take the gradient in \(\Delta\theta\), set it to zero, then apply the push-through identity to move to the cheap \(m\times m\) form.

Set the gradient to zero: \(2J^T(J\Delta\theta-e)+2\lambda^2\Delta\theta=0\)
Collect terms: \((J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)\,\Delta\theta = J^T e\)
Invert the SPD matrix: \(\Delta\theta=(J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}J^T e\)
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = J^T(JJ^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} e}\).

Prove the push-through identity that swaps the order of the damped inverse.
Formula: target \((J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}J^T = J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}\); start from \(J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)=(J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)J^T\).
Hint: verify both sides of the start equation expand to \(J^TJJ^T+\lambda^2 J^T\), then left- and right-multiply by the two inverses.

Both sides expand to \(J^TJJ^T+\lambda^2 J^T\), so \(J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)=(J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)J^T\)
Left-multiply by \((J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}\), right-multiply by \((JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{(J^TJ+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}J^T = J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}}\), valid for any \(\lambda>0\).

Derive step 1 of the pseudoinverse step in SVD coordinates from \(J=U\Sigma V^T\).
Formula: \(J^\dagger = V\Sigma^\dagger U^T\) (\(\Sigma^\dagger\) sends each \(\sigma_i\to 1/\sigma_i\)); target \(\Delta\theta = \sum_i \frac{1}{\sigma_i}(u_i^T e)\,v_i\).
Hint: apply \(J^\dagger\) to \(e\), then read \(U^T e\) as the coefficients \(u_i^T e\).

\(J^\dagger = V\Sigma^\dagger U^T\), with \(\Sigma^\dagger\) inverting each \(\sigma_i\to 1/\sigma_i\)
Apply to the error: \(\Delta\theta = J^\dagger e = V\Sigma^\dagger U^T e\)
Expand the sum over columns
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = \sum_i \frac{1}{\sigma_i}\,(u_i^T e)\,v_i}\).

Derive step 2: from \(\Delta\theta=\sum_i \frac{1}{\sigma_i}(u_i^Te)v_i\), show why a singularity blows the step up.
Formula: the coefficient on \(v_i\) is \(\frac{1}{\sigma_i}(u_i^Te)\); near a singularity some \(\sigma_i\to 0\).
Hint: hold \(u_i^Te\) fixed and let \(\sigma_i\to0\); look at the limit of \(1/\sigma_i\).

As the arm nears a singularity, some \(\sigma_i\to 0\)
The coefficient \(1/\sigma_i\to\infty\) even for a tiny \(u_i^Te\)
\(\boxed{\sigma_i\to 0 \Rightarrow \|\Delta\theta\|\to\infty}\): one nearly-lost direction demands near-infinite joint motion.

Derive step 1 of the SVD form of the DLS step from \(\Delta\theta=J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}e\).
Formula: \(J=U\Sigma V^T\), so \(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I=U(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)U^T\); target \(\Delta\theta = V\,\Sigma(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}U^T e\).
Hint: substitute the SVD; the orthogonal \(U^TU=I\) cancels in the middle.

Substitute \(J=U\Sigma V^T\), so \(JJ^T=U\Sigma^2U^T\) and \(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I=U(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)U^T\)
Then \(J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}=V\Sigma U^T\,U(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}U^T\)
Cancel \(U^TU=I\)
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = V\,\Sigma(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}U^T e}\).

Derive step 2: from \(\Delta\theta=V\,\Sigma(\Sigma^2+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}U^T e\), read off the per-direction filter that replaces \(1/\sigma_i\).
Formula: the \(i\)-th diagonal entry is \(\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda^2}\); target \(\frac{1}{\sigma_i}\to\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda^2}\).
Hint: compare the entry against \(1/\sigma_i\) in the two regimes \(\sigma_i\gg\lambda\) and \(\sigma_i\to0\).

Each diagonal entry maps \(\sigma_i\) through \(\sigma_i/(\sigma_i^2+\lambda^2)\)
For \(\sigma_i\gg\lambda\) this \(\approx 1/\sigma_i\); for \(\sigma_i\to 0\) this \(\approx \sigma_i/\lambda^2\to 0\)
\(\boxed{\frac{1}{\sigma_i}\ \longrightarrow\ \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda^2}}\): the reciprocal becomes a bounded filter.

Derive the maximum gain of the DLS filter and where it occurs.
Formula: \(g(\sigma)=\frac{\sigma}{\sigma^2+\lambda^2}\), with \(g'(\sigma)=\frac{\lambda^2-\sigma^2}{(\sigma^2+\lambda^2)^2}\); target \(g_{\max}=\frac{1}{2\lambda}\) at \(\sigma=\lambda\).
Hint: set the numerator \(\lambda^2-\sigma^2\) of \(g'\) to zero to find the maximizer, then plug \(\sigma=\lambda\) back into \(g\).

Derive the large-\(\lambda\) limit of the DLS step and name the method it becomes.
Formula: \(\Delta\theta = J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}e\); target \(\Delta\theta \approx \frac{1}{\lambda^2}J^T e\).
Hint: when \(\lambda^2\) dominates \(\|JJ^T\|\), approximate \(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I\approx\lambda^2 I\).

\(\Delta\theta = J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}e\)
For \(\lambda^2\gg\|JJ^T\|\), \(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I\approx\lambda^2 I\)
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta \approx \tfrac{1}{\lambda^2}J^T e}\): the Jacobian-transpose / gradient-descent method up to scale.

Derive the position Jacobian of the planar 2R tip from its forward kinematics.
Formula: \(x=c_1+c_{12},\ y=s_1+s_{12}\) (with \(c_1=\cos\theta_1,\ s_{12}=\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), etc.); target \(J=\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(\theta_1,\theta_2)}\).
Hint: differentiate each of \(x,y\) w.r.t. \(\theta_1\) then \(\theta_2\); note \(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta_1}c_{12}=-s_{12}\) and \(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta_2}c_{12}=-s_{12}\).

\(\partial x/\partial\theta_1=-s_1-s_{12}\), \(\partial x/\partial\theta_2=-s_{12}\)
\(\partial y/\partial\theta_1=c_1+c_{12}\), \(\partial y/\partial\theta_2=c_{12}\)
\(\boxed{J=\begin{bmatrix}-s_1-s_{12} & -s_{12}\\ c_1+c_{12} & c_{12}\end{bmatrix}}\).

Worked example (2R, well-conditioned, links in m). Compute the DLS step at \(\theta=(0^\circ,30^\circ)\) with \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-0.5&-0.5\\1.866&0.866\end{bmatrix}\), \(e=(-1.5,0.866)\), \(\lambda=0.5\).
Formula: \(\Delta\theta = J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1} e\).
Hint: first build the \(2\times2\) matrix \(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I\), solve \((JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)\,y=e\) for \(y\), then set \(\Delta\theta=J^Ty\).

Worked example (same 2R, plain inverse for comparison). Compute the undamped step at \(\theta=(0^\circ,30^\circ)\) with \(e=(-1.5,0.866)\) and \(\det J=0.5\).
Formula: \(\Delta\theta=J^{-1}e\) with \(J^{-1}=\frac{1}{\det J}\begin{bmatrix}J_{22}&-J_{12}\\-J_{21}&J_{11}\end{bmatrix}\), \(J=\begin{bmatrix}-0.5&-0.5\\1.866&0.866\end{bmatrix}\).
Hint: form \(J^{-1}\) from the \(2\times2\) adjugate over \(\det J=0.5\) first, then multiply by \(e\).

\(J^{-1}=\frac{1}{0.5}\begin{bmatrix}0.866&0.5\\-1.866&-0.5\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1.732&1.0\\-3.732&-1.0\end{bmatrix}\)
\(\Delta\theta=J^{-1}e=(-1.732,4.732)\) rad
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta\approx(-99^\circ,+271^\circ)}\): a wild overshoot the damped step tames.

Worked example (near-singular, arm near full extension). At \(\theta=(0^\circ,10^\circ)\) the 2R has \(\sigma_{\min}=0.078\); with \(e=(0,0.1)\) compare the plain step size to DLS with \(\lambda=0.5\).
Formula: plain gain \(\approx 1/\sigma_{\min}\) so \(\|\Delta\theta\|\approx\|e\|/\sigma_{\min}\); DLS gain capped at \(\frac{1}{2\lambda}\) so \(\|\Delta\theta\|\lesssim\|e\|/(2\lambda)\).
Hint: compute the plain magnitude \(\|e\|/\sigma_{\min}\) first, then the DLS cap \(\|e\|/(2\lambda)\), and compare.

Plain step rides the \(1/\sigma_{\min}\) gain (\(\sim 1/0.078\approx 13\) on the lost direction): \(\|\Delta\theta\|=\|J^{-1}e\|\approx 0.141\) rad
DLS gain capped at \(1/(2\lambda)=1\), so \(\|\Delta\theta\|\approx 0.043\) rad
\(\boxed{\text{DLS}\approx 0.043\ \text{rad} \ll 0.141\ \text{rad}}\): damping shrinks the near-singular spike \(\sim 3\times\).

Worked example (SVD filter, two regimes). With \(\lambda=0.1\), evaluate the reciprocal vs the DLS filter for a small \(\sigma=0.05\) and a large \(\sigma=2.0\).
Formula: reciprocal \(1/\sigma\) vs filter \(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma^2+\lambda^2}\) (use \(\lambda^2=0.01\)).
Hint: plug each \(\sigma\) into both expressions; watch the small \(\sigma\) get attenuated while the large \(\sigma\) is essentially unchanged.

Test yourself (verify the gain bound). With \(\lambda=0.5\), check the DLS filter never exceeds \(1/(2\lambda)\).
Formula: \(g(\sigma)=\frac{\sigma}{\sigma^2+\lambda^2}\), bound \(\frac{1}{2\lambda}\), peak at \(\sigma=\lambda\).
Hint: a pass means \(g(\lambda)\) equals the bound exactly and every other \(\sigma\) gives a smaller \(g\); test \(\sigma=0.5,\,2,\,0.05\).

Bound \(1/(2\lambda)=1.0\)
At \(\sigma=\lambda=0.5\): \(g=0.5/(0.25+0.25)=1.0\) (hits the bound)
At \(\sigma=2\): \(g=2/4.25=0.47<1\); at \(\sigma=0.05\): \(g=0.05/0.2525=0.198<1\)
\(\boxed{\max_\sigma g=1.0=1/(2\lambda)\ \checkmark}\): bound confirmed.

Test yourself (units check). In the body-twist error \(\mathcal{V}_b=(\omega_b,v_b)\), justify why the solver uses two separate stopping thresholds.
Formula: stop when \(\|\omega_b\|\le\varepsilon_\omega\) (rad) AND \(\|v_b\|\le\varepsilon_v\) (m).
Hint: a pass means you can name the units of each part and explain why one scalar tolerance cannot compare rad against m.

\(\omega_b\) has units rad, \(v_b\) has units m, so one scalar tolerance cannot gate both
Loop while \(\|\omega_b\|>\varepsilon_\omega\) (rad) OR \(\|v_b\|>\varepsilon_v\) (m)
\(\boxed{\varepsilon_\omega,\varepsilon_v\ \text{distinct because rad and m are unlike units}}\).

Derive the body-frame pose error: explain why poses cannot be subtracted and build the correct error.
Formula: relative pose \(T_{sb}^{-1}(\theta_i)\,T_{sd}\); target \([\mathcal{V}_b]=\log\!\big(T_{sb}^{-1}(\theta_i)\,T_{sd}\big)\).
Hint: first form the relative pose that carries current frame to goal, then take the matrix log to turn it into a body twist.

\(SE(3)\) matrices do not subtract to a meaningful pose; rotations live on a manifold
Form the relative pose \(T_{sb}^{-1}(\theta_i)\,T_{sd}\), then take the matrix log
\(\boxed{[\mathcal{V}_b]=\log\!\big(T_{sb}^{-1}(\theta_i)\,T_{sd}\big)}\): the constant body twist carrying current frame onto goal in unit time.

When-to-use: when must you switch from the plain right pseudoinverse to the damped form?
Formula: plain \(J^\dagger=J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}\) vs damped \(J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}\).
Hint: ask what happens to \(JJ^T\) at a singularity, and which of the two forms still inverts a non-singular matrix there.

\(J^\dagger=J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}\) needs full-row-rank fat \(J\); at a singularity \(JJ^T\) is singular and uninvertible
\(\boxed{\text{Switch to } J^T(JJ^T+\lambda^2 I)^{-1}\ \text{near/through singularities}}\): \(\lambda^2 I\) keeps the matrix invertible.

When-to-use: how do joint limits enter a redundant-arm (\(n>m\)) IK step without disturbing the task?
Formula: null-space projector \((I-J^\dagger J)\) and secondary motion \(z=-k\nabla H(\theta)\); target \(\Delta\theta = J^\dagger e + (I-J^\dagger J)\,z\).
Hint: add a term living in the null space of \(J\), so \(J(I-J^\dagger J)=0\) leaves the task error unchanged.

Any \(J^\dagger e\) admits a null-space addition that leaves the task error unchanged
Pick \(z=-k\nabla H(\theta)\) with \(H\) penalizing limit proximity
\(\boxed{\Delta\theta = J^\dagger e + (I-J^\dagger J)\,z}\).

Pitfall: what goes wrong if you pair a body Jacobian with a spatial-frame error, and what is the rule?
Formula: consistent pairs \(\mathcal{V}_b\) with \(J_b\), \(\mathcal{V}_s\) with \(J_s\).
Hint: the fix is to match the error's frame to the Jacobian's frame; name what a mismatch does to the iteration.

Frame mismatch makes the linear model inconsistent and the iteration silently diverges
\(\boxed{\text{Match frames: }\mathcal{V}_b\ \text{with}\ J_b,\quad \mathcal{V}_s\ \text{with}\ J_s}\).
The damped least squares method is the robotics name for {{c1::Levenberg-Marquardt}} regularization applied to the IK residual.

Robotics · Inverse Kinematics
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

The DLS filter \(\sigma/(\sigma^2+\lambda^2)\) peaks at \(\sigma={{c1::\lambda}}\) with value {{c2::1/(2\lambda)}}, which bounds every joint-rate gain.
hint: set the derivative's numerator \(\lambda^2-\sigma^2\) to zero.

Robotics · Inverse Kinematics
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
For the DLS solve to use Cholesky, the matrix \(JJ^T + \lambda^2 I\) must be {{c1::symmetric positive definite}}, which holds for any \(\lambda > 0\).

Robotics · Inverse Kinematics
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
In Eigen, the damped step is computed as `J.transpose() * A.ldlt().solve(e)` where `A` equals {{c1::J * J.transpose() + lambda*lambda*I}}.

Robotics · Inverse Kinematics
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2
Inverse kinematics is hard because the pose-to-joints map is {{c1::nonlinear and non-unique}}.

Robotics · Inverse Kinematics
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent · NN Ch.2

Derive forward dynamics: solve the equations of motion for the joint acceleration \(\ddot q\).
Formula: \(M(q)\ddot q + C(q,\dot q)\dot q + g(q)=\tau\) (target: \(\ddot q=M^{-1}(\tau-C\dot q-g)\))
Hint: move the \(C\dot q\) and \(g\) terms to the right, then left-multiply by \(M^{-1}\) (it is SPD, hence invertible).

Move non-inertial terms right: \(M(q)\ddot q=\tau-C(q,\dot q)\dot q-g(q)\)
\(M\) is SPD hence invertible, so left-multiply by \(M^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{\ddot q=M(q)^{-1}\big(\tau-C(q,\dot q)\dot q-g(q)\big)}\).

Rewrite the second-order dynamics as a first-order state ODE \(\dot y=f(y)\) ready for RK4.
Formula: \(\ddot q=M^{-1}(\tau-C\dot q-g)\), state \(y=[q;\dot q]\)
Hint: the top block of \(\dot y\) is just \(\dot q\) (already a state); the bottom block is \(\ddot q\).

Choose state \(y=[q;\dot q]\)
\(\dot y_{\text{top}}=\dot q\) (already a state)
\(\dot y_{\text{bot}}=\ddot q=M^{-1}(\tau-C\dot q-g)\)
\(\boxed{\dot y=\begin{bmatrix}\dot q\\ M(q)^{-1}(\tau-C\dot q-g)\end{bmatrix}}\) then integrate \(y\) with RK4.

Computed torque, step 1 of 3: pick \(\tau\) so the closed loop becomes \(\ddot q=a\) for a chosen input \(a\).
Formula: \(M\ddot q+C\dot q+g=\tau\); try \(\tau=M\,a+C\dot q+g\)
Hint: substitute that \(\tau\) into the EOM and cancel the matching \(C\dot q+g\) on both sides.

Pick \(\tau=M\,a+C\dot q+g\) and substitute
\(M\ddot q+C\dot q+g=M\,a+C\dot q+g\)
cancel \(C\dot q+g\) and left-multiply by \(M^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{\ddot q=a}\) (feedback-linearized double integrator).

Computed torque, step 2 of 3: choose the input \(a\) so the tracking error decays.
Formula: \(\ddot q=a\), error \(e=q_d-q\); try \(a=\ddot q_d+K_d\dot e+K_p e\)
Hint: substitute, then use \(\ddot e=\ddot q_d-\ddot q\) to eliminate \(\ddot q\).

Set \(a=\ddot q_d+K_d\dot e+K_p e\)
substitute: \(\ddot q=\ddot q_d+K_d\dot e+K_p e\)
use \(\ddot e=\ddot q_d-\ddot q\) so \(\ddot q_d-\ddot q=-(K_d\dot e+K_p e)\)
\(\boxed{\ddot e+K_d\dot e+K_p e=0}\).

Computed torque, step 3 of 3: assemble the full torque and split it into feedforward and feedback.
Formula: \(\tau=M\,a+C\dot q+g\) with \(a=\ddot q_d+K_d\dot e+K_p e\)
Hint: substitute \(a\), then group the \(\ddot q_d,C\dot q,g\) terms (model) apart from the \(K_p,K_d\) terms (corrector).

Substitute \(a\): \(\tau=M(\ddot q_d+K_d\dot e+K_p e)+C\dot q+g\)
group feedforward (model) and feedback (corrector)
\(\boxed{\tau=\underbrace{M\ddot q_d+C\dot q+g}_{\text{inverse dynamics}}+\underbrace{M(K_p e+K_d\dot e)}_{\text{feedback}}}\).

From the closed-loop error ODE, read off the natural frequency \(\omega_n\) and damping ratio \(\zeta\).
Formula: \(\ddot e+K_d\dot e+K_p e=0\) vs the standard \(\ddot e+2\zeta\omega_n\dot e+\omega_n^2 e=0\)
Hint: match the constant (\(e\)) coefficient first to get \(\omega_n\), then the \(\dot e\) coefficient.

Compare to \(\ddot e+2\zeta\omega_n\dot e+\omega_n^2 e=0\)
match coefficients: \(\omega_n^2=K_p\) and \(2\zeta\omega_n=K_d\)
\(\boxed{\omega_n=\sqrt{K_p},\quad \zeta=\dfrac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p}}}\).

Derive the \(K_d\) that gives critical damping.
Formula: \(\zeta=K_d/(2\sqrt{K_p})\), critical means \(\zeta=1\)
Hint: set \(\zeta=1\) and solve for \(K_d\).

Critical damping means \(\zeta=1\)
\(1=K_d/(2\sqrt{K_p})\)
solve for \(K_d\)
\(\boxed{K_d=2\sqrt{K_p}}\) (e.g. \(K_p=100\Rightarrow K_d=20\)).

Derive the inverse-dynamics feedforward torque needed to hold a pose at rest.
Formula: \(M\ddot q+C\dot q+g=\tau\) with \(\dot q=\ddot q=0\) at rest
Hint: set both \(\dot q\) and \(\ddot q\) to zero and see which term survives.

Set \(\dot q=0,\ \ddot q=0\) in \(M\ddot q+C\dot q+g=\tau\)
\(M\cdot 0+C\cdot 0+g(q)=\tau\)
\(\boxed{\tau_{ff}=g(q)}\) — at rest the feedforward only fights gravity.

Derive the steady-state error of a P controller when the gravity model is off by a constant bias \(\Delta\).
Formula: at rest the residual proportional torque balances the bias: \(K_p e_{ss}=\Delta\)
Hint: at steady state \(\ddot q=\dot q=0\), so only \(K_p e\) is left to supply the missing \(\Delta\); solve for \(e_{ss}\).

At steady state \(\ddot q=\dot q=0\), so the residual torque \(K_p e\) balances the unmodeled bias \(\Delta\)
\(K_p e_{ss}=\Delta\)
\(\boxed{e_{ss}=\Delta/K_p}\) (nonzero offset).

Show that adding an integral term drives the constant-bias offset \(e_{ss}=\Delta/K_p\) to zero.
Formula: \(\tau=\dots+K_i\int e\,dt\)
Hint: note a nonzero constant \(e_{ss}\) makes \(\int e\,dt\) ramp forever; ask what happens once \(K_i\int e\,dt\) has grown to supply \(\Delta\).

A constant \(e_{ss}\) makes \(\int e\,dt\) ramp without bound, so \(K_i\int e\,dt\) grows until it supplies \(\Delta\) on its own
then the P term no longer needs error, so \(e\) can return to \(0\)
\(\boxed{e_{ss}\to 0\ \text{(integral cancels the constant bias)}}\).

For a trapezoidal velocity profile, derive the distance covered during one acceleration ramp.
Formula: \(t_a=v_{\max}/a_{\max}\) and \(d_a=\tfrac12 a_{\max}t_a^2\)
Hint: compute \(t_a\) first, then substitute it into the constant-acceleration distance.

Time to reach cruise: \(t_a=v_{\max}/a_{\max}\)
distance under constant accel: \(d_a=\tfrac12 a_{\max}t_a^2\)
\(\boxed{d_a=\dfrac{v_{\max}^2}{2a_{\max}}}\).

Derive the total move time of a trapezoidal profile over distance \(D\).
Formula: \(d_a=v_{\max}^2/(2a_{\max})\), \(t_a=v_{\max}/a_{\max}\); cruise distance \(d_c=D-2d_a\) at \(v_{\max}\), total \(T=2t_a+t_c\)
Hint: find the cruise time \(t_c=d_c/v_{\max}\), then add the two ramps; the \(d_a\) terms cancel.

Two ramps cover \(2d_a\); cruise distance \(d_c=D-2d_a\) at \(v_{\max}\)
\(t_c=d_c/v_{\max}=D/v_{\max}-v_{\max}/a_{\max}\)
total \(T=2t_a+t_c\)
\(\boxed{T=\dfrac{D}{v_{\max}}+\dfrac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}}}\) (valid when \(D\ge v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}\)).

Worked example (hold a pose, small joint). Single joint \(J=2\), gravity \(g(q)=5\sin q\), target \(q_d=\pi/2\) at rest; joint at \(q=1.4\) rad, \(\dot q=0\), \(K_p=20,K_d=8\). Find the commanded torque.
Formula: \(\tau=\underbrace{g(q_d)}_{\tau_{ff}}+\underbrace{K_p e+K_d\dot e}_{\text{feedback}},\ e=q_d-q\)
Hint: compute the gravity feedforward \(g(\pi/2)\) first, then the error \(e=\pi/2-1.4\).

Feedforward at rest: \(\tau_{ff}=g(\pi/2)=5\sin(\pi/2)=5\)
error \(e=\pi/2-1.4\approx0.1708\), \(\dot e=0\)
feedback \(K_p e+K_d\dot e=20(0.1708)+0=3.42\)
\(\boxed{\tau=5+3.42=8.42}\).

Worked example (different regime: light joint, \(\pi/3\)). \(J=0.5\), \(g(q)=2\sin q\), \(q_d=\pi/3\) at rest; \(q=0.9\) rad, \(\dot q=0\), \(K_p=40,K_d=12\). Find \(\tau\).
Formula: \(\tau=g(q_d)+K_p e+K_d\dot e,\ e=q_d-q\)
Hint: compute \(\tau_{ff}=2\sin(\pi/3)\) first, then \(e=\pi/3-0.9\) (\(\dot e=0\) so the \(K_d\) term drops).

Worked example (critical damping, two gain sets). Tune \(K_d\) for critical damping of \(\ddot e+K_d\dot e+K_p e=0\) when (a) \(K_p=20\) and (b) \(K_p=100\).
Formula: \(K_d=2\sqrt{K_p}\)
Hint: plug each \(K_p\) straight into the formula; \(\sqrt{20}\approx4.47\).

Worked example (trapezoidal, m-scale move). Joint travels \(D=3\) rad with \(v_{\max}=2\) rad/s, \(a_{\max}=4\) rad/s\(^2\). Find the total time and check it reaches cruise.
Formula: cruise exists iff \(D\ge v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}\); then \(T=D/v_{\max}+v_{\max}/a_{\max}\)
Hint: first test the cruise condition \(v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}\) against \(D\), then plug into \(T\).

Reach-cruise distance \(v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}=4/4=1\le3\), so a cruise phase exists
\(T=D/v_{\max}+v_{\max}/a_{\max}=3/2+2/4=1.5+0.5\)
\(\boxed{T=2.0\ \text{s}}\).

Worked example (mm-scale linear move). A scribe tip travels \(D=120\) mm with \(v_{\max}=60\) mm/s and \(a_{\max}=300\) mm/s\(^2\). Find the total move time.
Formula: cruise exists iff \(D\ge v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}\); then \(T=D/v_{\max}+v_{\max}/a_{\max}\)
Hint: check \(v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}=3600/300\) against \(D=120\) first, then evaluate \(T\).

Cruise check: \(v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}=3600/300=12\,\text{mm}\le120\), so a cruise phase exists
\(T=120/60+60/300=2.0+0.2\)
\(\boxed{T=2.2\ \text{s}}\).

Test yourself (close the loop). For the \(K_p=20\) joint above, the P controller leaves \(e_{ss}=\Delta/K_p\) under a gravity bias \(\Delta=1\). Compute \(e_{ss}\) and verify it shrinks if you raise \(K_p\) to \(100\).
Formula: \(e_{ss}=\Delta/K_p\)
Hint: a pass means \(e_{ss}\) drops by the same factor \(K_p\) rises (here \(5\times\)), and stays nonzero.

\(e_{ss}=1/20=0.05\) rad
raise gain: \(e_{ss}'=1/100=0.01\) rad
\(\boxed{0.05\to0.01\ \text{as }K_p:20\to100}\) — higher \(K_p\) shrinks (but does not kill) the offset; only the integral drives it to \(0\). Limit check: \(K_p\to\infty\Rightarrow e_{ss}\to0\).

Test yourself (units + limiting case). Verify \(T=D/v_{\max}+v_{\max}/a_{\max}\) is dimensionally a time, then check the limit \(a_{\max}\to\infty\).
Formula: \(T=D/v_{\max}+v_{\max}/a_{\max}\) (use rad, rad/s, rad/s\(^2\))
Hint: a pass means each term reduces to seconds, and the second term vanishes as \(a_{\max}\to\infty\).

Units: \(\frac{\text{rad}}{\text{rad/s}}+\frac{\text{rad/s}}{\text{rad/s}^2}=\text{s}+\text{s}=\text{s}\) (ok)
as \(a_{\max}\to\infty\) the ramps vanish: \(T\to D/v_{\max}\)
\(\boxed{T\to D/v_{\max}}\) = the pure constant-velocity time, as expected.

Test yourself (forward-dynamics sanity). With \(\tau=g(q)\) at rest (\(\dot q=0\)), plug into the forward dynamics and confirm equilibrium.
Formula: \(\ddot q=M^{-1}(\tau-C\dot q-g)\)
Hint: a pass means \(\ddot q=0\); note \(C\dot q=0\) when \(\dot q=0\), so \(\tau-g\) should cancel.

\(C\dot q=0\) since \(\dot q=0\)
\(\ddot q=M^{-1}(g(q)-0-g(q))=M^{-1}\,0\)
\(\boxed{\ddot q=0}\) — gravity-balancing torque holds the pose, confirming equilibrium.

When do you use computed-torque (inverse-dynamics) control instead of plain PID?
Hint: think about what extra ingredient computed torque needs, and what it buys for the error dynamics.
When you have a good dynamics model and want the closed-loop error to follow \(\ddot e+K_d\dot e+K_p e=0\) with model-independent, predictable gains (model cancels the nonlinearities).

Lagrangian route to the EOM: which energy expression and which operator give \(\tau\)?
Formula: \(L=T-V\), operator \(\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial\dot q}-\frac{\partial L}{\partial q}=\tau\)
Hint: \(T\) is kinetic, \(V\) is potential energy.
\(L=T-V\) (kinetic minus potential), with \(\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial\dot q}-\frac{\partial L}{\partial q}=\tau\).

Pitfall — integral windup: what is it and one fix?
Hint: think about what the integrator does while the actuator is saturated and cannot deliver more torque.
The integral keeps accumulating while the actuator is saturated, overshooting badly; fix with clamping / anti-windup on the integral term.

Pitfall — why is pure position control unsafe when carving stone?
Hint: think about what happens to force the instant a stiff tool meets a stiff stone surface.
Carving creates contact forces; rigid position control spikes force on contact, so use impedance or force control.

Code idiom: which Eigen call solves \(M\ddot q=b\) for an SPD inertia matrix, and why that one?
Hint: the factorization name reflects "symmetric positive-definite".
`M.ldlt().solve(b)` — LDL\(^T\) is the fast, stable factorization for a symmetric positive-definite \(M\).
In PID, the {{c1::integral}} term removes steady-state error and the {{c2::derivative}} term damps oscillation.

Robotics · Dynamics & Control
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations (pendulum) · DE Ch.1
Computed-torque control linearizes the closed loop to {{c1::\(\ddot e + K_d\dot e + K_p e = 0\)}}, so the gains set \(\omega_n=\sqrt{K_p}\) and \(\zeta=K_d/(2\sqrt{K_p})\).

Robotics · Dynamics & Control
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations (pendulum) · DE Ch.1

Forward dynamics solves \(\ddot q = {{c1::M(q)^{-1}(\tau - C\dot q - g)}}\) and is integrated with {{c2::RK4}}.
hint: isolate \(\ddot q\) from \(M\ddot q+C\dot q+g=\tau\).

Robotics · Dynamics & Control
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations (pendulum) · DE Ch.1
At rest the inverse-dynamics feedforward reduces to {{c1::\(\tau_{ff}=g(q)\)}}, just balancing gravity.

Robotics · Dynamics & Control
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Differential equations (pendulum) · DE Ch.1

Derive the optimal translation \(t\) for fixed \(R\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: objective \(\sum_i\|Rp_i+t-q_i\|^2\); centroids \(\bar p=\tfrac1N\sum_i p_i,\ \bar q=\tfrac1N\sum_i q_i\); target result \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\).
Hint: take \(\partial/\partial t\), set it to \(0\), and pull the constant \(Nt\) out of the sum.

\(\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\sum_i\|Rp_i+t-q_i\|^2=2\sum_i(Rp_i+t-q_i)=0\)
\(\sum_i(Rp_i)+Nt-\sum_i q_i=0\)
divide by \(N\): \(R\bar p+t-\bar q=0\)
\(\boxed{t=\bar q-R\bar p}\).

Substitute \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\) back in and derive the centered, pure-rotation objective (step 2 of 2).
Formula: centered points \(\tilde p_i=p_i-\bar p,\ \tilde q_i=q_i-\bar q\); target result \(\min_{R\in SO(3)}\sum_i\|R\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2\).
Hint: plug \(t\) into \(Rp_i+t-q_i\) and group the \(\bar p,\bar q\) terms with their points.

\(Rp_i+t-q_i=Rp_i+\bar q-R\bar p-q_i=R(p_i-\bar p)-(q_i-\bar q)\)
rename \(\tilde p_i=p_i-\bar p,\ \tilde q_i=q_i-\bar q\)
\(\boxed{\min_{R\in SO(3)}\sum_i\|R\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2}\) — translation has dropped out.

Show the centered rotation objective reduces to a trace maximization (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(\|R\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2=\|R\tilde p_i\|^2+\|\tilde q_i\|^2-2\,\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i\); target result \(\max_R\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i\).
Hint: use \(\|R\tilde p_i\|=\|\tilde p_i\|\) (orthogonal \(R\)) to drop the \(R\)-independent terms.

expand one term: \(\|R\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2=\|R\tilde p_i\|^2+\|\tilde q_i\|^2-2\,\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i\)
\(\|R\tilde p_i\|=\|\tilde p_i\|\), so the first two terms are constant in \(R\)
minimizing the sum \(\equiv\) \(\boxed{\max_R\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i}\).

Turn \(\max_R\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i\) into the trace form and identify \(H\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: identity \(a^Tb=\operatorname{tr}(ba^T)\); target result \(\max_R\operatorname{tr}(RH),\ H=\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T\).
Hint: rewrite each scalar \(\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i\) as a trace, then pull \(R\) and the sum out.

\(\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i=\operatorname{tr}(R\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T)\)
\(\sum_i\operatorname{tr}(R\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T)=\operatorname{tr}\!\big(R\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T\big)\)
\(\boxed{\max_R\operatorname{tr}(RH),\quad H=\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T}\) (source-left, target-right convention).

From \(H=U\Sigma V^T\), derive the orthogonal maximizer of \(\operatorname{tr}(RH)\) before any reflection fix (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(\operatorname{tr}(RH)=\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,V^TRU)\), with \(\Sigma=\mathrm{diag}(\sigma_1,\sigma_2,\sigma_3)\); target result \(R=VU^T\).
Hint: set \(M=V^TRU\) (orthogonal, so \(|M_{jj}|\le1\)) and bound \(\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma M)\).

\(\operatorname{tr}(RH)=\operatorname{tr}(RU\Sigma V^T)=\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,V^TRU)\)
with \(M=V^TRU\): \(\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma M)=\sum_j\sigma_jM_{jj}\le\sum_j\sigma_j\)
bound hit when \(M=I\), i.e. \(V^TRU=I\)
\(\boxed{R=VU^T}\).

From \(R=VU^T\), derive the Umeyama-corrected rotation that stays in \(SO(3)\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(d=\det(VU^T)\in\{+1,-1\}\); target result \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\).
Hint: if \(\det(VU^T)=-1\) flip the axis tied to the smallest \(\sigma_3\) by inserting \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\).

\(VU^T\) may have \(\det=-1\) (a reflection); to enforce \(\det R=+1\) flip the \(\sigma_3\) axis, costing only \(2\sigma_3\)
set \(d=\det(VU^T)\) and insert \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\)
\(\boxed{R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T,\ \ d=\det(VU^T)}\).

Once \(R\) is known, derive the translation that re-attaches the clouds.
Formula: optimal \(t\) sends \(\bar p\mapsto\bar q\), i.e. \(R\bar p+t=\bar q\); target result \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\).
Hint: require the source centroid to land on the target centroid and solve for \(t\).

optimal \(t\) maps source centroid onto target centroid
\(R\bar p+t=\bar q\)
\(\boxed{t=\bar q-R\bar p}\).

Derive the Umeyama optimal scale \(c\) for a similarity fit.
Formula: \(\min_c\sum_i\|cR\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2\) gives \(c=\dfrac{\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i}{\sum_i\|\tilde p_i\|^2}\), with \(\sigma_p^2=\tfrac1N\sum_i\|\tilde p_i\|^2\); target result \(c=\tfrac{1}{\sigma_p^2}\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d))\).
Hint: differentiate in \(c\), set to \(0\), then replace the numerator by \(\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d))\) and the denominator by \(N\sigma_p^2\).

\(\dfrac{d}{dc}\sum_i\|cR\tilde p_i-\tilde q_i\|^2=0\Rightarrow c=\dfrac{\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i}{\sum_i\|\tilde p_i\|^2}\)
numerator \(=\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d))\), denominator \(=N\sigma_p^2\)
\(\boxed{c=\dfrac{1}{\sigma_p^2}\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d))}\); for rigid registration set \(c=1\).

Derive the per-correspondence row \(a_i\) and value \(b_i\) for the linearized point-to-plane step.
Formula: residual \(\approx n_i^T(p_i-q_i)+n_i^T([r]_\times p_i)+n_i^Tt\), with \(R\approx I+[r]_\times\) and identity \(n^T([r]_\times p)=(p\times n)^Tr\); target row \(a_i^Tx=b_i\).
Hint: swap \(n_i^T([r]_\times p_i)\) for \((p_i\times n_i)^Tr\), then read off \(a_i\) as the coefficient of \(x=(r;t)\).

residual \(\approx n_i^T(p_i-q_i)+n_i^T([r]_\times p_i)+n_i^Tt\)
\(n_i^T([r]_\times p_i)=(p_i\times n_i)^Tr\); stack \(x=(r;t)\)
\(\boxed{a_i=\begin{bmatrix}p_i\times n_i\\ n_i\end{bmatrix},\ b_i=-\,n_i^T(p_i-q_i)}\).

Worked example (2D, clean rotation). Source \(p\): \((0,0),(2,0),(2,1),(0,1)\); target \(q\): \((1,1),(1,3),(0,3),(0,1)\). Compute \(H\).
Formula: \(\bar p=\tfrac1N\sum p_i,\ \bar q=\tfrac1N\sum q_i\), \(\tilde p_i=p_i-\bar p,\ \tilde q_i=q_i-\bar q\), \(H=\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T\).
Hint: get the two centroids first, then center every point before summing the outer products.

Worked example (continue, 2D). With \(H=\begin{bmatrix}-2&4\\-1&2\end{bmatrix}\) and candidate \(R=\begin{bmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}\) (\(90^\circ\) CCW), verify optimality.
Formula: \(R\) is the Procrustes optimizer iff \(RH\) is symmetric PSD, and then \(\operatorname{tr}(RH)=\sigma_1+\sigma_2\).
Hint: multiply \(RH\), check symmetry, then get its eigenvalues from \(\operatorname{tr}\) and \(\det\).

\(RH=\begin{bmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}-2&4\\-1&2\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1&-2\\-2&4\end{bmatrix}\)
symmetric; trace \(5\), det \(0\) give eigenvalues \(0,5\ge0\), so \(RH\succeq0\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{tr}(RH)=5=\sigma_1+\sigma_2\Rightarrow R\text{ is the maximizer}}\).

Worked example (3D, mm shop scale, identity case). Two scans of a block are already aligned: \(p_i=q_i\) for all \(i\). Solve \((R,t)\).
Formula: \(H=\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T\), \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\), \(d=\det(VU^T)\), \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\).
Hint: with \(p_i=q_i\) note \(\tilde p_i=\tilde q_i\), so \(H\) is symmetric PSD and \(U=V\).

\(\bar p=\bar q\), so \(\tilde p_i=\tilde q_i\)
\(H=\sum\tilde p_i\tilde p_i^T\succeq0\) symmetric \(\Rightarrow U=V\), \(d=\det(VU^T)=+1\)
\(R=VU^T=I\), \(t=\bar q-I\bar p=0\)
\(\boxed{(R,t)=(I,0)}\) — no motion, residual \(0\,\)mm.

Worked example (similarity fit, scale at site scale). Centered clouds match in shape but the target is \(\times4\) bigger: \(\tilde q_i=4R\tilde p_i\) with \(R\) a pure rotation. Find \(c\).
Formula: \(c=\dfrac{\sum_i\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i}{\sum_i\|\tilde p_i\|^2}\).
Hint: substitute \(\tilde q_i=4R\tilde p_i\) and use \(\tilde q_i^TR\tilde p_i=4\|R\tilde p_i\|^2=4\|\tilde p_i\|^2\).

\(R\) from SVD aligns directions, \(d=+1\)
\(c=\dfrac{\sum 4\,(R\tilde p_i)^TR\tilde p_i}{\sum\|\tilde p_i\|^2}=\dfrac{4\sum\|\tilde p_i\|^2}{\sum\|\tilde p_i\|^2}\)
\(\boxed{c=4}\) — the scale ratio is recovered exactly.

Worked example (reflection regime). The naive \(R_0=VU^T\) returns \(\det R_0=-1\) on a noisy near-planar cloud. Give the corrected \(R\) and the cost of the fix.
Formula: \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\), \(d=\det(VU^T)\); flipping the smallest-\(\sigma\) axis adds \(2\sigma_3\) to the squared error.
Hint: here \(d=-1\), so insert \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,-1)\) and recompute \(\det R\).

\(d=\det(VU^T)=-1\), insert \(\mathrm{diag}(1,1,-1)\)
\(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,-1)\,U^T\), now \(\det R=(-1)\det(VU^T)=+1\)
flipping the \(\sigma_3\) axis raises the objective by \(2\sigma_3\)
\(\boxed{\text{extra squared error}=2\sigma_3,\ \det R=+1}\).

Closing test (close the loop, 2D). Using \(R=\begin{bmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}\), \(\bar p=(1,0.5)\), \(\bar q=(0.5,2)\), find \(t\) then verify the fit on \(p_1=(0,0)\) (target \(q_1=(1,1)\)).
Formula: \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\), then check \(Rp_1+t=q_1\).
Hint: a pass means the residual is \(0\) and \(\det R=+1\).

\(t=\bar q-R\bar p=(0.5,2)-\begin{bmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}(1,0.5)=(0.5,2)-(-0.5,1)=(1,1)\)
check: \(Rp_1+t=R(0,0)+(1,1)=(1,1)=q_1\)
\(\boxed{\text{residual}=0,\ \det R=+1}\) — exact fit on clean data.

Closing test (limiting case). Verify that when \(H\) is symmetric positive-definite the solved rotation must be \(R=I\).
Formula: SPD \(H=U\Sigma U^T\), so \(V=U\); \(R=V\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,U^T\) with \(d=\det(VU^T)\).
Hint: a pass is \(R=UU^T=I\); substitute \(V=U\) and confirm \(d=+1\).

SPD \(H\) has eigendecomposition \(H=U\Sigma U^T\), so \(V=U\)
\(d=\det(UU^T)=+1\), so \(R=U\,I\,U^T=UU^T=I\)
\(\boxed{R=I}\) — already-aligned centered clouds need no rotation.

Closing test (convention check). A colleague computes the swapped \(H'=\sum_i\tilde q_i\tilde p_i^T\). What rotation does the solve return, and how do you fix it?
Formula: swapping gives \(H'=H^T\), which swaps \(U\leftrightarrow V\) in the SVD, so \(R'=R^T\).
Hint: a pass means recognizing \(R^T\) is the inverse (target-onto-source) rotation.

\(H'=H^T\Rightarrow\) SVD swaps \(U\leftrightarrow V\), so \(R'=U\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,d)\,V^T=R^T\)
\(R^T\) is the inverse rotation
\(\boxed{R'=R^T;\ \text{transpose it, or fix }H=\sum\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T}\).

What problem does Kabsch-Umeyama solve in closed form, and what is its one expensive step?
Formula: \(\min_{R\in SO(3),t}\sum_i\|Rp_i+t-q_i\|^2\) via \(H=\sum_i\tilde p_i\tilde q_i^T\).
Hint: name the matrix decomposition done on \(H\).

optimal rigid \((R,t)\) minimizing \(\sum_i\|Rp_i+t-q_i\|^2\) for known correspondences
\(\boxed{\text{one }3\times3\text{ SVD of }H}\).

Why does ICP only reach a local minimum?
Hint: think about what it alternates between and whether either step can increase the error.
it is coordinate descent on (correspondences, transform); each step is non-increasing but the fixed point depends entirely on the start pose.

When do you pick point-to-plane over point-to-point ICP?
Formula: point-to-plane cost \(\sum_i\big(n_i^T(Rp_i+t-q_i)\big)^2\).
Hint: note what the normal \(n_i\) lets the surfaces do tangentially.
when reliable target normals exist; penalizing only the along-normal distance lets surfaces slide tangentially and converges in far fewer iterations.

What does Go-ICP guarantee that plain ICP cannot?
Hint: think about searching all of \(SE(3)\) with bounds rather than from one start pose.
globally optimal rigid registration via branch-and-bound over \(SE(3)\) with L2 bounds, needing no initial guess.

Degeneracy guard: which geometries leave the rotation under-constrained, and what do you check?
Formula: inspect the singular values of \(H\): \(\sigma_2-\sigma_3\) and \(\sigma_3\), both relative to \(\sigma_1\).
Hint: a near-zero gap means the rotation about that axis is free.
spheres, planes, straight lines, surfaces of revolution; check the gaps \(\sigma_2-\sigma_3\) and \(\sigma_3\) relative to \(\sigma_1\).

Eigen and PCL entry points for the rigid solve and full ICP.
Hint: one closed-form call for matched points, one iterative class for raw clouds.
`Eigen::umeyama(src, dst, false)` for the closed-form rigid fit (points as columns); `pcl::IterativeClosestPoint` then `getFinalTransformation()` for iterative ICP.
The optimal translation always maps the source centroid onto the {{c1::target centroid}}, giving \(t=\bar q-R\bar p\).
Without the \(d=\det(VU^T)\) term the solve can return a {{c1::reflection}} with \(\det R=-1\) on noisy or planar data.

Point-to-plane ICP linearizes the rotation as \(R\approx I+{{c1::[r]_\times}}\) with \(r\) a small-angle vector.
hint: the skew-symmetric cross-product matrix of \(r\).
The ICP increment must {{c1::left}}-multiply (\(T\leftarrow\Delta T\,T\)) because the correction lives in the current frame and \(SE(3)\) does not commute.
ICP convergence is tested relative to {{c1::object size}} (\(\varepsilon\cdot L\)), never a fixed absolute tolerance across scales.

Derive the edge count \(E\) for a closed triangle mesh by counting face-edge incidences two ways.
Formula: \(3F = 2E\) (each face has 3 edges; each interior edge is shared by 2 faces)
Hint: Write the total incidences as \(3F\) from the face side and \(2E\) from the edge side, then equate.

Each triangular face has 3 edges, so total face-edge incidences \(= 3F\)
Every interior edge is shared by exactly 2 faces, so the same incidences \(= 2E\)
Equate the two counts: \(3F = 2E\)
\(\boxed{E = \tfrac{3}{2}F}\)

For a closed genus-0 triangle mesh, derive \(F\) and \(E\) in terms of \(V\).
Formula: \(E=\tfrac32 F\) and \(\chi=V-E+F=2\); target \(F=2V-4,\ E=3V-6\)
Hint: Substitute \(E=\tfrac32 F\) into the Euler equation first, then solve for \(F\).

Substitute \(E=\tfrac32 F\) into \(\chi=V-E+F=2\): \(V-\tfrac32 F+F=2\)
\(V-\tfrac12 F=2 \Rightarrow F=2V-4\)
Then \(E=\tfrac32 F=\tfrac32(2V-4)=3V-6\)
\(\boxed{F=2V-4,\quad E=3V-6}\)

Derive the genus \(g\) as a function of \(\chi\) for a closed orientable surface.
Formula: \(\chi = 2 - 2g\); target \(g=\tfrac{2-\chi}{2}\)
Hint: Isolate the \(2g\) term, then divide by 2.

Start from the closed-surface relation \(\chi = 2 - 2g\)
Solve for \(g\): \(2g = 2 - \chi\)
\(\boxed{g = \dfrac{2 - \chi}{2}}\)

A tetrahedron has \(V=4\), \(F=4\) and is closed. Compute \(\chi\).
Formula: \(E=\tfrac32 F\), then \(\chi=V-E+F\)
Hint: Get \(E\) from \(\tfrac32 F\) first, then plug \(V,E,F\) into the Euler sum.

The tetrahedron has \(\chi=2\). Compute its genus.
Formula: \(g=\tfrac{2-\chi}{2}\)
Hint: Substitute \(\chi=2\) directly.

A regular tetrahedron has equilateral faces with 3 faces meeting at each vertex. Compute the angle defect \(d_i\) at one vertex.
Formula: \(d_i = 2\pi - \sum_{f\ni i}\theta_i^f\), with each \(\theta_i^f=\tfrac{\pi}{3}\)
Hint: Sum the three \(\tfrac{\pi}{3}\) interior angles first, then subtract from \(2\pi\).

Each interior angle of an equilateral triangle is \(\theta=\pi/3\)
The angle sum at the vertex is \(\sum_{f\ni i}\theta_i^f = 3\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{3}=\pi\)
Defect \(d_i=2\pi-\sum\theta_i^f=2\pi-\pi\)
\(\boxed{d_i=\pi}\)

Each of the 4 tetrahedron vertices has defect \(d_i=\pi\) and \(\chi=2\). Find the total curvature and check Gauss-Bonnet.
Formula: \(\sum_i d_i = 2\pi\chi\)
Hint: Add the four defects, then compare with \(2\pi\chi\).

Sum over all 4 vertices: \(\sum_i d_i = 4\cdot\pi=4\pi\)
Gauss-Bonnet predicts \(2\pi\chi=2\pi(2)=4\pi\)
They agree, confirming total curvature is set by topology
\(\boxed{\sum_i d_i = 2\pi\chi = 4\pi}\)

A regular icosahedron has 5 equilateral faces meeting at each vertex. Derive the angle defect per vertex.
Formula: \(d_i = 2\pi - \sum_{f\ni i}\theta_i^f\), with each \(\theta_i^f=\tfrac{\pi}{3}\)
Hint: Compute the angle sum \(5\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{3}\), then subtract from \(2\pi\) over a common denominator.

Interior angle of an equilateral face \(\theta=\pi/3\)
Five faces meet, so angle sum \(=5\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{3}=\tfrac{5\pi}{3}\)
\(d_i=2\pi-\tfrac{5\pi}{3}=\tfrac{6\pi-5\pi}{3}\)
\(\boxed{d_i=\tfrac{\pi}{3}}\)

Delete one face of the tetrahedron (open/disk case), leaving \(V=4,\ E=6,\ F=3\). Compute \(\chi\).
Formula: \(\chi=V-E+F\); cross-check with \(\chi=2-2g-b\)
Hint: Plug \(V,E,F\) into the Euler sum, then verify with \(g=0,\ b=1\).

Deleting a face removes a face but keeps all vertices and edges: \(V=4,\ E=6,\ F=3\)
\(\chi=V-E+F=4-6+3\)
Cross-check with \(\chi=2-2g-b\) for \(g=0\), one boundary loop \(b=1\): \(2-0-1=1\)
\(\boxed{\chi=1}\) (a topological disk)

Generalize the closed-surface Euler relation to surfaces with boundary.
Formula: closed: \(\chi=2-2g\); target with boundary: \(\chi=2-2g-b\)
Hint: Each open boundary loop is one missing disk-cap; a disk contributes \(\chi=1\), so removing \(b\) caps subtracts \(b\).

A closed genus-\(g\) surface has \(\chi=2-2g\)
Each open boundary loop is a missing cap; removing one disk lowers \(\chi\) by 1 (a disk has \(\chi=1\))
Removing \(b\) caps subtracts \(b\)
\(\boxed{\chi=2-2g-b}\)

Worked example (large mesh). A closed triangle mesh reports \(V=1000,\ F=2000\). Find \(E\), \(\chi\), \(g\).
Formula: \(E=\tfrac32 F\), \(\chi=V-E+F\), \(g=\tfrac{2-\chi}{2}\)
Hint: Compute \(E\) from \(\tfrac32 F\) first, then \(\chi\), then \(g\).

Worked example (small mesh, octahedron). \(V=6,\ F=8\), closed. Find \(E\), \(\chi\), \(g\).
Formula: \(E=\tfrac32 F\), \(\chi=V-E+F\), \(g=\tfrac{2-\chi}{2}\)
Hint: Get \(E=\tfrac32(8)\) first, then the Euler sum, then the genus.

Worked example (open slab patch). A scanned slab triangulates to \(V=50,\ E=129,\ F=80\) with one boundary loop. Find \(\chi\) and the implied genus.
Formula: \(\chi=V-E+F\), then open-mesh \(\chi=2-2g-b\) with \(b=1\)
Hint: Compute \(\chi\) from the counts, then solve \(1=2-2g-1\) for \(g\).

\(\chi=V-E+F=50-129+80=1\)
Open mesh, so use \(\chi=2-2g-b\) with \(b=1\): \(1=2-2g-1\)
\(2g=0 \Rightarrow g=0\)
\(\boxed{\chi=1,\ g=0}\) (a flat disk, as expected for a slab)

Worked example (regime check, fine sphere scan). A scanned boulder mesh has \(V=100000\) and is genus 0. Predict \(F\), \(E\), and the total curvature.
Formula: \(F=2V-4\), \(E=3V-6\), \(\sum d_i=2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi=2\)
Hint: Plug \(V\) into the closed genus-0 size formulas; note the curvature does not depend on \(V\).

Closed genus-0: \(F=2V-4=2(100000)-4=199996\)
\(E=3V-6=3(100000)-6=299994\)
Total curvature \(\sum d_i=2\pi\chi=2\pi(2)=4\pi\) regardless of resolution
\(\boxed{F=199996,\ E=299994,\ \sum d_i=4\pi}\)

Test yourself (close the loop). You computed \(g=1\) for \(V=1000,\ F=2000\). Verify it.
Formula: \(\chi=2-2g\) should match \(\chi=V-E+F\) (with \(E=3000\))
Hint: A pass means both routes give the same \(\chi\). Compute each side and compare.

Plug \(g=1\) into \(\chi=2-2g=2-2(1)=0\)
Independently \(\chi=V-E+F=1000-3000+2000=0\)
Both give \(\chi=0\), so the genus-1 (torus) classification is consistent
\(\boxed{\chi=0 \text{ both ways} \Rightarrow g=1 \text{ verified}}\)

Test yourself (handshake lemma). For the tetrahedron with \(E=6\), verify the degree sum.
Formula: \(\sum_i \deg(i)=2E\)
Hint: A pass means the degree sum equals \(2E\). Each vertex connects to the other 3, so add up the degrees.

Each of the 4 vertices connects to the other 3, so \(\deg(i)=3\) for all \(i\)
\(\sum_i \deg(i)=4\cdot 3=12\)
Check: \(2E=2\cdot 6=12\)
\(\boxed{\sum_i\deg(i)=2E=12 \checkmark}\)

Test yourself (limit check on Gauss-Bonnet). For a closed genus-0 mesh, confirm \(\sum d_i\) is dimensionless and equals \(4\pi\) regardless of \(V\).
Formula: \(d_i=2\pi-\sum\theta_i^f\) and \(\sum_i d_i=2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi=2-2g\)
Hint: A pass means no length/count symbol survives. Check the units of \(d_i\), then evaluate \(2\pi\chi\) at \(g=0\).

\(d_i=2\pi-\sum\theta_i^f\) is a sum of radians (dimensionless angles)
\(\sum_i d_i=2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi=2-2(0)=2\) gives \(4\pi\)
No \(V\), \(E\), or length appears, so refinement leaves it fixed
\(\boxed{\sum d_i=4\pi \text{ for all genus-0 closed meshes}}\)

Construct the operator that visits all outgoing half-edges of vertex \(v\), given only \(\mathrm{next}\) and \(\mathrm{twin}\).
Formula: target \(\mathrm{rotateCW}(h)=\mathrm{next}(\mathrm{twin}(h))\)
Hint: Start from an outgoing \(h\); apply \(\mathrm{twin}\) to point back into \(v\), then \(\mathrm{next}\) to step CCW around the next face.

From an outgoing half-edge \(h\) (tail at \(v\)), \(\mathrm{twin}(h)\) points back into \(v\) along the same edge
\(\mathrm{next}(\mathrm{twin}(h))\) is the next half-edge CCW around the neighboring face, again outgoing from \(v\)
Iterating returns to \(h\) after \(\deg(v)\) steps
\(\boxed{\mathrm{rotateCW}(h)=\mathrm{next}(\mathrm{twin}(h))}\)

What three sets define a triangle mesh as a combinatorial object?
Hint: The triple is written \(M=(V,E,F)\).

When is the formula \(g=(2-\chi)/2\) invalid, and what replaces it?
Formula: replacement \(\chi=2-2g-b\)
Hint: Think about what changes when the mesh has an edge that touches only one face.
It is invalid on an open mesh (with boundary); use \(\chi=2-2g-b\) where \(b\) is the number of boundary loops.

How do you distinguish a scan hole from a real handle, since both lower \(\chi\)?
Hint: Check the boundary loops returned by `boundary_loop`.
A hole has a boundary loop (fill it); a handle has no boundary loop (real tunnel, keep it).

Define an edge-manifold mesh, and say why it matters before a Laplacian solve.
Hint: State the allowed face-counts per edge, then link to twin pairing.
Every edge is incident to exactly 1 face (boundary) or exactly 2 faces (interior); non-manifold edges break twin pairing and make the Laplacian asymmetric.
A mesh is data not a picture: its value lives in the {{c1::connectivity}} (who is adjacent to whom), separate from the geometric embedding \(\mathbf{p}: V \to \mathbb{R}^3\).
Geometry · Mesh Topology
The twin pointer is an involution: \(\mathrm{twin}(\mathrm{twin}(h))={{c1::h}}\) with \(\mathrm{twin}(h)\neq h\).
Geometry · Mesh Topology

A boundary edge is a half-edge whose {{c1::twin has no incident face}}.
hint: equivalently, the edge is used by only one face.
Geometry · Mesh Topology
For a surface with boundary the generalized Euler relation is \(\chi = {{c1::2 - 2g - b}}\), where \(b\) counts boundary loops.
Geometry · Mesh Topology

Derive the angle defect \(K_i\) at an interior vertex, starting from the flat-case reference that the tip angles around a flat vertex make a full turn.
Formula: start from \(\sum_{ijk}\phi_i^{jk}=2\pi\) (flat) and target \(K_i = 2\pi - \sum_{ijk}\phi_i^{jk}\)
Hint: write the actual angle sum as the flat value minus a shortfall \(K_i\), then solve for \(K_i\).

A flat (developable) vertex tiles a full disc: \(\sum_{ijk}\phi_i^{jk}=2\pi\)
a cone is missing a wedge, so the actual sum falls short by the defect: \(\sum_{ijk}\phi_i^{jk}=2\pi-K_i\)
solve for the shortfall
\(\boxed{K_i = 2\pi - \sum_{ijk}\phi_i^{jk}}\).

Show why \(K_i\) is the *integrated* Gaussian curvature and derive the pointwise density \(\kappa_i\) from it.
Formula: \(K_i=\int_{A_i}K\,dA\) and target \(\kappa_i \approx K_i/A_i\)
Hint: treat \(K\) as roughly constant over the small dual cell, so the integral becomes \(\kappa_i A_i\); then divide by \(A_i\).

\(K_i\) is a number attached to the dual cell of \(i\), i.e. \(K_i=\int_{A_i}K\,dA\) (a discrete 2-form, units of angle)
assume \(K\) roughly constant over the small cell: \(\int_{A_i}K\,dA \approx \kappa_i\,A_i\)
divide by the dual area \(A_i\)
\(\boxed{\kappa_i \approx K_i / A_i}\).

Derive the per-face angle-sum total used in Gauss-Bonnet (step 1 of 3).
Formula: each triangle's interior angles sum to \(\pi\); target \(\sum_{\text{faces}}(\text{angles}) = \pi F\)
Hint: just sum the per-triangle value \(\pi\) over all \(F\) faces.

Each triangle has interior angles summing to \(\pi\)
sum over all \(F\) faces
\(\boxed{\sum_{\text{faces}} (\text{3 interior angles}) = \pi F}\).

Continue the Gauss-Bonnet derivation (step 2 of 3): eliminate the angle sum.
Formula: \(\sum_i K_i = \sum_i\big(2\pi-\sum\phi\big)\) and \(\sum_F(\text{angles})=\pi F\); target \(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi V - \pi F\)
Hint: the total of all interior angles is the same whether you group by vertex or by face, so substitute \(\pi F\) for \(\sum\phi\).

Every interior angle belongs to exactly one vertex's defect sum and one face's angle sum, so the two total angle sums are equal
\(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi V - \sum_{\text{all angles}}\phi = 2\pi V - \pi F\)
\(\boxed{\sum_i K_i = 2\pi V - \pi F}\).

Finish Gauss-Bonnet (step 3 of 3): reach \(\chi\) from the angle/face count.
Formula: \(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi V - \pi F\) with closed-mesh relation \(3F=2E\); target \(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi\chi\), \(\chi=V-E+F\)
Hint: use \(3F=2E\) to rewrite \(\pi F = 2\pi(E-F)\), then substitute and factor \(2\pi\).

Closed triangle mesh: each face has 3 edges, each edge shared by 2 faces, so \(3F=2E\), i.e. \(\pi F = 2\pi(E-F)\)
substitute: \(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi V - 2\pi(E-F) = 2\pi(V-E+F)\)
\(\boxed{\sum_i K_i = 2\pi\,\chi(M)},\quad \chi = V-E+F\).

Construct the scalar (integrated) mean curvature \(H_i\) at a vertex from dihedral angles and edge lengths.
Formula: per-edge bend \(\theta_{ij}\lVert e_{ij}\rVert\); target \(H_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{ij}\theta_{ij}\,\lVert e_{ij}\rVert\)
Hint: sum the per-edge term over incident edges, then halve because each edge is shared by two vertices.

Each incident edge bends by signed dihedral angle \(\theta_{ij}\) over length \(\lVert e_{ij}\rVert\), contributing curvature \(\theta_{ij}\lVert e_{ij}\rVert\)
each edge is shared by two vertices, so split its contribution in half
sum over incident edges
\(\boxed{H_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{ij}\theta_{ij}\,\lVert e_{ij}\rVert}\).

From the continuous identity \(\Delta\mathbf{x}=2H\mathbf{n}\), write the discrete cotan mean-curvature normal it becomes.
Formula: cotan weights \(\tfrac{1}{2}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\) with \(\alpha_{ij},\beta_{ij}\) opposite edge \(ij\); target \(HN_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{ij}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\,e_{ij}\)
Hint: replace the Laplacian of position by its cotan-weighted one-ring sum, each weight multiplying the edge vector \(e_{ij}=x_j-x_i\).

Discretize the Laplacian of position over the one-ring with cotan weights; \(\alpha_{ij},\beta_{ij}\) are the angles opposite edge \(ij\) in its two triangles
\(\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\mathbf{x}\) collects \(\tfrac{1}{2}(\cot\alpha+\cot\beta)\) per edge times the edge vector
\(\boxed{HN_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{ij}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\,e_{ij}}\).

Derive the pointwise mean-curvature magnitude \(H_i\) from \(HN_i\).
Formula: \(\lVert HN_i\rVert \approx 2H_i\,A_i\) (since \(HN_i\) integrates \(2H\mathbf{n}\) over the dual cell); target \(H_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert HN_i\rVert/A_i\)
Hint: take the magnitude of \(HN_i\) and divide out the \(2A_i\) to isolate \(H_i\).

\(HN_i\) integrates \(2H\mathbf{n}\) over the dual cell, so \(\lVert HN_i\rVert \approx 2H_i\,A_i\)
solve for \(H_i\)
\(\boxed{H_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lVert HN_i\rVert / A_i}\).

Derive the barycentric dual area \(A_i^{\text{bary}}\) from splitting each triangle's area equally among its 3 vertices.
Formula: each incident face gives \(\tfrac{1}{3}A_{ijk}\); target \(A_i^{\text{bary}} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{ijk}A_{ijk}\)
Hint: sum the one-third share over every face incident to \(i\).

Triangle \(ijk\) has area \(A_{ijk}\); give each of its 3 vertices an equal third
vertex \(i\) collects one third from every incident face
\(\boxed{A_i^{\text{bary}} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{ijk}A_{ijk}}\).

State the sign convention of the angle defect and the flat-reference relation it starts from.
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - \sum\phi_i^{jk}\), flat reference \(\sum\phi=2\pi\)
Hint: ask whether the actual angle sum falls short of, equals, or exceeds \(2\pi\).

Given (flat reference): \(\sum\phi_i^{jk}=2\pi\) for a developable vertex
shortfall \(2\pi-\sum\phi>0\) means a convex cone, \(<0\) means a saddle (too much angle), \(=0\) means flat
\(\boxed{K_i>0\ \text{cone},\ K_i<0\ \text{saddle},\ K_i=0\ \text{developable}}\).

Worked example (convex apex, radians). Four isosceles triangles meet at a pyramid apex, each tip angle \(\phi=80^\circ=1.3963\) rad. Compute \(K_i\).
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - \sum\phi = 2\pi - 4\phi\)
Hint: multiply the tip angle by 4, then subtract from \(2\pi=6.2832\).

Worked example (saddle regime). Same four-triangle vertex but each tip angle is now \(\phi=100^\circ=1.7453\) rad. Compute \(K_i\) and name the sign.
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - 4\phi\)
Hint: compute \(4\phi\) first; if it exceeds \(2\pi\) the defect goes negative.

\(K_i = 2\pi - 4(1.7453) = 6.2832 - 6.9813\)
\(\boxed{K_i \approx -0.6981\ \text{rad}}\)
negative defect = saddle: the angles overflow a full turn.

Worked example (pointwise density, m scale). For the convex apex \(K_i = 0.6981\) rad with dual area \(A_i = 0.5\ \text{m}^2\), give \(\kappa_i\) and its units.
Formula: \(\kappa_i = K_i / A_i\)
Hint: divide the defect by the area; the units are (radians are dimensionless) inverse area.

\(\kappa_i = K_i / A_i = 0.6981 / 0.5\)
\(\boxed{\kappa_i \approx 1.40\ \text{m}^{-2}}\)
units of inverse-length-squared, the Gaussian-curvature density.

Worked example (mm vs m, mean curvature of a sphere). A fillet locally matches a sphere of radius \(R\); mean curvature is \(H=1/R\). Compare a coarse arris \(R=2\ \text{m}\) with a fine ground edge \(R=50\ \text{mm}\).
Formula: \(H = 1/R\)
Hint: convert \(50\ \text{mm}\) to \(0.05\ \text{m}\) first so both radii are in meters, then take reciprocals.

Coarse: \(H = 1/2 = 0.5\ \text{m}^{-1}\)
fine: \(R=0.05\ \text{m}\Rightarrow H = 1/0.05 = 20\ \text{m}^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{H_{\text{fine}}/H_{\text{coarse}} = 40\times}\) — tighter rounding reads as far higher mean curvature.

Closing test (tetrahedron). Predict \(\sum_i K_i\) from per-vertex defects, then verify against Gauss-Bonnet.
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - 3(\pi/3)\) per vertex; check \(2\pi\chi\), \(\chi=V-E+F\)
Hint: a pass means the 4-vertex sum equals \(2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi=4-6+4=2\).

Each vertex: 3 equilateral triangles, tip \(60^\circ=\pi/3\), so \(K_i = 2\pi - 3(\pi/3) = 2\pi-\pi = \pi\)
sum over 4 vertices: \(4\pi\)
verify: \(\chi=V-E+F=4-6+4=2\), so \(2\pi\chi = 4\pi\)
\(\boxed{4\pi = 4\pi\ \checkmark}\).

Closing test (icosahedron). 12 vertices, 5 equilateral triangles per vertex. Compute one defect, the total, and check Gauss-Bonnet.
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - 5(\pi/3)\); total \(=12K_i\); check \(2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi=2\)
Hint: a pass means \(12K_i\) matches \(4\pi\) regardless of the triangulation.

Closing test (limiting case). Show the angle-defect formula gives the right answer when the four pyramid triangles are coplanar (flatten the cone).
Formula: \(K_i = 2\pi - 4\phi\) with \(\phi=\pi/2\) when flat
Hint: a pass means the defect collapses to exactly \(0\) for the flat case.

Coplanar = each tip angle \(90^\circ=\pi/2\)
\(K_i = 2\pi - 4(\pi/2) = 2\pi - 2\pi\)
\(\boxed{K_i = 0}\) — a flat interior vertex has zero defect, the developable limit. \(\checkmark\)

Why use `atan2` rather than `acos` to compute the signed dihedral angle \(\theta_{ij}\)?
Formula: \(\theta_{ij} = \operatorname{atan2}\!\big((e_{ij}/\lVert e_{ij}\rVert)\cdot(N_{ijk}\times N_{ijl}),\ N_{ijk}\cdot N_{ijl}\big)\)
Hint: compare the output ranges of the two functions and ask which keeps a sign.

`acos` returns only \([0,\pi]\) and loses sign
`atan2` of (cross-product-along-edge, dot-of-normals) returns the full \((-\pi,\pi]\) and keeps the sign
\(\boxed{\text{sign separates convex ridges from concave valleys}}\).

When is the mixed (Meyer) dual area required instead of raw circumcentric area, and what does it substitute?
Formula: \(A_i \mathrel{+}= \tfrac{1}{2}\text{area}(T)\) if obtuse at \(i\), \(\tfrac{1}{4}\text{area}(T)\) if obtuse elsewhere, else the Voronoi region
Hint: recall that the circumcenter leaves the triangle when it is obtuse, which is what makes the raw area go negative.

Raw Voronoi area goes negative on obtuse triangles (circumcenter falls outside), flipping the sign of \(\kappa_i=K_i/A_i\)
mixed area uses Voronoi when non-obtuse, else \(\tfrac{1}{2}\text{area}(T)\) if obtuse at \(i\), \(\tfrac{1}{4}\text{area}(T)\) otherwise
\(\boxed{\text{always positive, the production choice for sliver-ridden scans}}\).

Pitfall recall: name two code guards that keep the curvature operators numerically safe on scan meshes.
Formula: clamp dot to \([-1,1]\) before `acos`; floor \(\sin=\lVert u\times w\rVert\) at \(\varepsilon\) before dividing in \(\cot=\cos/\sin\)
Hint: one guard protects the `acos` domain, the other protects the `cot` divide on slivers.

Clamp the dot product to \([-1,1]\) before `acos` (kills NaN from fp drift)
floor the `sin` (cross-product magnitude) at a small \(\varepsilon\) before dividing in `cot`
\(\boxed{\text{guard }acos\text{ domain and }cot\text{ slivers}}\).

Gauss-Bonnet makes the defect sum depend only on topology: \(\sum_i K_i = 2\pi\chi\) with \(\chi = {{c1::2-2g}}\).
hint: \(\chi = V-E+F\) written in terms of the genus \(g\).
Geometry · Discrete Curvature
\(K_i\) and \(H_i\) from the formulas are {{c1::integrated}} quantities; you must divide by \(A_i\) to get a pointwise {{c2::density}} comparable across mesh resolutions.
Geometry · Discrete Curvature

For a boundary vertex the angle defect is {{c1::\(\pi\)}} minus the sum of interior angles, not \(2\pi\).
hint: a boundary vertex sees only a half-disc instead of a full turn.
Geometry · Discrete Curvature
The cotan mean-curvature normal \(HN_i\) is exactly one row of the {{c1::cotangent Laplacian}}, which is why this topic unlocks mean-curvature flow.
Geometry · Discrete Curvature
Normalize a weighted vertex normal {{c1::after}} summing the face normals, never before, or you discard the weighting.
Geometry · Discrete Curvature

Derive the cotangent of the angle \(\alpha\) at the vertex opposite an edge, written using only the two edge vectors \(\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\) from that vertex (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(\cot\alpha=\dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha}\), \(\ \mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}=\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\cos\alpha\), \(\ \|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|=\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\sin\alpha\); target \(\cot\alpha=\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|}\)
Hint: divide the dot-product identity by the cross-product identity so \(\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\) cancels.

\(\cot\alpha=\dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha}\)
divide the two identities so \(\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\) cancels
\(\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|}=\dfrac{\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\cos\alpha}{\|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\sin\alpha}\)
\(\boxed{\cot\alpha=\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|}}\)

Derive the cotan edge weight \(w_{ij}\) for an interior edge \((i,j)\) shared by two triangles (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: each triangle contributes a half-cotangent \(\tfrac12\cot(\cdot)\); target \(w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\)
Hint: add the half-cotangent from the \(\alpha\)-side triangle to the half-cotangent from the \(\beta\)-side triangle.

triangle on the \(\alpha\) side gives \(\tfrac12\cot\alpha_{ij}\)
triangle on the \(\beta\) side gives \(\tfrac12\cot\beta_{ij}\)
sum the two faces sharing the edge
\(\boxed{w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})}\)

Derive the cotangent Laplacian at vertex \(i\) in fully expanded form (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \((L\mathbf{f})_i=\sum_{j\in N(i)} w_{ij}(f_j-f_i)\) with \(w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\)
Hint: substitute the edge weight into the sum and pull the \(\tfrac12\) out front.

substitute the edge weight into the sum
\((L\mathbf{f})_i=\sum_{j\in N(i)}\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})(f_j-f_i)\)
