\(\boxed{(L\mathbf{f})_i=\tfrac12\sum_{j\in N(i)}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})(f_j-f_i)}\)

Derive the pointwise Laplace-Beltrami value \((\Delta f)_i\) from the integrated form (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \((L\mathbf{f})_i=\tfrac12\sum_{j}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})(f_j-f_i)\) and \(\Delta f=M^{-1}L\mathbf{f}\) with \(M_{ii}=A_i\)
Hint: \(M\) is diagonal, so \(M^{-1}\) just divides row \(i\) by the dual area \(A_i\).

\(M\) is diagonal so \((\Delta f)_i=\tfrac{1}{A_i}(L\mathbf{f})_i\)
divide the integrated sum by the dual area \(A_i\)
\(\boxed{(\Delta f)_i=\dfrac{1}{2A_i}\sum_{j\in N(i)}(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})(f_j-f_i)}\)

Derive the diagonal entry \(L_{ii}\) of the stiffness matrix from the requirement that constant functions vanish.
Formula: \((L\mathbf{f})_i=L_{ii}f_i+\sum_{j\in N(i)} L_{ij}f_j\) with off-diagonals \(L_{ij}=+w_{ij}\), and the constraint \((L\mathbf{1})_i=0\)
Hint: set \(f_i=f_j=1\) and solve the resulting equation for \(L_{ii}\).

\((L\mathbf{f})_i=L_{ii}f_i+\sum_{j\in N(i)} w_{ij} f_j\)
a constant \(f\equiv1\) must give \(0\): \(L_{ii}+\sum_j w_{ij}=0\)
\(\boxed{L_{ii}=-\sum_{j\in N(i)} w_{ij}}\) (negative diagonal, rows sum to zero)

Derive the discrete Poisson system from the continuous equation \(\Delta u=\rho\).
Formula: \((\Delta u)_i=\tfrac{1}{A_i}(L\mathbf{u})_i\), \(\ A_i=M_{ii}\); target \(L\mathbf{u}=M\boldsymbol\rho\)
Hint: set the pointwise Laplacian equal to \(\rho_i\), then clear the \(\tfrac{1}{A_i}\) by multiplying through by \(A_i\).

Derive the solvability (compatibility) condition for the Poisson system \(L\mathbf{u}=M\boldsymbol\rho\).
Formula: \(L\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0}\), \(L=L^\top\); target \(\sum_i A_i\rho_i=0\)
Hint: left-multiply both sides by \(\mathbf{1}^\top\) and use \(\mathbf{1}^\top L=(L\mathbf{1})^\top=\mathbf{0}\) to kill the left side.

\(\mathbf{1}^\top L\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{1}^\top M\boldsymbol\rho\)
\(L\) symmetric and \(L\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0}\) give \(\mathbf{1}^\top L=\mathbf{0}\)
so the left side is \(0\)
\(\boxed{\mathbf{1}^\top M\boldsymbol\rho=\sum_i A_i\rho_i=0}\) (source must integrate to zero)

Derive the implicit (backward-Euler) smoothing step (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: flow \(\dot{\mathbf{V}}=M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}\); backward Euler evaluates the right side at the NEW state: \(\dfrac{\mathbf{V}^{k+1}-\mathbf{V}^k}{\delta}=M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}^{k+1}\)
Hint: multiply through by \(\delta\) and gather both \(\mathbf{V}^{k+1}\) terms on the left.

Derive the symmetric SPD smoothing system (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\mathbf{V}^{k+1}-\delta M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}^{k+1}=\mathbf{V}^k\); target \((M-\delta L)\mathbf{V}^{k+1}=M\mathbf{V}^k\)
Hint: left-multiply the whole equation by \(M\) to clear \(M^{-1}\), then factor \(\mathbf{V}^{k+1}\).

\(M\mathbf{V}^{k+1}-\delta L\mathbf{V}^{k+1}=M\mathbf{V}^k\)
factor the unknown on the left
\(\boxed{(M-\delta L)\,\mathbf{V}^{k+1}=M\mathbf{V}^k}\) (\(-L\) is positive, so \(M-\delta L\) is SPD for any \(\delta\))

Worked example (small, 2D). For the triangle \(P_i=(0,0)\), \(P_j=(4,0)\), \(P_k=(0,3)\), compute the half-weight from the angle at \(P_k\) opposite edge \((i,j)\).
Formula: \(\mathbf{u}=P_i-P_k\), \(\mathbf{v}=P_j-P_k\), \(\ \cot\alpha=\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|}\), \(\ w^{\text{half}}=\tfrac12\cot\alpha\); 2D cross \(=|u_xv_y-u_yv_x|\)
Hint: first form the two edge vectors from \(P_k\), then plug into the dot and cross.

Worked example (two-triangle quad). Mirror the triangle above with \(P_{k'}=(0,-3)\) so edge \((i,j)\) is interior with \(\cot\beta_{ij}=0.75\) by symmetry. Find the full weight and \((\Delta f)_i\).
Formula: \(w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\); libigl \(L_{ij}=+w_{ij}\), \(L_{ii}=-w_{ij}\); \(\ (\Delta f)_i=\tfrac{1}{A_i}(L\mathbf{f})_i\) with \(A_i=4\)
Hint: compute \(w_{ij}\) first, then assemble the single-neighbor row \((L\mathbf{f})_i=w_{ij}(f_j-f_i)\) before dividing by \(A_i\).

Worked example (unit right triangle, Practice #1). For vertices \((0,0)\), \((1,0)\), \((0,1)\), give the single-triangle stiffness diagonals \(L_{00},L_{11},L_{22}\).
Formula: half-weight \(w=\tfrac12\cot(\text{opposite angle})\); \(\cot90^\circ=0\), \(\cot45^\circ=1\); diagonal \(L_{ii}=-\sum_{j}w_{ij}\) over edges at \(i\)
Hint: find the three corner angles first, convert each to its half-weight, then each diagonal is minus the sum of its two incident half-weights.

Worked example (equilateral, regular regime). On a mesh of equilateral triangles every angle opposite an interior edge is \(60^\circ\); find the interior edge weight.
Formula: \(\cot\theta=\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}\), \(\ w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\); \(\cos60^\circ=\tfrac12\), \(\sin60^\circ=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\)
Hint: evaluate \(\cot60^\circ\) first, then both opposite angles are equal so just double the half-cotangent.

Worked example (large scale, units). A quarry slab mesh in meters has mean edge length \(h\approx0.2\) m; pick the smoothing step and rescale it to millimeters.
Formula: \(\delta=\tfrac12 h^2\) (choose \(\delta\) relative to \(h^2\)); same physical mesh, \(1\text{ m}=1000\text{ mm}\)
Hint: compute \(\delta\) in \(\text{m}^2\) first, then redo with \(h=200\) mm to see \(\delta\) jump by \(1000^2\).

meters: \(\delta=\tfrac12(0.2)^2=0.02\ \text{m}^2\)
in mm, \(h=200\) mm so \(\delta=\tfrac12(200)^2=2.0\times10^4\ \text{mm}^2\)
\(\boxed{\delta\propto h^2\text{; choose it from mean edge length, never mix units}}\)

Test yourself (close the loop). For the unit right triangle stiffness row \(L_{00}=-1,L_{01}=0.5,L_{02}=0.5\), verify the row by applying it to the constant \(\mathbf{f}=(1,1,1)\).
Formula: \((L\mathbf{f})_0=L_{00}f_0+L_{01}f_1+L_{02}f_2\); a correct stiffness row satisfies \((L\mathbf{1})_0=0\)
Hint: a pass means the three entries sum to zero.

\((L\mathbf{f})_0=L_{00}\cdot1+L_{01}\cdot1+L_{02}\cdot1\)
\(=-1+0.5+0.5\)
\(=0\)
\(\boxed{(L\mathbf{1})_0=0\ \checkmark}\) constants are in the null space, as required

Test yourself (limiting case + units). Verify the pointwise Laplacian \(\Delta f=M^{-1}L\mathbf{f}\) has units of \(f\) per length\(^2\).
Formula: cotangents are dimensionless so \((L\mathbf{f})_i\sim\text{area}\cdot[f]\sim[f]\cdot\text{length}^2\); \(M_{ii}=A_i\sim\text{length}^2\)
Hint: a pass means dividing \((L\mathbf{f})\) by \(M\) leaves exactly \([f]/\text{length}^2\), matching the smooth \(\nabla^2 f\).

\(L\) entries (cotangents) are dimensionless, but \((L\mathbf{f})_i\approx \text{area}\cdot\Delta f\) carries one factor of area
\(M_{ii}=A_i\sim\text{length}^2\)
\(M^{-1}L\mathbf{f}\sim\dfrac{[f]\cdot\text{length}^2}{\text{length}^2\cdot\text{length}^2}=\dfrac{[f]}{\text{length}^2}\)
\(\boxed{[\Delta f]=[f]/\text{length}^2\ \checkmark}\) matching the smooth \(\nabla^2 f\)

Test yourself (sphere, analytic check). On a sphere of radius \(r=2\) m, check the discrete mean curvature against the closed form.
Formula: \(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{n}=-M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}\), row norm \(\|(M^{-1}L\mathbf{V})_i\|=2|H_i|\); analytic sphere \(|H|=1/r\)
Hint: a pass means the row norm clusters at \(2/r\), i.e. \(|H_i|\to1/r\), as the mesh refines.

analytic mean curvature of a sphere is \(|H|=1/r\)
\(|H|=1/2=0.5\ \text{m}^{-1}\)
the row norm \(\|(M^{-1}L\mathbf{V})_i\|=2|H_i|\) should concentrate at \(1.0\ \text{m}^{-1}\)
\(\boxed{|H_i|\to0.5\ \text{m}^{-1}\ \checkmark}\) as the mesh refines

When should you reach for the cotan operator in the scan->CAD->IFC pipeline?
Hint: think of the four jobs that share one matrix.

To smooth/fair a noisy scan before NURBS fitting, to compute the mean-curvature field for feature-aware segmentation, to solve harmonic/Poisson UV parameterizations, and as the stiffness/mass for the Signed Heat SDF.

Why does the Poisson right-hand side carry the mass matrix \(M\) (recall)?
Formula: \(L\mathbf{u}=M\boldsymbol\rho\)
Hint: compare the units of the two sides — \(L\mathbf{u}\) is already integrated over each dual cell.
\(M\) integrates the pointwise source \(\rho\) over each dual cell, matching the integrated left side \(L\mathbf{u}\); without it the units and scaling do not balance.

Pitfall: what is the single most common cotan-Laplacian bug, and the one-line check for it?
Formula: libigl uses \(-M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}\) and \(M-\delta L\); check \(L\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0}\)
Hint: a pass means a constant function maps to the zero vector.
A flipped sign convention. libigl \(L\) is negative semidefinite, so use \(-M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}\) and \(M-\delta L\); check that \(L\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0}\) or the surface inflates and explodes.

Pitfall: what goes wrong on a very obtuse triangle, and the fix?
Formula: \(w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})\)
Hint: ask what happens to \(\cot\theta\) as \(\theta\to180^\circ\).
Its cotangent is large and negative, so an edge weight can go negative and smoothing loses the maximum principle; use the Voronoi/mixed (obtuse-safe) mass area or an intrinsic Delaunay flip.
The cotan Laplacian splits into a {{c1::stiffness}} matrix \(L\) and a {{c2::mass}} matrix \(M\), with the pointwise operator equal to \(M^{-1}L\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl
In the libigl convention the cotmatrix is {{c1::negative semidefinite}}, so mean curvature carries a leading minus sign in \(-M^{-1}L\mathbf{V}\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl

The Poisson system \(L\mathbf{u}=M\boldsymbol\rho\) is singular because constants lie in the {{c1::null space}} of \(L\), so you pin a vertex or add a mean-zero constraint.
hint: \(L\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0}\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl
The cotangent of an angle from its two edge vectors is \(\cot\theta={{c1::\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}}/{{c2::\|\mathbf{u}\times\mathbf{v}\|}}\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl
Implicit fairing is stable for any time step because backward Euler solves the SPD system \(({{c1::M-\delta L}})\mathbf{V}'=M\mathbf{V}\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl
To assemble the operators in libigl you call {{c1::igl::cotmatrix}} for \(L\) and {{c2::igl::massmatrix}} for \(M\), then evaluate \(-M^{-1}LV\).

Geometry · Laplacian / Poisson
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl

Write the signed distance function \(\phi(\mathbf{x})\) to a closed oriented surface \(S\), with the sign convention stated.
Formula: \(\phi(\mathbf{x})=\pm\min_{\mathbf{y}\in S}\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert\)
Hint: take the nearest-point distance, then attach the sign by inside/outside (\(+\) outside, \(-\) inside).

Nearest-point distance is \(\min_{\mathbf{y}\in S}\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert\)
attach sign: \(+\) outside, \(-\) inside (fix the convention once)
\(\boxed{\phi(\mathbf{x})=\operatorname{sgn}\cdot\min_{\mathbf{y}\in S}\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert}\)

Derive the eikonal PDE for an exact SDF from the fact that \(\phi\) is arc-length along the shortest path to \(S\).
Formula: start from "\(\phi\) increases by \(ds\) per \(ds\) moved straight away from \(S\)"; target \(\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert=1,\ \phi|_S=0\).
Hint: the directional derivative away from the nearest point is exactly \(1\), and that direction is the max, so it equals \(\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert\).

Moving \(ds\) straight away from the nearest point increases \(\phi\) by exactly \(ds\)
so the directional derivative along that direction is \(1\) and is maximal, i.e. \(\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert\) equals that rate
with \(\phi=0\) on \(S\)
\(\boxed{\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert=1\ \text{a.e.},\quad \phi|_S=0}\)

From the heat equation \(\dot u=\Delta u\), derive the one-step backward-Euler system for a short diffusion time \(t\) (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: backward-Euler \(\frac{u_t-u_0}{t}=\Delta u_t\), \(u_0=\delta_\gamma\); target \((A-tL)u_t=\delta_\gamma\).
Hint: first move all \(u_t\) terms to one side, then discretize \(\Delta\to A^{-1}L\) and multiply through by \(A\).

Backward-Euler: \(\frac{u_t-u_0}{t}=\Delta u_t\) with \(u_0=\delta_\gamma\)
rearrange: \(u_t-t\,\Delta u_t=\delta_\gamma\)
discretize \(\Delta\to A^{-1}L\) and multiply by \(A\)
\(\boxed{(A-t\,L)\,u_t=\delta_\gamma}\)

Starting FROM the diffused heat \(u_t\) of \((A-tL)u_t=\delta_\gamma\), derive the unit direction field used by the heat method (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(X=-\dfrac{ abla u_t}{\lVert abla u_t Vert}\).
Hint: the gradient direction is right but the magnitude decays, so divide by \(\lVert abla u_t Vert\) and negate so \(X\) points away from the source.

\(u_t\) has the right gradient direction but a decaying/smeared magnitude
discard magnitude, keep direction by normalizing, and flip sign so it points away from the source
\(\boxed{X=-\dfrac{\nabla u_t}{\lVert\nabla u_t\rVert}}\)

For the SIGNED method, derive the backward-Euler vector-diffusion system starting from \(Y_t=e^{t\Delta}Y_0\) with \(Y_0\) the oriented surface normals.
Formula: \(Y_t=e^{t\Delta}Y_0\); target \((A-tL_{\text{vec}})Y_t=Y_0\).
Hint: approximate the propagator \(e^{t\Delta}\) by one implicit step \((I-t\Delta)^{-1}\), then discretize with the vector Laplacian \(L_{\text{vec}}\) and multiply by \(A\).

\(e^{t\Delta}\) is the heat propagator; one implicit step approximates it by \((I-t\Delta)^{-1}\) acting on \(Y_0\)
so \((I-t\Delta)Y_t=Y_0\) with the vector (connection) Laplacian
discretize and multiply by \(A\)
\(\boxed{(A-t\,L_{\text{vec}})\,Y_t=Y_0}\)

Given the diffused normal field \(Y_t\), derive the Stage-2 normalization that carries the sign.
Formula: \(X=\dfrac{Y_t}{\lVert Y_t\rVert}\).
Hint: the direction (outward outside, inward inside) is already correct; just strip the magnitude.

\(Y_t\) points outward on the outside and inward on the inside, but with the wrong length
strip magnitude to get a pure unit direction field
\(\boxed{X=\dfrac{Y_t}{\lVert Y_t\rVert}}\)

From the least-squares objective \(\phi=\arg\min_u\int_M\lVert abla u-X Vert^2\,dV\), derive the Euler-Lagrange equation (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: set the first variation \( rac{d}{d\epsilon}\int\lVert abla(u+\epsilon v)-X Vert^2\,dV ig|_0=0\); target \( abla\!\cdot abla\phi= abla\!\cdot X\).
Hint: differentiate to get \(2\int abla v\cdot( abla u-X)\,dV=0\), then integrate by parts (boundary terms cancel).

First variation: \( rac{d}{d\epsilon}\int\lVert abla(u+\epsilon v)-X Vert^2\,dV ig|_{0}=0\)
\(\Rightarrow 2\int abla v\cdot( abla u-X)\,dV=0\ orall v\)
integrate by parts (boundary terms cancel): \(\int v\, abla\!\cdot( abla u-X)\,dV=0\ orall v\)
\( oxed{ abla\!\cdot abla\phi= abla\!\cdot X}\)

Starting FROM \( abla\!\cdot abla\phi= abla\!\cdot X\), write the discrete Poisson solve for the SDF (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \( abla\!\cdot abla=\Delta\); target \(L\phi=\operatorname{div}X\).
Hint: rewrite \( abla\!\cdot abla\) as \(\Delta\), then discretize \(\Delta o L\) and \( abla\!\cdot X o\operatorname{div}X\).

\( abla\!\cdot abla=\Delta\), so \(\Delta\phi= abla\!\cdot X\)
discretize \(\Delta o L\) and \( abla\!\cdot X o\operatorname{div}X\)
\( oxed{L\,\phi=\operatorname{div}X}\)

Derive the \(t\propto h^2\) timestep scaling from dimensional analysis of \(tL\).
Formula: \(L\sim\Delta\) has units \(1/\text{length}^2\); require \(tL\) dimensionless. Target \(t=m\,h^2\).
Hint: set up the requirement that \(tL\) be unitless, solve for the units of \(t\), then use mean spacing \(h\) as the length scale.

\(L\) approximates \(\Delta\), units \(1/\text{length}^2\)
\(tL\) must be dimensionless in \((A-tL)\)
so \(t\) must carry units of \(\text{length}^2\); with mean spacing \(h\) the natural scale is \(h^2\)
\(\boxed{t=m\,h^2\ (m=\texttt{tCoef}\approx1)}\)

The source sits at \(x=2\) with \(+x\) normal on nodes \(x=0,1,2,3,4\). Derive the unit direction field \(X\) at the five nodes (derive, step 1 of 3).
Formula: signed field consistent with increasing-outward SDF: \(\partial\phi/\partial x<0\) left, \(=0\) at source, \(>0\) right, unit length.
Hint: set the left two nodes to \(-1\), the source to \(0\), the right two to \(+1\).

Away-from-surface points \(-x\) on the left, \(+x\) on the right; the source node is \(0\)
as a signed field consistent with an increasing-outward SDF, \(\partial\phi/\partial x<0\) left, \(>0\) right
\(\boxed{X=(-1,-1,0,+1,+1)}\)

Starting FROM \(X=(-1,-1,0,+1,+1)\) on \(x=0,1,2,3,4\), compute the divergence row \(\operatorname{div}X\) by central differences with \(h=1\) (derive, step 2 of 3).
Formula: \((\operatorname{div}X)_i=(X_{i+1}-X_{i-1})/2\).
Hint: plug in \(i=1,2,3\) using the neighbour values of \(X\).

Starting FROM the pinned constraint \(\phi_2=0\) and unit slope away from the source at \(x=2\), write the recovered 1D SDF and its slope (derive, step 3 of 3).
Formula: \(\phi(x)=x-2\) with \(\phi'=\pm1\) (sign flips across the source).
Hint: integrate slope \(\pm1\) outward from \(\phi_2=0\) to get the five node values.

Unit gradient \(\pm1\) away from \(x=2\), sign flips across the source, zero at the source
integrate slope outward from \(\phi_2=0\)
\(\boxed{\phi=(-2,-1,0,1,2)=x-2,\ \ \phi'=\pm1}\)

Worked example (mm shop frame). A shop scan has mean node spacing \(h=0.5\ \text{mm}\) and \(\texttt{tCoef}=1\). Compute the diffusion time \(t\).
Formula: \(t=\texttt{tCoef}\cdot h^2\).
Hint: square \(h\) first, then multiply by \(\texttt{tCoef}\).

Worked example (m site frame). A quarry scan has mean spacing \(h=0.2\ \text{m}\) and you set \(\texttt{tCoef}=10\) to bridge bigger holes. Compute \(t\).
Formula: \(t=\texttt{tCoef}\cdot h^2\).
Hint: compute \(h^2=0.04\ \text{m}^2\) first, then multiply by \(10\).

Worked example (sphere, 3D). A sphere of radius \(R=2\ \text{m}\) has analytic SDF \(\phi=r-R\). Evaluate \(\phi\) at \(r=0\) (center) and \(r=3\ \text{m}\).
Formula: \(\phi(r)=r-R\).
Hint: substitute each \(r\); a negative result means inside, positive means outside.

Worked example (divergence at a different scale). On a grid with \(h=2\), \(X=(-1,-1,0,1,1)\) at \(x=0,2,4,6,8\). Compute \(\operatorname{div}X\) at the source node \(x=4\).
Formula: \((\operatorname{div}X)_i=(X_{i+1}-X_{i-1})/(2h)\).
Hint: the source node's neighbours are \(X=+1\) (right) and \(X=-1\) (left); divide the difference by \(2h=4\).

Test yourself (close the loop). Take the recovered \(\phi=(-2,-1,0,1,2)\) from the 1D example and verify the interior Laplacian row \(i=2\) is consistent.
Formula: \((L\phi)_i=\phi_{i-1}-2\phi_i+\phi_{i+1}\).
Hint: a pass is \((L\phi)_2=0\) (a linear SDF has zero second derivative).

\((L\phi)_2=\phi_1-2\phi_2+\phi_3=(-1)-0+(1)=0\)
the linear SDF has zero second derivative; the kink in \(\operatorname{div}X\) at the source is what the solver lightly smooths
\(\boxed{(L\phi)_2=0\ \Rightarrow\ \phi\approx x-2\ \text{verified away from the kink}}\)

Test yourself (units + limiting case). For a sphere \(R=2\ ext{m}\), verify \(\phi=r-R\) obeys the eikonal equation and gives the right sign at \(r=R\).
Formula: eikonal \(\lVert abla\phi Vert=1\) with \( abla r=\hat{r}\); zero set check \(\phi(R)=0\).
Hint: a pass is \(\lVert abla\phi Vert=1\) AND \(\phi(R)=0\) (zero set lands on the surface).

\( abla\phi= abla r=\hat{r}\), so \(\lVert abla\phi Vert=1\) (units: m/m, dimensionless)
at \(r=R\): \(\phi=R-R=0\), the zero level set lands exactly on the surface
\( oxed{\lVert abla\phi Vert=1\ ext{and}\ \phi(R)=0\ \checkmark}\)

Why must you pin a value before solving \(L\phi=\operatorname{div}X\), and what does forgetting it cause?
Formula: \(L\mathbf{1}=0\) (constant null space), so \(\phi\) is unique only up to an additive constant.
Hint: name the fix (pin \(\phi=0\) on the source or fix one vertex) and the failure if you skip it.

\(L\) has a constant null space (\(L\mathbf{1}=0\)), so \(\phi\) is unique only up to an additive constant
pin one value (the level-set constraint \(\phi=0\) on the source, or fix one vertex)
\(\boxed{\text{forget it}\Rightarrow\text{singular system, no/garbage solution}}\)

When should you build an SDF instead of fitting NURBS directly to a scan?
Hint: think about what diffusion does to holes and noise.
When the scan is broken (holes, noise, self-intersection); the SDF is the defect-tolerant cleanup that diffusion bridges before fitting.

How do you extract the clean surface once you have \(\phi\)?
Formula: zero level set \(\{\mathbf{x}:\phi(\mathbf{x})=0\}\).
Hint: name the extraction algorithm for a grid vs a tet mesh.
Take the zero level set \(\{\mathbf{x}:\phi(\mathbf{x})=0\}\) with marching cubes (grid) or marching tetrahedra (tet mesh).

Pitfall: what single input assumption breaks the sign of \(\phi\) if violated, and how does it manifest?
Hint: it is the method's one hard assumption about the normals.
Consistent orientation of the normals; if normals flip between patches the sign of \(\phi\) flips region to region and the zero set is garbage.

Pitfall: a sphere test returns \(\phi=R-r\) instead of \(r-R\). What bug does this point to?
Formula: correct sphere SDF is \(\phi=r-R\); a global sign flip gives \(R-r\).
Hint: a global negation comes from the gradient/divergence operators.
A flipped cross product or left-handed frame in the gradient/divergence operators negates \(\phi\); validate on the known sphere before trusting a scan.

Why is a tetrahedral discretization preferred over a uniform grid for sharp stone features?
Hint: think about features thinner than the grid spacing.
Tet degrees of freedom adapt to the input, so thin features below grid spacing are not lost; better reconstruction at equal cost.

The Signed Heat method is three sparse solves: diffuse the {{c1::normal vector field}} via \((A-tL_{\text{vec}})Y_t=Y_0\), normalize \(X=Y_t/\lVert Y_t\rVert\), then Poisson-integrate {{c2::\(L\phi=\operatorname{div}X\)}}.

Geometry · SDF / Signed Heat
▶ 3Blue1Brown — What is a PDE? (heat equation)

An exact SDF satisfies the {{c1::eikonal}} equation \(\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert=1\), a nonlinear hyperbolic PDE; Signed Heat replaces it with {{c2::linear elliptic}} solves, trading exact unit-gradient magnitude for robustness and speed.

Geometry · SDF / Signed Heat
▶ 3Blue1Brown — What is a PDE? (heat equation)
The timestep is \(t=\texttt{tCoef}\cdot h^2\) because \(tL\) must be {{c1::dimensionless}}, and \(L\) carries units of \(1/\text{length}^2\).

Geometry · SDF / Signed Heat
▶ 3Blue1Brown — What is a PDE? (heat equation)

Derive the CVT quantization energy \(F(X)\) (step 1 of 2). Start from the per-region density-weighted squared error of points \(y\) from their seed \(x_i\), namely \(\int_{V_i}\rho(y)\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy\).
Formula: \(F(X)=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\int_{V_i}\rho(y)\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy\)
Hint: sum the one-region error over all \(k\) Voronoi regions; each point belongs to exactly its nearest seed's region.

One region contributes \(\int_{V_i}\rho(y)\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy\)
The regions \(V_i\) tile the domain, so sum over \(i=1,\dots,k\)
\(\boxed{F(X)=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\int_{V_i}\rho(y)\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy}\)

Derive the CVT energy gradient (step 2 of 2). Start from \(F=\sum_i\int_{V_i}\rho\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy\); cell-boundary motion contributes nothing.
Formula: \(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x_i}=2\,m_i\,(x_i-c_i)\) with \(m_i=\int_{V_i}\rho\,dy,\ c_i=\frac1{m_i}\int_{V_i}\rho\,y\,dy\)
Hint: differentiate only the integrand using \(\partial_{x_i}\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2=-2(y-x_i)\), then split the integral into \(m_i\) and \(c_i\).

Boundary terms cancel (each cell wall is equidistant from two seeds), so \(\partial F/\partial x_i=\int_{V_i}\rho\,\partial_{x_i}\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy=-2\int_{V_i}\rho(y)(y-x_i)\,dy\)
Split: \(-2\big(\int_{V_i}\rho\,y\,dy-x_i\,m_i\big)\)
Substitute \(\int_{V_i}\rho\,y\,dy=m_i c_i\): \(-2(m_i c_i-m_i x_i)\)
\(\boxed{\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x_i}=2\,m_i\,(x_i-c_i)}\)

From the gradient \(\partial F/\partial x_i=2m_i(x_i-c_i)\), derive the centroidal (stationarity) condition.
Formula: set \(\nabla F=0\); result \(x_i=c_i\ \forall i\)
Hint: a non-empty cell has mass \(m_i>0\), so decide which factor of the product must be zero.

A critical point needs \(2m_i(x_i-c_i)=0\) for every \(i\)
Mass \(m_i>0\) for any non-empty cell, so the vanishing factor is \((x_i-c_i)\)
\(\boxed{x_i=c_i\ \ \forall i}\) — every seed sits at its region's centroid

Derive the centroid \(c_i\) that minimizes a single cell's variance \(g(x_i)=\int_{V_i}\rho\lVert y-x_i\rVert^2\,dy\).
Formula: \(c_i=\dfrac{1}{m_i}\int_{V_i}y\,\rho(y)\,dy,\quad m_i=\int_{V_i}\rho\,dy\)
Hint: set \(g'(x_i)=0\) using \(g'(x_i)=-2\int_{V_i}\rho(y-x_i)\,dy\), then solve for \(x_i\).

Find the Voronoi boundary \(b\) between two 1D seeds \(x_1=0.2,\ x_2=0.6\).
Formula: \(b=(x_1+x_2)/2\) (point equidistant from both seeds)
Hint: set \(\lvert b-x_1\rvert=\lvert b-x_2\rvert\) and solve — it is just the midpoint.

Worked example (1D, \(\rho\equiv1\)). Compute both centroids of cells \(V_1=[0,0.4],\ V_2=[0.4,1]\).
Formula: uniform density \(\Rightarrow c_i=(\text{lo}+\text{hi})/2\) (interval midpoint)
Hint: just average the two endpoints of each interval.

Worked example. With \(m_2=0.6,\ x_2=0.6,\ c_2=0.7\), evaluate \(\partial F/\partial x_2\) and state which way \(x_2\) moves.
Formula: \(\partial F/\partial x_2=2m_2(x_2-c_2)\)
Hint: plug in the three numbers; the sign of the result tells the descent direction (move opposite the gradient).

\(\partial F/\partial x_2=2(0.6)(0.6-0.7)=1.2\times(-0.1)\)
\(\boxed{\partial F/\partial x_2=-0.12}\)
Negative gradient \(\Rightarrow\) \(x_2\) increases (moves right toward \(0.7\))

Closing test (close the loop). One Lloyd step sends \(x_2\leftarrow c_2=0.7\). Verify the gradient at the frozen cell is now zero, and that a finite-difference estimate at \(x_2=0.6\) matched \(-0.12\).
Formula: \(\partial F/\partial x_2=2m_2(x_2-c_2)\); FD: \(\partial F/\partial x_2\approx[F(0.61)-F(0.59)]/0.02\)
Hint: a pass means the analytic value is \(0\) after the move and the FD slope agrees with \(2m_2(x_2-c_2)\) to ~3 digits.

After the move \(x_2=c_2=0.7\), so \(2m_2(c_2-c_2)=0\) — the descent fixed point
FD at \(x_2=0.6\): \([F(0.61)-F(0.59)]/0.02\approx-0.12\), matching \(2(0.6)(0.6-0.7)=-0.12\)
\(\boxed{\text{grad}=0\text{ after move; FD }\approx-0.12\ \checkmark}\)

Worked example (energy before a step). Compute \(F\) for seeds \(0.2,0.6\), cells \([0,0.4],[0.4,1]\), \(\rho\equiv1\).
Formula: \(F=\int_0^{0.4}(y-0.2)^2dy+\int_{0.4}^{1}(y-0.6)^2dy\), with \(\int(y-a)^2dy=(y-a)^3/3\)
Hint: integrate each cell separately as \([(y-a)^3/3]\) over its endpoints, then add.

Closing test (energy must fall). After Lloyd (\(x_2\to0.7\), cells \([0,0.45],[0.45,1]\)) recompute \(F\) and confirm Lloyd's monotone-decrease guarantee.
Formula: \(F=\int_0^{0.45}(y-0.2)^2dy+\int_{0.45}^{1}(y-0.7)^2dy\); pass if \(F<0.029333\)
Hint: integrate each cell as \([(y-a)^3/3]\), add, then compare to the pre-step \(F\approx0.029333\).

Derive the squared 6D distance under the normal-lifting map (step 1 of 2). Start from \(\Phi(x)=(x,\,sN(x))\).
Formula: \(\lVert\Phi(x)-\Phi(x')\rVert^2=\lVert x-x'\rVert^2+s^2\lVert N(x)-N(x')\rVert^2\)
Hint: the difference \(\Phi(x)-\Phi(x')\) is two stacked blocks; the squared norm of stacked blocks is the sum of each block's squared norm.

\(\Phi(x)-\Phi(x')=\big(x-x',\ s(N(x)-N(x'))\big)\)
\(\lVert(a,b)\rVert^2=\lVert a\rVert^2+\lVert b\rVert^2\), and the second block carries the factor \(s\)
\(\boxed{\lVert\Phi(x)-\Phi(x')\rVert^2=\lVert x-x'\rVert^2+s^2\lVert N(x)-N(x')\rVert^2}\)

From the 6D squared distance (step 2 of 2), explain why flat regions get big triangles and creases get small ones.
Formula: \(\lVert\Phi-\Phi'\rVert^2=\lVert x-x'\rVert^2+s^2\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\)
Hint: ask what the normal-penalty term does when \(N\approx N'\) (flat) versus when \(N\) swings fast (crease).

Flat patch: \(N(x)\approx N(x')\), so \(s^2\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\approx0\); CVT may space seeds far apart \(\Rightarrow\) large triangles
Crease: \(\lVert N-N'\rVert\) large, penalty blows up; CVT packs seeds tightly \(\Rightarrow\) small triangles
\(\boxed{\text{seed spacing}\downarrow\ \text{as}\ s^2\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\uparrow}\) — anisotropy is curvature adaptivity in disguise

Worked example (anisotropy, mm-scale crease). Across a \(90^\circ\) crease with unit normals and \(s=0.5\), compute the extra 6D squared distance added to the 3D term.
Formula: extra \(=s^2\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\), with \(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2=2-2\,N\cdot N'\) for unit normals
Hint: first compute \(N\cdot N'\) for perpendicular normals, then plug into \(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\), then multiply by \(s^2\).

Perpendicular unit normals: \(N\cdot N'=0\), so \(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2=2-0=2\)
Extra \(=s^2\cdot2=0.25\times2\)
\(\boxed{=0.5}\) added to the 3D squared distance, pushing seeds apart across the crease

Worked example (anisotropy, larger \(s\)). Same \(90^\circ\) crease but \(s=2.0\). Compute the extra term and compare to the \(s=0.5\) value (\(0.5\)).
Formula: extra \(=s^2\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\), with \(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2=2\) here
Hint: the geometry (hence \(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2\)) is unchanged; only \(s^2\) changes, so take the ratio of \(s^2\) values.

\(\lVert N-N'\rVert^2=2\) (geometry unchanged)
Extra \(=s^2\cdot2=4\times2=8\)
Ratio to \(s=0.5\): \(8/0.5=16\) (also \(s^2\) ratio \(2^2/0.5^2=16\))
\(\boxed{=8,\ \ 16\times\text{ the }s=0.5\text{ value}}\) — stronger \(s\) forces far more triangles across the same crease

From the sizing field \(\rho\propto\kappa_{\max}^{4/5}\), derive how target edge length \(h\) scales with curvature on a surface (\(\dim=2\)).
Formula: \(h\propto\rho^{-1/\dim}=\rho^{-1/2}\), and \(\rho\propto\kappa_{\max}^{4/5}\)
Hint: substitute the curvature law into \(h\propto\rho^{-1/2}\) and multiply the exponents.

\(h\propto\rho^{-1/2}\)
Substitute \(\rho\propto\kappa_{\max}^{4/5}\): \(h\propto(\kappa_{\max}^{4/5})^{-1/2}=\kappa_{\max}^{-2/5}\)
\(\boxed{h\propto\kappa_{\max}^{-2/5}}\) — flat (\(\kappa_{\max}\to0\)) gets long edges, creases get short ones

Worked example (sizing field). A fillet has \(\kappa_{\max}\) that is \(32\times\) the flat region's. By what factor does target edge length \(h\) shrink?
Formula: \(h\propto\kappa_{\max}^{-2/5}\), so \(h_{\text{fillet}}/h_{\text{flat}}=32^{-2/5}\)
Hint: write \(32=2^5\) to make the fractional power clean.

\(h_{\text{fillet}}/h_{\text{flat}}=(32)^{-2/5}\)
\(32=2^5\), so \(32^{-2/5}=2^{5\cdot(-2/5)}=2^{-2}=1/4\)
\(\boxed{h\ \text{shrinks to }1/4}\) on the fillet relative to the flat

Derive the feature-edge test from two adjacent unit face normals \(n_f,n_g\).
Formula: \(\theta_e=\arccos(n_f\cdot n_g)\); feature edge \(\iff\theta_e>\theta_{\text{crease}}\)
Hint: the dot product of unit normals is the cosine of the dihedral angle; a crease is where that angle exceeds the threshold.

For unit normals, \(n_f\cdot n_g=\cos\theta_e\), so \(\theta_e=\arccos(n_f\cdot n_g)\in[0,\pi]\)
A crease is a sharp deviation, i.e. \(\theta_e\) above a threshold \(\theta_{\text{crease}}\)
\(\boxed{\text{feature edge}\iff\theta_e=\arccos(n_f\cdot n_g)>\theta_{\text{crease}}}\)

Worked example (segmentation). Two faces have unit normals with \(n_f\cdot n_g=0.5\). Is the edge a feature edge at \(\theta_{\text{crease}}=30^\circ\)?
Formula: \(\theta_e=\arccos(n_f\cdot n_g)\); feature edge if \(\theta_e>\theta_{\text{crease}}\)
Hint: compute \(\arccos(0.5)\) in degrees first, then compare to \(30^\circ\).

\(\theta_e=\arccos(0.5)=60^\circ\)
\(60^\circ>30^\circ\)
\(\boxed{\text{Yes — feature edge}}\), so region growing stops here (crease fence)

Closing test (segmentation, flat-face limit). Two nearly coplanar faces have \(n_f\cdot n_g=0.99\). Classify the edge at \(\theta_{\text{crease}}=30^\circ\) and check the perfectly-flat limit.
Formula: \(\theta_e=\arccos(n_f\cdot n_g)\); not a feature edge if \(\theta_e\le\theta_{\text{crease}}\)
Hint: a pass means \(0.99\) gives \(\theta_e\) well under \(30^\circ\) (crossed by region growing) and \(n_f\cdot n_g=1\) gives \(\theta_e=0\) (always merged).

\(\theta_e=\arccos(0.99)\approx8.1^\circ\)
\(8.1^\circ\le30^\circ\Rightarrow\) NOT a feature edge — region growing crosses it (same smooth face)
Flat limit: \(n_f\cdot n_g=1\Rightarrow\theta_e=\arccos(1)=0^\circ\le\theta_{\text{crease}}\), always merged
\(\boxed{\text{not a feature edge; flat limit }\theta_e\to0\ \checkmark}\)

When-to-use: which regions does the segmentation gate route to NURBS/CADFit vs freeform?
Hint: the gate keys on curvature plus whether a RANSAC primitive reaches consensus under \(\varepsilon\).

Low-curvature regions with a RANSAC plane/cylinder consensus (residual under \(\varepsilon\)) \(\to\) analytic primitive or NURBS fitting
High-curvature regions with no consensus primitive \(\to\) freeform mesh or tessellated IFC fallback
\(\boxed{\text{smooth+consensus}\to\text{CAD};\ \text{rough/no-fit}\to\text{freeform}}\)

Why prefer L-BFGS over plain Lloyd for CVT?
Hint: recall the smoothness class of the CVT energy on convex domains.

The CVT energy is \(C^2\) on convex domains (Liu et al. 2009)
Quasi-Newton (L-BFGS) uses that curvature to converge faster than Lloyd's slow linear tail near the minimum
\(\boxed{C^2\ \text{energy}\Rightarrow\text{L-BFGS beats Lloyd's slow tail}}\)

Pitfall: what does a localized RVD (LRVD) fix, and why does it matter?
Hint: think about a thin plate where one cell touches the surface in two disconnected patches.

On thin plates or near self-intersections a single Voronoi cell can split into disconnected surface patches; its centroid then lands off the surface
LRVD (or reprojecting centroids onto \(S\)) keeps seeds on the surface
\(\boxed{\text{LRVD/reproject}\Rightarrow\text{centroids stay on }S}\)

Pitfall: why is \(\theta_{\text{crease}}\) the most sensitive knob in stone segmentation?
Hint: consider what happens to region count as the threshold goes too low, then too high.

Too low fragments one rough boulder face into hundreds of regions; too high merges a sawn face with the natural rock behind it
It is unit-free, so calibrate on labelled stone, not by guessing
\(\boxed{\text{mis-set }\theta_{\text{crease}}\Rightarrow\text{wrong freeform-vs-CAD routing}}\)
Lloyd's algorithm is gradient descent on the CVT energy with a fixed full step, because moving each seed to its centroid sets {{c1::\(x_i \leftarrow c_i\)}} and never increases \(F\).
Geometry · Remeshing / Segmentation
In normal lifting, large triangles form where normals barely change ({{c1::flat/low-curvature}} regions) and small triangles form where normals swing fast (sharp features).
Geometry · Remeshing / Segmentation

Before anisotropic remeshing on a reconstructed scan you must {{c1::Laplacian-smooth the normals}}, or refinement chases normal noise.
hint: raw scan normals are noisy.
Geometry · Remeshing / Segmentation
In Geogram the effective anisotropy scale is the user value times {{c1::\(0.02\)}}, via `set_anisotropy(mesh, anisotropy*0.02)`.
Geometry · Remeshing / Segmentation

Derive the knot-count relation \(m=n+p+1\) (step 1 of 2): find the highest-indexed knot that the basis functions reference.
Formula: \(C(u)=\sum_{i=0}^{n}N_{i,p}(u)P_i\), and Cox-de Boor makes \(N_{i,p}\) reference knots up to \(u_{i+p+1}\).
Hint: there are \(n+1\) basis functions; plug the largest index \(i=n\) into \(u_{i+p+1}\).

There are \(n+1\) basis functions, indexed \(i=0\dots n\)
\(N_{i,p}\) reaches up to knot \(u_{i+p+1}\)
the highest, \(N_{n,p}\), references \(u_{n+p+1}\)
\(\boxed{u_{n+p+1}\text{ is the last knot needed}}\).

Finish the knot-count derivation (step 2 of 2): solve for \(m\) and the number of knot values.
Formula: knots are indexed \(u_0\dots u_m\) and the last one needed is \(u_{n+p+1}\).
Hint: set the last index equal to \(m\), then count values from \(0\) to \(m\) inclusive.

The last index must equal \(m\), so \(m=n+p+1\)
knot values run \(u_0\dots u_m\), a count of \(m+1=n+p+2\)
\(\boxed{m=n+p+1,\quad \#\text{knots}=n+p+2}\).

Derive the support interval of \(N_{i,p}\) (step 1 of 2): find the interval on which \(N_{i,1}\) can be nonzero.
Formula: \(N_{i,0}\ne0\) only on \([u_i,u_{i+1})\); the recursion blends \(N_{i,p-1}\) with \(N_{i+1,p-1}\).
Hint: \(N_{i,1}\) mixes \(N_{i,0}\) and \(N_{i+1,0}\) — take the union of their two boxes.

\(N_{i,1}\) blends \(N_{i,0}\) on \([u_i,u_{i+1})\) and \(N_{i+1,0}\) on \([u_{i+1},u_{i+2})\)
the union spans \([u_i,u_{i+2})\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{supp}N_{i,1}=[u_i,u_{i+2})}\).

Finish the support derivation (step 2 of 2): generalize \(\operatorname{supp}N_{i,p}\) and state how many basis functions are nonzero at a generic \(u\).
Formula: each degree step extends the right end by one knot: \([u_i,u_{i+2})\to[u_i,u_{i+3})\to\dots\)
Hint: start at width \(2\) for \(p=1\) and add one knot of reach per degree, \(p\) times.

After \(p\) steps the support is \([u_i,u_{i+p+1})\)
a generic \(u\) lies inside \(p+1\) overlapping supports
\(\boxed{\operatorname{supp}N_{i,p}=[u_i,u_{i+p+1}),\ \text{at most }p+1\text{ nonzero}}\).

Derive the convex-hull property of a B-spline from two basis properties.
Formula: \(C(u)=\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)P_i\), with \(N_{i,p}\ge0\) and \(\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)=1\).
Hint: nonnegative weights that sum to \(1\) define a convex combination — name where such a combination must lie.

\(C(u)\) is a weighted sum of the \(P_i\) with weights \(N_{i,p}\ge0\) summing to \(1\)
that is by definition a convex combination of the \(P_i\)
a convex combination lies in the convex hull; local support restricts it to the \(p+1\) active points
\(\boxed{C(u)\in\operatorname{conv}\{P_i:N_{i,p}(u)>0\}}\).

Derive affine invariance: show \(T(C(u))=\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)\,T(P_i)\) for affine \(T\).
Formula: \(T(x)=Ax+b\), \(C(u)=\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)P_i\), and \(\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)=1\).
Hint: apply \(T\), then use partition of unity to rewrite the lone \(b\) as \(\big(\sum_i N_{i,p}\big)b\) so it folds into the sum.

\(T(C(u))=A\big(\sum_i N_{i,p}P_i\big)+b=\sum_i N_{i,p}(AP_i)+b\)
rewrite \(b=\big(\sum_i N_{i,p}\big)b=\sum_i N_{i,p}\,b\) via partition of unity
\(=\sum_i N_{i,p}(AP_i+b)=\sum_i N_{i,p}\,T(P_i)\)
\(\boxed{T(C(u))=\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)\,T(P_i)}\) — transform only the poles.

Derive the rational basis \(R_{i,p}\) and show it still sums to \(1\).
Formula: \(C(u)=\dfrac{\sum_i w_iP_iN_{i,p}(u)}{\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}(u)}\); target \(R_{i,p}=\dfrac{w_iN_{i,p}}{\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}}\).
Hint: pull the common denominator inside the sum so each \(P_i\) gets its own coefficient, then add those coefficients.

Move the common denominator inside: \(C(u)=\sum_i\dfrac{w_iN_{i,p}}{\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}}P_i\)
define \(R_{i,p}=\dfrac{w_iN_{i,p}}{\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}}\)
then \(\sum_i R_{i,p}=\dfrac{\sum_i w_iN_{i,p}}{\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}}=1\)
\(\boxed{C(u)=\sum_i R_{i,p}(u)P_i,\ \sum_i R_{i,p}=1}\).

Derive the NURBS projection formula from the homogeneous (4D) lift.
Formula: 4D poles \(P_i^w=(w_ix_i,w_iy_i,w_iz_i,w_i)\), run \(C^w(u)=\sum_i N_{i,p}(u)P_i^w\), then divide by the last coordinate.
Hint: write out the four components of \(C^w(u)\), then perspective-divide the first three by the fourth.

\(C^w(u)=\big(\sum_i N_{i,p}w_ix_i,\dots,\sum_i N_{i,p}w_i\big)\)
divide first three by the 4th: \(x=\dfrac{\sum_i N_{i,p}w_ix_i}{\sum_i N_{i,p}w_i}\) (same for \(y,z\))
\(\boxed{C(u)=\dfrac{\sum_i w_iP_iN_{i,p}(u)}{\sum_i w_iN_{i,p}(u)}}\).

Derive the continuity at an interior knot of multiplicity \(k\) in a degree-\(p\) spline.
Formula: \(N_{i,p}\) is \(C^\infty\) between knots and loses one derivative per coincident knot; target \(C^{\,p-k}\).
Hint: a simple knot (\(k=1\)) gives \(C^{p-1}\); subtract one more derivative for each extra coincidence, \(k-1\) of them.

Between knots \(N_{i,p}\) is \(C^\infty\); across a simple knot it drops to \(C^{p-1}\)
raising multiplicity by one removes one more derivative
after \(k\) coincidences: \(p-1-(k-1)=p-k\)
\(\boxed{C^{\,p-k}\text{ continuous at a knot of multiplicity }k}\).

Derive the middle weight that makes a rational quadratic Bezier an exact circular arc.
Formula: symmetric control legs \(|P_0P_1|=|P_1P_2|\), end weights \(1\), arc half-angle \(\theta\) at \(P_1\); target \(w_1=\cos\theta\).
Hint: the conic is a circle exactly when the apex weight equals the cosine of the half-angle subtended at \(P_1\).

A rational quadratic with symmetric legs traces a conic
it is a circular arc exactly when \(w_1\) equals the cosine of the half-angle at the apex \(P_1\)
\(\boxed{w_1=\cos\theta}\).

Evaluate the worked-example basis (step 1 of 2): find the nonzero degree-2 basis values at \(u=\tfrac12\).
Formula: \(U=\{0,0,0,\tfrac12,1,1,1\}\), \(p=2\); \(N_{i,2}=\frac{u-u_i}{u_{i+2}-u_i}N_{i,1}+\frac{u_{i+3}-u}{u_{i+3}-u_{i+1}}N_{i+1,1}\), with active \(N_{2,1}=1\).
Hint: only the box \(N_{3,0}\) is active, so \(N_{2,1}=1,N_{3,1}=0\); substitute the knot values into \(N_{1,2}\) and \(N_{2,2}\).

Finish the worked-example evaluation (step 2 of 2): compute \(C(\tfrac12)\).
Formula: \(C(\tfrac12)=N_{1,2}P_1+N_{2,2}P_2\) with \(N_{1,2}=N_{2,2}=\tfrac12\), \(P_1=(1,2)\), \(P_2=(3,2)\).
Hint: it is just the average of \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) — add the coordinates and halve.

\(C(\tfrac12)=\tfrac12P_1+\tfrac12P_2\)
\(=\tfrac12(1,2)+\tfrac12(3,2)=(2,2)\)
\(\boxed{C(\tfrac12)=(2,2)}\) — midpoint of the top edge, inside the hull.

State the Boehm knot-insertion corner-cut coefficient \(\alpha_i\).
Formula: \(Q_i=(1-\alpha_i)P_{i-1}+\alpha_iP_i\) with \(\alpha_i=\dfrac{\bar u-u_i}{u_{i+p}-u_i}\), for \(k-p+1\le i\le k\) when \(\bar u\in[u_k,u_{k+1})\).
Hint: \(\alpha_i\) is just how far \(\bar u\) sits along the interval \([u_i,u_{i+p}]\).

\(\alpha_i\) measures the position of \(\bar u\) within \([u_i,u_{i+p}]\)
\(\boxed{\alpha_i=\dfrac{\bar u-u_i}{u_{i+p}-u_i},\quad k-p+1\le i\le k}\).

Worked example (mm shop scale, small): a cubic (\(p=3\)) clamped B-spline fits a chamfer with \(n+1=6\) control points in millimeters. Find the number of knot values and the two end multiplicities.
Formula: \(m=n+p+1\), knot values \(=m+1\), clamp multiplicity \(=p+1\).
Hint: first get \(n\) from \(n+1=6\), then plug into \(m\).

\(n=5\), so \(m=n+p+1=5+3+1=9\)
knot values \(=m+1=10\)
clamped ends each have multiplicity \(p+1=4\)
\(\boxed{10\text{ knots},\ \text{end mult}=4,4}\) (units don't affect counts).

Worked example (site scale, large, meters): a quartic (\(p=4\)) NURBS edge of a quarry-block boundary has \(14\) control points in meters. Find the knot count and the clamp multiplicity.
Formula: \(m=n+p+1\), knot values \(=m+1\), clamp multiplicity \(=p+1\).
Hint: \(n=14-1=13\); substitute into \(m\), then add one for the value count.

\(n=13\), \(m=n+p+1=13+4+1=18\)
knot values \(=m+1=19\)
clamp mult \(=p+1=5\)
\(\boxed{19\text{ knots},\ \text{end mult}=5}\).

Worked example (continuity regime): a cubic has interior knots at \(0.3\) (mult 1), \(0.6\) (mult 2), \(0.8\) (mult 3). Give \(C^{?}\) at each and name the corner.
Formula: continuity \(=C^{\,p-k}\) with \(p=3\).
Hint: compute \(3-k\) for each \(k\); the corner is the knot where continuity hits \(C^0\).

Worked example (exact quarter circle, 2D): build a rational quadratic arc \(P_0=(1,0),P_1=(1,1),P_2=(0,1)\) for a \(90^\circ\) arc; find \(w_1\) and the end weights.
Formula: \(w_1=\cos\theta\) with \(\theta\) the arc half-angle; end weights \(w_0=w_2=1\).
Hint: a \(90^\circ\) arc has half-angle \(\theta=45^\circ\); evaluate \(\cos45^\circ\).

Closing test (verify the arc lies on the circle): sample the quarter-circle NURBS at \(u=\tfrac12\) and check the radius.
Formula: midpoint \(=\dfrac{\tfrac14 P_0+\tfrac{w_1}{2} P_1+\tfrac14 P_2}{\tfrac14+\tfrac{w_1}{2}+\tfrac14}\) with \(w_1=\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\); pass if \(x^2+y^2=1\).
Hint: by symmetry \(x=y\); compute one component, then check \(x^2+y^2\) equals \(1\).

numerator \(=\tfrac14(1,0)+\tfrac{\sqrt2}{4}(1,1)+\tfrac14(0,1)=(\tfrac14+\tfrac{\sqrt2}{4},\ \tfrac{\sqrt2}{4}+\tfrac14)\)
denominator \(=\tfrac12+\tfrac{\sqrt2}{4}\)
by symmetry \(x=y=\dfrac{1+\sqrt2}{2+\sqrt2}\approx0.7071\)
\(x^2+y^2=2(0.7071)^2=1.000\)
\(\boxed{\text{on the unit circle, radius }1\ \checkmark}\).

Closing test (knot insertion is shape-preserving): insert \(\bar u=0.75\) into the worked-example quadratic via Boehm and verify the shape is unchanged.
Formula: new poles \(Q_i=(1-\alpha_i)P_{i-1}+\alpha_iP_i\) (convex, \(\alpha_i\in[0,1]\)); clamp keeps end multiplicity \(p+1\). Pass if endpoints still match.
Hint: a clamped curve interpolates its ends, so re-evaluate \(C(0)\) and \(C(1)\) — they should be unchanged.

insertion adds one pole and re-blends affected interior poles by convex combos (\(\alpha_i\in[0,1]\))
end knots keep multiplicity \(p+1=3\), so clamping is untouched
re-evaluate: \(C(0)=P_0=(0,0)\), \(C(1)=P_3=(4,0)\) as before
\(\boxed{\text{geometry identical, }+1\text{ pole}\ \checkmark}\).

Closing test (IFC knot-count validation): you serialize an `IfcBSplineCurveWithKnots` with degree \(p=3\), \(7\) control points, and \(10\) knot values. Does it validate?
Formula: required knot values \(=n+p+2\).
Hint: \(n=7-1=6\); compute \(n+p+2\) and compare to \(10\) — a pass means they're equal.

required count \(=n+p+2=6+3+2=11\)
you wrote \(10\ne11\)
\(\boxed{\text{invalid: need }11\text{ knot values, not }10}\).

When-to-use: why insert a triple knot in a cubic for stone work?
Formula: continuity \(=C^{\,p-k}\); here \(p=3\), \(k=3\).
Hint: compute \(p-k\) to see the continuity a triple knot forces.

a triple knot (\(k=p=3\)) gives \(C^{3-3}=C^0\), a deliberate corner for a chisel line
keep multiplicity 1 elsewhere (\(C^2\)) for a smooth fabricable edge
\(\boxed{\text{triple knot}\Rightarrow C^0\text{ corner}}\).

Pitfall: what happens if a NURBS weight is zero or negative?
Formula: denominator \(D(u)=\sum_j w_jN_{j,p}(u)\) in \(C(u)=\dfrac{\sum_i w_iP_iN_{i,p}}{D(u)}\).
Hint: ask what a non-positive \(w_i\) does to \(D(u)\) and whether the convex-combination argument still holds.

a weight \(w_i\le0\) can drive \(D(u)\) to zero (a pole / division by zero)
the weights are no longer all positive, so the convex-hull guarantee fails and the curve can escape the hull
\(\boxed{\text{keep all }w_i>0}\).
Pitfall: in the OCCT `Geom_BSplineCurve` constructor, how is the knot vector supplied?

as distinct knots plus a parallel array of integer multiplicities, not the expanded vector
e.g. \(\{0,0,0,\tfrac12,1,1,1\}\) is knots \(\{0,\tfrac12,1\}\) with mults \(\{3,1,3\}\)
\(\boxed{\text{knots}+\text{mults arrays}}\).
When-to-use: which OCCT class fits/approximates a B-spline through ordered scan points, and which parameterization for noisy data?

`GeomAPI_PointsToBSpline` fits/approximates through ordered points
choose centripetal parameterization for irregular/noisy spacing
\(\boxed{\texttt{GeomAPI\_PointsToBSpline},\ \text{centripetal}}\).
At a knot of multiplicity \(k\) in a degree-\(p\) B-spline, the curve is {{c1::\(C^{p-k}\)}} continuous.

CAD · B-splines & NURBS
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bézier curves (Freya Holmér)
A clamped (open) knot vector repeats the first and last knots {{c1::\(p+1\)}} times so the curve interpolates its end control points.

CAD · B-splines & NURBS
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bézier curves (Freya Holmér)
The B-spline basis functions satisfy partition of unity, meaning {{c1::\(\sum_i N_{i,p}(u) = 1\)}} on the valid domain.

CAD · B-splines & NURBS
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bézier curves (Freya Holmér)

For irregularly spaced scan samples, prefer {{c1::centripetal}} (or chord-length) parameterization over uniform to avoid loops and overshoot.
hint: uniform parameterization on uneven spacing causes loops and overshoot.

CAD · B-splines & NURBS
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bézier curves (Freya Holmér)
A NURBS curve equals a non-rational B-spline run in {{c1::homogeneous (4D)}} coordinates and then projected by dividing by the weight coordinate.

CAD · B-splines & NURBS
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bézier curves (Freya Holmér)

Construct the non-rational tensor-product B-spline surface from a \(u\)-curve basis \(N_{i,p}(u)\) and a \(v\)-curve basis \(M_{j,q}(v)\) over a control net \(P_{ij}\).
Formula: surface basis \(=N_{i,p}(u)\,M_{j,q}(v)\); target \(S(u,v)=\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m}P_{ij}\,N_{i,p}(u)\,M_{j,q}(v)\)
Hint: "tensor product" means multiply the two bases, then weight each product by its control point \(P_{ij}\) and sum over the net.

Tensor product: surface basis at \((u,v)\) is the product \(N_{i,p}(u)M_{j,q}(v)\)
weight each by control point \(P_{ij}\) and sum over all \((i,j)\)
\(\boxed{S(u,v)=\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} P_{ij}\,N_{i,p}(u)\,M_{j,q}(v)}\).

Derive the rational NURBS surface (step 1 of 2): write the homogeneous numerator and the normalizing denominator from weights \(w_{ij}>0\).
Formula: \(\text{num}=\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}P_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}\), \(\text{den}=\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}\)
Hint: the denominator is the numerator with \(P_{ij}\) deleted -- it just renormalizes the weighted basis.

Numerator weights each term by \(w_{ij}\): \(\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}P_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}\)
denominator is the same sum without \(P_{ij}\): \(\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}\)
\(\boxed{\text{num}=\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}P_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q},\quad \text{den}=\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}}\).

Derive the rational NURBS surface (step 2 of 2): form \(S=\text{num}/\text{den}\), then show all \(w_{ij}=1\) recovers the B-spline surface.
Formula: \(S=\dfrac{\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}P_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}}{\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}}\); partition of unity \(\sum_i N_{i,p}=1\), \(\sum_j M_{j,q}=1\)
Hint: set every \(w_{ij}=1\) and use the two partition-of-unity sums to collapse the denominator to \(1\cdot1\).

Derive the rational basis \(R_{ij}(u,v)\) so that \(S=\sum_{ij}R_{ij}P_{ij}\), and show it sums to 1.
Formula: \(R_{ij}=\dfrac{w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}}{\sum_k\sum_l w_{kl}N_{k,p}M_{l,q}}\)
Hint: factor \(P_{ij}\) out of the rational \(S\); to sum \(R_{ij}\), notice summing the numerator over \(ij\) gives exactly the denominator.

Pull \(P_{ij}\) out of the rational form: \(R_{ij}=\dfrac{w_{ij}N_{i,p}M_{j,q}}{\sum_k\sum_l w_{kl}N_{k,p}M_{l,q}}\)
summing over \(ij\) makes numerator \(=\) denominator
\(\boxed{\sum_i\sum_j R_{ij}(u,v)=1}\) (partition of unity \(\Rightarrow\) affine invariance + convex hull).

Surface approximation (chain, step 1 of 3): write what you minimize given grid data \(Q_{rs}\) and surface samples \(S(\bar u_r,\bar v_s)\).
Formula: \(\min_{\{P_{ij}\}}\sum_r\sum_s\big\|S(\bar u_r,\bar v_s)-Q_{rs}\big\|^2\)
Hint: the misfit at each node is the residual \(S-Q\); penalize its squared 3D length and sum over the grid.

For each data node the misfit is the residual \(S(\bar u_r,\bar v_s)-Q_{rs}\)
penalize the squared 3D length, summed over the whole grid
\(\boxed{\min_{\{P_{ij}\}}\sum_r\sum_s\big\|S(\bar u_r,\bar v_s)-Q_{rs}\big\|^2}\).

Surface fit (chain, step 2 of 3): from \(S=\sum_{ij}P_{ij}N_{i,p}(\bar u_r)M_{j,q}(\bar v_s)\), write the linear model \(S=BP\) for fixed knots and parameters.
Formula: \(S=BP\) with \(B_{(r,s),(i,j)}=N_{i,p}(\bar u_r)M_{j,q}(\bar v_s)\)
Hint: at fixed knots/params \(S\) is linear in \(P_{ij}\); build \(B\) by putting one row per node \((r,s)\), one column per \((i,j)\).

\(S\) is linear in the unknown \(P_{ij}\) at fixed knots/params
stack one row per node \((r,s)\) with entries \(N_{i,p}(\bar u_r)M_{j,q}(\bar v_s)\) across all \((i,j)\)
\(\boxed{S=BP,\ \ B_{(r,s),(i,j)}=N_{i,p}(\bar u_r)M_{j,q}(\bar v_s)}\).

Surface fit (chain, step 3 of 3): from \(\min_P\|BP-Q\|^2\), derive the normal equations.
Formula: \(\nabla_P\|BP-Q\|^2=2B^\top(BP-Q)\); target \(B^\top B\,P=B^\top Q\)
Hint: set the gradient to zero and divide by 2, then move the \(B^\top Q\) term to the right.

Set the gradient to zero: \(\nabla_P\|BP-Q\|^2=2B^\top(BP-Q)=0\)
rearrange
\(\boxed{B^\top B\,P=B^\top Q}\) (solve once per coordinate \(x,y,z\)).

Derive the centripetal parameter increment (step 1 of 2): from the rule "advance by \(\sqrt{\text{chord}}\), normalized to the total," write \(\Delta\bar u_r\).
Formula: \(\Delta\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sqrt{|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}\)
Hint: the numerator is the square root of this one chord; the denominator is the sum of all square-root chords (so \(\bar u\) ends at 1).

Each chord \(|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|\) contributes \(\sqrt{|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|}\)
normalize by the total of all square-root chords so \(\bar u\) ends at 1
\(\boxed{\Delta\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sqrt{|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}}\).

Centripetal (step 2 of 2): from \(\bar u_r=\bar u_{r-1}+\Delta\bar u_r\) with \(\bar u_0=0\), write the running parameter as a partial sum.
Formula: \(\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{r}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}\)
Hint: unroll the recursion -- the numerator accumulates square-root chords up to index \(r\), the denominator stays the full total.

\(\bar u_r=\bar u_{r-1}+\Delta\bar u_r\) with \(\bar u_0=0\)
unrolling the recursion gives the partial sum
\(\boxed{\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{r}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}}}\).

From the general rule \(\Delta\bar u_r\propto|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|^{\alpha}\), derive uniform, chord-length, and centripetal as the three special cases.
Formula: \(\alpha=0\) (uniform), \(\alpha=\tfrac12\) (centripetal), \(\alpha=1\) (chord-length)
Hint: substitute each \(\alpha\) into the weight \(|Q_r-Q_{r-1}|^{\alpha}\) -- start with \(\alpha=0\), which makes every chord weigh \(1\).

\(\alpha=0\): every chord weighted \(1\Rightarrow\bar u_r=r/R\) (uniform)
\(\alpha=1\): weight \(=\) raw distance (chord-length)
\(\alpha=\tfrac12\): weight \(=\sqrt{\text{distance}}\)
\(\boxed{\alpha\in\{0,\tfrac12,1\}\to\{\text{uniform, centripetal, chord-length}\}}\).

Worked example (bilinear, step 1 of 2): bidegree \((1,1)\) over knots \(\{0,0,1,1\}\). Find the four basis products at \((u,v)=(0.5,0.5)\).
Formula: degree-1 hats \(N_0(u)=1-u,\ N_1(u)=u\); same in \(v\): \(M_0(v)=1-v,\ M_1(v)=v\)
Hint: evaluate each hat at \(0.5\) first, then multiply \(N_i\cdot M_j\) for the four combinations.

Worked example (bilinear, step 2 of 2): with \(P_{00}=(0,0,0),P_{10}=(1,0,2),P_{01}=(0,1,1),P_{11}=(1,1,4)\) and each basis product \(=0.25\), evaluate \(S(0.5,0.5)\).
Formula: \(S(0.5,0.5)=0.25\,(P_{00}+P_{10}+P_{01}+P_{11})\)
Hint: add the four control points component-by-component first, then multiply the sum by \(0.25\).

Worked example (centripetal, irregular row): three chords measure \(1,1,8\) (one big gap). Compute the four centripetal parameters.
Formula: \(\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{r}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}\)
Hint: take the square root of each chord (\(\sqrt8\approx2.828\)), then accumulate and divide by the total of the roots.

Worked example (chord-length, same row): chords \(1,1,8\), total \(10\). Compute the chord-length parameters and compare to centripetal.
Formula: \(\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{r}|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|}{d}\), \(d=\sum|Q_k-Q_{k-1}|\)
Hint: here \(\alpha=1\), so use raw distances (no square root); accumulate and divide by total \(10\).

Cumulative raw distance over total: \(0,\ 1/10,\ 2/10,\ 10/10\)
\(\bar u=\{0,0.1,0.2,1.0\}\)
\(\boxed{\text{crowded into }[0,0.2]\text{ vs }[0,0.414]\text{ centripetal}}\) -- chord-length squeezes harder, inviting overshoot.

Worked example (mm vs m scale): a row spans chords \(2,2,16\) in mm; recompute in m (\(0.002,0.002,0.016\)). Do the centripetal parameters change?
Formula: \(\bar u_r=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{r}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}\)
Hint: compute the normalized ratio in each unit; a common scale factor cancels top and bottom.

Worked example (knot count): a cubic (\(p=3\)) B-spline has \(n+1=10\) control points in \(u\). How many knots \(m+1\) are there?
Formula: \(m=n+p+1\)
Hint: read off \(n=9\) and \(p=3\), plug into \(m=n+p+1\), then the knot count is \(m+1\).

Test yourself (bilinear corner check): evaluate the worked patch at \((u,v)=(1,0)\) and verify it returns \(P_{10}\).
Formula: \(N_0(1)=0,N_1(1)=1\); \(M_0(0)=1,M_1(0)=0\); \(S=\sum_{ij}N_iM_jP_{ij}\)
Hint: a pass means only one product survives -- find the \((i,j)\) where both hats equal \(1\).

Products: only \(N_1M_0=1\cdot1=1\) is nonzero
so \(S=1\cdot P_{10}\)
\(\boxed{S(1,0)=(1,0,2)=P_{10}}\) -- bilinear interpolates its corners. Loop closed.

Test yourself (partition of unity at the center): for the bilinear patch at \((0.5,0.5)\), verify the four basis products sum to 1.
Formula: \(\sum_{ij}N_i(u)M_j(v)=1\)
Hint: a pass means the four products (\(0.25\) each) total exactly \(1.00\), so \(S\) is a convex average of the corners.

Each product is \(0.25\)
\(0.25\times4=1.00\)
\(\boxed{\sum_{ij}N_iM_j=1}\) -- so \(S\) is an affine (convex) average of the corners, inside their hull. Verified.

Test yourself (centripetal endpoint): confirm the irregular-row centripetal parameters end at exactly \(\bar u_R=1\).
Formula: \(\bar u_R=\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{R}\sqrt{\text{chord}_k}}\)
Hint: a pass means the final numerator equals the denominator (full total), so the ratio is exactly \(1\).

Final partial sum \(=\) total: \(1+1+2.828=4.828\)
\(4.828/4.828=1\)
\(\boxed{\bar u_3=1.0}\) -- normalization guarantees the last parameter is \(1\). Loop closed.

Test yourself (fit-error gate): after a fit, you measure max sample-to-surface distance \(0.6\) mm against \(\text{Tol3D}=1.0\) mm. Does the fit pass?
Formula: pass iff \(\max_{r,s}\|S(\bar u_r,\bar v_s)-Q_{rs}\|\le\text{Tol3D}\)
Hint: a pass means the largest residual is at or below Tol3D; compare \(0.6\) to \(1.0\).

Max residual \(=0.6\) mm, Tol3D \(=1.0\) mm
\(0.6\le1.0\), so the tolerance is met
\(\boxed{\text{PASS}}\) -- OCCT stops raising the control count once \(\max\le\text{Tol3D}\).

When to use approximation vs interpolation for a scan, and why?
Hint: think about what interpolation does to noisy samples.
Approximate (fewer control points than data) -- interpolation forces the surface through every noisy sample, turning noise into geometry and rippling; approximation acts as a low-pass filter.

Pitfall -- you pass an unorganized cloud straight to the surface fitter. What breaks and what is the fix?
Hint: recall what the array index \((i,j)\) is required to be.
The index \((i,j)\) must be a true topological grid (row monotone in \(u\), column in \(v\)); a raw cloud yields garbage. Fix: template-guided UV resample onto an ordered grid first.

Pitfall -- a grid in mm fit with \(\text{Tol3D}=1.0\text{e-}3\). What goes wrong?
Hint: what units is Tol3D in, and how small is \(0.001\) in mm?
Tol3D is in model units, so \(0.001\) mm over-refines and explodes the control count. Keep grid units and Tol3D in one system; set Tol3D to a physical fraction of feature size.

Why must an overhang be split into separate patches before fitting?
Hint: count how many surface points a tensor-product surface allows per \((u,v)\).

A tensor-product surface is single-valued: each \((u,v)\) maps to exactly one point. An overhang has two surface points over one template location, so one patch cannot represent it -- segment into height-field patches.
The B-spline count relation linking knots, control points, and degree is {{c1::\(m=n+p+1\)}}, with \(n+1\) control points and \(m+1\) knots in \(u\).
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting
A non-rational B-spline surface is the special case of a NURBS surface where all weights are {{c1::equal (e.g. \(w_{ij}=1\))}}, making the denominator collapse to 1.
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting

Surface approximation solves the normal equations {{c1::\(B^\top B\,P=B^\top Q\)}} once per coordinate \(x,y,z\).
hint: least squares, set the gradient of \(\|BP-Q\|^2\) to zero.
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting
Centripetal parameterization raises each chord to the power {{c1::\(\alpha=1/2\)}}, compressing the dynamic range of irregular spacings.
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting
In OCCT the centripetal parameterization type is the enum {{c1::Approx_Centripetal}}, and the surface fitter is {{c2::GeomAPI_PointsToBSplineSurface}}.
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting
OCCT surface-fit defaults are DegMin={{c1::3}}, DegMax={{c2::8}}, continuity {{c3::GeomAbs_C2}}, Tol3D={{c4::1.0e-3}}.
CAD · NURBS Surfaces & Fitting

Derive the un-flipped unit normal \(\hat n_{uv}\) of a face from its surface parameterization \(S(u,v)\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(S_u=\partial S/\partial u,\ S_v=\partial S/\partial v,\ \hat n_{uv}=\dfrac{S_u\times S_v}{\lVert S_u\times S_v\rVert}\)
Hint: the cross product of the two tangents is normal to the patch; divide by its length to make it a unit vector.

The two tangent directions are \(S_u=\partial S/\partial u\) and \(S_v=\partial S/\partial v\)
their cross product is normal to the patch, with magnitude \(\lVert S_u\times S_v\rVert\) (the area scale)
normalize to get the parameterization normal
\(\boxed{\hat n_{uv}=\dfrac{S_u\times S_v}{\lVert S_u\times S_v\rVert}}\)

Fold the stored `Orientation` flag into \(\hat n_{uv}\) to get the effective outward normal \(\hat n_{\text{out}}\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\hat n_{\text{out}}=\sigma\,\hat n_{uv}\), with \(\sigma=+1\) (FORWARD), \(\sigma=-1\) (REVERSED)
Hint: just multiply the step-1 unit normal by the sign \(\sigma\); REVERSED flips it.

The face stores a sign \(\sigma=+1\) for FORWARD and \(\sigma=-1\) for REVERSED, which may flip the parameterization normal
multiply through so the result points out of the solid regardless of how \((u,v)\) was laid out
\(\boxed{\hat n_{\text{out}}=\sigma\,\dfrac{S_u\times S_v}{\lVert S_u\times S_v\rVert}}\)

Derive the point set of the solid produced by sweeping a face \(F\) along \(\vec d\) (the `MakePrism` set).
Formula: \(\text{Prism}(F,\vec d)=\{\,p+s\vec d:\;p\in F,\;s\in[0,1]\,\}\)
Hint: let each base point \(p\) ride the direction \(\vec d\) as the travel fraction \(s\) runs from 0 to 1, then take the union.

Every base point \(p\in F\) travels along the fixed direction \(\vec d\)
a fractional travel \(s\in[0,1]\) places it at \(p+s\vec d\) (\(s=0\) is the base face, \(s=1\) the translated copy)
the solid is the union over all base points and all travel fractions
\(\boxed{\text{Prism}(F,\vec d)=\{\,p+s\vec d:\;p\in F,\;s\in[0,1]\,\}}\)

From the prism set, derive the slab thickness \(h\) produced by sweeping a planar face along \(\vec d\).
Formula: \(\text{Prism}(F,\vec d)=\{p+s\vec d:p\in F,s\in[0,1]\}\Rightarrow h=\lVert\vec d\rVert\)
Hint: the base is at \(s=0\) and its copy at \(s=1\); the separation is the full length of \(\vec d\).

The base face sits at \(s=0\), its copy at \(s=1\), displaced by the full vector \(\vec d\)
for a planar face the thickness is the distance between those parallel copies, i.e. the length of \(\vec d\)
\(\boxed{h=\lVert\vec d\rVert}\)

Define the worst-case gap \(g\) between two free edges carrying curves \(C_1(t),C_2(t')\) (sewing, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(g=\max_{t}\,\min_{t'}\,\lVert C_1(t)-C_2(t')\rVert\)
Hint: for each point on \(C_1\) first take the closest approach on \(C_2\) (the inner \(\min\)), then the worst of those over \(C_1\) (the outer \(\max\)).

For each point \(C_1(t)\) the closest approach on the other curve is \(\min_{t'}\lVert C_1(t)-C_2(t')\rVert\)
the curves are at most as far apart as the worst such closest approach
\(\boxed{g=\max_{t}\,\min_{t'}\,\lVert C_1(t)-C_2(t')\rVert}\) (a one-sided Hausdorff gap)

State the sewing decision rule that merges two free edges, given the gap \(g\) and tolerance \(\tau\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(g\le\tau\;\Longrightarrow\;\text{merge }C_1,C_2\text{ into one edge}\)
Hint: compare the worst gap \(g\) to the tolerance band \(\tau\); merge only when it fits inside.

Two free edges become one shared edge only if their worst gap fits inside the tolerance band
compare the gap to \(\tau\)
\(\boxed{g\le\tau\;\Longrightarrow\;\text{merge }C_1,C_2\text{ into one edge}}\)

Derive a scale-robust default sewing tolerance \(\tau\) for data of characteristic size \(L\).
Formula: size-relative term \(10^{-3}L\), confusion floor \(10^{-7}\), so \(\tau=\max(10^{-3}L,\,10^{-7})\)
Hint: take the larger of the size-relative term and the kernel floor so \(\tau\) is never below `Precision::Confusion()`.

A fixed absolute \(\tau\) is wrong across scales (too big in mm, too small in m), so tie it to size: \(\tau\approx 10^{-3}L\)
but it must never drop below the kernel confusion floor \(10^{-7}\)
\(\boxed{\tau=\max\!\left(10^{-3}L,\;10^{-7}\right)}\)

From the edge-share count \(n_e\) (faces sharing an edge), derive the watertight 2-manifold condition over a shell.
Formula: \(n_e=1\) free, \(n_e=2\) contiguous, \(n_e\ge3\) non-manifold; watertight 2-manifold \(\iff\) every edge has \(n_e=2\)
Hint: watertight bans \(n_e=1\) (no boundary), 2-manifold bans \(n_e\ge3\) (no triple edge); only \(n_e=2\) survives.

\(n_e=1\) is a free (boundary) edge, \(n_e=2\) a contiguous seam, \(n_e\ge3\) non-manifold
watertight means no boundary survives, so no edge has \(n_e=1\)
2-manifold means no edge is shared by three or more faces, so no \(n_e\ge3\)
\(\boxed{\text{every edge has }n_e=2\iff\text{NbFreeEdges}=0\text{ and no multiple edges}}\)

Specialize the Euler-Poincaré formula to a simply-connected single-shell solid (step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\), with \(S=1,\ G=0\)
Hint: substitute one shell and zero handles into the right-hand side and evaluate \(2(S-G)\).

A single closed shell gives \(S=1\); no through-handles gives genus \(G=0\)
substitute into the right-hand side: \(2(S-G)=2(1-0)\)
\(\boxed{V-E+F=2}\)

Specialize the Euler-Poincaré formula to a single-shell solid with one through-hole (step 2 of 2, torus regime).
Formula: \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\), with \(S=1,\ G=1\)
Hint: one through-handle means \(G=1\); plug into \(2(S-G)\).

One through-handle means genus \(G=1\); still a single shell \(S=1\)
right-hand side: \(2(S-G)=2(1-1)=0\)
\(\boxed{V-E+F=0}\)

Count \(V,E,F\) for the unit cube prism and verify Euler-Poincaré.
Formula: \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\); cube has \(V=8,E=12,F=6,S=1,G=0\)
Hint: count corners, edges, faces, then check the left side equals \(2(1-0)\).

8 corners so \(V=8\), 12 edges so \(E=12\), 6 faces so \(F=6\)
\(V-E+F=8-12+6=2\)
\(S=1,G=0\Rightarrow 2(S-G)=2\)
\(\boxed{8-12+6=2=2(1-0)\ \checkmark}\)

Worked example (m site scale): set the sewing tolerance for a slab whose parts span \(L=2\) m.
Formula: \(\tau=\max(10^{-3}L,\,10^{-7})\)
Hint: compute the size-relative term \(10^{-3}\cdot 2\) first, then compare it to the \(10^{-7}\) floor.

Use \(\tau=\max(10^{-3}L,10^{-7})\) with \(L=2\)
\(10^{-3}\cdot 2=2\times10^{-3}\) m, well above the \(10^{-7}\) floor
\(\boxed{\tau=2\times10^{-3}\text{ m}=2\text{ mm}}\)

Worked example (mm shop scale): a carved detail has characteristic size \(L=5\) mm \(=0.005\) m. Compute \(\tau\).
Formula: \(\tau=\max(10^{-3}L,\,10^{-7})\)
Hint: work in metres: evaluate \(10^{-3}\cdot0.005\), then take the max with \(10^{-7}\).

\(\tau=\max(10^{-3}\cdot0.005,\,10^{-7})=\max(5\times10^{-6},\,10^{-7})\)
\(5\times10^{-6}>10^{-7}\), so the size-relative term wins
\(\boxed{\tau=5\times10^{-6}\text{ m}=5\ \mu\text{m}}\)

Worked example (site/large scale): a quarry block model has \(L=40\) m. Compute \(\tau\) and contrast with the mm-scale case.
Formula: \(\tau=\max(10^{-3}L,\,10^{-7})\)
Hint: evaluate \(10^{-3}\cdot40\); the floor \(10^{-7}\) is irrelevant here.

\(\tau=\max(10^{-3}\cdot40,\,10^{-7})=\max(0.04,\,10^{-7})\)
\(\boxed{\tau=0.04\text{ m}=40\text{ mm}}\)
40 mm at site scale vs 5 µm at detail scale shows why \(\tau\) must be size-relative, not a fixed constant

Worked example (boolean Cut): from \(A=[0,1]^3\) cut \(B=[0.5,1.5]^3\). Find the overlap region and verify Euler on the result.
Formula: overlap \(=A\cap B\); check \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\) with result counts \(V=10,E=15,F=7\)
Hint: intersect the two boxes coordinate-by-coordinate first, then plug the post-cut counts into Euler.

Overlap is the intersection \([0.5,1]^3\), a corner of \(A\)
the notched solid has \(V=10,E=15,F=7\)
\(\boxed{10-15+7=2=2(1-0)\ \checkmark}\) still a valid genus-0 single shell

Worked example (prism thickness, m scale): sweep a fitted top face along \(\vec d=(0,0,0.3)\) m. Give the slab thickness.
Formula: \(h=\lVert\vec d\rVert\)
Hint: the vector is axis-aligned, so its length is just the magnitude of the nonzero component.

Thickness is the sweep length \(h=\lVert\vec d\rVert=\lVert(0,0,0.3)\rVert\)
\(\boxed{h=0.3\text{ m}=300\text{ mm}}\)

Worked example (oblique prism, mm scale): sweep a face along \(\vec d=(3,4,12)\) mm. Compute the slab thickness \(h\).
Formula: \(h=\lVert\vec d\rVert=\sqrt{d_x^2+d_y^2+d_z^2}\)
Hint: square the three components, add, and take the square root (it is a Pythagorean triple).

Closing test (close the loop): verify Euler-Poincaré for a triangular prism (\(V=6,E=9,F=5\)) as a single genus-0 shell.
Formula: \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\), with \(S=1,\ G=0\)
Hint: a pass means \(V-E+F\) comes out to \(2(1-0)=2\).

\(V-E+F=6-9+5=2\)
single shell \(S=1\), no handle \(G=0\Rightarrow2(S-G)=2\)
\(\boxed{2=2\ \checkmark}\) the necessary condition holds (analyzer still needed for sufficiency)

Closing test (close the loop): cut a cylindrical through-hole in a slab so genus \(G=1\). Predict \(V-E+F\) and check the torus limit.
Formula: \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\), with \(S=1,\ G=1\)
Hint: a pass means the prediction equals the known one-handle (torus) Euler characteristic of \(0\).

From \(V-E+F=2(S-G)\) with \(S=1,G=1\): predicted value \(=2(1-1)=0\)
verify against the known torus regime: a one-handle solid indeed gives Euler characteristic \(0\)
\(\boxed{V-E+F=0\ \checkmark}\) matches the genus-1 limiting case

Closing test (orientation sanity check): a face's \(\sigma\) flag flips FORWARD\(\to\)REVERSED. Show the effect on \(\hat n_{\text{out}}\) and why Cut then fails.
Formula: \(\hat n_{\text{out}}=\sigma\,\hat n_{uv}\), with \(\sigma:+1\to-1\)
Hint: a pass is realizing the normal negates, so the inside/outside classification inverts.

\(\hat n_{\text{out}}=\sigma\,\hat n_{uv}\), so \(\sigma:+1\to-1\) sends \(\hat n_{\text{out}}\to-\hat n_{\text{out}}\)
the boolean classifies face centers by the outward normal; a reversed normal labels "inside" as "outside"
\(\boxed{\text{Cut removes the wrong region (inside-out solid)}}\) confirming \(\sigma\) must be consistent before any boolean

When do you tessellate in the pipeline, and why not earlier?
Hint: think about which gate must pass first, and what a mesh can hide.
Last, only after `BRepCheck_Analyzer.IsValid()` passes; meshing a broken B-Rep hides the defect behind plausible triangles that still reach IFC.

Is Euler-Poincaré (\(V-E+F=2(S-G)\)) sufficient to prove a B-Rep is valid?
Hint: recall it is a one-way test; ask what it cannot see.
No. It is necessary only; a self-intersecting or misoriented solid can still satisfy it. The real gate is `BRepCheck_Analyzer`.

Name the OCCT topological hierarchy from highest to lowest cell.
Hint: eight names, starting at `Compound` and ending at `Vertex`.
Compound, CompSolid, Solid, Shell, Face, Wire, Edge, Vertex.

What are the three set-operation booleans and their OCCT classes?
Hint: union / difference / intersection map to Fuse / Cut / Common.
Union `BRepAlgoAPI_Fuse`, difference `BRepAlgoAPI_Cut`, intersection `BRepAlgoAPI_Common` (and `_Section` for intersection curves only).

Pitfall: why is `BRepAlgoAPI_Cut` order-sensitive?
Formula: \(A\setminus B\ne B\setminus A\)
Hint: set difference is not commutative; identify which argument is kept.
Difference is non-commutative, \(A\setminus B\ne B\setminus A\); always pass (shape-to-keep, shape-to-remove).

What does `SetFuzzyValue(f)` do and what is its default?
Hint: it is an extra coincidence tolerance; recall the default is zero.
Adds tolerance \(f\) so near-coincident entities are treated as touching; default is \(0\) (no fuzzing). Raise it near the real gap size for imported/scan geometry.
An OCCT `Edge` carries a 3D curve plus one or two {{c1::p-curves}} (the curve in each adjacent face's (u,v) space).
CAD · B-Rep & Booleans
The kernel requires the tolerance nesting {{c1::\(t_V \ge t_E \ge t_F\)}} so the vertex sphere contains the edge tube contains the face slab.
CAD · B-Rep & Booleans
After sewing, a watertight 2-manifold solid has {{c1::zero}} free edges and every edge is contiguous (shared by two faces).
CAD · B-Rep & Booleans

OCCT's default angular deflection is {{c1::0.5}} rad, while the linear deflection has {{c2::no default}} (the caller must supply it).
hint: one is a fixed radian value, the other the caller must always pass.
CAD · B-Rep & Booleans
Default `Precision::Confusion()` in OCCT model units is {{c1::\(10^{-7}\)}}.
CAD · B-Rep & Booleans

Derive the volumetric IoU score (step 1 of 2): partition the union of two solids into shared, excess, and missing volumes.
Formula: \(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)=\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)+\mathrm{Vol}(A\setminus B)+\mathrm{Vol}(B\setminus A)\)
Hint: the three pieces are disjoint; rename \(A\setminus B\) as excess \(R^-\) and \(B\setminus A\) as missing \(R^+\).

The union splits into three disjoint pieces: shared \(A\cap B\), excess \(A\setminus B\), missing \(B\setminus A\)
rename \(A\setminus B=R^-\) and \(B\setminus A=R^+\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)=\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)+\mathrm{Vol}(R^-)+\mathrm{Vol}(R^+)}\).

Derive the IoU score (step 2 of 2) from the partition.
Formula: \(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)=\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)+R^-+R^+\) and \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\text{shared}}{\text{combined}}\)
Hint: put shared volume over the partitioned union, then collapse the denominator back to \(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)\).

IoU is the fraction of the combined region the solids share
\(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)}{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)+R^-+R^+}\)
collapse the denominator
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}(A,B)=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)}{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)}}\).

Derive the CADFit reconstruction objective from "find the program whose solid best matches the target."
Formula: score \(=\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})\); target \(\Pi^\*=\arg\max_{\Pi\in\mathcal{S}}\,\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})\)
Hint: wrap the IoU score in an \(\arg\max\) over the space \(\mathcal{S}\) of valid programs.

A program's quality is \(\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})\)
maximize it over \(\mathcal{S}\), the syntactically and geometrically valid programs
\(\boxed{\Pi^\*=\arg\max_{\Pi\in\mathcal{S}}\,\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})}\).

Derive the forward greedy gain (step 1 of 2): the value of adding candidate \(c\) to current solid \(S\).
Formula: \(\mathrm{gain}(c\mid S)=\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\)
Hint: adding \(c\) updates the solid to \(S\cup c\); gain is value-after minus value-before.

Adding \(c\) updates the solid to \(S\cup c\), with value \(\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})\)
marginal gain is value-after minus value-before
\(\boxed{\mathrm{gain}(c\mid S)=\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})}\).

Derive which candidate the greedy step picks (step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(c^\*=\arg\max_{c\in\mathcal{C}\setminus\Pi}\big[\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\big]\)
Hint: take the \(\arg\max\) of the gain over unused candidates \(\mathcal{C}\setminus\Pi\), and accept only if it is positive.

Greedy keeps the largest gain over unused candidates
pick \(c\) maximizing \(\mathrm{gain}(c\mid S)\), accept only if the max is \(>0\)
\(\boxed{c^\*=\arg\max_{c\in\mathcal{C}\setminus\Pi}\big[\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\big]}\).

Derive the backward-pruning marginal \(\Delta^-(c\mid S)\) for removing op \(c\).
Formula: \(\Delta^-(c\mid S)=\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi\setminus\{c\}),\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\)
Hint: removing \(c\) gives program \(\Pi\setminus\{c\}\); the effect is value-after-removal minus current value.

Removing \(c\) gives program \(\Pi\setminus\{c\}\) with solid \(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi\setminus\{c\})\)
effect is value-after-removal minus current value
\(\boxed{\Delta^-(c\mid S)=\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi\setminus\{c\}),\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})}\)
remove the candidate with the largest (least negative) \(\Delta^-\).

Derive the two residual sets used in refinement from "what is missing" and "what is excess."
Formula: \(R^+=\mathcal{M}\setminus S\), \(R^-=S\setminus\mathcal{M}\)
Hint: missing = target minus current solid (Union it in); excess = solid minus target (Cut it out).

Missing = target not yet covered by \(S\): \(\mathcal{M}\setminus S\)
excess = solid not in the target: \(S\setminus\mathcal{M}\)
\(\boxed{R^+=\mathcal{M}\setminus S\ (\text{Union in}),\quad R^-=S\setminus\mathcal{M}\ (\text{Cut out})}\).

Compute \(\mathrm{IoU}(S_1,\mathcal{M})\) for the first L-bracket candidate: a \(2\times2\times1\) box \(S_1\) over the L-shaped target (true volume 3).
Formula: \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cap\mathcal{M})}{\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cup\mathcal{M})}\)
Hint: the box contains the whole L, so \(S_1\cap\mathcal{M}=\mathcal{M}\) and \(S_1\cup\mathcal{M}=S_1\); compute \(\mathrm{Vol}(S_1)\) first.

\(\mathrm{Vol}(S_1)=2\cdot2\cdot1=4\); box contains the L so \(\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cap\mathcal{M})=3\)
union: box already contains L, so \(\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cup\mathcal{M})=4\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}(S_1,\mathcal{M})=\tfrac34=0.75}\).

From \(\mathrm{IoU}(S_1,\mathcal{M})=0.75\), compute the IoU after Cutting the corner cube, and the greedy gain.
Formula: \(R^-=S_1\setminus\mathcal{M}\), \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(S_2\cap\mathcal{M})}{\mathrm{Vol}(S_2\cup\mathcal{M})}\), \(\mathrm{gain}=\mathrm{IoU}(S_2)-\mathrm{IoU}(S_1)\)
Hint: the Cut removes the \(1\times1\times1\) corner of volume 1, making \(S_2=\mathcal{M}\) exactly.

Cut removes \(R^-=S_1\setminus\mathcal{M}\) (corner volume 1), so \(\mathrm{Vol}(S_2)=4-1=3\) and \(S_2=\mathcal{M}\)
intersection \(=3\), union \(=3\)
\(\mathrm{IoU}(S_2,\mathcal{M})=\tfrac33=1.0\); gain \(=1.0-0.75=0.25>0\) so kept
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}(S_2,\mathcal{M})=1.0}\).

Worked example (3D, different numbers): a \(3\times3\times2\) slab with a centered \(1\times1\times2\) through-hole. Compute \(\mathrm{IoU}(S_1,\mathcal{M})\) after the extrude-only box \(S_1\).
Formula: \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cap\mathcal{M})}{\mathrm{Vol}(S_1\cup\mathcal{M})}\)
Hint: the solid box contains the holed target, so intersection \(=\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\) and union \(=\mathrm{Vol}(S_1)\); subtract the hole to get \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\).

\(\mathrm{Vol}(S_1)=3\cdot3\cdot2=18\); hole \(=1\cdot1\cdot2=2\) so \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})=18-2=16\)
box contains target: intersection \(=16\), union \(=18\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}(S_1,\mathcal{M})=\tfrac{16}{18}\approx0.889}\), then \(1.0\) after the Cut.

Worked example (mm shop scale): the true sawn slab is \(300\times200\times40\) mm; candidate \(S\) is over-swept to \(300\times200\times50\) mm. Compute \(\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\).
Formula: \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(S\cap\mathcal{M})}{\mathrm{Vol}(S\cup\mathcal{M})}\)
Hint: \(S\) contains \(\mathcal{M}\) (same footprint, deeper), so intersection \(=\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\) and union \(=\mathrm{Vol}(S)\); compute both volumes first.

\(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})=300\cdot200\cdot40=2.4\times10^6\); \(\mathrm{Vol}(S)=300\cdot200\cdot50=3.0\times10^6\ \mathrm{mm^3}\)
intersection \(=2.4\times10^6\), union \(=3.0\times10^6\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}=2.4/3.0=0.8}\), signalling depth is 10 mm too deep.

Worked example (greedy gain): current \(\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})=0.62\); adding a dowel-hole Cut raises it to \(0.74\). What is the gain, and is it accepted?
Formula: \(\mathrm{gain}(c\mid S)=\mathrm{IoU}(S\cup c,\mathcal{M})-\mathrm{IoU}(S,\mathcal{M})\), accept iff gain \(>0\)
Hint: subtract the before-IoU from the after-IoU, then check the sign.

Closing test (close the loop): plug the final L-bracket program back and confirm Vol and IoU.
