Formula: executed program Extrude(\(2\times2\), d=1) then Cut(\(1\times1\) corner, d=1); check \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi))=\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\) and \(\mathrm{IoU}=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(\cap)}{\mathrm{Vol}(\cup)}\)
Hint: a pass means executed volume equals \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})=3\) and IoU \(=1\).

Executed volume \(=4-1=3=\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\) (volumes match)
intersection \(=3\), union \(=3\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}=3/3=1.0}\), confirming exact reconstruction.

Closing test (limiting cases): show \(\mathrm{IoU}(A,A)=1\), \(\mathrm{IoU}(A,B)=0\) for disjoint solids, and that the range is \([0,1]\).
Formula: \(\mathrm{IoU}(A,B)=\dfrac{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)}{\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)}\)
Hint: substitute \(B=A\), then \(A\cap B=\varnothing\); a pass uses \(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)\le\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)\) for the bound.

Identical: \(A\cap A=A\), \(A\cup A=A\), so \(\mathrm{IoU}=\mathrm{Vol}(A)/\mathrm{Vol}(A)=1\)
disjoint: \(A\cap B=\varnothing\) so numerator \(=0\), \(\mathrm{IoU}=0\)
\(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)\le\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)\) always
\(\boxed{\mathrm{IoU}\in[0,1]}\), attaining 1 only at coincidence.

Write the symmetric Chamfer distance \(\mathrm{CD}(P,\hat P)\) between sampled point sets \(P\) (target) and \(\hat P\) (result).
Formula: \(\mathrm{CD}(P,\hat P)=\tfrac12\Big[\tfrac1{|P|}\sum_{x\in P}\min_{y\in\hat P}\lVert x-y\rVert_2+\tfrac1{|\hat P|}\sum_{y\in\hat P}\min_{x\in P}\lVert y-x\rVert_2\Big]\)
Hint: average each point's nearest-neighbour distance in both directions, then halve the sum.

For each \(x\in P\) take the nearest \(y\in\hat P\); average over \(P\)
do the same from \(\hat P\) to \(P\); halve the sum of the two means
\(\boxed{\mathrm{CD}(P,\hat P)=\tfrac12\Big[\tfrac1{|P|}\!\sum_{x\in P}\min_{y\in\hat P}\lVert x-y\rVert_2+\tfrac1{|\hat P|}\!\sum_{y\in\hat P}\min_{x\in P}\lVert y-x\rVert_2\Big]}\) (lower is better; catches drift IoU hides).

Construct the stone extended objective \(E(\Pi)\) from base CADFit plus four manufacturability penalties.
Formula: \(E(\Pi)=-\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})+\lambda_1 C_{\text{complexity}}+\lambda_2 C_{\text{fab}}+\lambda_3 C_{\text{assembly}}+\lambda_4 C_{\text{heritage}}\)
Hint: turn "maximize IoU" into "minimize \(-\mathrm{IoU}\)," then add four \(\lambda_i\)-weighted cost terms.

Maximizing IoU = minimizing \(-\mathrm{IoU}\)
add weighted costs for complexity, fabricability, assembly, heritage
\(\boxed{E(\Pi)=-\mathrm{IoU}(\mathrm{Solid}(\Pi),\mathcal{M})+\lambda_1 C_{\text{complexity}}+\lambda_2 C_{\text{fab}}+\lambda_3 C_{\text{assembly}}+\lambda_4 C_{\text{heritage}}}\)
all \(\lambda_i=0\) recovers base CADFit (a designed objective, not in the paper).

How is each operation represented formally?
Formula: \(o_t=(\tau_t,\theta_t,R_t)\)
Hint: name what each of the three slots holds.

A tuple \(o_t=(\tau_t,\theta_t,R_t)\): \(\tau_t\) type, \(\theta_t\) continuous params, \(R_t\) geometric references
\(\boxed{o_t=(\tau_t,\theta_t,R_t)}\).

What are the six CADFit operation types?
Hint: two add solids (sweep), two finish edges, two combine solids.

Sweeps: Extrude, Revolve
edge finishers: Fillet, Chamfer
booleans: Union, Cut
\(\boxed{\text{Extrude, Revolve, Fillet, Chamfer, Union, Cut}}\).

Pitfall: why must CADFit input be watertight?
Hint: think about what a single boundary gap does to \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\).

Volume, hence IoU and Chamfer distance, is undefined on an open mesh
one boundary gap makes \(\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})\) meaningless and the solid classifier returns garbage
\(\boxed{\text{close the mesh before fitting}}\).

Pitfall: why must fillet and chamfer run last?
Hint: recall that operations are non-commutative and which entities a fillet references.

Fillet/chamfer reference edges that earlier booleans create or destroy
operations are non-commutative: fillet-then-cut \(\ne\) cut-then-fillet
\(\boxed{\text{finish on edges that still exist, so do them last}}\).

When-to-use: apply CADFit vs leave geometry freeform on stone?
Hint: sort faces by whether you must dimension and tolerance them; the \(\lambda_4\) term enforces this.

CAD-ize sawn / slot / template / drilled faces you must dimension and tolerance
keep rough natural (tooled or weathered) surfaces freeform
\(\boxed{\text{gate via the } \lambda_4 \text{ heritage term or a hard mask}}\).

Map Extrude and Cut to OCCT classes.
Hint: Extrude is a linear sweep (prism); Cut is a boolean subtraction.

Map Revolve, Fillet, Union to OCCT classes.
Hint: Revolve sweeps about an axis; Fillet rounds edges; Union fuses.
The union partitions as \(\mathrm{Vol}(A\cup B)=\mathrm{Vol}(A\cap B)+{{c1::R^-}}+{{c2::R^+}}\), which is the denominator of IoU.
CAD · CADFit Synthesis
CADFit emits executable {{c1::CadQuery}} programs over an {{c2::OpenCASCADE}} kernel.
CAD · CADFit Synthesis
Targets are normalized to the box {{c1::\([-1,1]^3\)}} so IoU fitting is {{c2::scale invariant}}.
CAD · CADFit Synthesis
CADFit is released under {{c1::CC BY-NC 4.0}} with a {{c2::provisional patent}} filed, so commercial use needs licensing.
CAD · CADFit Synthesis

Build the rotation matrix \(R\) of an `IfcAxis2Placement3D` from the Axis \(\hat z\) and RefDirection \(x\) (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(x'=x-(x\cdot\hat z)\hat z,\ \ \hat x=x'/\lVert x'\rVert,\ \ \hat y=\hat z\times\hat x\)
Hint: first project \(x\) onto the plane perpendicular to \(\hat z\) (subtract the part along \(\hat z\)), then normalize.

\(\hat z\) is the unit Axis
remove any component of \(x\) along \(\hat z\): \(x' = x-(x\cdot\hat z)\hat z\), then \(\hat x = x'/\lVert x'\rVert\)
the third axis closes the right-handed frame: \(\hat y=\hat z\times\hat x\)
\(\boxed{R=[\,\hat x\ \ \hat y\ \ \hat z\,]}\) (columns)

From \(R=[\hat x\ \hat y\ \hat z]\) and origin \(t\), assemble the homogeneous local placement \(T\) (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(p_\text{parent}=Rp+t\), target \(T=\begin{bmatrix}R & t\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: write the affine map \(Rp+t\) as a single \(4\times4\) times \((p,1)\).

a local point \(p\) maps as \(p_\text{parent}=Rp+t\)
stack into homogeneous form so one matrix carries rotation and translation
\(\boxed{T=\begin{bmatrix}R & t\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}}\)

Each frame's placement is relative to its parent. Write the world transform of an element (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(T_\text{world}=T_\text{site}\,T_\text{building}\,T_\text{storey}\,T_\text{element}\)
Hint: start from \(T_\text{site}\,T_\text{building}\) and left-multiply each child placement by the accumulated parent transform.

composition runs parent-to-child: \(T_\text{world\leftarrow building}=T_\text{site}\,T_\text{building}\)
append storey then element, each left-multiplied by its parent's accumulated transform
\(\boxed{T_\text{world}=T_\text{site}\,T_\text{building}\,T_\text{storey}\,T_\text{element}}\)

Given \(T_\text{world}=T_\text{site}T_\text{building}T_\text{storey}T_\text{element}\), derive the world position of a local point \(p\) (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(\tilde p=(p,1),\ \ \tilde p_w=T_\text{world}\,\tilde p\)
Hint: lift \(p\) to homogeneous coordinates, apply \(T_\text{world}\) once, then drop the trailing 1.

lift \(p\) to homogeneous \(\tilde p=(p,1)\)
apply the composed transform once: \(\tilde p_w=T_\text{world}\,\tilde p\)
drop the homogeneous coordinate
\(\boxed{p_w=R_\text{world}\,p+t_\text{world}}\)

Coordinates are stored in model units; you need SI metres. Derive how the length-unit scale acts on a coordinate (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: \(s=\) metres per model unit (mm \(\Rightarrow s=10^{-3}\)), \(p_\text{SI}=s\,p\)
Hint: each stored value is a count of units, so multiply componentwise by \(s\).

let \(s\) = metres per model unit (mm \(\Rightarrow s=10^{-3}\))
each stored axis value is a count of units, so multiply: \(x_\text{SI}=s\,x\)
\(\boxed{p_\text{SI}=s\,p}\)

Starting from \(p_\text{SI}=s\,p\), derive the error factor from forgetting the unit scale (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \(p_\text{wrong}=p\), ratio \(p_\text{wrong}/p_\text{SI}=1/s\)
Hint: set the applied scale to \(1\) and take the ratio to the true \(p_\text{SI}\); plug \(s=10^{-3}\).

skipping the scale uses \(p\) directly as if metres: \(p_\text{wrong}=p\)
ratio to truth: \(p_\text{wrong}/p_\text{SI}=1/s\)
for mm, \(1/s=1000\)
\(\boxed{p_\text{wrong}=1000\,p_\text{SI}\ \text{(mm model)}}\)

Derive the matrix of a \(90^\circ\) rotation about \(Z\) to plug into a building placement (derive).
Formula: \(R_z(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: substitute \(\theta=90^\circ\) so \(\cos\theta=0,\ \sin\theta=1\).

Block point \(p=(1,0,0)\) m; storey translates by \((10,4,3)\); building applies \(90^\circ\) about \(Z\). Find \(p_\text{world}\) (worked, m scale).
Formula: \(p_1=p+(10,4,3)\), then \(R_z(90^\circ):(x,y,z)\to(-y,x,z)\)
Hint: apply the storey translation first, then the building rotation.

Same setup as the m-scale example, but the model length unit is millimetres and you forget to scale. Where does \(p\) land? (worked, mm regime).
Formula: true answer \((-4,11,3)\) m; unscaled error factor \(1/s=1000\)
Hint: multiply the correct metres answer by \(1000\).

true metres answer is \((-4,11,3)\)
unscaled treats mm-counts as metres, factor \(1/s=1000\)
\((-4,11,3)\times1000\)
\(\boxed{p_\text{wrong}=(-4000,11000,3000)\ \text{(off by 1000)}}\)

A 2 mm scan feature is stored in a metre-unit IFC (\(s=1\)) but you wrongly assume mm and multiply by \(s_\text{assumed}=10^{-3}\). What size do you report? (worked, shop scale).
Formula: \(p_\text{reported}=p_\text{stored}\cdot s_\text{assumed}\), with \(p_\text{stored}=2\times10^{-3}\) m
Hint: the stored value is already in metres; just multiply it by the wrong \(10^{-3}\).

stored value is already metres: \(2\times10^{-3}\) m
wrong assumption multiplies by \(s_\text{assumed}=10^{-3}\): \(2\times10^{-3}\cdot10^{-3}\)
\(\boxed{2\times10^{-6}\ \text{m}=2\,\mu\text{m}}\) (1000x too small — scale bugs cut both ways)

Axis \(\hat z=(0,0,1)\), RefDirection \(x=(2,0,0)\), origin \(t=(5,5,0)\). Build the \(4\times4\) placement \(T\) (worked, 3D build).
Formula: \(\hat x=x/\lVert x\rVert,\ \ \hat y=\hat z\times\hat x,\ \ T=\begin{bmatrix}R & t\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\)
Hint: normalize \(x\) first (it is already perpendicular to \(\hat z\)), then form \(\hat y\) by the cross product.

Axis \(\hat z=(0,0,1)\), RefDirection \(x=(1,1,0)\). Find \(\hat x,\hat y\) (worked, non-axis-aligned X).
Formula: \(x'=x-(x\cdot\hat z)\hat z,\ \ \hat x=x'/\lVert x'\rVert,\ \ \hat y=\hat z\times\hat x\)
Hint: check \(x\cdot\hat z\) first (it is \(0\) here), then divide \(x\) by its length \(\sqrt2\).

Check that \(\hat x=(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2},\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2},0)\), \(\hat y=(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2},\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2},0)\), \(\hat z=(0,0,1)\) form a valid rotation (closing test).
Formula: \(R\in SO(3)\iff \hat x\cdot\hat y=0,\ \lVert\hat x\rVert=1,\ \det[\hat x\,\hat y\,\hat z]=+1\)
Hint: a pass means all three hold; compute the dot product, one norm, and the determinant.

\(\hat x\cdot\hat y=-\tfrac12+\tfrac12+0=0\) (orthogonal)
\(\lVert\hat x\rVert^2=\tfrac12+\tfrac12=1\) (unit)
\(\det[\hat x\,\hat y\,\hat z]=+1\) (right-handed)
\(\boxed{R\in SO(3)\ \checkmark}\)

World point \((-4,11,3)\) m. Invert the chain to recover \(p_\text{local}\) and confirm it is \((1,0,0)\) (closing test).
Formula: inverse rotation \((x,y,z)\to(y,-x,z)\), then subtract storey shift \((10,4,3)\)
Hint: undo in reverse order — rotation first, then the translation; a pass returns exactly \((1,0,0)\).

inverse rotation \((x,y,z)\to(y,-x,z)\): \((-4,11,3)\to(11,4,3)\)
subtract storey shift \((10,4,3)\): \((1,0,0)\)
matches the input
\(\boxed{p_\text{local}=(1,0,0)\ \checkmark}\)

A wall has width \(200\) in an IFC whose length unit is millimetre. Convert to metres and sanity-check it (closing test).
Formula: \(w_\text{SI}=s\,w\) with \(s=10^{-3}\) m/unit
Hint: multiply by \(10^{-3}\); a pass is a plausible wall thickness (tens of cm), not metres.

\(s=10^{-3}\) m/unit
\(200\times10^{-3}=0.2\) m
\(0.2\) m \(=20\) cm, a sensible wall thickness
\(\boxed{0.2\ \text{m}\ \checkmark}\) (had we read it as metres, \(200\) m is absurd — the check catches it)

When do you export tessellation vs an advanced B-Rep?
Hint: weigh universal viewer support against exact curvature.

Tessellated (`IfcTriangulatedFaceSet`) when universal viewer support matters; `IfcAdvancedBrep` when exact curvature must be preserved and consumers support IFC4.
Ship a tessellated fallback if interoperability outweighs exactness.

What does IFC add beyond a mesh?
Hint: think semantics, not just triangles.
Semantics — named building elements, materials, placements, and parent/containment relationships, i.e. an entity graph, not just loose triangles.

Why must you compose the whole placement chain rather than read one element's frame?
Hint: recall what `IfcLocalPlacement` is relative to.
Each `IfcLocalPlacement` is relative to its parent, so a single frame is incomplete; compose the full chain (or request `use-world-coords` to bake placements into vertices).

Why validate B-Reps before IFC export?
Hint: think about what a consumer does with invalid topology.
Invalid topology or out-of-tolerance edges make downstream consumers choke; check with `BRepCheck_Analyzer` first.

When do you use IfcOpenShell's geometry iterator?
Hint: consider model size and repeated geometry.
On large models — it gives multicore processing, caching, and reuse of repeated geometry, returning per-element vertex/face arrays plus the 4x4 placement.
An `IfcAxis2Placement3D` is defined by an origin, a {{c1::Z axis}} (Axis) and an {{c2::X axis}} (RefDirection); the remaining axis is rebuilt as \(Y=Z\times X\).
CAD · IFC / BIM

The world transform is the product of relative placements up the chain, \(T_\text{world}=T_\text{site}\,T_\text{building}\,T_\text{storey}\,T_\text{element}\), composed {{c1::parent-to-child}} (each parent left-multiplies).
CAD · IFC / BIM
To reach SI metres, multiply every coordinate by the length-unit scale \(s\); for a millimetre model \(s={{c1::10^{-3}}}\), and forgetting it scales coordinates by {{c2::1000}}.
CAD · IFC / BIM
IfcOpenShell's `create_shape` can return triangulated geometry or an {{c1::OpenCASCADE}} B-Rep (`TopoDS_Shape`).
CAD · IFC / BIM

The objective is \(J(\theta)\) and you are at a point where the gradient \(\nabla_\theta J\) points uphill. Write the gradient-descent update that steps \(\theta\) downhill.
Formula: \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\,\nabla_\theta J(\theta)\)
Hint: downhill means the direction opposite the gradient; scale that direction by a small step size \(\eta>0\).

Steepest descent moves opposite the gradient: direction \(-\nabla_\theta J\)
scale by step size \(\eta>0\)
\(\boxed{\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\,\nabla_\theta J(\theta)}\).

Find the gradient of the regularized risk \(J( heta)= rac1N\sum_i\ell(f_ heta(x_i),y_i)+\lambda R( heta)\) with \(R( heta)= frac12\lVert heta Vert^2\) (derive, step 1 of 2).
Formula: target \( abla_ heta J= rac1N\sum_i abla_ heta\ell+\lambda heta\); use \( abla_ heta ig( frac12\lVert heta Vert^2 ig)= heta\)
Hint: the gradient is linear, so differentiate the data term and the penalty term separately.

Differentiate term by term
data term: \( abla_ heta ig[ rac1N\sum_i\ell ig]= rac1N\sum_i abla_ heta\ell(f_ heta(x_i),y_i)\)
penalty term: \( abla_ heta ig[ frac{\lambda}{2}\lVert heta Vert^2 ig]=\lambda heta\)
\( oxed{ abla_ heta J= rac1N\sum_i abla_ heta\ell+\lambda heta}\).

From \( abla_ heta J= rac1N\sum_i abla_ heta\ell+\lambda heta\), show that L2 regularization makes each GD step shrink \( heta\) — "weight decay" (derive, step 2 of 2).
Formula: \( heta\leftarrow heta-\eta abla_ heta J\); target \( heta\leftarrow(1-\eta\lambda) heta- frac{\eta}{N}\sum_i abla_ heta\ell\)
Hint: substitute the gradient into the update, then collect the two \( heta\) terms.

Substitute into \( heta\leftarrow heta-\eta abla_ heta J\)
\( heta\leftarrow heta-\eta ig( rac1N\sum_i abla_ heta\ell ig)-\eta\lambda heta\)
group the \( heta\) terms: \( heta\leftarrow(1-\eta\lambda) heta- rac{\eta}{N}\sum_i abla_ heta\ell\)
\( oxed{ heta\leftarrow(1-\eta\lambda) heta- frac{\eta}{N} extstyle\sum_i abla_ heta\ell}\) — each step shrinks \( heta\) by factor \((1-\eta\lambda)\).

Given logits \(z\in\mathbb{R}^K\), build probabilities \(p_k\) that are all positive and sum to 1 (derive softmax, step 1 of 3).
Formula: target \(p_k=\dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}}\)
Hint: first apply a function that makes everything positive, then divide by the total so the entries sum to 1.

Exponentiate to force positivity: \(e^{z_k}>0\)
normalize so the vector sums to 1: divide by \(\sum_j e^{z_j}\)
\(\boxed{p_k=\dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}}}\).
▶ 3Blue1Brown — But what is a neural network? · NN Ch.1

From \(p_k=e^{z_k}/\sum_j e^{z_j}\), find the softmax Jacobian \(\partial p_k/\partial z_m\) (derive, step 2 of 3).
Formula: quotient rule \(\frac{d}{dz}\frac{u}{S}=\frac{u'S-uS'}{S^2}\); target \(\partial p_k/\partial z_m=p_k(\delta_{km}-p_m)\)
Hint: let \(S=\sum_j e^{z_j}\) and split into the cases \(m=k\) and \(m\neq k\).

Quotient rule with \(S=\sum_j e^{z_j}\)
if \(m=k\): \(\partial p_k/\partial z_k=\frac{e^{z_k}S-e^{z_k}e^{z_k}}{S^2}=p_k(1-p_k)\)
if \(m\neq k\): \(\partial p_k/\partial z_m=\frac{-e^{z_k}e^{z_m}}{S^2}=-p_kp_m\)
\(\boxed{\partial p_k/\partial z_m=p_k(\delta_{km}-p_m)}\).
▶ 3Blue1Brown — But what is a neural network? · NN Ch.1

From \(\ell=-\sum_k y_k\log p_k\) and \(\partial p_k/\partial z_m=p_k(\delta_{km}-p_m)\) with one-hot \(y\), derive the cross-entropy logit gradient \(\partial\ell/\partial z_m\) (step 3 of 3).
Formula: chain rule \(\frac{\partial\ell}{\partial z_m}=\sum_k\frac{\partial\ell}{\partial p_k}\frac{\partial p_k}{\partial z_m}\); target \(\partial\ell/\partial z_m=p_m-y_m\)
Hint: use \(\partial\ell/\partial p_k=-y_k/p_k\), cancel the \(p_k\), then use \(\sum_k y_k=1\).

\(\frac{\partial\ell}{\partial z_m}=-\sum_k\frac{y_k}{p_k}\,p_k(\delta_{km}-p_m)=-\sum_k y_k(\delta_{km}-p_m)\)
\(=-y_m+p_m\sum_k y_k\), and \(\sum_k y_k=1\)
\(\boxed{\partial\ell/\partial z_m=p_m-y_m}\) (predicted minus true).

For a network \(a^{(l+1)}=g_{l+1}(a^{(l)})\) with adjoint \(\delta^{(l)}=\partial\ell/\partial a^{(l)}\), derive the backward recursion relating \(\delta^{(l)}\) to \(\delta^{(l+1)}\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: chain rule; target \(\delta^{(l)}=\big(\partial a^{(l+1)}/\partial a^{(l)}\big)^{\top}\delta^{(l+1)}\)
Hint: route \(\partial\ell/\partial a^{(l)}\) through \(a^{(l+1)}\), then recognize \(\partial\ell/\partial a^{(l+1)}\) as \(\delta^{(l+1)}\).

Chain rule through the next layer's input
\(\dfrac{\partial\ell}{\partial a^{(l)}}=\Big(\dfrac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}}\Big)^{\!\top}\dfrac{\partial\ell}{\partial a^{(l+1)}}\)
recognize the right factor as \(\delta^{(l+1)}\)
\(\boxed{\delta^{(l)}=\big(\partial a^{(l+1)}/\partial a^{(l)}\big)^{\top}\delta^{(l+1)}}\).

With layer params entering via \(a^{(l)}=g_l(a^{(l-1)};\theta_l)\) and cached adjoint \(\delta^{(l)}\), derive the parameter gradient \(\nabla_{\theta_l}\ell\) (step 2 of 2).
Formula: chain rule through \(a^{(l)}\); target \(\nabla_{\theta_l}\ell=\big(\partial a^{(l)}/\partial\theta_l\big)^{\top}\delta^{(l)}\)
Hint: the only path from \(\theta_l\) to the loss runs through \(a^{(l)}\); multiply the layer Jacobian transpose by \(\delta^{(l)}\).

Chain rule from loss to \(\theta_l\) routes through \(a^{(l)}\)
\(\nabla_{\theta_l}\ell=\Big(\dfrac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial\theta_l}\Big)^{\!\top}\dfrac{\partial\ell}{\partial a^{(l)}}\)
\(\boxed{\nabla_{\theta_l}\ell=\big(\partial a^{(l)}/\partial\theta_l\big)^{\top}\delta^{(l)}}\) — a layer-Jacobian-transpose times the cached adjoint.

For squared loss \(\ell= frac12\lVert r( heta) Vert^2\) with residual \(r\) and Jacobian \(J_r=\partial r/\partial heta\), derive \( abla_ heta\ell\) and show it equals the Gauss-Newton right-hand side.
Formula: chain rule \( abla_ heta\ell=(\partial r/\partial heta)^{ op}\,\partial\ell/\partial r\); target \( abla_ heta\ell=J_r^{ op}r\)
Hint: first compute the inner derivative \(\partial\ell/\partial r\) for \(\ell= frac12\lVert r Vert^2\).

Chain rule: \(\nabla_\theta\ell=\big(\partial r/\partial\theta\big)^{\top}\,\partial\ell/\partial r\)
\(\partial\ell/\partial r=r\)
\(\boxed{\nabla_\theta\ell=J_r^{\top}r}\) — the Gauss-Newton right-hand side, identical to calibration/ICP/IK.

\(\lVert\theta\rVert\) keeps \(0.999\) of its value each step
per 100 steps: \(0.999^{100}\approx 0.905\), \(\boxed{\approx 9.5\%\text{ decay}}\).

Closing test (the step moved \(p\) the right way): after the logistic step \((w,b)=(0.3333,0.1667)\), recompute \(p_1\) for point 1 (\(x=1,y=1\)) and check it moved toward the target.

Closing test (loss decreased): stepped logistic model gives \(p_1=0.6225,\ p_2=0.6971,\ p_3=0.7625\) for targets \(1,0,1\). Compute the mean loss and check it fell below the initial \(0.6931\).
Formula: \(\ell_i=-[y_i\log p_i+(1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)]\), then average over \(N=3\)
Hint: a pass means the mean is \(<0.6931\); for \(y=0\) use the \(-\log(1-p)\) branch.

Closing test (stability/limit check): in the weight-decay update \(\theta\leftarrow(1-\eta\lambda)\theta\), what range of \(\eta\lambda\) keeps it a stable shrink, and what happens as \(\lambda\to0\)?
Formula: stable shrink requires \(|1-\eta\lambda|<1\)
Hint: solve \(|1-\eta\lambda|<1\) for \(\eta\lambda\); then set \(\lambda=0\) in the factor.

need \(0\le\eta\lambda<1\) so \(|1-\eta\lambda|<1\) (shrinks, not flips/grows)
limit \(\lambda\to0\): factor \(\to1\Rightarrow\) no decay, plain GD recovered
\(\boxed{0\le\eta\lambda<1,\ \ \lambda\to0\Rightarrow\text{plain GD}}\).

When-to-use: which loss for predicting a continuous geometric target (a per-point normal) versus a discrete label (stone/not-stone)?
Formula: regression \(\ell=\tfrac12\lVert\hat y-y\rVert^2\); classification softmax + cross-entropy with adjoint \(p-y\)
Hint: match the loss to the output type — continuous output to squared error, discrete output to cross-entropy.

Continuous: squared error \(\tfrac12\lVert\hat y-y\rVert^2\) (the NLS residual)
Discrete: softmax + cross-entropy, so the logit adjoint stays \(p-y\)
\(\boxed{\text{regression}\to\text{MSE},\ \text{classification}\to\text{softmax-CE}}\).

Pitfall: why use plain first-order SGD for deep nets instead of Gauss-Newton?
Formula: Gauss-Newton needs \(J^{\top}J\in\mathbb{R}^{p\times p}\) formed and inverted
Hint: think about the size of \(p\) (the parameter count) for a deep network.

Gauss-Newton forms and inverts \(J^{\top}J\), which is \(p\times p\)

\(\boxed{p\text{ too large}\Rightarrow\text{first-order descent}}\).

Formula: central difference \(\big(J(\theta+e)-J(\theta-e)\big)/(2e)\) with \(e\approx10^{-5}\)
Hint: a pass means the finite-difference value matches the analytic gradient elementwise to about 5 decimals.

Perturb each component by \(e\approx10^{-5}\) and form \((J(\theta+e)-J(\theta-e))/(2e)\)
compare elementwise to analytic \(\nabla J\)
agreement to ~5 decimals \(\Rightarrow\) \(\boxed{\text{gradient verified}}\).

▶ 3Blue1Brown — But what is a neural network? · NN Ch.1

Derive the raw similarity score between one query \(q\) and the \(m\) keys (rows of \(K\)), starting from "compare query to key by content match."
Formula: dot-product similarity \(s_j = q\cdot k_j = \sum_t q_t k_{jt}\); target \(s = qK^\top\)
Hint: write the single dot product first, then stack the \(m\) of them as one row times \(K^\top\).

An item compares its query \(q\) to each key \(k_j\) by inner product: \(s_j = q\cdot k_j = \sum_t q_t k_{jt}\)
stacking the \(m\) scores, \(k_j\) are the rows of \(K\) so \(q\cdot k_j\) is the \(j\)-th entry of \(qK^\top\)
\(\boxed{s_j = q\cdot k_j,\quad s = qK^\top}\)

From the score vector \(s=qK^\top\), derive the scaled scores and justify the divisor.
Formula: for unit-variance entries in \(\mathbb{R}^{d_k}\), \(\mathrm{Var}(q\cdot k)=d_k\); target \(\tilde s = \dfrac{qK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\)
Hint: divide by the standard deviation (the square root of the variance) to renormalize to variance \(\approx 1\).

Each score \(q\cdot k=\sum_{t=1}^{d_k} q_t k_t\) is a sum of \(d_k\) unit-variance terms, so \(\mathrm{Var}(q\cdot k)=d_k\) and the typical magnitude is \(\sqrt{d_k}\)
divide by the standard deviation \(\sqrt{d_k}\) so the scaled variance is \(\approx 1\), stopping the softmax from saturating
\(\boxed{\tilde s = \dfrac{qK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}}\)

From scaled scores \(\tilde s\), derive the weights and the final per-query output \(o\).
Formula: \(w_j = e^{\tilde s_j}/\sum_l e^{\tilde s_l}\) and \(o = \sum_j w_j v_j\); target \(o = \mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{qK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V\)
Hint: turn scores into weights with softmax first, then take the weighted sum of the value rows \(v_j\).

Softmax the scaled scores into weights that sum to 1: \(w_j = e^{\tilde s_j}/\sum_l e^{\tilde s_l}\)
read a weighted blend of the value rows \(v_j\) of \(V\): \(o = \sum_j w_j v_j = wV\)
\(\boxed{o = \mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{qK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V}\)

Promote the single-query attention result to the full matrix form for \(n\) queries.
Formula: single query gives \(o = \mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{qK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V\); target \(\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V\)
Hint: stack the \(n\) query rows into \(Q\) and apply the softmax row-wise.

Stack \(n\) queries as rows of \(Q\); the scaled scores become the \(n\times m\) matrix \(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\)
apply softmax to each row (each row's weights sum to 1), then right-multiply by \(V\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V}\)

Derive the softmax of a vector \(z\) as a normalized exponential.
Formula: requirements: \(w_i>0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\), monotone in \(z_i\); target \(\mathrm{softmax}(z)_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}\)
Hint: exponentiate to force positivity, then divide by the total to normalize.

To make weights positive and monotone in \(z_i\), exponentiate: \(e^{z_i}>0\) and increasing in \(z_i\)
normalize by the sum so the weights add to 1
\(\boxed{\mathrm{softmax}(z)_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}}\)

Derive the numerically stable softmax using shift invariance.
Formula: \(\mathrm{softmax}(z)_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}\); target \(\dfrac{e^{z_i-\max_j z_j}}{\sum_l e^{z_l-\max_j z_j}}\)
Hint: multiply top and bottom by \(e^{-c}\) and pick \(c=\max_j z_j\) so the largest exponent becomes \(0\).

For any constant \(c\), multiply numerator and denominator by \(e^{-c}\): \(\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}=\dfrac{e^{z_i-c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j-c}}\) (the factor cancels)
choose \(c=\max_j z_j\) so the largest exponent is \(0\) and every \(e^{z_i-c}\le 1\) (no overflow)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{softmax}(z)_i=\dfrac{e^{z_i-\max_j z_j}}{\sum_l e^{z_l-\max_j z_j}}}\)

From \(Q=XW_Q,\ K=XW_K,\ V=XW_V\), derive the self-attention output of input \(X\).
Formula: \(\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V\) with the three projections above
Hint: just substitute the three projection expressions into the attention formula.

All three of \(Q,K,V\) are linear projections of the same input \(X\)
substitute into scaled dot-product attention
\(\boxed{\mathrm{SelfAttn}(X)=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{(XW_Q)(XW_K)^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)XW_V}\)

Show that self-attention is permutation-equivariant: reorder the inputs and the outputs reorder the same way.
Formula: permutation matrix \(P\) with \(P^\top P=I\) sends \(X\to PX\); target \(\mathrm{Attn}(PX)=P\,\mathrm{Attn}(X)\)
Hint: push \(P\) through the projections, then simplify the scores \((PQ)(PK)^\top\) and use that softmax acts row-wise.

Permuting inputs by \(P\) gives \(Q\to PQ\), \(K\to PK\), \(V\to PV\)
scores become \((PQ)(PK)^\top=P(QK^\top)P^\top\); row-wise softmax with the trailing \(P^\top\) relabels columns, and the result times \(PV\) relabels rows by \(P\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{Attn}(PX)=P\,\mathrm{Attn}(X)}\)

Compute the attention weights (2D, \(d_k=2\)): \(q=(1,0)\), keys \(k_1=(1,0),\,k_2=(0,1),\,k_3=(1,1)\).
Formula: \(w=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{q\cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)\) with \(e^{0.707}\approx2.028,\ e^{0}=1\)
Hint: compute the three dot products first, then divide each by \(\sqrt2\approx1.414\).

Close the loop: with \(w\approx(0.401,0.198,0.401)\) and values equal to the keys \((1,0),(0,1),(1,1)\), compute the output and check the weights are a valid distribution.
Formula: \(o=\sum_j w_j v_j\); valid iff \(\sum_j w_j = 1\)
Hint: form the weighted sum component-wise, then add the weights — a pass means they total \(1.000\).

Worked example (small scale, \(d_k=1\)): scaled scores \(s=(2,0)\), values \(v_1=10,\ v_2=0\). Compute the attention output.
Formula: \(w=\mathrm{softmax}(s/\sqrt{d_k})\), \(o=\sum_j w_j v_j\), with \(e^{2}\approx7.389,\ e^{0}=1\)
Hint: \(\sqrt{1}=1\) so the scores are unchanged; exponentiate and normalize first.

Worked example (large-\(d_k\), why scaling matters): two raw dot products are \(20\) and \(0\) at \(d_k=64\). Compare the top weight with vs without the \(1/\sqrt{d_k}\) scale.
Formula: \(w_1=\dfrac{e^{s_1}}{e^{s_1}+e^{s_2}}\); scaled uses \(s/\sqrt{64}\), with \(\sqrt{64}=8\) and \(e^{2.5}\approx12.18\)
Hint: compute \(w_1\) for the raw scores \((20,0)\), then for the scaled scores \((2.5,0)\).

Unscaled: \(w_1=e^{20}/(e^{20}+1)\approx 1-2\times10^{-9}\) — saturated, near-zero gradient
scaled by \(\sqrt{64}=8\): scores \((2.5,0)\), \(w_1=e^{2.5}/(e^{2.5}+1)\approx0.924\) — soft, useful gradient
\(\boxed{\text{scaling keeps the softmax in its informative range}}\)

Worked example (uniform case, 3 keys): scaled scores are all equal, \(\tilde s=(c,c,c)\). Find the weights and the output.
Formula: \(w_j=e^{\tilde s_j}/\sum_l e^{\tilde s_l}\), \(o=\sum_j w_j v_j\)
Hint: equal numerators over their sum — what does each \(w_j\) reduce to?

\(w_j=e^{c}/(3e^{c})=1/3\) for every \(j\)
the output is the plain mean of the values
\(\boxed{o=\tfrac13(v_1+v_2+v_3)}\)

Limiting case: as inverse temperature \(\beta\to\infty\) (scores scaled by \(\beta\)), what does attention compute? Verify on two scores \(\tilde s=(3,1)\).
Formula: \(w_1=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-\beta(\tilde s_1-\tilde s_2)}}\)
Hint: plug the score gap \(3-1=2\) in; let \(\beta\to\infty\) to see the limit, then set \(\beta=1\) to verify it is already leaning.

With gap \(\Delta=3-1=2\): \(w_1=1/(1+e^{-2\beta})\to 1\) as \(\beta\to\infty\), so all weight goes to the max-score key and \(o\to v_{\arg\max}\) (hard lookup)
check at \(\beta=1\): \(w_1=1/(1+e^{-2})=0.881\), already leaning to \(v_1\); larger \(\beta\) pushes it toward \(1\)
\(\boxed{\lim_{\beta\to\infty}\mathrm{Attn}=v_{\arg\max s}\ \checkmark}\)

Derive multi-head attention from the single-head function.
Formula: \(\mathrm{head}_i=\mathrm{Attention}(XW_Q^i,XW_K^i,XW_V^i)\); target \(\mathrm{MultiHead}(X)=[\mathrm{head}_1,\dots,\mathrm{head}_h]\,W_O\)
Hint: run \(h\) heads on their own projections, concatenate the outputs, then mix with one output matrix \(W_O\).

Run \(h\) attention heads, each with its own learned projections: \(\mathrm{head}_i=\mathrm{Attention}(XW_Q^i,XW_K^i,XW_V^i)\)
concatenate the \(h\) head outputs side by side and mix with \(W_O\)
\(\boxed{\mathrm{MultiHead}(X)=[\mathrm{head}_1,\dots,\mathrm{head}_h]\,W_O}\)

Derive how a causal mask makes token \(i\) attend only to tokens \(j\le i\).
Formula: mask \(M\) with \(M_{ij}=-\infty\) for \(j>i\) else \(0\); target \(\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}+M\big)V\)
Hint: add \(M\) to the scores before the softmax and use \(e^{-\infty}=0\).

Add the mask \(M\) (\(M_{ij}=-\infty\) for \(j>i\), else \(0\)) to the scaled scores before softmax
\(e^{-\infty}=0\) zeroes the future weights, leaving a lower-triangular weight matrix
\(\boxed{\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}+M\big)V}\)

Closing test (equivariance check): permute two input rows of \(X\) and confirm self-attention outputs permute identically.
Formula: \(\mathrm{Attn}(PX)=P\,\mathrm{Attn}(X)\) for a permutation matrix \(P\)
Hint: a pass means the output rows swap in exactly the same order as the inputs, with values unchanged.

Apply \(P\) that swaps rows \(1\) and \(2\); recompute attention to get \(\mathrm{Attn}(PX)\)
compare to swapping the same two rows of the original output \(P\,\mathrm{Attn}(X)\): they match entry-for-entry
\(\boxed{\mathrm{Attn}(PX)=P\,\mathrm{Attn}(X)\ \checkmark}\)

Closing test (causal mask check): after adding the mask \(M\), verify the \(4\times4\) weight matrix is lower-triangular and each row still sums to \(1\).
Formula: \(W=\mathrm{softmax}\!\big(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}+M\big)\), with \(W_{ij}=0\) for \(j>i\)
Hint: a pass means every above-diagonal entry is \(0\) and each row of the surviving entries sums to \(1\).

For each \(j>i\) the masked score is \(-\infty\) so \(W_{ij}=e^{-\infty}/(\cdot)=0\) — the matrix is lower-triangular
within each row \(i\) the softmax over the \(j\le i\) entries still normalizes to \(1\)
\(\boxed{W\ \text{lower-triangular},\ \textstyle\sum_j W_{ij}=1\ \checkmark}\)

What roles do the query, key, and value play in attention?
Hint: think of asking a question, wearing a name tag, and the answer you pass on.

Query = what an item seeks; key = what an item offers; value = what it passes on
\(\boxed{\text{query asks, key advertises, value is read out}}\)

When do you add positional encodings, and when do you omit them?
Hint: attention alone is permutation-equivariant, so think about whether input order carries meaning.

Add a positional signal when input order matters (sequences) so the model can tell positions apart
omit it for pure sets such as point clouds, where self-attention's permutation-equivariance is exactly what you want
\(\boxed{\text{add for ordered sequences, omit for unordered sets}}\)

Pitfall: what breaks if you omit the \(1/\sqrt{d_k}\) scale at large \(d_k\)?
Formula: \(\mathrm{Var}(q\cdot k)=d_k\), so scores scale like \(\sqrt{d_k}\)
Hint: trace large scores through the softmax to its gradient.

Scores grow like \(\sqrt{d_k}\), so at large \(d_k\) they become huge
the softmax saturates (one weight \(\to1\), the rest \(\to0\)) and its gradient vanishes, so the projection matrices stop learning
\(\boxed{\text{saturated softmax} \Rightarrow \text{vanishing gradients}}\)

State the computational cost of attention in the number of elements \(n\) and explain why.
Formula: \(QK^\top\) is \(n\times n\)
Hint: the cost is dominated by forming and storing the score matrix.

Forming \(QK^\top\) produces an \(n\times n\) score matrix, so both compute and memory scale as \(O(n^2)\)
large point clouds therefore need sampling or efficient-attention variants
\(\boxed{O(n^2)\ \text{in compute and memory}}\)

How does attention relate to message passing on a graph?
Hint: picture a fully connected graph and ask what sets each edge's weight.

Treat the elements as nodes of a fully connected graph; each node sends a message (its value) along every edge
attention is message passing where each message is softmax-weighted by query-key similarity
\(\boxed{\text{attention} = \text{softmax-weighted message passing}}\)
In self-attention, \(Q\), \(K\), \(V\) are all {{c1::linear projections of the same input}} (\(Q=XW_Q\), etc.).

hint: it is the square root of the key dimension.

Derive the GCN aggregation matrix \(\hat A\) (step 1 of 2): start from raw adjacency \(A\), add self-loops, then symmetric-normalize.
Formula: self-loops \(\tilde A=A+I\); degree \(\tilde D_{ii}=\sum_j\tilde A_{ij}\); target \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\)
Hint: first add \(I\) to \(A\) so a node keeps its own feature, then rescale each edge end by \(1/\sqrt{\deg}\).

add self-loops: \(\tilde A=A+I\)
degree matrix \(\tilde D_{ii}=\sum_j\tilde A_{ij}\)
sandwich with \(\tilde D^{-1/2}\) on both sides
\(\boxed{\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}(A+I)\tilde D^{-1/2}}\).

Derive the full GCN layer (step 2 of 2) from the aggregator \(\hat A\), a learned weight \(W^{(l)}\), and a nonlinearity \(\sigma\).
Formula: start \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\); target \(H^{(l+1)}=\sigma(\hat A\,H^{(l)}W^{(l)})\)
Hint: apply the three operations in order — aggregate (\(\hat A H^{(l)}\)), transform (\(\cdot\,W^{(l)}\)), then squash (\(\sigma\)).

aggregate neighbours: \(\hat A H^{(l)}\)
apply the learned linear map: \(\hat A H^{(l)}W^{(l)}\)
pass through the nonlinearity
\(\boxed{H^{(l+1)}=\sigma(\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}H^{(l)}W^{(l)})}\).

Derive the GCN–Laplacian link (step 1 of 2): from the symmetric normalized Laplacian, write the normalized adjacency it implies.
Formula: \(L_\text{sym}=I-D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\)
Hint: just solve algebraically for the \(D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\) term.

move the adjacency term to one side
\(D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}=I-L_\text{sym}\)
\(\boxed{D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}=I-L_\text{sym}}\).

Show the GCN aggregator is a Laplacian smoother (step 2 of 2) using the self-looped Laplacian \(\tilde L_\text{sym}\).
Formula: \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\) and \(\tilde L_\text{sym}=I-\hat A\)
Hint: substitute the self-looped version of \(D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}=I-L_\text{sym}\), then read off what one layer does to \(H^{(l)}\).

fold self-loops into the previous identity: \(\hat A=I-\tilde L_\text{sym}\)
one layer applies \(H^{(l)}\mapsto(I-\tilde L_\text{sym})H^{(l)}\)
that is one explicit diffusion (smoothing) step
\(\boxed{\hat A=I-\tilde L_\text{sym}}\).

Derive the GCN message+update as a special case of the MPNN template.
Formula: \(h_u^{(l+1)}=\phi\big(h_u^{(l)},\bigoplus_{v\in N(u)}\psi(h_u^{(l)},h_v^{(l)},e_{uv})\big)\); target \(h_u^{(l+1)}=\sigma\big(W\sum_{v\in N(u)\cup\{u\}}\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\tilde d_v}}h_v^{(l)}\big)\)
Hint: set \(\psi=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\tilde d_v}}h_v^{(l)}\), \(\bigoplus=\sum\) (over neighbours plus self), and \(\phi(\cdot)=\sigma(\cdot\,W)\).

choose message \(\psi=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\tilde d_v}}h_v^{(l)}\)
choose aggregator \(\bigoplus=\sum\) over \(N(u)\cup\{u\}\)
choose update \(\phi(\cdot)=\sigma(\cdot\,W)\)
\(\boxed{h_u^{(l+1)}=\sigma\!\Big(W\sum_{v\in N(u)\cup\{u\}}\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\tilde d_v}}h_v^{(l)}\Big)}\).

Derive the symmetric-normalized weight on a single edge \(u\!-\!v\) from the aggregator matrix.
Formula: \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\), with \(\tilde A_{uv}=1\) and \((\tilde D^{-1/2})_{ii}=\tilde d_i^{-1/2}\)
Hint: the entry \(\hat A_{uv}\) is the product of the row factor, the unit edge, and the column factor.

row factor \((\tilde D^{-1/2})_{uu}=\tilde d_u^{-1/2}\), column factor \((\tilde D^{-1/2})_{vv}=\tilde d_v^{-1/2}\)
multiply through the unit entry \(\tilde A_{uv}=1\)
\(\boxed{\hat A_{uv}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\,\tilde d_v}}}\).

Worked example (small, mean aggregator). Path \(1\!-\!2\!-\!3\), features \(h=(1,0,0)\), self-loops added, identity weight. Compute the new node-2 value.
Formula: \(h_u'=\tfrac{1}{|\tilde N(u)|}\sum_{v\in\tilde N(u)}h_v\) where \(\tilde N(u)\) includes \(u\) itself
Hint: node 2's self-looped neighbourhood is \(\{1,2,3\}\), so divide the feature sum by 3.

Worked example (same path graph). Compute the new node-1 and node-3 values after one mean round.
Formula: \(h_u'=\tfrac{1}{|\tilde N(u)|}\sum_{v\in\tilde N(u)}h_v\), self-loops included
Hint: nodes 1 and 3 are endpoints with self-looped neighbourhoods \(\{1,2\}\) and \(\{2,3\}\), so divide by 2 each.

Worked example (symmetric GCN norm, contrast with mean). Same path \(1\!-\!2\!-\!3\), \(h=(1,0,0)\), degrees \(\tilde d=(2,3,2)\). Compute node 2's value.
Formula: \(h_u'=\sum_{v\in\tilde N(u)}\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tilde d_u\tilde d_v}}\,h_v\)
Hint: only \(h_1=1\) is nonzero, so compute the single weight \(1/\sqrt{\tilde d_2\tilde d_1}=1/\sqrt{3\cdot2}\).

Worked example (large 3D mesh scale). A scan mesh has \(V=2{,}000{,}000\) vertices, average one-ring degree \(\bar d=6\). Count directed messages for one GCN layer and for \(k=4\) layers.
Formula: messages/layer \(\approx V\bar d\); for \(k\) layers \(\approx kV\bar d\)
Hint: compute \(V\bar d\) first, then multiply by \(k=4\).

per layer \(\approx V\bar d=2\times10^6\times6=1.2\times10^7\)
over \(k=4\) layers \(\approx 4\times1.2\times10^7=4.8\times10^7\)
\(\boxed{\sim1.2\times10^7\text{/layer},\ \sim4.8\times10^7\text{ for }k=4}\) (why full-graph training is memory-heavy).

Worked example (receptive field, mm scale). A kNN point-graph has mean spacing \(\delta=5\) mm. What physical radius does a node see after \(k=3\) layers (one hop \(\approx\) one spacing)?
Formula: receptive radius \(\approx k\,\delta\)
Hint: the receptive field is \(k\) hops; multiply hops by the per-hop spacing \(\delta\).

receptive field \(=k\) hops \(=3\)
physical reach \(\approx k\,\delta=3\times5\text{ mm}\)
\(\boxed{\approx 15\text{ mm}}\); for an \(m\)-scale block you would need more hops or a coarser graph.

Closing test (verify, mean aggregator). Check the claim that one mean round on path \(1\!-\!2\!-\!3\), \(h=(1,0,0)\) gives \((0.5,0.333,0)\) by testing total-mass conservation.
Formula: compare input sum \(\sum_i h_i\) with output sum \(\sum_i h_i'\)
Hint: a pass here is recognizing the sums need NOT match — mean rows are not column-stochastic, so a drop is expected, not a bug.

input sum \(=1+0+0=1\)
output sum \(=0.5+0.333+0=0.833\ne1\)
mean aggregation is not mass-conserving, so the drop is expected
\(\boxed{(0.5,0.333,0)\text{ confirmed; sum }0.833}\).

Closing test (verify limiting case). Confirm \(\hat A=I-\tilde L_\text{sym}\) is a low-pass (Laplacian) smoother by checking its action on the smoothest eigenmode.
Formula: \(\hat A=I-\tilde L_\text{sym}\); on eigenmode with eigenvalue \(\lambda\), \(\hat A\) scales by \(1-\lambda\); the flat mode has \(\lambda=0\)
Hint: a pass = the \(\lambda=0\) (constant) mode is preserved while large-\(\lambda\) modes shrink.

flat mode has \(\lambda=0\), so \(\hat A\) scales it by \(1-0=1\) (preserved)
high-frequency modes have large \(\lambda\), scaled by \(1-\lambda<1\) (shrunk)
\(\boxed{\text{constant mode preserved, oscillations damped}=\text{low-pass}}\).

Closing test (over-smoothing limit). Predict node-feature variance as layers \(k\to\infty\) on a connected graph, then state the fix.
Formula: each layer scales mode \(\lambda\) by \((1-\lambda)\) with \(0<1-\lambda<1\) for \(\lambda>0\)
Hint: a pass = realizing only the flat mode (constant across nodes) survives repeated shrinking, so per-node variance collapses.

each layer shrinks every non-flat mode by \(1-\lambda<1\)
as \(k\to\infty\) only the flat mode survives, all nodes converge
variance \(\to0\)
\(\boxed{\operatorname{Var}\to0\ \Rightarrow\ \text{over-smoothing; use residual/jumping connections or fewer layers}}\).

Write the general message-passing (MPNN) update for node \(u\) (recall).
Formula: \(h_u^{(l+1)}=\phi\big(h_u^{(l)},\bigoplus_{v\in N(u)}\psi(h_u^{(l)},h_v^{(l)},e_{uv})\big)\)
Hint: read it inside-out — message \(\psi\) per neighbour, aggregate with \(\bigoplus\), update with \(\phi\).

Definition: what are \(\psi\), \(\bigoplus\), and \(\phi\) in an MPNN?
Hint: map each symbol to one of message / aggregate / update.

\(\psi\) = message function (builds a message from a neighbour + edge feature)
\(\bigoplus\) = permutation-invariant aggregator (sum/mean/max)
\(\phi\) = update function (refreshes the node state).

When to use a GNN here?
Hint: ask whether the data is naturally a graph and whether you want per-node/per-edge outputs.
When the data is a graph (mesh faces/vertices, kNN point neighbourhoods, CAD-element adjacency, kinematic chains) and you want per-node or per-edge predictions.

Pitfall: what breaks if you forget degree normalization or self-loops?
Hint: think about high-degree nodes and a node's own feature.
Exploding or vanishing activations, especially on high-degree nodes, and loss of the node's own feature.

Pitfall: for a point-cloud kNN graph, how do \(k\) (or radius) choices fail?
Hint: consider the two extremes, too sparse and too dense.
Too sparse loses context; too dense over-smooths and blurs distinct regions.

Code idiom: what do you precompute once for all GCN layers, and why?
Formula: \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\), then each layer is \(\sigma(\hat A H W)\)
Hint: ask which factor does not change between layers.
The normalized adjacency \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\), because it is constant across layers; each layer is then just \(\sigma(\hat A H W)\).
The message aggregator \(\bigoplus\) must be {{c1::permutation-invariant}} (sum, mean, or max) because neighbour order is meaningless.

A Graph Attention Network is message passing with {{c1::attention-weighted}} messages, linking GNNs to transformers.

In a GCN, \(\tilde A=A+I\) adds {{c1::self-loops}} so a node keeps its own feature during aggregation.

Stacking \(k\) GNN layers gives each node a {{c1::\(k\)-hop}} receptive field.
hint: one layer = one ring of diffusion.

Derive the general permutation-invariant form for a function of an unordered set \(\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}\) (step 1 of 2).
Formula: target is \(f(\{x_i\}) = \operatorname{POOL}_{i=1}^{n} h(x_i)\) with \(h\) a per-point map and \(\operatorname{POOL}\) a symmetric (order-free) aggregator
Hint: first make every point go through the SAME map \(h\) so no index is special; then ask what kind of aggregator ignores order.

Process each point with the same map \(h\), so per-point features carry no index identity
aggregate the multiset \(\{h(x_i)\}\) with an operator POOL whose value is independent of order (symmetric)
any permutation \(P\) reorders POOL's inputs but leaves its output fixed
\(\boxed{f(\{x_i\}) = \operatorname{POOL}_{i=1}^{n} h(x_i)}\).

From the symmetric core \(\operatorname{POOL}_i h(x_i)\), derive PointNet's concrete instance (step 2 of 2).
Formula: target is \(f(\{x_i\}) = \gamma\!\big(\max_i h(x_i)\big)\)
Hint: pick the specific symmetric aggregator PointNet uses (\(\max\)), then wrap the pooled feature in a final learned readout \(\gamma\).

Choose POOL \(=\max\), which is order-free, giving global feature \(g=\max_i h(x_i)\)
append a final learned map \(\gamma\) to read out the answer from \(g\)
\(\boxed{f(\{x_i\}) = \gamma\!\big(\max_i h(x_i)\big)}\).

Prove max-pooling is permutation-invariant for any permutation \(\pi\) of the indices.
Formula: show \(\max_i h(x_{\pi(i)}) = \max_i h(x_i)\)
Hint: note that \(\{h(x_{\pi(1)}),\dots\}\) is the SAME multiset as \(\{h(x_1),\dots\}\), then use that \(\max\) of a multiset ignores listing order.

\(g_\pi = \max_i h(x_{\pi(i)})\) ranges over the same multiset \(\{h(x_1),\dots,h(x_n)\}\)
the maximum of a multiset does not depend on the listing order
so \(g_\pi = g\) for every \(\pi\)
\(\boxed{\max_i h(x_{\pi(i)}) = \max_i h(x_i)}\).

Derive the DeepSDF surface set from a network \(f_\theta(x)\) that outputs signed distance (\(f_\theta<0\) inside, \(f_\theta>0\) outside).
Formula: target is \(S=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^3 : f_\theta(x)=0\}\)
Hint: the sign flips at the boundary, so set the value to the level that is neither \(+\) nor \(-\).

Signed distance is negative inside and positive outside, so it changes sign exactly at the boundary
by continuity the boundary is where the value is neither \(+\) nor \(-\), i.e. \(f_\theta(x)=0\)
collect all such points
\(\boxed{S=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^3 : f_\theta(x)=0\}}\).

Derive the DeepSDF training objective from samples \(x_i\) with known signed distances \(s_i\).
Formula: target is \(\min_\theta \sum_i |f_\theta(x_i)-s_i|\)
Hint: write the per-sample mismatch you want near zero (\(f_\theta(x_i)-s_i\)), wrap it in an L1 penalty, then sum and minimize over \(\theta\).

We want the prediction to match the target at every sample: \(f_\theta(x_i)\approx s_i\)
penalize the mismatch with an L1 residual \(|f_\theta(x_i)-s_i|\) (robust, often clamped)
sum over samples and minimize over \(\theta\)
\(\boxed{\min_\theta \sum_i |f_\theta(x_i)-s_i|}\).

Derive where an Occupancy Network places the surface, given \(o_\theta(x)\in[0,1]\) = probability the point is inside.
Formula: target is \(S=\{x : o_\theta(x)=0.5\}\)
Hint: inside \(\to 1\), outside \(\to 0\); the boundary is the level halfway between the two classes.

Inside has \(o_\theta\to 1\), outside has \(o_\theta\to 0\), so the boundary is the maximally-uncertain level
that is the decision threshold halfway between the two classes
set \(o_\theta(x)=\tfrac12\)
\(\boxed{S=\{x : o_\theta(x)=0.5\}}\).

Construct the analytic SDF of a sphere of center \(c\) and radius \(r\), and state its sign regime.
Formula: target is \(f(x)=\lVert x-c\rVert - r\)
Hint: compute the distance from the query to the center first, then subtract the radius.

Signed distance is the gap between the point's distance-to-center and the radius
distance to center is \(\lVert x-c\rVert\); subtract \(r\)
sign is \(-\) for \(\lVert x-c\rVertr\) (outside), \(0\) on the shell
\(\boxed{f(x)=\lVert x-c\rVert - r}\).

Worked example (2D, unit circle). Evaluate \(f\) at \((1,0)\), \((2,0)\), \((0,0)\) and classify each.
Formula: \(f(x)=\lVert x\rVert-1\)
Hint: compute the norm \(\lVert x\rVert\) first, then subtract 1 and read the sign.

Worked example (3D, mm scale). Sphere center \(c=(0,0,0)\), radius \(r=50\) mm; query \(x=(30,40,0)\) mm. Compute \(f\) and classify.
Formula: \(f(x)=\lVert x-c\rVert - r\)
Hint: compute \(\lVert x-c\rVert=\sqrt{30^2+40^2+0^2}\) first, then subtract 50.

Worked example (3D, m scale, far query). Block-pile bound by sphere \(c=(2,2,2)\) m, \(r=1\) m; query \(x=(5,2,2)\) m. Compute \(f\).
Formula: \(f(x)=\lVert x-c\rVert - r\)
Hint: form the difference \(x-c=(3,0,0)\) first, take its norm, then subtract 1.

\(\lVert x-c\rVert=\sqrt{3^2+0+0}=3\) m
\(f=3-1=2\) m \(>0\)
\(\boxed{f=+2\text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{outside by 2 m}}\).

Worked example (PointNet max-pool, 2D). Points \(x_1=(1,0)\), \(x_2=(0,1)\), \(x_3=(-1,0)\), \(x_4=(0,-1)\), identity \(h\). Compute the global feature \(g\).
Formula: \(g=\max_i h(x_i)\), element-wise across coordinates
Hint: take the max of all first coordinates, then the max of all second coordinates, separately.

Closing test (invariance). Reshuffle the four points above to order \(x_3,x_1,x_4,x_2\) and recompute \(g\); check it matches \((1,1)\).
Formula: \(g_\pi=\max_i h(x_{\pi(i)})\) element-wise
Hint: recompute both per-coordinate maxes on the new order; a pass means \(g_\pi=g=(1,1)\).

first coord: \(\max(-1,1,0,0)=1\)
second coord: \(\max(0,0,-1,1)=1\)
\(g=(1,1)\), identical to the unshuffled result
\(\boxed{g_\pi=(1,1)=g \;\checkmark\text{ invariant}}\).

Closing test (SDF zero set). Take the claimed surface point \((\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2},\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2})\) and verify it lies on the unit circle.
Formula: \(f(x)=\lVert x\rVert-1\); on-surface means \(f=0\)
Hint: compute \(\lVert x\rVert=\sqrt{\tfrac12+\tfrac12}\); a pass is \(f=0\).

\(\lVert x\rVert=\sqrt{\tfrac12+\tfrac12}=\sqrt1=1\)
\(f=1-1=0\)
matches the claimed zero level set
\(\boxed{f=0 \;\checkmark\text{ point lies on }S}\).

Closing test (occupancy boundary). A network gives \(o_\theta=0.5\) at \(x^\star\). Is \(x^\star\) on \(S\), and which way does \(o_\theta\) move just inside?
Formula: \(S=\{x:o_\theta(x)=0.5\}\), with \(o_\theta\to1\) inside, \(o_\theta\to0\) outside
Hint: check whether \(o_\theta(x^\star)\) equals the threshold \(0.5\); a pass means \(x^\star\in S\) and moving inward raises \(o_\theta\) above \(0.5\).

inside means \(o_\theta\to1\), so moving inward must push \(o_\theta>0.5\)
at \(x^\star\) exactly \(o_\theta=0.5\), the threshold
\(\boxed{x^\star\in S,\; o_\theta\text{ increases inward}\;\checkmark}\).

Derive the cost of sampling an implicit on an \(n\times n\times n\) grid with one \(f\) call per cell.
Formula: target is \(\text{cost}=O(n^3)\)
Hint: multiply the cell counts along the three axes.

grid has \(n\) cells per axis in 3 axes
total cells \(=n\cdot n\cdot n=n^3\), each one \(f\) call
\(\boxed{\text{cost}=O(n^3)}\) (resolution trades against compute).

When should you use PointNet instead of voxelizing a scan?
Hint: think about sparse, unordered clouds and memory cost.
Use PointNet when the cloud is sparse/unordered; voxelizing wastes memory and resolution.

What breaks if you feed set points to an order-sensitive net?
Hint: the defining property of a set is that order does not matter.
You break permutation invariance; the output depends on listing order, which is a bug for sets.

What is the limitation of vanilla PointNet's max-pool, and the fix?
Hint: global vs local geometry.
It captures global features but weak local geometry; use PointNet++ or local groupings.

Why can a plain stone block overfit a per-object learned implicit?
Hint: consider how much distinctive geometry a featureless convex shape carries.
It is featureless/convex with little distinctive geometry, so limited data overfits (prefer classical methods).

How do you turn a learned implicit into an explicit CAD mesh?
Formula: surface is \(\{x:f_\theta(x)=0\}\)
Hint: sample \(f_\theta\) on a grid, then extract the zero crossings.
Evaluate \(f_\theta\) on a grid and run Marching Cubes on the zero crossings.

Code idiom: which Eigen op implements the symmetric max-pool?
Hint: an element-wise max accumulated over per-point features.
Element-wise `cwiseMax` accumulated over the per-point features.
A neural implicit represents a surface as the {{c1::zero level set}} of a learned function \(f_\theta(x)\).

hint: first the per-point map, then the order-free aggregator.

▶ 3Blue1Brown — Neural networks · NN Ch.1

Derive the 1-D Kalman gain from the general gain by setting the measurement model to identity.
Formula: general \(K = P^- H^\top (H P^- H^\top + R)^{-1}\); target \(K = \dfrac{P^-}{P^- + R}\)
Hint: substitute \(H = 1\) (scalar) so every \(H\) and \(H^\top\) becomes \(1\), then simplify.

Derive the 1-D covariance update from the general one with \(H=1\).
Formula: general \(P^+ = (I - KH)P^-\); target \(P^+ = (1-K)P^-\)
Hint: replace \(I\) with \(1\) and \(H\) with \(1\) in the scalar case.

Show that with \(H=1\) the general state update becomes the 1-D form.
Formula: general \(x^+ = x^- + K(z - Hx^-)\); target \(x^+ = x^- + K(z - x^-)\)
Hint: set \(H=1\) inside the innovation \(z - Hx^-\).

Innovation \(\tilde y = z - Hx^- = z - 1\cdot x^- = z - x^-\)
\(x^+ = x^- + K\tilde y\)
\(\boxed{x^+ = x^- + K(z - x^-)}\)

From the predict–update diagram, write the two Kalman predict equations.
Formula: \(x^- = Fx + Bu\), \(\;P^- = FPF^\top + Q\)
Hint: one line pushes the state through the motion model, the other grows the covariance by adding \(Q\).

State: \(x^- = Fx + Bu\)
Covariance: \(P^- = FPF^\top + Q\)
\(\boxed{x^- = Fx + Bu,\quad P^- = FPF^\top + Q}\)

Write the full Kalman update sequence (innovation, its covariance, gain, state, covariance).
Formula: \(\tilde y = z - Hx^-\), \(\;S = HP^-H^\top + R\), \(\;K = P^-H^\top S^{-1}\), \(\;x^+ = x^- + K\tilde y\), \(\;P^+ = (I-KH)P^-\)
Hint: compute \(\tilde y\) and \(S\) first, then \(K\) from them, then update \(x\) and \(P\).

\(\tilde y = z - Hx^-\)
\(S = HP^-H^\top + R\)
\(K = P^-H^\top S^{-1}\)
\(x^+ = x^- + K\tilde y\)
\(\boxed{P^+ = (I-KH)P^-}\)

Worked 1-D fuse. Prediction \(x^-=10\), \(P^-=4\); sensor \(z=12\), \(R=1\). Compute \(K\), \(x^+\), \(P^+\).
Formula: \(K = \dfrac{P^-}{P^-+R}\), \(\;x^+ = x^- + K(z-x^-)\), \(\;P^+ = (1-K)P^-\)
Hint: compute the gain \(K\) first, then plug it into the state and covariance updates.

Worked 1-D fuse at mm scale with a noisier sensor. Prediction \(x^-=50\) mm, \(P^-=2\); sensor \(z=53\) mm, \(R=6\). Compute \(K\) and \(x^+\).
Formula: \(K = \dfrac{P^-}{P^-+R}\), \(\;x^+ = x^- + K(z-x^-)\)
Hint: \(R\) is larger than \(P^-\) here, so expect \(K<0.5\) — the estimate stays near the prediction.

Worked 1-D fuse at metre scale, very confident sensor. Prediction \(x^-=3.0\) m, \(P^-=0.9\); sensor \(z=3.4\) m, \(R=0.1\). Compute \(K\), \(x^+\), \(P^+\).
Formula: \(K = \dfrac{P^-}{P^-+R}\), \(\;x^+ = x^- + K(z-x^-)\), \(\;P^+ = (1-K)P^-\)
Hint: small \(R\) means large \(K\) — the estimate jumps most of the way to \(z\).

Worked predict-only step (2-D covariance grows). \(P = \begin{psmallmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{psmallmatrix}\), \(F = I\), process noise \(Q = \begin{psmallmatrix}0.2&0\\0&0.2\end{psmallmatrix}\). Compute \(P^-\).
Formula: \(P^- = FPF^\top + Q\)
Hint: with \(F=I\) the term \(FPF^\top = P\), so just add \(Q\) entrywise.

Closing test — does the fused variance beat both inputs? Use the worked numbers \(P^-=4\), \(R=1\), \(P^+=0.8\).
Formula: check \(P^+ < \min(P^-, R)\)
Hint: a pass means the posterior variance is below BOTH \(P^-\) and \(R\); compute \(\min(4,1)\) and compare.

\(\min(P^-, R) = \min(4, 1) = 1\)
Is \(0.8 < 1\)? Yes
\(\boxed{\text{Pass: } P^+=0.8 < 1 \text{ — fusing beats either alone}}\)

Closing test — is the Kalman gain a valid weight? Use \(P^-=4\), \(R=1\).
Formula: \(K = \dfrac{P^-}{P^-+R}\), valid iff \(0 \le K \le 1\)
Hint: compute \(K\) and confirm it lies in \([0,1]\); \(K\to1\) as \(R\to0\), \(K\to0\) as \(P^-\to0\).

Closing test — should the covariance stay symmetric positive-definite? Given \(P^+ = (I-KH)P^-\) lost symmetry to round-off, name the fix and write it.
Formula: Joseph form \(P^+ = (I-KH)P^-(I-KH)^\top + KRK^\top\)
Hint: a pass is a \(P\) that stays symmetric PSD; the Joseph form is symmetric by construction.

Replace the plain update with the Joseph form
\(P^+ = (I-KH)P^-(I-KH)^\top + KRK^\top\)
Each term is symmetric, so \(P^+\) stays symmetric PSD
\(\boxed{P^+ = (I-KH)P^-(I-KH)^\top + KRK^\top}\)

How does the EKF handle nonlinear motion \(f\) or measurement \(h\)?
Formula: \(F = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\), \(\;H = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\)
Hint: linearize, then reuse the standard KF predict/update with these Jacobians.

Linearize \(f,h\) at the current estimate using Jacobians \(F=\partial f/\partial x\), \(H=\partial h/\partial x\)
Then apply the ordinary KF predict and update
\(\boxed{\text{EKF = KF on the linearized model}}\)

What does a filter do that no single sensor can?
Hint: think about the posterior variance versus each input variance.

It fuses prediction and measurement into one estimate that is more certain than either input alone
\(\boxed{P^+ < \min(P^-, R)}\)

The Kalman filter is the recursive form of which static estimator?
Hint: it runs the same Gaussian MLE one step at a time.

Least squares
\(\boxed{\text{recursive least squares (running Gaussian MLE)}}\)

In predict vs update, when does uncertainty grow and when does it shrink?
Formula: \(P^- = FPF^\top + Q\) (predict), \(\;P^+ = (I-KH)P^-\) (update)
Hint: look at which step adds \(Q\) and which multiplies by \((I-KH)\).

Predict adds \(Q\), so uncertainty grows
Update multiplies by \((I-KH)\) with \(0\le K\le1\), so it shrinks
\(\boxed{\text{grows in predict, shrinks in update}}\)

Symptom and meaning of persistently large innovations \(\tilde y = z - Hx^-\)?
Formula: \(\tilde y = z - Hx^-\)
Hint: the innovation is what the measurement says minus what the model predicted.

Large, persistent \(\tilde y\) means the prediction keeps missing the measurement
\(\boxed{\text{model is wrong / mistuned — the filter is diverging}}\)
The Kalman gain weights {{c1::prediction uncertainty}} against {{c2::total uncertainty}}; small \(R\) gives large \(K\) (trust the sensor).

Fab Loop · State Estimation
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bayes theorem

For numerical stability use the {{c1::Joseph form}} of the covariance update to keep \(P\) positive-definite.
hint: \(P^+ = (I-KH)P^-(I-KH)^\top + KRK^\top\).

Fab Loop · State Estimation
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bayes theorem
Too-small \(R\) makes the filter {{c1::chase sensor noise}}; too-small \(Q\) makes it {{c2::ignore real motion and diverge}}.

Fab Loop · State Estimation
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bayes theorem
In code the innovation is {{c1::`y = z - H * x`}} — measurement minus predicted measurement.

Fab Loop · State Estimation
▶ 3Blue1Brown — Bayes theorem

A 2-joint arm has tree node \(q_{\text{near}}=(0.2,0.5)\) and samples \(q_{\text{rand}}=(1.2,1.5)\) rad. Take one RRT step with \(\eta=0.3\).
Formula: \(q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \eta\,\frac{q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}}{\lVert q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert}\)
Hint: First compute the difference vector \(q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\) and its norm, then scale the unit direction by \(\eta\).

A 2-joint arm at \(q_{\text{near}}=(0,0)\) samples \(q_{\text{rand}}=(0.6,0.8)\) rad. Take one RRT step with \(\eta=0.5\).
Formula: \(q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \eta\,\frac{q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}}{\lVert q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert}\)
Hint: The difference is \((0.6,0.8)\) — a 3-4-5 vector, so the norm is exactly 1.0; the unit direction is the difference itself.

A planner samples a straight segment of length \(L=120\) mm. The thinnest obstacle feature is \(5\) mm. How many interior collision-check samples are needed if you set \(\Delta = 2\) mm?
Formula: \(\#\text{samples} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{\Delta}\right\rceil\), with the rule \(\Delta <\) smallest obstacle feature.
Hint: First confirm \(\Delta=2\) mm is finer than the \(5\) mm feature (so no tunneling), then divide \(L\) by \(\Delta\).

Check: \(\Delta=2 < 5\) mm, so resolution is safe (no tunneling)
\(\#\text{samples} = \lceil 120/2\rceil = 60\)
\(\boxed{60 \text{ samples}}\)

A 3-joint arm moves from \(q_{\text{start}}=(0,0,0)\) to \(q_{\text{goal}}=(0.9,1.2,0.8)\) rad along a straight C-space line. What is the Euclidean path length in C-space?
Formula: \(\text{length} = \lVert q_{\text{goal}}-q_{\text{start}}\rVert = \sqrt{\sum_i (q_{\text{goal},i}-q_{\text{start},i})^2}\)
Hint: Subtract componentwise to get \((0.9,1.2,0.8)\), then take the root of the sum of squares.

Goal-biased sampling: with probability \(p=0.1\) the planner samples the goal directly. Over \(N=50\) iterations, what is the expected number of goal-biased samples?
Formula: \(\mathbb{E}[\text{goal samples}] = pN\)
Hint: This is just the mean of a binomial — multiply the bias probability by the iteration count.

Derive the RRT steer step: show that moving a fixed distance \(\eta\) from \(q_{\text{near}}\) toward \(q_{\text{rand}}\) gives the boxed formula.
Formula: start from "displacement = (step length) \(\times\) (unit direction)"; target \(q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \eta\,\frac{q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}}{\lVert q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert}\)
Hint: First write the unit direction from \(q_{\text{near}}\) to \(q_{\text{rand}}\), then multiply by step length \(\eta\) and add to \(q_{\text{near}}\).

Direction vector \(= q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\)
Unit direction \(= \dfrac{q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}}{\lVert q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert}\)
Displacement \(= \eta \times\) unit direction
Add to start: \(q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \text{displacement}\)
\(\boxed{q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \eta\,\frac{q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}}{\lVert q_{\text{rand}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert}}\)

From the C-space picture, write the validity (collision-free) condition for a path \(\tau:[0,1]\to\mathcal{C}\).
Formula: \(\tau(t)\in\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}\ \forall t\in[0,1]\), equivalently \(\tau\cap\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}=\varnothing\)
Hint: State the endpoint conditions too — \(\tau(0)\) and \(\tau(1)\) are pinned.

Every point on the path must lie in free space
Endpoints: \(\tau(0)=q_{\text{start}},\ \tau(1)=q_{\text{goal}}\)
\(\boxed{\tau(t)\in\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}\ \forall t\in[0,1],\quad \tau\cap\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}=\varnothing}\)

Write the trajectory-optimization objective that turns a path into a smooth timed trajectory.
Formula: \(\min_{q(t)}\int_0^T \lVert\ddot q(t)\rVert^2\,dt\) subject to \(q(t)\in\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}\), \(\lvert\dot q\rvert\le\dot q_{\max}\), \(\lvert\ddot q\rvert\le\ddot q_{\max}\)
Hint: The integrand penalizes acceleration (effort); the constraints keep it free and within velocity/acceleration limits.

Minimize integrated squared acceleration (effort)
Subject to staying collision-free and within velocity/acceleration limits
\(\boxed{\min_{q(t)}\int_0^T\lVert\ddot q\rVert^2\,dt \ \text{ s.t. } q(t)\in\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}},\ \lvert\dot q\rvert\le\dot q_{\max},\ \lvert\ddot q\rvert\le\ddot q_{\max}}\)

Closing test — verify a candidate RRT step lands the right distance from \(q_{\text{near}}=(1,1)\): is \(q_{\text{new}}=(1.3,1.4)\) a valid \(\eta=0.5\) step?
Formula: a valid step satisfies \(\lVert q_{\text{new}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert = \eta\)
Hint: Compute the displacement norm; a pass means it equals \(\eta=0.5\).

Displacement \(=(0.3,0.4)\)
Norm \(=\sqrt{0.09+0.16}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.5\)
\(0.5 = \eta\), so the step length checks out
\(\boxed{\text{valid: } \lVert q_{\text{new}}-q_{\text{near}}\rVert = 0.5 = \eta}\)

Closing test — collision-resolution check. A wall is \(4\) mm thick and you set \(\Delta=6\) mm. Will the discrete check reliably catch the wall?
Formula: safe iff \(\Delta < \) (smallest obstacle feature)
Hint: Compare \(\Delta\) against the \(4\) mm feature; a pass requires \(\Delta\) strictly smaller.

Compare: \(\Delta=6\) mm vs feature \(=4\) mm
\(6 \not< 4\), so a sample can step across the wall
\(\boxed{\text{fails — tunneling possible; need } \Delta < 4 \text{ mm}}\)

What is a configuration space \(\mathcal{C}\)?
Hint: Think one point = one whole pose.

The space of all robot configurations; one point is one full pose (all joint values).
\(\boxed{\mathcal{C}\subset\mathbb{R}^n,\ n=\#\text{joints}}\)

Why plan in configuration space instead of the workspace?
Hint: Consider how a whole robot pose maps to a single object.

One C-space point is a whole robot pose
So planning is just drawing a curve that avoids the obstacle region
\(\boxed{\text{path = curve in } \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \text{ from } q_{\text{start}} \text{ to } q_{\text{goal}}}\)

How is collision along a path segment usually checked (no closed form)?
Formula: sample at resolution \(\Delta\) and test each \(\tau(t_i)\) for robot–obstacle overlap
Hint: Broad-phase bounding volumes first, then narrow-phase mesh tests.

Discretize the segment at resolution \(\Delta\)
Test each sample for overlap (broad-phase, then narrow-phase)
\(\boxed{\text{discrete check at spacing } \Delta}\)

Difference between RRT and PRM?
Hint: One is single-query (tree), one is multi-query (roadmap).

RRT grows a tree for a single query
PRM builds a reusable roadmap for many queries in a static scene
\(\boxed{\text{RRT = single-query tree; PRM = multi-query roadmap}}\)

What turns RRT into an asymptotically optimal planner?
Hint: Think about rewiring, or post-processing the raw path.

RRT* (rewiring) gives asymptotic optimality
Or shortcut/smooth the plain-RRT path
\(\boxed{\text{RRT* or shortcutting/smoothing}}\)

Why is the robot's own body part of \(\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}\)?
Hint: Consider configurations where two links overlap.

Self-collision must be avoided
So the robot's own geometry forbids some configurations
\(\boxed{\text{self-collision} \Rightarrow \text{those } q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}}\)

Final step before execution after planning a geometric path?
Hint: Raw paths have corners and no timestamps.

Smooth the corners
Time-parameterize (assign timestamps within velocity/acceleration limits)
\(\boxed{\text{smooth + time-scale}}\)
RRT is {{c1::probabilistically complete}}: the chance of finding a path tends to 1 as samples increase, but it is not {{c2::optimal}}.
Fab Loop · Trajectory Planning

Sampling planners struggle with {{c1::narrow passages}} because the chance of sampling inside them is low.
hint: bias sampling or use connectors to help.
Fab Loop · Trajectory Planning
The RRT inner loop first finds the {{c1::nearest}} tree node to \(q_{\text{rand}}\), then steers a step \(\eta\) toward it.
Fab Loop · Trajectory Planning
Set the collision-check resolution \(\Delta\) smaller than the {{c1::smallest obstacle feature}} to avoid tunneling through thin obstacles.
Fab Loop · Trajectory Planning

Derive the exact cusp height \(h\) in terms of stepover \(s\) and tool radius \(R\) from the diagram's right triangle.
Formula: start from \(R^2 = (s/2)^2 + (R-h)^2\), target \(h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (s/2)^2}\).
Hint: isolate the \((R-h)^2\) term, then take the square root of both sides and solve for \(h\).

Derive the small-stepover approximation \(h \approx s^2/(8R)\) from the exact cusp height.
Formula: \(h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (s/2)^2}\), target \(h \approx \dfrac{s^2}{8R}\).
Hint: factor \(R\) out of the square root and Taylor-expand \(\sqrt{1-x}\approx 1-\tfrac{x}{2}\) for small \(x\).

\(h = R - R\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{(s/2)^2}{R^2}}\)
Let \(x = \dfrac{(s/2)^2}{R^2} = \dfrac{s^2}{4R^2}\) (small)
\(\sqrt{1-x} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{2}\), so \(h \approx R - R\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{2}\right) = R\dfrac{x}{2}\)
\(h \approx \dfrac{R}{2}\cdot\dfrac{s^2}{4R^2} = \boxed{\dfrac{s^2}{8R}}\)

Derive the stepover \(s\) that hits a target cusp height \(h\).
Formula: start from \(h \approx \dfrac{s^2}{8R}\), target \(s \approx \sqrt{8Rh}\).
Hint: multiply both sides by \(8R\) to clear the fraction, then take the square root.

Worked example (mm scale): a finish pass uses ball-end radius \(R=6\) mm with target cusp \(h=0.01\) mm. Find the stepover \(s\).
Formula: \(s = \sqrt{8 R h}\).
Hint: substitute the numbers under the root and multiply \(8\cdot 6\cdot 0.01\) first.

Worked example (coarser finish): ball-end radius \(R=3\) mm, target cusp \(h=0.02\) mm. Find the stepover \(s\).
Formula: \(s = \sqrt{8 R h}\).
Hint: compute \(8\cdot 3\cdot 0.02\) inside the root, then take the square root.

Worked example (larger tool): ball-end radius \(R=10\) mm, target cusp \(h=0.005\) mm. Find the stepover \(s\).
Formula: \(s = \sqrt{8 R h}\).
Hint: compute \(8\cdot 10\cdot 0.005\) inside the root first.

Worked example (predict the finish): with \(R=6\) mm and stepover \(s=0.69\) mm, what cusp height \(h\) results?
Formula: \(h \approx \dfrac{s^2}{8R}\).
Hint: square the stepover first, then divide by \(8R = 48\).

Worked example (3-axis tool center): a contact point \(p=(10,20,5)\) mm has outward unit normal \(n=(0,0,1)\); the ball-end radius is \(R=6\) mm. Find the tool-center point \(c\).
Formula: \(c = p + R\,n\).
Hint: scale the normal by \(R\) first, then add it componentwise to \(p\).

Worked example (MRR): axial depth \(a_p=2\) mm, radial width \(a_e=3\) mm, feed \(v_f=500\) mm/min. Find the material removal rate.
Formula: \(\mathrm{MRR} = a_p\, a_e\, v_f\).
Hint: just multiply the three numbers together.

State the Pythagorean relation encoded by the cusp diagram's right triangle (legs \(s/2\) and \(R-h\), hypotenuse \(R\)).
Formula: \(R^2 = (s/2)^2 + (R-h)^2\).
Hint: hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the two legs squared.

The hypotenuse is the tool radius \(R\); the legs are the half-stepover \(s/2\) and the vertical \(R-h\).
\(\boxed{R^2 = (s/2)^2 + (R-h)^2}\)

For a ball-end tool, where does the tool center sit relative to the contact point \(p\) with unit normal \(n\)?
Formula: \(c = p + R\,n\).
Hint: the offset is along the surface normal by exactly the tool radius.

The toolpath is the surface offset outward along the normal by \(R\).
\(\boxed{c = p + R\,n}\)

Closing test (finish target met?): for \(R=6\) mm and chosen stepover \(s=0.69\) mm, verify the cusp meets the \(h=0.01\) mm target using the exact formula.
Formula: \(h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (s/2)^2}\).
Hint: a pass means the computed \(h\) comes out at or below \(0.01\) mm.

Closing test (gouge check): a ball-end tool of radius \(R=6\) mm machines a concave fillet of radius of curvature \(\rho=4\) mm. Does it gouge?
Formula: local gouge when \(R > \rho_{\text{concave}}\).
Hint: a pass (no gouge) needs \(R \le \rho_{\text{concave}}\).

Compare: \(R = 6 > \rho = 4\)
The tool is larger than the smallest concave radius, so it cannot reach in.
\(\boxed{\text{GOUGES — fail; pick } R \le 4\text{ mm}}\)

Write the local gouge condition for a ball-end tool.
Formula: \(R > \rho_{\text{concave}}\).
Hint: name what each side is — tool radius vs smallest concave radius.

Gouging occurs when the tool radius exceeds the smallest concave radius of curvature.
\(\boxed{R > \rho_{\text{concave}}}\)

Write the material removal rate in terms of axial depth, radial width, and feed.
Formula: \(\mathrm{MRR} = a_p\, a_e\, v_f\).
Hint: it is just the product of the three cutting quantities.

Axial depth \(a_p\), radial width \(a_e\), feed \(v_f\).
\(\boxed{\mathrm{MRR} = a_p\, a_e\, v_f}\)

Which earlier topic supplies the curvature that bounds the tool radius for gouge avoidance?
Hint: it is the per-point bending of the surface.
Discrete curvature ([[18_discrete-curvature]]) gives \(\rho_{\text{concave}}\), the smallest concave radius the tool must respect.

Why might stone carving need a 5-axis (tilting) toolpath instead of fixed 3-axis?
Hint: think about features a straight-down tool axis cannot enter.
A fixed tool axis cannot reach undercuts or steep walls; tilting adds an orientation degree of freedom to reach them.

What does each toolpath contact point plus the tool axis define, and what is it turned into?
Hint: position plus orientation is a rigid pose.
A tool pose in SE(3) ([[05_transforms-se3]]); the sequence is post-processed to G-code or robot joint targets.

Units pitfall in toolpaths — what goes wrong mixing shop and site units?
Hint: shop is millimetres, site is metres.
Mixing millimetre shop units with metre site coordinates scales the stepover by 1000×, corrupting the path.
Halving the stepover {{c1::quarters}} the cusp height (since \(h\propto s^2\)) but {{c2::doubles}} the number of passes.
Fab Loop · Toolpath / CAM

The toolpath is the surface {{c1::offset}} by the tool radius along the {{c2::normal}}.
hint: \(c = p + R\,n\).
Fab Loop · Toolpath / CAM
For a ball-end tool, local gouging happens when the tool radius \(R\) {{c1::exceeds}} the smallest {{c2::concave}} radius of curvature.
Fab Loop · Toolpath / CAM
